topics.title: Evolution from 1G to 5G: Analog Voice Systems in 1G
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT741 - ADVANCED MOBILE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Evolution from 1G to 5G: Analog Voice Systems in 1G

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of 1G (First Generation Mobile Networks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 1.1)**
> **First Generation (1G)** refers to the first generation of wireless cellular telecommunication systems, commercially deployed in the early **1980s**, which used **analog modulation** techniques (primarily Frequency Modulation / FM) to provide **voice-only** services over a circuit-switched network architecture.

The 1G systems were entirely **analog** in nature, meaning that human voice was transmitted as a continuously varying electrical waveform over radio channels, rather than being converted into binary digits. This generation laid the foundation for cellular mobile communication by introducing the **cellular concept** (frequency reuse across non-adjacent cells) proposed originally by AT&T Bell Labs.

### Key 1G Standards Worldwide

| Standard | Country | Year | Operating Band | Channel Spacing |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **AMPS** (Advanced Mobile Phone System) | USA | 1983 | **824–894 MHz** | **30 kHz** |
| **NMT** (Nordic Mobile Telephone) | Scandinavia | 1981 | **450 MHz / 900 MHz** | 25 kHz |
| **TACS** (Total Access Communication System) | UK | 1985 | **890–960 MHz** | **25 kHz** |
| **C-Netz** | Germany | 1985 | **450 MHz** | 20 kHz |
| **Radiocom 2000** | France | 1985 | **400 MHz / 900 MHz** | 12.5 / 25 kHz |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Vintage Carved-Telephone of Wireless"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: 1G is to Mobile Phones what a Rotary Bakelite Telephone is to Smartphones.**

Imagine you have two friends in a large noisy room. To talk privately, you give each of them a **single dedicated wire** that physically connects their microphones to each other's speakers. While they talk, **no one else can use that wire** — it stays open even when they are silent. This is **1G**: every caller is given a *dedicated analog pipeline* (a frequency channel) that is reserved for them alone, even during silent pauses. Conversations travel as smoothly varying electrical currents (like radio ripples), not as digital codes.

If you walk from one room (cell) to another (cell), an invisible butler (the base station controller) hands your wire over to the new room — this handover in 1G was rough and often dropped calls, just like a clumsy butler unplugging your line mid-sentence.

---

## 1.3 Characteristic Features of 1G Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Hallmark Properties of 1G**
> - **Modulation:** Analog **Frequency Modulation (FM)**
> - **Multiple Access Technique:** **FDMA** (Frequency Division Multiple Access)
> - **Service:** **Voice-only** (no SMS, no data)
> - **Switching:** **Circuit-switched**
> - **Data Rate:** Approximately **2.4 kbps**
> - **Security:** **None** (eavesdropping possible with RF scanners)
> - **Handoff:** Hard handoff (break-before-make)
> - **Roaming:** Limited international roaming (different standards incompatible)

---

## 1.4 Why 1G Was Revolutionary Yet Flawed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight**
> 1G proved that *mobility + wide-area cellular coverage* is commercially viable. However, its **three fatal flaws** set the agenda for all future generations:
> 1. **Capacity bottleneck** — analog channels are wide (30 kHz each).
> 2. **Zero security** — anyone with an RF scanner could listen in.
> 3. **No data services** — pagers & landlines still dominated text/data.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a 1G Cellular System

A 1G network is built from four logical entities:

1. **Mobile Station (MS)** — the handheld phone, often a large "brick" device.
2. **Base Station (BS)** — fixed tower + antenna + transceiver; one BS serves one cell.
3. **Mobile Switching Center (MSC)** — also called **MTSO** (Mobile Telephone Switching Office); central controller.
4. **Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN)** — the existing landline backbone.

When a user dials a number:

> **MS** → radio uplink → **BS** → landline/microwave link → **MSC** → **PSTN** → destination.

The MSC continuously monitors signal strength from nearby BSs and, when the MS moves out of one cell, executes a **handoff** to the next BS using a dedicated control channel (the *setup channel*).

---

## 2.2 FDMA — How 1G Shared the Air

> [!NOTE]
> **FDMA = Frequency Division Multiple Access**
> The available system bandwidth is partitioned into **disjoint frequency bands**. Each active call is assigned **one** such band for the entire duration of the call.

Because each FM voice channel occupies **30 kHz** in AMPS, and the total uplink band is **25 MHz** (825–850 MHz), the **raw number of channels** is:

$$ N_{\text{channels}} = \frac{B_{\text{total}}}{B_{\text{channel}}} = \frac{25 \text{ MHz}}{30 \text{ kHz}} = 833 $$

Of these, **21** are reserved as **control channels** (paging + access), leaving roughly **416** duplex voice channels per system. Since each cell must reuse frequencies with a **cluster size N**, the **channels per cell** drop further:

$$ C_{\text{per cell}} = \frac{N_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{cluster}}} $$

where $N_{\text{cluster}}$ is the cluster size (typically **7** in AMPS for the omni-directional case).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam favourites for 1G capacity and reuse calculations.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | **Number of channels per system** | $N = B_{\text{total}} / B_{\text{ch}}$ | Total simultaneous calls supported by operator |
| 2 | **Channels per cell** | $C = N / N_{\text{cluster}}$ | Channels a single cell can serve |
| 3 | **Cluster size (omni-directional)** | $N_{\text{cluster}} = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$ | Standard hexagonal reuse geometry |
| 4 | **Co-channel Reuse Ratio (Q)** | $Q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3 N_{\text{cluster}}}$ | Separation between co-channel cells |
| 5 | **Carrier-to-Interference Ratio (C/I)** | $\frac{C}{I} = \frac{(D/R)^{4}}{6}$ (omni) | Voice quality threshold; AMPS requires **≥ 18 dB** |
| 6 | **Signal-to-Noise (FM)** | $\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{out}} = \frac{3}{2}\,\beta^{2}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{in}}$ | FM noise improvement factor |
| 7 | **Erlang B (blocking probability)** | $P_{\text{block}} = \frac{A^{C}/C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^{k}/k!}$ | Grade of Service (GoS) of trunked system |
| 8 | **Carrier Frequency** | $f_c = 825 \text{ MHz}$ (AMPS) | The RF center of the uplink band |
| 9 | **Cell Radius (typical 1G)** | $R \approx 2$–$20$ km | Macro-cell coverage |
| 10 | **Hard Handoff Latency** | $\sim 100$–$300$ ms | Noticeable "click" during cell change |

---

## 2.4 Frequency Modulation in 1G — A Closer Look

The baseband voice signal (300 Hz – 3 kHz) is used to modulate a high-frequency carrier via FM. The **Carson's rule** approximates the FM bandwidth:

$$ B_{\text{FM}} = 2(\Delta f + f_m) $$

where $\Delta f$ is the peak frequency deviation ($\approx \pm 12 \text{ kHz}$ for AMPS) and $f_m$ is the highest modulating frequency ($\approx 3 \text{ kHz}$).

For AMPS:
$$ B_{\text{FM}} = 2(12 \text{ kHz} + 3 \text{ kHz}) = 30 \text{ kHz} $$

This **30 kHz** is precisely why AMPS chose a **30 kHz channel spacing**.

---

## 2.5 Handoff in 1G — Break-Before-Make

1G used **hard handoff** (also called *break-before-make*). The MSC constantly monitors RSSI (Received Signal Strength Indicator) from neighboring BSs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Procedure:**
> 1. MS reports BS identity & RSS via the *reverse control channel*.
> 2. MSC decides that the new BS has stronger RSS.
> 3. **Old BS releases the frequency** (the call momentarily breaks).
> 4. **New BS allocates a new frequency** (a fresh 30 kHz channel).
> 5. The MS re-tunes to the new frequency.

Because the new channel has a different frequency, 1G hands the call over to a *different physical RF channel*, hence "hard" handoff.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Study 1G in 2024?**
> - **Historical foundation** — every concept (cell, cluster, handoff, MSC) used in 5G traces back to 1G.
> - **Spectrum economics** — 1G demonstrates the high cost of wide analog channels, motivating digital compression.
> - **Security lessons** — 1G's lack of encryption directly led to GSM's A5/1 cipher in 2G.
> - **Backward compatibility** — modern networks still allocate spectrum for legacy analog services in some regions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — AMPS Channel Count & Cell Capacity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU-style):** The AMPS system uses a **25 MHz** uplink band from **825 MHz to 850 MHz**, with a **30 kHz** channel bandwidth. The system operator uses a **7-cell reuse pattern** with **21 control channels** reserved. Calculate: (a) total voice channels per system, (b) voice channels per cell.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total bandwidth available for uplink (reverse link):**
$$ B_{\text{total}} = 850 \text{ MHz} - 825 \text{ MHz} = 25 \text{ MHz} = 25 \times 10^{6} \text{ Hz} $$

**Step 2 — Total raw channels (including control):**
$$ N_{\text{raw}} = \frac{B_{\text{total}}}{B_{\text{ch}}} = \frac{25 \times 10^{6}}{30 \times 10^{3}} = 833.33 $$

So roughly **833** channels total per operator (uplink).

**Step 3 — Subtract control channels (21):**
$$ N_{\text{voice}} = 833 - 21 = 812 \text{ voice channels} $$

(In practice, AMPS reserves 21 *per system*, giving ~416 paired duplex — the above simplification considers half the band. For exam purposes, present whichever the question demands.)

**Step 4 — Channels per cell with cluster size $N_c = 7$:**
$$ C_{\text{cell}} = \frac{N_{\text{voice}}}{N_c} = \frac{812}{7} \approx 116 \text{ channels per cell} $$

Each cell can therefore host ~116 simultaneous calls using analog 1G FDMA.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Co-channel Interference (C/I) for 1G AMPS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** For an AMPS cellular system with omni-directional antennas at every cell, compute the **carrier-to-interference ratio** in dB when the cluster size is $N_c = 7$. AMPS requires C/I $\geq$ **18 dB** for acceptable voice quality.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Co-channel Reuse Ratio:**
$$ Q = \frac{D}{R} = \sqrt{3 N_c} = \sqrt{3 \times 7} = \sqrt{21} \approx 4.58 $$

**Step 2 — For omni-directional 6-interferer case, C/I ratio is:**
$$ \frac{C}{I} = \frac{(Q)^{4}}{6} = \frac{(4.58)^{4}}{6} $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $(Q)^{4}$:**
$$ (Q)^{4} = (4.58)^{2} \times (4.58)^{2} = 20.98 \times 20.98 \approx 440.16 $$

**Step 4 — Divide by 6 interfering co-channel cells:**
$$ \frac{C}{I} = \frac{440.16}{6} \approx 73.36 $$

**Step 5 — Convert to dB:**
$$ \left(\frac{C}{I}\right)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(73.36) \approx 18.65 \text{ dB} $$

**Step 6 — Compare with threshold:**
$$ 18.65 \text{ dB} \geq 18 \text{ dB} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Specification is JUST satisfied} $$

This is why AMPS uses $N_c = 7$ as the *minimum* cluster size.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Erlang-B Trunking for 1G

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A 1G cell has **C = 116** channels. If the offered traffic per user is **0.02 Erlangs** and **2000 users** are active, find the blocking probability.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Total offered traffic per cell:**
$$ A = (\text{traffic per user}) \times (\text{users per cell}) $$
$$ A = 0.02 \times 2000 = 40 \text{ Erlangs} $$

**Step 2 — Apply Erlang-B formula:**
$$ P_{\text{block}} = \frac{A^{C}/C!}{\sum_{k=0}^{C} A^{k}/k!} $$

With $A = 40$ and $C = 116$, the numerator is dominated by huge factorial terms, and the result (from tables/calculators) is:

$$ P_{\text{block}} \approx 0.002 \;\; (0.2\,\%) $$

**Interpretation:** Only **2 calls in 1000** are blocked — an excellent Grade of Service (GoS).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — 1G vs Modern Generations

| Parameter | **1G (Analog)** | 2G (GSM) | 3G (WCDMA) | 4G (LTE) | 5G (NR) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Year | **1980s** | 1990s | 2000s | 2010s | 2020s |
| Modulation | **FM** | GMSK | QPSK/16-QAM | up to 256-QAM | up to 1024-QAM |
| Multiple Access | **FDMA** | TDMA + FDMA | CDMA | OFDMA | OFDMA + NOMA |
| Channel BW | **30 kHz** | 200 kHz | 5 MHz | up to 20 MHz | up to 400 MHz |
| Data Rate | **2.4 kbps** | 64 kbps | 2 Mbps | 100 Mbps | 1–10 Gbps |
| Switching | **Circuit** | Circuit + Packet | Packet | All-IP | All-IP + slicing |
| Handoff | **Hard** | Hard | Soft | Soft / X2 | Beam-level |
| Security | **None** | A5/1 cipher | KASUMI | SNOW 3G / AES | 5G-AKA / SUCI |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — 1G Cellular Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MS1["Mobile Station MS A"]:::ms
    MS2["Mobile Station MS B"]:::ms
    BS1["Base Station BS1 Cell 1"]:::bs
    BS2["Base Station BS2 Cell 2"]:::bs
    BS3["Base Station BS3 Cell 3"]:::bs
    MSC["Mobile Switching Center MSC MTSO"]:::msc
    PSTN["Public Switched Telephone Network PSTN"]:::pstn
    DEST["Called Party Landline or Mobile"]:::dest

    MS1 -->|FDMA 30 kHz FM uplink 825 MHz| BS1
    MS2 -->|FDMA 30 kHz FM uplink 825 MHz| BS2
    BS1 -->|Microwave or leased line| MSC
    BS2 -->|Microwave or leased line| MSC
    BS3 -->|Microwave or leased line| MSC
    MSC -->|SS7 signaling trunk| PSTN
    PSTN --> DEST

    classDef ms fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A0522D,color:#000
    classDef bs fill:#80C8FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef msc fill:#B0E0A0,stroke:#1B4D2E,color:#000
    classDef pstn fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef dest fill:#F08080,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A mobile's voice is captured as an analog FM waveform on a 30 kHz uplink channel. The BS demodulates, sends it over a wired link to the **MSC**, which routes the call through the **PSTN**. The called party (mobile or landline) receives the analog waveform. All call control (handoffs, channel assignment) happens at the MSC.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — FDMA Channel Allocation in 1G

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Total Band 25 MHz 825 to 850 MHz"]:::total
    B1["Ch 1 30 kHz Voice User A"]:::ch
    B2["Ch 2 30 kHz Voice User B"]:::ch
    B3["Ch 3 30 kHz Voice User C"]:::ch
    B4["Guard Band 0 kHz adjacent"]:::gap
    B5["Ch N 30 kHz Voice User N"]:::ch
    B6["21 Control Channels Setup and Paging"]:::ctrl

    A --> B1
    A --> B2
    A --> B3
    A --> B4
    A --> B5
    A --> B6

    classDef total fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef ch fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef gap fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation:** Each voice user monopolises a 30 kHz slot for the *entire* call, including silent gaps. This is the fundamental spectral inefficiency that motivated 2G's digital TDMA approach.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Evolution Roadmap from 1G to 5G

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1["1G 1980s Analog FM FDMA 2.4 kbps"]:::g1
    G2["2G 1990s Digital GSM TDMA 64 kbps SMS"]:::g2
    G25["2.5G GPRS 171 kbps packet data"]:::g2b
    G3["3G 2000s WCDMA CDMA 2 Mbps Mobile Internet"]:::g3
    G35["3.5G HSPA 14 Mbps"]:::g3b
    G4["4G 2010s LTE OFDMA All IP 100 Mbps"]:::g4
    G45["4.5G LTE Advanced 1 Gbps"]:::g4b
    G5["5G 2020s NR mmWave massive MIMO 10 Gbps URLLC eMBB mMTC"]:::g5

    G1 --> G2 --> G25 --> G3 --> G35 --> G4 --> G45 --> G5

    classDef g1 fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef g2 fill:#1E90FF,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef g2b fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef g3 fill:#228B22,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef g3b fill:#2E8B57,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef g4 fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
    classDef g4b fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef g5 fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,color:#FFF
```

> [!NOTE]
> The roadmap highlights three *generational transitions* triggered by 1G's flaws:
> - 1G → 2G: Analog → Digital (security, capacity)
> - 2G → 3G: Voice-centric → Data-centric
> - 4G → 5G: Mobile broadband → Hyper-connected IoT/URLLC

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — 1G Hard Handoff Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MS as Mobile Station
    participant BS1 as Old Base Station
    participant MSC as Mobile Switching Center
    participant BS2 as New Base Station

    MS->>BS1: Reverse control channel RSSI report
    BS1->>MSC: Forward measurements
    MSC->>MSC: Compare RSSI thresholds
    MSC->>BS1: Order to release channel
    BS1-->>MS: Disconnect command break before make
    MSC->>BS2: Allocate new 30 kHz voice channel
    BS2->>MS: Tune to new frequency
    MS->>BS2: Resume voice on new channel
    Note over MS,BS2: Hard handoff complete audible click may occur
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-A question carries **3 marks**. Answer in *3–4 crisp sentences*. Avoid essays.

### Q1. Briefly explain the salient features of the First Generation (1G) analog mobile communication systems.
`[KTU University Exam – May 2018 / Dec 2022 – Module 1]`

**Model Answer:**
1G systems, introduced in the early 1980s, were the first commercial cellular networks. They employed **analog Frequency Modulation (FM)** for voice and **FDMA** for channel access with **30 kHz** channel spacing (AMPS). Services were limited to **voice-only**; no SMS or data was supported. Switching was **circuit-based** and handoff was **hard** (break-before-make). Security was minimal and capacity per cell was low.

---

### Q2. Compare the AMPS and NMT 1G standards in terms of channel bandwidth and frequency band.
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2019 – Module 1]`

**Model Answer:**
**AMPS** (USA, 1983) operates in the **800–900 MHz** band with a **30 kHz** channel spacing. **NMT** (Scandinavia, 1981) operates at **450 MHz / 900 MHz** with a **25 kHz** channel spacing. AMPS later became the de-facto reference for 1G; NMT was the first fully automatic cellular system. Both used analog FM but differed in their signaling and duplexing (FDD) implementations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Rule:** For every 14-mark question in Module 1, students must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B. Both choices carry equal marks. Each Part-B question typically has two sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### ⭐ Choice A — Question A (14 Marks)

#### Q-A (a) [7 marks] — Explain the evolution of mobile communication from 1G to 5G with a neat comparison table.
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 1]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define each generation (1G):**
1G (1980s) introduced analog FM cellular systems like AMPS, NMT, TACS. Voice-only, FDMA, no security.

**Step 2 — 2G (1990s):**
2G digitised the air interface using GSM, IS-95. Introduced **TDMA**, **SMS**, encryption (A5/1). Data via CSD at 9.6 kbps.

**Step 3 — 2.5G / 3G (2000s):**
GPRS, EDGE bridged to 3G. 3G (UMTS/WCDMA) introduced **CDMA**, video calling, mobile internet at 2 Mbps.

**Step 4 — 4G (2010s):**
LTE / WiMAX. **All-IP**, **OFDMA**, peak 100 Mbps. Paved the way for smartphones, VoLTE, streaming.

**Step 5 — 5G (2020s):**
NR (New Radio). Three pillars — **eMBB**, **URLLC**, **mMTC**. Peak 10 Gbps, **mmWave**, **massive MIMO**, **network slicing**.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | 1G | 2G | 3G | 4G | 5G |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Year | 1980s | 1990s | 2000s | 2010s | 2020s |
| Signal | Analog | Digital | Digital | Digital | Digital |
| Multiple Access | FDMA | TDMA | CDMA | OFDMA | OFDMA + NOMA |
| Switching | Circuit | Circuit + Packet | Packet | All-IP | All-IP + Slicing |
| Peak Data | 2.4 kbps | 64 kbps | 2 Mbps | 100 Mbps | 10 Gbps |
| Handoff | Hard | Hard | Soft | Soft / X2 | Beam-level |
| Security | None | A5/1 | KASUMI | AES | 5G-AKA / SUCI |

---

#### Q-A (b) [7 marks] — With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a 1G AMPS cellular system and describe the function of the MTSO.
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 1]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Architecture components:**
1G AMPS has four components — **MS** (handset), **BS** (cell tower), **MTSO/MSC** (switching center), **PSTN** (landline network).

**Step 2 — Block diagram (textual):**
```
MS A ----FDMA 30kHz FM----> BS1 --->
                                 |
MS B ----FDMA 30kHz FM----> BS2 ---|--> MTSO ---> PSTN ---> Called Party
                                 |
                              BS3 --->
```

**Step 3 — Functions of MTSO:**
- Allocates and deallocates **voice channels** to MSs.
- Performs **handoff** decisions between BSs based on RSSI.
- Interfaces with **PSTN** via **SS7** signaling.
- Manages **billing, authentication, and location registration**.
- Controls **paging** — broadcast of incoming calls.

**Step 4 — Significance:**
The MTSO is the *brain* of 1G; without it, mobility, handoff, and interconnectivity are impossible.

---

### ⭐ Choice B — Question B (14 Marks)

#### Q-B (a) [7 marks] — Discuss the multiple-access technique used in 1G. How does it differ from the techniques used in 2G and 3G?
`[KTU University Exam – May 2019 – Module 1]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — 1G uses FDMA:**
In FDMA, the spectrum is sliced into non-overlapping 30 kHz bands. Each user gets **one frequency slot** for the entire call. Simple hardware but **wasteful** — silent periods waste bandwidth.

**Step 2 — 2G uses TDMA + FDMA:**
GSM combines FDMA (carrier frequency) with **TDMA** (8 time-slots per 200 kHz carrier). Slots can serve different users in turn, raising efficiency. Each user transmits only **1/8** of the time.

**Step 3 — 3G uses CDMA:**
WCDMA spreads each user's signal across the **entire 5 MHz band** using a unique **pseudo-random code**. Multiple users share the same spectrum simultaneously; codes allow separation. Enables **soft handoff** (same frequency in new cell).

**Step 4 — Comparison:**

| Aspect | 1G (FDMA) | 2G (TDMA/FDMA) | 3G (CDMA) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Bandwidth/User | **30 kHz** | **200 kHz / 8 = 25 kHz** | Spread over 5 MHz |
| Sharing | Frequency only | Frequency + Time | Code |
| Soft Handoff | No | No | Yes |
| Capacity | Low | Medium | High |

---

#### Q-B (b) [7 marks] — Calculate the number of voice channels per cell in an AMPS system with total bandwidth 25 MHz, channel bandwidth 30 kHz, 21 control channels, and cluster size 7.
`[KTU University Exam – May 2019 – Module 1]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Raw channels:**
$$ N_{\text{raw}} = \frac{25 \times 10^{6}}{30 \times 10^{3}} = 833.33 \approx 833 $$

**Step 2 — Voice channels after subtracting control channels:**
$$ N_{\text{voice}} = 833 - 21 = 812 $$

**Step 3 — Channels per cell (cluster size $N_c = 7$):**
$$ C_{\text{cell}} = \frac{N_{\text{voice}}}{N_c} = \frac{812}{7} \approx 116 \text{ channels per cell} $$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:**
A single AMPS cell with $N_c = 7$ can support **~116 simultaneous calls**. If Grade of Service of 2% is needed (Erlang-B), and each user generates 0.025 Erlangs, then the cell can serve about:
$$ \text{Users} \approx \frac{95 \text{ Erlangs (from Erlang-B for C=116)}}{0.025} \approx 3800 \text{ subscribers} $$

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing AMPS (USA) with TACS (UK) channel bandwidths.** AMPS = 30 kHz; TACS = 25 kHz. Using the wrong value will mark your numerical answer **wrong**.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract control channels.** The 21 paging/access channels are *not* usable for voice. Always subtract before computing per-cell capacity.
> 3. **Mixing up hard vs. soft handoff.** 1G/2G use **hard handoff**; 3G and beyond use **soft**. Examiners award 1–2 marks for this distinction.
> 4. **Calling 1G "digital" or "secure".** 1G is **analog** and **insecure** — instant loss of 2 marks if stated otherwise.
> 5. **Skipping the formula for cluster size $N_c = i^{2} + ij + j^{2}$** in derivation questions. Always derive this in HCF reuse problems.
> 6. **Not converting C/I to dB.** Always end co-channel interference problems with the **dB** conversion using $10 \log_{10}(C/I)$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the topic "Analog Voice Systems in 1G"**
>
> - [x] **1G = Analog + FM + FDMA + Circuit-switched + Voice-only + Insecure.**
> - [x] Major 1G standards: **AMPS (USA, 30 kHz), NMT (Nordic, 25 kHz), TACS (UK, 25 kHz), C-Netz (Germany).**
> - [x] AMPS uplink band: **825–850 MHz**; downlink: **870–890 MHz**.
> - [x] Channel bandwidth (AMPS): **30 kHz** = $2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(12 + 3)$ kHz.
> - [x] Multiple access: **FDMA** — each user = one 30 kHz channel for the whole call.
> - [x] Handoff: **Hard (break-before-make)**; latency ~100–300 ms.
> - [x] Key entities: **MS + BS + MTSO/MSC + PSTN**.
> - [x] MTSO duties: **channel allocation, handoff, paging, billing**.
> - [x] Capacity formula: $C = (B_{\text{total}}/B_{\text{ch}} - N_{\text{ctrl}}) / N_c$.
> - [x] Co-channel reuse: $Q = \sqrt{3 N_c}$; C/I threshold (AMPS) ≥ **18 dB**.
> - [x] Total system channels in AMPS ≈ **833 raw, 416 paired duplex** (after accounting for control + duplex).
> - [x] **Flaws of 1G** (causes of 2G): capacity, security, no data, expensive handsets.
> - [x] 1G used **circuit-switching** — call set-up reserves a dedicated physical path.
> - [x] No **encryption, no SMS, no roaming across standards** — major drawbacks.
> - [x] 1G operates in the **800–900 MHz** macro-cell band; typical cell radius **2–20 km**.
> - [x] The "cellular" concept of **frequency reuse** in 1G is the *single most important* contribution that survived to 5G.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Radio Systems in 2G, Voice and Messaging Services, TDMA-Based GSM, CDMA, 2.5G (GPRS), 2.75G (EDGE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT741 - ADVANCED MOBILE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Digital Radio Systems in 2G, Voice and Messaging Services, TDMA-Based GSM, CDMA, 2.5G (GPRS), 2.75G (EDGE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Digital Revolution in Mobile Communications (2G)

The transition from **1G (Analog)** to **2G (Second Generation)** digital cellular systems represents the most fundamental paradigm shift in mobile history: the migration from continuous analog radio signals to discrete, bit-streamed digital radio transmission. The KTU 2024 syllabus defines 2G as a family of digital cellular standards engineered primarily for **circuit-switched voice telephony**, **short messaging (SMS)**, and limited low-speed data, operating predominantly in the **900 MHz** and **1800 MHz** bands.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PEECT741):** 2G is NOT a single standard. It is an *umbrella family* of digital radio access technologies, each based on a different **multiple-access technique**: TDMA, FDMA, or CDMA. GSM dominates globally; IS-95 (CDMAOne) dominates in the USA and parts of Korea.

**Key Standardized Metrics of 2G (Bolded for Recall):**
- **Carrier Bandwidth:** **200 kHz** per RF carrier (GSM)
- **Gross Bit Rate per Carrier:** **270.833 kbps** (GSM)
- **Voice Codec Rate:** **13 kbps** (GSM Full-Rate, after channel coding source rate)
- **Frequency Bands:** **GSM-900** (Uplink 890–915 MHz, Downlink 935–960 MHz); **DCS-1800** (1710–1785 / 1805–1880 MHz)
- **Frame Duration:** **4.615 ms** (GSM TDMA frame)
- **Timeslot Duration:** **577 µs** (8 slots per GSM TDMA frame)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Hotel Reservation System

To intuitively grasp the difference between the multiple-access schemes in 2G, imagine a **hotel with one elevator (the radio channel)** serving many guests (mobile users).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — TDMA (GSM):** The hotel gives each guest a strict **time slot**. Guest A rides at minute 0–1, Guest B at minute 1–2, and so on. Everyone shares the *same* elevator (same frequency), but at *different times*. This is **Time Division Multiple Access**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — FDMA (1G analog, AMPS):** The hotel builds **multiple elevators side-by-side**, each at a slightly different shaft position (different frequency). Each guest is permanently assigned to one elevator. This is **Frequency Division Multiple Access**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — CDMA (IS-95):** The hotel gives every guest a **unique secret language** (a pseudo-random code). All guests ride the *same* elevator *at the same time*, but the receptionist can only understand her assigned guest's language because she alone holds the deciphering key. This is **Code Division Multiple Access**.

### 1.3 Voice and Messaging Services in 2G

The two flagship services introduced or popularized by 2G are:

**A. Voice Service (Circuit-Switched)**
Voice is digitized by a **speech codec**, error-protected by channel coding, interleaved across bursts, and transmitted over a dedicated radio channel. In GSM, the codec is the **RPE-LTP (Regular Pulse Excitation – Long Term Prediction)** codec producing a **13 kbps** net bitstream, channel-coded up to **22.8 kbps**, and finally mapped into bursts at **270.833 kbps** gross.

**B. Short Messaging Service (SMS)**
SMS is a **store-and-forward** text service supporting up to **160 7-bit ASCII characters** (140 octets). It travels over the **SDCCH (Stand-alone Dedicated Control Channel)** during call setup or the **SACCH (Slow Associated Control Channel)** during a call, using the **SS7 signaling backbone** between MSCs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** SMS was a *side-effect* of the GSM signaling design (the unused capacity in the control channels). It was never intended as a primary revenue service — yet it became the most-used data service in human history, fundamentally shaping modern OTT messaging.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The GSM Architecture (TDMA-Based 2G)

GSM is a **BSS (Base Station Subsystem) + NSS (Network Switching Subsystem)** architecture. Each functional block has a specific role:

| Functional Block | Acronym | Role | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mobile Station | MS | User Equipment (ME + SIM) | The customer's car |
| Base Transceiver Station | BTS | Radio transmission/reception | Petrol pump antenna |
| Base Station Controller | BSC | Radio resource management, handover | Station manager |
| Mobile Switching Center | MSC | Circuit-switched call routing | City telephone exchange |
| Home Location Register | HLR | Permanent subscriber DB | Permanent address book |
| Visitor Location Register | VLR | Temporary subscriber DB (roaming) | Hotel guest book |
| Authentication Center | AuC | Security keys, Ki storage | Vault |
| Equipment Identity Register | EIR | Stolen/blacklisted handset DB | Police wanted list |

**The "Why" behind GSM's TDMA Frame Hierarchy:**

GSM organizes its radio resource into a strict hierarchy to enable **precise synchronization** and **frequency hopping**. The hierarchy is:

$$\text{Hyperframe} \; (2048 \text{ superframes}) \rightarrow \text{Superframe} \; (1326 \text{ frames}) \rightarrow \text{Multiframe} \rightarrow \text{Frame} \rightarrow \text{Burst}$$

- **Multiframe (Traffic):** 26 frames → **120 ms** (carries TCH)
- **Multiframe (Control):** 51 frames → **235.4 ms** (carries BCCH, CCCH, SDCCH, SACCH)
- **TDMA Frame:** 8 timeslots × **577 µs** = **4.615 ms**
- **Burst:** 156.25 bits → **0.577 ms**

### 2.2 Logical Channels in GSM

GSM distinguishes between **traffic channels (TCH)** and **signaling/control channels**. The hierarchy below is critical for KTU questions:

> [!NOTE]
> **Traffic Channels (TCH):** Carry user payload (voice/data).
> - TCH/F — Full rate (**13 kbps** net, **22.8 kbps** coded)
> - TCH/H — Half rate (**6.5 kbps**)
>
> **Control Channels (CCH):** Carry signaling.
> - **BCH (Broadcast):** BCCH, CCCH (PCH, RACH, AGCH)
> - **DCCH (Dedicated):** SDCCH, SACCH, FACCH

### 2.3 GSM Burst Structure (High-Yield)

Each burst occupies **one timeslot (577 µs)** and contains **156.25 bit periods** (148 data bits + training sequence + tail + guard). Five burst types exist:

| Burst Type | Bits | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Normal Burst (NB)** | 156 | Voice/data + 26-bit training sequence midamble |
| **Frequency Correction Burst (FB)** | 156 | Carries all zeros → pure sine wave (carrier recovery) |
| **Synchronization Burst (SB)** | 156 | Carries TDMA frame number + BSIC |
| **Access Burst (AB)** | 156 | RACH uplink; long guard period (68 bits) for first contact |
| **Dummy Burst (DB)** | 156 | Filler for unused timeslots |

### 2.4 CDMA — The Spread-Spectrum Foundation (IS-95 / CDMAOne)

IS-95 is the **2G CDMA standard** deployed in the **800 MHz** and **1900 MHz** PCS bands. Its core idea is **Direct-Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)**:

- Each user's narrowband voice signal (≈ **9.6 kbps**) is multiplied (XORed) by a high-rate **Pseudo-Noise (PN) code** running at the **chip rate of 1.2288 Mcps** (Mega-chips per second).
- All users transmit simultaneously in the **same 1.25 MHz** band.
- The receiver correlates the incoming composite signal with the *known* PN code of the desired user, collapsing (despreading) only that user's signal back to narrowband; all other users' signals remain spread and appear as **noise**.

**Processing Gain (Pg)** — the hallmark CDMA metric:

$$P_g = \frac{\text{Chip Rate}}{\text{Information Bit Rate}} = \frac{W}{R}$$

For IS-95 reverse link: $W = 1.2288 \text{ Mcps}$, $R = 9.6 \text{ kbps}$, hence **Pg = 128 (≈ 21 dB)**.

### 2.5 The 2.5G Bridge — GPRS (General Packet Radio Service)

GPRS overlays **packet-switched** data onto the existing GSM radio infrastructure by:
- Releasing the **TCH** reservation philosophy — users share **PDCH (Packet Data CHannel)** time slots dynamically.
- Introducing the **GPRS Tunneling Protocol (GTP)** for IP packets between **SGSN** (Serving GPRS Support Node) and **GGSN** (Gateway GPRS Support Node).
- Supporting **multislot classes** — a single MS can use **up to 8 timeslots** simultaneously.

Theoretical per-user throughput (with all 8 slots and CS-4 coding):

$$\text{Throughput}_{\max} = 8 \times 21.4 \text{ kbps/slot} = 171.2 \text{ kbps}$$

### 2.6 The 2.75G Bridge — EDGE (Enhanced Data rates for GSM Evolution)

EDGE keeps the **GSM 200 kHz carrier bandwidth** and **8-timeslot TDMA frame** but upgrades the **modulation scheme** and **channel coding**:

- **GMSK** (Gaussian Minimum Shift Keying) → **8-PSK** (8-Phase Shift Keying)
- 8-PSK packs **3 bits per symbol** instead of 1, tripling the raw bit rate.
- Combined with **MCS-1 to MCS-9** coding schemes (Link Adaptation), peak throughput rises to:

$$\text{EDGE Peak} = 8 \text{ slots} \times 59.2 \text{ kbps/slot} \approx 473.6 \text{ kbps}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EDGE = 2.75G** because it is *not* a new radio; it is a **modulation/coding upgrade** over GPRS on the same GSM carrier.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (No Pipe Symbols Used)

| Concept | Formula | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| GSM carrier bandwidth | $BW_{\text{GSM}}$ | **200 kHz** |
| GSM gross bit rate | $R_{\text{gross}} = 148/0.577$ | **270.833 kbps** |
| GSM TDMA frame duration | $T_{\text{frame}}$ | **4.615 ms** |
| GSM timeslot duration | $T_{\text{slot}}$ | **577 µs** |
| GSM voice codec net rate | $R_{\text{voice}}$ | **13 kbps** |
| IS-95 chip rate | $R_{\text{chip}}$ | **1.2288 Mcps** |
| IS-95 processing gain (dB) | $P_g = 10 \log_{10}(W/R)$ | **21 dB** |
| GPRS peak (CS-4 × 8 slots) | $8 \times 21.4$ | **171.2 kbps** |
| EDGE peak (MCS-9 × 8 slots) | $8 \times 59.2$ | **473.6 kbps** |
| Spectral efficiency (GSM voice) | $\eta = \text{Users}/BW$ | ~**0.17 users/kHz** |
| Frequency reuse factor (GSM) | $N$ typical | **3, 4, 7, 12** |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — GSM Gross Bit Rate from Timeslot Geometry

The GSM timeslot duration is **577 µs**, holding **156.25 bit periods**. Derivation of the gross bit rate $R_{\text{gross}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{slot}} &= 577 \;\mu\text{s} \\
\text{Bits per slot} &= 156.25 \\
R_{\text{gross}} &= \frac{\text{Bits per slot}}{T_{\text{slot}}} \\
&= \frac{156.25}{577 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= 270{,}833 \;\text{bits/second} \\
&= 270.833 \;\text{kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** We divide the bit count by the time window to obtain bits per second, then convert microseconds to seconds. The 156.25 bit periods consist of 3 tail bits + 58 data bits + 26 training bits + 58 data bits + 3 tail bits + 8.25 guard bits = 156.25 bits.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of GSM Speech Channels per Carrier

A single GSM RF carrier is **200 kHz** wide. Each carrier supports **8 timeslots**, but only **7** are usable for traffic (one is reserved for control signaling in the baseband):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{channels per carrier}} &= \text{Number of usable timeslots} \\
&= 8 - 1 \\
&= 7 \;\text{voice channels per carrier}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** 8 slots exist per TDMA frame; the 0th slot typically carries BCCH/CCCH control signaling, leaving 7 for TCH traffic. Therefore, one 200 kHz GSM carrier multiplexes **7 simultaneous voice calls**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — IS-95 Processing Gain in Linear and dB Scales

For the IS-95 reverse (uplink) link, voice data is encoded at $R = 9.6$ kbps and spread with a PN sequence at chip rate $W = 1.2288$ Mcps:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{g,\text{linear}} &= \frac{W}{R} \\
&= \frac{1.2288 \times 10^{6}}{9.6 \times 10^{3}} \\
&= 128
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to decibels:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{g,\text{dB}} &= 10 \log_{10}(P_{g,\text{linear}}) \\
&= 10 \log_{10}(128) \\
&= 10 \times 2.1072 \\
&= 21.07 \;\text{dB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Processing gain is a *ratio* of bandwidth expansion; in dB, a factor of 128 corresponds to ≈ 21 dB. This represents the **anti-jamming margin** — CDMA can tolerate 21 dB of interference from other users and still recover the signal below the noise floor.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Theoretical Number of CDMA Users (Simplified Pole Capacity)

For a CDMA system, the **single-cell pole capacity** (ignoring sectorization) is approximated by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{max}} &= 1 + \frac{W/R}{E_b/N_0} \\
&= 1 + \frac{P_g}{(E_b/N_0)_{\text{required}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For IS-95 voice: $P_g = 128$, required $E_b/N_0 \approx 7$ dB = 5.012 (linear):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{max}} &= 1 + \frac{128}{5.012} \\
&= 1 + 25.54 \\
&\approx 26.5 \\
&\approx 26 \;\text{users per cell (single-sector)}
\end{aligned}
$$

With **3-sector antennas** (a typical deployment), this scales to approximately **78 users per cell site**, illustrating CDMA's **soft capacity** vs. GSM's **hard capacity** of 7 × frequency-reuse-pattern users.

**Conversion logic:** The +1 term accounts for the in-cell "self" user; the ratio $P_g/(E_b/N_0)$ represents the *number of interferers* the link budget can withstand.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — GPRS Throughput per Timeslot for Each Coding Scheme

GPRS defines four coding schemes (CS-1 to CS-4) with different code rates. The throughput per slot is calculated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{slot}} &= \frac{\text{RLP payload bits per radio block}}{4 \;\text{frames} \times T_{\text{frame}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For CS-4 (no FEC, header-only protection), 428 bits per 20 ms × 4 frame block:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{CS-4}} &= \frac{428}{4 \times 4.615 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \frac{428}{0.01846} \\
&= 23.18 \;\text{kbps/slot} \\
&\approx 21.4 \;\text{kbps/slot (with RLC/MAC overhead)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For CS-1 (½-rate convolutional coding):

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{CS-1}} &= \frac{240}{0.01846} \\
&\approx 13 \;\text{kbps/slot (gross 9.05 kbps)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** CS-4 trades robustness for speed (no FEC), while CS-1 trades speed for robustness (½-rate). GPRS terminals advertise *multislot classes* specifying how many slots they can simultaneously allocate.

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6 — EDGE Throughput Tripling Rationale

EDGE replaces GMSK (1 bit/symbol) with **8-PSK (3 bits/symbol)**. For the same symbol rate and 200 kHz carrier:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{R_{\text{EDGE}}}{R_{\text{GMSK}}} &= \frac{\log_2(8)}{\log_2(2)} \\
&= \frac{3}{1} \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

Combined with **MCS-9** coding (code rate ≈ 1.0 with 8-PSK → ≈ 59.2 kbps/slot), the per-slot data rate is **3× the GMSK raw rate**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{MCS-9}} &= 3 \times R_{\text{CS-4 baseline}} \\
&\approx 3 \times 21.4 \\
&\approx 64.2 \;\text{kbps gross, 59.2 \;\text{kbps net per slot}
\end{aligned}
$$

Across **8 slots**: $8 \times 59.2 \approx 473.6$ kbps peak.

**Conversion logic:** The same symbol clock produces 3 bits per symbol under 8-PSK instead of 1 under GMSK — a clean tripling of bit throughput with no additional bandwidth.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 GSM System Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    MS[Mobile Station - ME plus SIM] -->|Um Air Interface| BTS1[BTS]
    MS2[Mobile Station] -->|Um| BTS2[BTS]
    BTS1 -->|Abis Interface| BSC[Base Station Controller]
    BTS2 -->|Abis| BSC
    BSC -->|A Interface| MSC[Mobile Switching Center]
    MSC --> HLR[Home Location Register]
    MSC --> VLR[Visitor Location Register]
    MSC --> AuC[Authentication Center]
    MSC --> EIR[Equipment Identity Register]
    MSC -->|PSTN Gateway| PSTN[Public Switched Telephone Network]
    MSC --> GMSC[Gateway MSC]
    GMSC --> PSTN
```

**Reading the diagram:** The **Um** air interface connects MS to BTS; **Abis** connects BTS to BSC; the **A interface** links BSC to MSC; the MSC interconnects with HLR/VLR/AuC/EIR databases and routes calls via the **GMSC** to the PSTN.

---

### 4.2 GSM Radio Frame Hierarchy (Burst → Hyperframe)

```mermaid
graph TD
    HYP[Hyperframe 3 hours 28 minutes 53 seconds 760 ms] --> SF1[Superframe Type 1 - 1326 frames - 6.12 seconds]
    HYP --> SF2[Superframe Type 2 - 1326 frames - 6.12 seconds]
    SF1 --> MF1[Multiframe Traffic - 26 frames - 120 ms]
    SF2 --> MF2[Multiframe Control - 51 frames - 235.4 ms]
    MF1 --> FR1[TDMA Frame - 8 timeslots - 4.615 ms]
    MF2 --> FR2[TDMA Frame - 8 timeslots - 4.615 ms]
    FR1 --> TS1[Timeslot 577 microseconds]
    FR1 --> TS2[Timeslot 577 microseconds]
    FR1 --> TS3[Timeslot 577 microseconds]
    FR1 --> TS4[Timeslot 577 microseconds]
    FR1 --> TS5[Timeslot 577 microseconds]
    FR1 --> TS6[Timeslot 577 microseconds]
    FR1 --> TS7[Timeslot 577 microseconds]
    FR1 --> TS8[Timeslot 577 microseconds]
    TS1 --> BURST[Burst 156.25 bits]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each TDMA frame comprises 8 timeslots of 577 µs each, totaling 4.615 ms. 26 frames aggregate into a traffic multiframe (120 ms), while 51 frames form a control multiframe (235.4 ms). Both multiframe types repeat within a 1326-frame superframe.

---

### 4.3 CDMA Spread Spectrum — Transmission and Reception

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Voice Data 9.6 kbps] -->|XOR| M[Multiplier]
    PN[PN Code 1.2288 Mcps] -->|XOR| M
    M --> RF[RF Modulator]
    RF --> ANT[TX Antenna - 1.25 MHz Bandwidth]
    ANT -->|Over the Air| RXANT[RX Antenna]
    RXANT --> CORR[Correlator]
    PNLOCAL[Local PN Code replica] --> CORR
    CORR --> RESTORED[Restored 9.6 kbps Data plus Narrowband Noise]
    ANT -->|Interferers| NOISE[Other Users Remain Spread Spectrum Noise]
    NOISE -.-> CORR
```

**Reading the diagram:** User data is XOR-multiplied with a high-rate PN code, spreading the bandwidth from ~10 kHz to 1.25 MHz. The receiver uses a synchronized local PN replica in a correlator to despread only the desired signal; other users' signals stay spread and contribute only as wideband noise.

---

### 4.4 Evolution Path from 2G → 2.5G → 2.75G

```mermaid
graph LR
    GSM[2G GSM - GMSK - 9.6 kbps] -->|Add Packet Core| GPRS[2.5G GPRS - GMSK - 171 kbps peak]
    GPRS -->|8-PSK plus MCS-9| EDGE[2.75G EDGE - 8-PSK - 473 kbps peak]
    EDGE -->|UMTS WCDMA| THREE[3G UMTS - 2 Mbps]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The same GSM radio channel is reused at each step. GPRS adds a packet core (SGSN/GGSN) over the existing circuit core; EDGE swaps the modulator from GMSK to 8-PSK without changing the air-interface frame structure. Only at 3G does the radio access technology itself change.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]** *(3 Marks)*

**Q: Differentiate between FDMA, TDMA, and CDMA with one example standard for each.**

**Model Answer:**

- **FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access):** Divides the available spectrum into non-overlapping frequency bands; each user is permanently assigned one band. **Example:** 1G AMPS (Advanced Mobile Phone System).
- **TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access):** Shares the same frequency band by allocating different time slots to different users within a repeating frame. **Example:** 2G GSM.
- **CDMA (Code Division Multiple Access):** Allows all users to occupy the same frequency band simultaneously, separated by unique pseudo-random spreading codes. **Example:** 2G IS-95 (CDMAOne).

Key distinguishing axis: FDMA separates in **frequency**, TDMA in **time**, CDMA in **code**.

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]** *(3 Marks)*

**Q: List the four coding schemes of GPRS and state which one offers the highest throughput. Why?**

**Model Answer:**

The four GPRS coding schemes are **CS-1, CS-2, CS-3, and CS-4**.

- **CS-4** offers the highest throughput (≈ 21.4 kbps per slot, 171.2 kbps peak with 8 slots).
- **Reason:** CS-4 uses **no Forward Error Correction (FEC)** — it transmits only a short header for block detection and leaves the remaining 428 bits as raw payload per 20 ms radio block. It therefore achieves the highest code rate (≈ 1.0) at the cost of robustness; it is only usable when the radio channel is in excellent condition (high C/I).

---

### 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q-A]** *(14 Marks)*

### Question A:
**(a) Explain the GSM system architecture with a neat block diagram.** *(7 Marks)*
**(b) Describe the GSM TDMA frame hierarchy from burst to hyperframe.** *(7 Marks)*

### Model Solution:

**(a) GSM System Architecture** *(7 Marks)*

GSM is structured into three major subsystems:

1. **Mobile Station (MS):** The user equipment consisting of the **Mobile Equipment (ME)** and the **Subscriber Identity Module (SIM)**. The SIM stores the **IMSI, Ki (authentication key), and TMSI** for security and identity.

2. **Base Station Subsystem (BSS):** Comprises:
   - **BTS (Base Transceiver Station):** Handles the radio transmission/reception over the **Um** air interface. Performs channel coding/decoding and encryption.
   - **BSC (Base Station Controller):** Manages radio resources for multiple BTSs; performs **handover decisions**, frequency hopping control, and power control.

3. **Network Switching Subsystem (NSS):** The circuit-switched core:
   - **MSC (Mobile Switching Center):** Routes voice calls and SS7 signaling; interfaces with the **GMSC** (Gateway MSC) for PSTN interconnection.
   - **HLR (Home Location Register):** Permanent subscriber database holding IMSI, MSISDN, current VLR address, and service subscription info.
   - **VLR (Visitor Location Register):** Temporary database of all MSs currently roaming in the MSC's area; dynamically created entries.
   - **AuC (Authentication Center):** Stores **Ki** and computes **SRES** triplets for authentication; protects against cloning.
   - **EIR (Equipment Identity Register):** Blacklists stolen or unapproved handsets via the **IMEI**.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the Mermaid-rendered GSM architecture.

**(b) GSM TDMA Frame Hierarchy** *(7 Marks)*

The hierarchy from smallest to largest time unit:

- **Burst (156.25 bits, 0.577 ms):** The basic physical transmission unit occupying one timeslot. Five types exist: **Normal, Frequency Correction, Synchronization, Access, Dummy.**
- **Timeslot (8 per frame, 577 µs):** A 577 µs window holding one burst.
- **TDMA Frame (8 timeslots, 4.615 ms):** The fundamental repeating unit.
- **Multiframe — Traffic (26 frames, 120 ms):** Carries TCH + SACCH.
- **Multiframe — Control (51 frames, 235.4 ms):** Carries BCCH, CCCH, SDCCH, SACCH.
- **Superframe (1326 frames, 6.12 s):** Composed of 51 traffic multiframes or 26 control multiframes.
- **Hyperframe (2048 superframes, 3 h 28 m 53 s 760 ms):** The largest cycle; used for ciphering and frequency hopping.

**Key derivations:** $T_{\text{frame}} = 8 \times 577 \;\mu\text{s} = 4615 \;\mu\text{s}$ ✓; 26-frame multiframe = $26 \times 4.615 = 120$ ms ✓; 51-frame multiframe = $51 \times 4.615 = 235.4$ ms ✓.

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q-B]** *(14 Marks)* *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

### Question B:
**(a) With a block diagram, explain the operation of a CDMA system using Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum.** *(7 Marks)*
**(b) Compare GPRS and EDGE. Explain how EDGE achieves higher data rates than GPRS.** *(7 Marks)*

### Model Solution:

**(a) CDMA / DSSS Operation** *(7 Marks)*

**Direct-Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)** spreads a narrowband signal over a wide bandwidth using a high-rate **pseudo-random (PN) code**.

**Transmitter side:**
1. The voice codec produces a **narrowband data signal** at rate $R = 9.6$ kbps.
2. This bit stream is **XOR-multiplied** (modulo-2) with a **PN spreading code** running at chip rate $W = 1.2288$ Mcps.
3. The resulting signal occupies a bandwidth of **1.25 MHz** — a "spread" signal.
4. The spread signal is BPSK-modulated onto an RF carrier and transmitted.

**Receiver side:**
1. The composite received signal contains the desired user's spread signal **plus** all other users' spread signals (which appear as pseudo-noise).
2. The receiver multiplies (correlates) the received signal with a **synchronized local replica** of the desired user's PN code.
3. The correlation **despreads** the desired signal back to its original 9.6 kbps narrowband.
4. Other users' signals remain *spread* because their codes do not correlate with the local replica; their energy stays distributed over 1.25 MHz and contributes only as low-level background noise after the despreading correlator's narrowband filtering.

**Processing gain** $P_g = W/R = 1.2288 \text{ Mcps} / 9.6 \text{ kbps} = 128 \approx 21$ dB.

**Block Diagram:** Refer to **Section 4.3** of these notes for the Mermaid-rendered CDMA flow.

**(b) GPRS vs EDGE Comparison and Throughput Enhancement** *(7 Marks)*

| Parameter | GPRS (2.5G) | EDGE (2.75G) |
|---|---|---|
| Modulation | GMSK | **GMSK + 8-PSK** |
| Bits per symbol | 1 | **3** (under 8-PSK) |
| Carrier bandwidth | 200 kHz | 200 kHz (unchanged) |
| Timeslots per frame | 8 | 8 (unchanged) |
| Coding schemes | CS-1 to CS-4 | **MCS-1 to MCS-9** |
| Peak throughput (8 slots) | **171.2 kbps** | **473.6 kbps** |
| Switching | Packet + circuit | Packet + circuit (same) |
| Core network | SGSN + GGSN added | Same (GPRS core reused) |

**Why EDGE is faster — engineering rationale:**

1. **Modulation upgrade (GMSK → 8-PSK):** 8-PSK encodes 3 bits per symbol instead of 1, tripling the raw bit rate. Mathematically, $R_{\text{EDGE}} / R_{\text{GPRS}} = \log_2(8) / \log_2(2) = 3$.

2. **Adaptive coding and modulation (Link Adaptation):** EDGE introduces **MCS-1 to MCS-9**, allowing the network to dynamically select the most efficient coding/modulation combination for each radio block based on instantaneous C/I measurements. MCS-9 uses 8-PSK with code rate ≈ 1.0, achieving **59.2 kbps per slot**.

3. **Incremental Redundancy (IR):** Unlike GPRS, EDGE retransmissions send *only additional parity bits*, progressively improving decoding — this raises effective throughput in poor radio conditions.

**Engineering trade-off:** 8-PSK has a smaller Euclidean distance between constellation points (vs. GMSK's binary constellation), so it requires a **higher C/I** (≈ 3–5 dB more) to operate reliably. Hence EDGE terminals fall back to GMSK in weak-signal regions.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing 2G with GSM alone:** 2G *includes* IS-95/CDMAOne. If the question says "2G", do not describe GSM only.
> 2. **Stating 270.833 kbps without derivation:** Examiners expect you to compute $156.25 / 577 \mu\text{s}$. Just writing "≈ 271 kbps" loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Ignoring GPRS/EDGE core network changes:** GPRS introduced the SGSN/GGSN packet core; EDGE did *not* introduce a new core. Many students wrongly claim EDGE has a new core network.
> 4. **Writing 8-PSK = 3 kbps/symbol:** The correct phrasing is "3 *bits* per symbol" or "3 bps/Hz".
> 5. **Confusing burst types:** Frequency Correction Burst carries all zeros (sine wave), not a frequency offset word. Synchronization Burst carries the TDMA frame number.
> 6. **Processing Gain derivation:** Forgetting to convert to dB; mixing up linear vs dB.
> 7. **SMS over TCH misconception:** SMS uses **SDCCH** for signaling transfer, *not* a TCH. This is a frequent error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **2G Definition:** Digital cellular, circuit-switched voice + SMS, 900/1800 MHz.
- **GSM = TDMA/FDMA hybrid:** 200 kHz carriers, 8 timeslots, 7 usable for traffic, 270.833 kbps gross.
- **Voice codec rate:** 13 kbps net (RPE-LTP), 22.8 kbps after channel coding.
- **Frame hierarchy:** Burst (0.577 ms) → Frame (4.615 ms) → Multiframe (120 ms traffic / 235.4 ms control) → Superframe (6.12 s) → Hyperframe (3 h 28 m 53 s).
- **Burst types:** Normal, Frequency Correction, Synchronization, Access, Dummy (memorize the role of each).
- **Logical channels:** TCH (traffic), BCH (BCCH/CCCH), DCCH (SDCCH/SACCH/FACCH).
- **GSM architecture:** MS ↔ BTS ↔ BSC ↔ MSC ↔ HLR/VLR/AuC/EIR ↔ PSTN (via GMSC).
- **IS-95 CDMA:** 1.2288 Mcps chip rate, 1.25 MHz bandwidth, processing gain ≈ 21 dB, soft capacity.
- **GPRS (2.5G):** Adds SGSN + GGSN packet core; PDCH dynamic timeslot allocation; CS-1 to CS-4; peak 171.2 kbps.
- **EDGE (2.75G):** Adds 8-PSK modulation + MCS-1 to MCS-9; triples GPRS peak to ≈ 473.6 kbps; reuses GPRS core.
- **Evolution arrow:** GSM (9.6 kbps) → GPRS (171 kbps) → EDGE (473 kbps) → UMTS (2 Mbps).
- **High-yield numbers to memorize:** 200 kHz BW, 270.833 kbps gross, 4.615 ms frame, 577 µs slot, 156.25 bits/burst, 1.2288 Mcps chip rate, 21 dB processing gain, 13 kbps voice codec, 7 voice channels/carrier.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IMT-2000: 3G UMTS, W-CDMA, HSPA, HSPA+, 3G Services and Data Rates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT741 - ADVANCED MOBILE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IMT-2000: 3G UMTS, W-CDMA, HSPA, HSPA+, 3G Services and Data Rates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 IMT-2000 — The ITU Framework for 3G

**IMT-2000 (International Mobile Telecommunications-2000)** is the global standard defined by the **ITU-R (International Telecommunication Union — Radiocommunication Sector)** for **3rd Generation (3G) mobile networks**. It specifies the minimum performance requirements, frequency bands, and multiple radio interface families that together enable global roaming, high-speed data, and multimedia services.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** IMT-2000 is **not a single air-interface**; it is a *family of standards* harmonized under one umbrella. The five recognized IMT-2000 radio interfaces are:
> 1. **UMTS / W-CDMA** (DS-FDD) — Direct-Sequence FDD — (Europe, Japan)
> 2. **CDMA2000 / 1xEV-DO** (MC-FDD) — Multi-Carrier FDD — (Americas, Korea)
> 3. **TD-CDMA / TD-SCDMA** (TDD mode)
> 4. **EDGE** (sometimes considered 2.5G/3G transition)
> 5. **DECT** (cordless extension)

### 1.2 UMTS — Universal Mobile Telecommunications System

**UMTS** is the **European-originated 3G cellular standard** developed by **3GPP (3rd Generation Partnership Project)** as the natural successor to GSM/GPRS. It uses a **5 MHz channel bandwidth**, supports both **Frequency Division Duplex (FDD)** and **Time Division Duplex (TDD)** modes, and delivers peak data rates of up to **2 Mbps**.

### 1.3 W-CDMA — Wideband Code Division Multiple Access

**W-CDMA** is the **dominant radio air-interface chosen for UMTS** in the FDD mode. Unlike narrowband CDMA (IS-95), it spreads each user's signal over a wide **3.84 Mcps (Mega-chips per second)** bandwidth using a unique orthogonal/quasi-orthogonal spreading code. The 5 MHz RF bandwidth accommodates the chip rate plus guard bands.

### 1.4 HSPA & HSPA+ — High-Speed Packet Access

| Feature | HSPA (Release 5/6) | HSPA+ (Release 7/8/9) |
|---|---|---|
| Downlink peak | **14.4 Mbps** | **42 Mbps** (64-QAM + 2×2 MIMO) |
| Uplink peak | **5.76 Mbps** | **11.5 Mbps** (16-QAM) |
| Channel coding | Fixed | Adaptive (variable SF + multi-code) |
| Latency | ~70 ms | ~25 ms |
| Shared-channel | Yes (HSDPA/HSUPA) | Yes + MIMO + CPC + FDPCH |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Highway Analogy**
> Imagine a city's transport network evolution:
> - **2G (GSM)** = A **single narrow lane** used mainly for voice (cars = voice calls).
> - **3G UMTS (W-CDMA)** = The road is **widened to 5 lanes**, every vehicle gets a unique paint color (spreading code) so multiple vehicles share the road simultaneously without colliding. This allows both voice and basic internet browsing.
> - **HSPA** = The lanes are **re-tarmacked with smoother asphalt** (better modulation, faster scheduling, hybrid ARQ) so cars travel much faster.
> - **HSPA+** = We **add parallel express lanes (MIMO)** and **upgrade to Formula-1 grade tarmac (64-QAM)** — now the same highway carries 42 Mbps of broadband traffic.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics Used in 3G (Bold for Emphasis)

- **Chip Rate (Rc) = 3.84 Mcps**
- **RF Channel Bandwidth = 5 MHz**
- **Frame Duration = 10 ms** (15 slots × 2560 chips/slot)
- **Spreading Factor (SF) = 4 to 256 (uplink)** and **4 to 512 (downlink)**
- **Peak User Data Rate (UMTS) = 2 Mbps**
- **Peak User Data Rate (HSDPA) = 14.4 Mbps**
- **Peak User Data Rate (HSPA+ with 64-QAM + 2×2 MIMO) = 42 Mbps**
- **Target BER for voice = 10⁻³** ; **Target BER for data = 10⁻⁶ (after ARQ)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of W-CDMA

W-CDMA is a **direct-sequence spread-spectrum (DS-SS)** system. The conceptual flow is:

**User data → Channel coding (convolutional/turbo) → Interleaving → Spreading (multiplication by chip-rate PN code) → Scrambling (cell/sector-unique code) → QPSK modulation → RF upconversion**.

### 2.2 Why W-CDMA and Not Narrowband CDMA?

| Parameter | Narrowband CDMA (IS-95) | W-CDMA (UMTS) |
|---|---|---|
| Chip rate | 1.2288 Mcps | **3.84 Mcps** |
| Bandwidth | 1.25 MHz | **5 MHz** |
| Frame length | 20 ms | **10 ms** |
| Pilot structure | Common pilot | Dedicated pilot possible |
| Asynchronous BS | No | **Yes** |
| Soft handover | Yes | Yes (more flexible) |

### 2.3 Core Theoretical Pillars (Bulleted Logic Steps)

1. **Spreading Process** — Multiply each user bit of duration Tb by a high-rate code (chips of duration Tc). The transmitted signal occupies bandwidth ≈ 1/Tc = Rc.
2. **Spreading Factor (SF)** — Number of chips per information bit, **SF = Tc/Tb = Rc/Rb**.
3. **Processing Gain (Gp)** — The "signal-to-noise advantage" gained from spreading: **Gp = W/Rb = Rc/Rb = SF** (in linear units); in dB: **Gp,dB = 10·log₁₀(SF)**.
4. **Despreading at Receiver** — Multiplication by the same code restores the narrowband data while spreading interferers. Net effect: interferer's power is suppressed by Gp.
5. **Near-Far Problem** — Because all users share the same band, a strong nearby user would drown a weak far user. **Fast closed-loop power control (1500 Hz update rate in UMTS)** is mandatory.
6. **Rake Receiver** — Multipath components resolved at chip level (1 chip = ~78 m at 3.84 Mcps) are combined using a Rake with multiple fingers.
7. **Soft Handover** — UE connects simultaneously to multiple cells, combining signals via maximum-ratio combining (make-before-break).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Rc = 3.84 Mcps | W-CDMA chip rate | Mcps |
| 2 | SF = Rc / Rb | Spreading factor | chips/bit |
| 3 | Gp = W / Rb = Rc / Rb = SF | Processing gain (linear) | dimensionless |
| 4 | Gp,dB = 10 log₁₀(SF) | Processing gain in dB | dB |
| 5 | Eb/N₀ = (S/N)·(B/Rb) | Energy per bit to noise spectral density ratio | dimensionless (dB) |
| 6 | C = B log₂(1 + S/N) | Shannon-Hartley channel capacity | bps |
| 7 | HSDPA_peak = N_codes · b_mod · R_symbol | HSDPA peak data rate | Mbps |
| 8 | HSPA+_peak (MIMO 2×2, 64-QAM) = 2 · N · 6 · 960 kbps | 64-QAM, 15 codes, 2 streams | bps |
| 9 | Soft handover gain ≈ 2 to 3 dB | Diversity combining gain | dB |
| 10 | Capacity (single cell, voice) = (G p / v · Eb/N₀_req)⁻¹ · (W / Rb) | Pole capacity formula | users/cell |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the Pipe Symbol:** Use `$\vert$` instead of `$\vert$ \vert$` when writing absolute values inside LaTeX expressions. The cheat sheet above replaces any ambiguous pipes with proper mathematical notation.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **W-CDMA's processing gain** allows low-power UEs to operate against high interference — the *foundation* of every cellular system from 3G onward.
- **Fast power control (1500 Hz)** invented for W-CDMA became the standard for **interference management in LTE/NR**.
- **Soft handover** in W-CDMA pioneered **make-before-break** continuity now replicated in **5G NR dual-connectivity**.
- **Turbo codes (used in HSPA)** introduced near-Shannon-limit performance — now used in LTE, 5G NR, WiMAX, and DVB.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Processing Gain from Chip Rate

**Starting Premise:** A user transmits data at bit rate Rb (bps). The transmitter multiplies each bit by a spreading code of rate Rc (chips/s). The signal bandwidth expands from Rb to Rc.

**Step 1 — Express bit duration Tb and chip duration Tc.**

$$T_b = \frac{1}{R_b}, \quad T_c = \frac{1}{R_c}$$

**Step 2 — The number of chips per information bit is the Spreading Factor.**

$$\mathrm{SF} = \frac{T_b}{T_c} = \frac{R_c}{R_b}$$

**Step 3 — Processing gain is the ratio of spread bandwidth to information bandwidth.**

$$G_p = \frac{W}{R_b}$$

Because the spread spectrum occupies bandwidth **W ≈ Rc**, we substitute.

$$\boxed{G_p = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \mathrm{SF}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to decibels (board-exam favourite).**

$$G_{p,\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R_c}{R_b}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(\mathrm{SF})$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** A higher SF trades **bit rate for interference immunity**. UMTS therefore varies SF (4 → 512) dynamically to match channel conditions — high SF for cell-edge, low SF for close-in users.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Eb/N₀ from SNR

**Starting Premise:** Define Eb as energy per information bit, N₀ as noise power spectral density (W/Hz).

**Step 1 — Energy per bit equals signal power divided by bit rate.**

$$E_b = \frac{S}{R_b}$$

**Step 2 — Noise power in bandwidth B is N = N₀ · B.**

**Step 3 — Form the ratio Eb/N₀.**

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{S/R_b}{N_0} = \frac{S}{N} \cdot \frac{B}{R_b}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Shannon capacity into the bound analysis (used for pole capacity).**

$$C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)$$

Substituting S/N = (Eb/N₀)(Rb/B):

$$\boxed{C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{E_b}{N_0}\cdot\frac{R_b}{B}\right)}$$

> This equation is the **master tool** for KTU numericals: given Eb/N₀ = 5 dB, Rb = 9.6 kbps, B = 5 MHz, find the **maximum** reliably supportable users (pole capacity).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Pole Capacity of a Single-Cell W-CDMA Voice System

**Step 1 — Total interference I_total = (M − 1) · S (M users, one is self, M−1 interferers, perfect power control assumed).**

**Step 2 — Using the standard pole-capacity formula (Viterbi):**

$$M_{\max} = 1 + \frac{G_p}{\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\text{req}}} \cdot \frac{1}{v \cdot \eta}$$

where v = voice activity factor ≈ 0.67, η = other-cell-interference factor ≈ 0.55 to 0.65.

**Step 3 — Numerical Example (KTU style):**

- Gp = SF = 128 (voice, 9.6 kbps, 3.84 Mcps)
- Required Eb/N₀ = 5 dB = **3.16** (linear)
- v = 0.67, η = 0.6

$$M_{\max} = 1 + \frac{128}{3.16 \cdot 0.67 \cdot 0.6} = 1 + \frac{128}{1.27} \approx 1 + 100.7 \approx 101 \text{ users/cell}$$

> **Engineering insight:** This is why W-CDMA cells were dimensioned for ~100 simultaneous voice users per 5 MHz — a huge jump over GSM's ~8 users per 200 kHz.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: HSDPA & HSPA+ Peak Data Rates

**Starting Premise:** In HSDPA, 15 channelization codes of SF = 16 are allocated in the downlink within one 2-ms TTI. Each code transmits QPSK or 16-QAM symbols at the rate determined by SF.

**Step 1 — Symbol rate per code.**

$$R_{\text{sym}} = \frac{R_c}{\mathrm{SF}} = \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{16} = 240{,}000 \text{ symbols/s}$$

**Step 2 — Bit rate per code with 16-QAM (4 bits/symbol).**

$$R_b,\text{code} = 240{,}000 \cdot 4 = 960 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate peak rate over 15 codes.**

$$R_{\text{HSDPA}} = 15 \cdot 960 \text{ kbps} = 14.4 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 4 — HSPA+ with 64-QAM (6 bits/symbol) and 2×2 MIMO (2 spatial streams).**

$$R_{\text{HSPA+}} = 2 \cdot 15 \cdot 240{,}000 \cdot 6 = 43.2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} \approx 42 \text{ Mbps}$$

(After MAC/PHY overhead the practical peak is reported as **42 Mbps** by 3GPP.)

**Analytical Matrix — Data Rate vs. Technology**

| Technology | Modulation | MIMO | Peak Data Rate | 3GPP Release |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| UMTS R99 (W-CDMA) | QPSK | No | 384 kbps (practical), 2 Mbps (theoretical) | R99 |
| HSDPA | 16-QAM | No | 14.4 Mbps | R5 |
| HSUPA | QPSK | No | 5.76 Mbps | R6 |
| HSPA+ | 64-QAM | 2×2 | 42 Mbps | R7 / R8 |
| HSPA+ (4×4) | 64-QAM | 4×4 | 168 Mbps (theoretical) | R9 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common student error is using **16-QAM = 4 bits/symbol** but writing it as **8 bits/symbol**. Always remember: M-QAM carries **log₂(M) bits/symbol**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UMTS Network Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The UMTS network is divided into three logical blocks: **UE (User Equipment)**, **UTRAN (UMTS Terrestrial Radio Access Network)**, and **CN (Core Network)**. The diagram below maps the interactions among key nodes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[UE SIDE]
        UE1[User Equipment UE]
        USIM[USIM Card]
        UE1 --- USIM
    end

    subgraph B[UTRAN ACCESS NETWORK]
        NODEB1[Node B Base Station 1]
        NODEB2[Node B Base Station 2]
        RNC1[Radio Network Controller RNC]
        NODEB1 -- lub interface --> RNC1
        NODEB2 -- lub interface --> RNC1
    end

    subgraph C[CORE NETWORK CN]
        SGSN[SGSN Packet Core]
        GGSN[GGSN Gateway to Internet]
        MSC[MSC for Voice Circuit Switched]
        HLR[HLR Home Location Register]
        SGSN --> GGSN
        MSC --- HLR
    end

    UE1 -- Uu radio interface --> NODEB1
    UE1 -- soft handover --> NODEB2
    RNC1 -- IuPS interface --> SGSN
    RNC1 -- IuCS interface --> MSC
    GGSN -- Gi interface --> INET1[Public Internet PDN]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **Uu** is the radio interface (W-CDMA FDD/TDD). **Iub** connects Node B to the RNC; **IuCS/IuPS** separate voice and packet domains in the core. The RNC owns radio-resource management, soft handover, and power control.

---

## 4.2 HSPA / HSPA+ Evolution Roadmap

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[UMTS R99 2 Mbps QPSK] --> B1[HSDPA R5 14.4 Mbps 16-QAM]
    B1 --> C1[HSUPA R6 5.76 Mbps uplink]
    C1 --> D1[HSPA Plus R7 28 Mbps DL 64-QAM]
    D1 --> E1[HSPA Plus R8 42 Mbps 64-QAM 2x2 MIMO]
    E1 --> F1[HSPA Plus R9 84 Mbps dual carrier]
    F1 --> G1[HSPA Plus R10 168 Mbps 4x4 MIMO theoretical]
    G1 --> H1[Migration path to LTE]

    stepA[Added Fast HARQ + Node B scheduling] -.- B1
    stepB[Added E-DCH and shorter 2 ms TTI] -.- C1
    stepC[Added higher order modulation + MIMO] -.- E1
    stepD[Added multi carrier aggregation] -.- F1
```

---

## 4.3 W-CDMA Spreading / Despreading Mechanism (Block Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DATA[User Data Bits Rb] --> COD[Channel Coding Conv Turbo]
    COD --> INTL[Interleaver]
    INTL --> SPREAD[Multiplication by Channelization Code OVSF]
    SPREAD --> SCRAM[Scrambling by Cell Specific Code]
    SCRAM --> MOD[QPSK Modulator]
    MOD --> RF[RF Transmitter 5 MHz]

    RF --> CHAN[Wireless Channel Multipath plus Noise]
    CHAN --> RXDEMOD[QPSK Demodulator]
    RXDEMOD --> DESCRAM[De scrambling]
    DESCRAM --> DESP[De spreading Correlation]
    DESP --> DEINTL[De interleaver]
    DEINTL --> DECOD[Channel Decoder]
    DECOD --> OUT[Recovered User Data]
```

> **Block-level narrative:** The scrambling code identifies the **cell/sector**, while the OVSF (Orthogonal Variable Spreading Factor) channelization code identifies the **individual user within that cell**. Despreading uses a correlation receiver (matched filter / Rake) that restores the original bit while spreading all other users' energy across the band — producing the celebrated **processing gain**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 — Define IMT-2000 and list its key objectives.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
**IMT-2000** (International Mobile Telecommunications-2000) is the **ITU-R specification** that defines the requirements and framework for **3rd-generation (3G) mobile communication systems**. It harmonizes multiple radio interface families (UMTS/W-CDMA, CDMA2000, TD-SCDMA, etc.) under a single global standard.

**Key objectives:**
1. **Global roaming** across IMT-2000 family networks through a single-user identity.
2. **High data rates** — up to **2 Mbps** for stationary users, **384 kbps** for pedestrians, **144 kbps** for vehicular users.
3. **Support for multimedia** services — voice, video, packet data, and internet.
4. **Spectrum efficiency** superior to 2G — target **1 bit/Hz/cell** in loaded conditions.
5. **Coexistence** with 2G systems through dual-mode terminals.
6. **QoS differentiation** for conversational, streaming, interactive, and background classes.

### Question 2 — Compare W-CDMA and CDMA2000 air interfaces.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | W-CDMA (UMTS) | CDMA2000 |
|---|---|---|
| Originating body | 3GPP | 3GPP2 |
| Chip rate | **3.84 Mcps** | 1.2288 Mcps (1x), 3.6864 Mcps (3x) |
| Bandwidth | **5 MHz** | 1.25 MHz (1x), 3.75 MHz (3x) |
| Frame length | 10 ms | 20 ms |
| Pilot | Dedicated or common | Common pilot on forward link |
| Handover | Soft handover | Soft handover |
| Power control | 1500 Hz | 800 Hz |
| Synchronous BS | Not required | Required (GPS) |
| Network core | GSM-MAP evolved | ANSI-41 evolved |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Exam Pattern Reminder:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts typically of **7 marks each**. Provide detailed, well-labelled answers as expected by the KTU board examiner.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1)** — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the W-CDMA air interface used in UMTS with its key parameters and the spreading process. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**W-CDMA (Wideband CDMA)** is the **DS-SS (Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum)** air interface standardized by **3GPP** for UMTS in both FDD and TDD modes.

**Key Parameters:**
- **Chip rate Rc = 3.84 Mcps**
- **Channel bandwidth = 5 MHz** (with ~3.84 MHz usable)
- **Frame length = 10 ms** (15 slots × 2560 chips per slot)
- **Slot = 2560 chips ≈ 0.667 ms**
- **Spreading factor SF = 4 to 256 (uplink)**, **4 to 512 (downlink)**
- **Modulation: QPSK (DL/UL)**, **HSDPA adds 16-QAM**, **HSPA+ adds 64-QAM**
- **Dual-mode operation:** FDD (paired bands) and TDD (unpaired)

**The Spreading Process:**
1. **Channel coding** — Convolutional (1/2, 1/3) or Turbo (R5 onwards) coding is applied.
2. **Interleaving** — Spreads burst errors; 10/20/40/80 ms interleave depths.
3. **Spreading** — Each user bit is multiplied by a high-rate **OVSF channelization code** of length SF. OVSF codes maintain orthogonality between users with different SFs.
4. **Scrambling** — A **cell-specific Gold code** (length 38,400 chips = 10 ms) is multiplied on top to identify the cell/sector. Uplink also uses a UE-specific scrambling code.
5. **Modulation & RF upconversion** — QPSK symbol rate is the chip rate (1 chip = 1 QPSK symbol).

**Advantages of spreading:**
- **Processing gain** of 10 log₁₀(SF) dB — typically **21 to 27 dB** for voice.
- **Rake receiver** captures multipath energy resolved at chip level (1 chip = ~78 m).
- **Soft capacity** — capacity gracefully degrades rather than hard-blocking.

---

**(b)** Discuss HSPA and HSPA+ enhancements with their peak data rates and key enabling features. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**HSPA (High Speed Packet Access)** is a 3GPP umbrella for **HSDPA (Release 5, downlink)** and **HSUPA (Release 6, uplink)**. It marks the shift from **dedicated channels to shared-channel packet access**.

**Key HSDPA Enhancements:**
1. **Shared channel (HS-DSCH)** — High-Speed Downlink Shared Channel with **2-ms TTI** (vs. 10–80 ms in R99).
2. **Adaptive Modulation & Coding (AMC)** — QPSK + 16-QAM, code rate adjusted every TTI.
3. **Fast Node-B scheduling** — Scheduler at Node B (not RNC) reacts in 2 ms to channel variations.
4. **Hybrid ARQ (H-ARQ)** — Chase combining and incremental redundancy for retransmissions.
5. **Multi-code transmission** — Up to **15 codes of SF=16** per UE.

**Peak HSDPA data rate = 15 codes × 4 bits × 240 ksps = 14.4 Mbps**.

**HSUPA (E-DCH)** introduces an enhanced uplink channel with similar TTI shortening, scheduling, and H-ARQ → **5.76 Mbps** uplink peak.

**HSPA+ (Release 7/8/9) Enhancements:**
1. **Higher-order modulation** — **64-QAM (6 bits/symbol)** in downlink.
2. **MIMO 2×2** — Two spatial streams double the peak rate.
3. **CPC (Continuous Packet Connectivity)** — DTX/DRX for battery savings.
4. **FDPCH (Fractional Dedicated Physical Channel)** — reduces downlink overhead.
5. **Dual-carrier HSDPA** (R9) — aggregates two 5 MHz carriers.

**Peak HSPA+ rate = 2 (MIMO) × 15 × 6 (64-QAM) × 240 k = 43.2 Mbps ≈ 42 Mbps.**

| Generation | Peak Rate | Release |
|---|---|---|
| UMTS R99 | 2 Mbps | R99 |
| HSDPA | 14.4 Mbps | R5 |
| HSUPA | 5.76 Mbps (UL) | R6 |
| HSPA+ | **42 Mbps** | R7/R8 |

---

### **Question B (Choice 2)** — 14 Marks

**(a)** Describe the UMTS network architecture with the functions of Node B, RNC, and core-network elements. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The UMTS network is partitioned into three domains:

**1. UE (User Equipment)**
- Contains **ME (Mobile Equipment)** and **USIM (UMTS Subscriber Identity Module)**.
- Performs physical-layer processing, channel coding, spreading, and measurement reporting.

**2. UTRAN (UMPS Terrestrial Radio Access Network)**
- **Node B** — The base-station transceiver. Functions:
  - Air-interface transmit/receive (W-CDMA FDD/TDD).
  - Inner-loop power control (1500 Hz).
  - **Fast scheduling** for HSDPA/HSUPA.
  - Soft-handover leg combining.
  - Reports measurements to the RNC.

- **RNC (Radio Network Controller)** — The intelligence of UTRAN. Functions:
  - **Radio Resource Management (RRM):** admission control, congestion control, code allocation.
  - **Outer-loop power control** (sets Eb/N₀ target).
  - **Macro-diversity combining** for soft handover.
  - **Iub interface** to Node B, **IuCS/IuPS** to the core network.
  - Owns the Serving RNS (SRNS) relocation.

**3. Core Network (CN)**
- **Circuit-Switched domain** — **MSC (Mobile Switching Center)**, **GMSC (Gateway MSC)** for voice.
- **Packet-Switched domain** — **SGSN (Serving GPRS Support Node)** for mobility/session, **GGSN (Gateway GPRS Support Node)** as the IP gateway to external PDNs.
- **HLR / VLR** — Subscriber databases.
- **AUC** — Authentication center.

**Interfaces:**
- **Uu** — Radio (UE ↔ Node B).
- **Iub** — Node B ↔ RNC.
- **IuCS / IuPS** — RNC ↔ MSC / SGSN.
- **Gn / Gi** — SGSN ↔ GGSN ↔ Internet.

---

**(b)** Explain the spreading and despreading process in W-CDMA. Compute the processing gain for a user transmitting at 12.2 kbps (voice). **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Spreading Process:**
The user data bit stream at rate **Rb** is multiplied by a high-rate **chip sequence c(t)** of rate **Rc**. Mathematically:

$$s(t) = b(t) \cdot c(t)$$

where b(t) is the bipolar data ±1 sequence and c(t) is the spreading code at chip rate Rc = **3.84 Mcps**.

**Step 1 — Choice of SF for voice (12.2 kbps):**

$$\mathrm{SF} = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{12{,}200} \approx 314.7$$

Since SF must be a power of 2 (for OVSF), we round to **SF = 256**, giving actual user bit rate:

$$R_b = \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{256} = 15 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2 — Processing gain:**

$$G_p = \frac{R_c}{R_b} = \frac{3.84 \times 10^6}{15{,}000} = 256$$

**In dB:**

$$G_{p,\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(256) = 10 \cdot 2.4082 = \mathbf{24.08 \text{ dB}}$$

**Despreading Process:**
The received signal is multiplied by a **synchronized local replica** of c(t). For the desired user, this collapses the spread spectrum back to narrowband. For all other users (treated as interference), the multiplication further spreads them across the full bandwidth — suppressing their effective power by Gp. A subsequent **matched filter / integrate-and-dump** delivers the original bit.

**Net effect:**
$$\left(\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\text{eff}} = \frac{E_b}{N_0 + I_0} \cdot G_p$$

Hence the **processing gain of 24 dB** lifts the desired user's Eb/N₀ well above the interference floor, enabling reliable detection at low transmit power.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in This Topic**
> 1. **Confusing chips with bits** — Always state clearly that 1 chip ≠ 1 bit. Bits are spread over many chips.
> 2. **Forgetting the 64-QAM = 6 bits/symbol rule** — Students routinely use 8 bits/symbol. Memorize **log₂(64) = 6**.
> 3. **Mixing FDD and TDD parameters** — W-CDMA FDD uses paired bands; TDD shares one band. Don't interchange.
