<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Mark Distribution for PEECT741 — Advanced Mobile Communication**
> - Part A: 3-mark short-answer questions (typically 2 questions per module in internal tests, 1–2 in ESE).
> - Part B: 14-mark descriptive questions (full KTU ESE pattern: 7 + 7 sub-parts, with internal choice between two question alternatives).

---

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. Define 5G. List any four key capabilities specified by ITU-R for IMT-2020.  [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Model Answer:**

> **5G** is the fifth-generation mobile broadband standard defined by the **ITU-R** under the **IMT-2020** framework, designed to support three generic service classes — **eMBB**, **URLLC**, and **mMTC** — over a unified, software-defined radio access network.
>
> **Four key capabilities (KPIs):**
> 1. **Peak data rate** of **20 Gbps** downlink and **10 Gbps** uplink.
> 2. **User-experienced data rate** of **100 Mbps** in dense urban deployments.
> 3. **Over-the-air latency** of **1 ms** for URLLC services.
> 4. **Connection density** of **$10^6$ devices per km²** for mMTC.

*(Word count target: 80–100 words. 3 marks.)*

---

### Q2. Differentiate between eMBB, URLLC, and mMTC service classes of 5G.  [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

| Service Class | Full Form | Focus | Example Use Case | Key KPI |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **eMBB** | Enhanced Mobile BroadBand | High data rate, capacity | 4K/8K streaming, VR | 20 Gbps peak, 100 Mbps edge |
| **URLLC** | Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Comm. | Low latency, high reliability | V2X, remote surgery, smart grid | 1 ms latency, $99.999\%$ reliability |
| **mMTC** | massive Machine-Type Comm. | Massive low-power devices | Smart agriculture, asset tracking | $10^6$ devices/km², 10-yr battery |

*(Tabular answer. 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each 14-mark question carries 7 + 7 marks across two sub-parts. Students choose **one** out of two alternatives (A or B). All sub-parts are compulsory within the chosen question.

---

### Question A — Option 1  [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **evolution of mobile communication from 1G to 5G**, with emphasis on the multiple-access techniques, peak data rates, and switching architectures used in each generation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **three generic 5G service classes (eMBB, URLLC, mMTC)** as defined by ITU-R IMT-2020. For each class, give at least **two real-world applications** and the **primary KPI** it must satisfy. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question A — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Evolution from 1G to 5G

**1G (1980s) — Analog Voice:**
- **Multiple Access:** FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access).
- **Standard:** AMPS, NMT, TACS.
- **Peak Data Rate:** **2.4 kbps** — voice only.
- **Switching:** Circuit-switched.
- **Limitation:** No security, poor handoff, analog noise.

**2G (1990s) — Digital Voice & SMS:**
- **Multiple Access:** TDMA + FDMA hybrid (GSM); CDMA (IS-95).
- **Standards:** GSM, IS-95, GPRS (2.5G), EDGE (2.75G).
- **Peak Data Rate:** **64 kbps** (GSM), **384 kbps** (EDGE).
- **Switching:** Circuit + packet.
- **Innovation:** Digital encryption, SIM card, SMS.

**3G (2000s) — Mobile Internet:**
- **Multiple Access:** W-CDMA, CDMA2000, TD-SCDMA.
- **Standards:** UMTS, HSPA, HSPA+ (3.5G).
- **Peak Data Rate:** **2 Mbps** (R99), **14 Mbps** (HSPA+).
- **Switching:** All-packet core (partial).
- **Innovation:** Video calling, mobile broadband, app stores.

**4G (2010s) — All-IP Broadband:**
- **Multiple Access:** **OFDMA** (downlink), **SC-FDMA** (uplink).
- **Standards:** LTE, LTE-Advanced, LTE-A Pro (4.5G).
- **Peak Data Rate:** **1 Gbps** (theoretical, with CA + MIMO).
- **Switching:** **All-IP**, flat SAE architecture.
- **Innovation:** HD streaming, mobile cloud, VoLTE, IoT start.

**5G (2020s) — Unified Multi-Service:**
- **Multiple Access:** **OFDMA + NOMA + SCMA + PDMA** with **flexible numerology** ($\Delta f$ from 15 to 240 kHz).
- **Standard:** **5G NR (New Radio)** — 3GPP Release 15 onwards.
- **Peak Data Rate:** **20 Gbps** (ITU target), practical **4–5 Gbps** mid-band.
- **Switching:** **Cloud-native 5GC**, SBA (Service-Based Architecture).
- **Innovations:** Massive MIMO, mmWave, network slicing, MEC, AI-driven RAN.

> **Conclusion:** The trajectory moved from **analog → digital → packet → IP → cloud-native**, while multiple-access evolved from **FDMA → TDMA → CDMA → OFDMA → flexible multi-access**.

#### Part (b) — Three 5G Service Classes

**eMBB (Enhanced Mobile BroadBand):**
- **Primary KPI:** High peak data rate (**20 Gbps**) and high user-experienced rate (**100 Mbps**).
- **Applications:** (i) 4K/8K video streaming, (ii) cloud gaming, (iii) VR/AR streaming, (iv) fixed wireless access (FWA).

**URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication):**
- **Primary KPI:** Over-the-air latency **$\le 1$ ms** and reliability **$\ge 99.999\%$** ($5$ nines).
- **Applications:** (i) Autonomous driving (V2X), (ii) remote robotic surgery, (iii) industrial automation, (iv) smart-grid protection.

**mMTC (massive Machine-Type Communication):**
- **Primary KPI:** Connection density **$10^6$ devices/km²**, device battery life $\ge 10$ years.
- **Applications:** (i) Smart agriculture, (ii) environmental monitoring, (iii) smart metering, (iv) asset tracking.

> **Engineering insight:** These three classes have *contradictory* requirements (URLLC needs short transmissions, mMTC needs low-power narrowband). 5G solves this through **network slicing** and **flexible OFDM numerology** on a single physical layer.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice Alternative)  [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** What are the **KPIs defined by ITU-R for 5G (IMT-2020)**? Explain any **five** in detail with their target values and engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **Shannon-limited peak data rate** of a 5G NR cell operating with **$200$ MHz** bandwidth, **$64\times 64$ Massive MIMO**, and an **average SNR of $15$ dB**. Compare your result with the **ITU-R peak data-rate target**. State the engineering implication. **(7 marks)**

---

### Question B — Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Five ITU-R 5G KPIs (ITU-R M.2410)

1. **Peak Data Rate** — **20 Gbps DL / 10 Gbps UL.**
   - *Significance:* Absolute theoretical maximum of a single user in ideal conditions. Drives mmWave + Massive MIMO design.

2. **User-Experienced Data Rate** — **100 Mbps DL / 50 Mbps UL** (dense urban).
   - *Significance:* What an actual user perceives — the *real-world* experience. Drives dense small-cell deployments.

3. **Latency** — **1 ms URLLC / 4 ms eMBB** (one-way, over-the-air).
   - *Significance:* Enables tactile internet, V2X, remote control. Drives flexible numerology + edge computing.

4. **Connection Density** — **$10^6$ devices/km²**.
   - *Significance:* Supports massive IoT. Drives grant-free access and narrowband IoT optimization.

5. **Spectrum Efficiency** — **3×** that of 4G.
   - *Significance:* More bits per Hz. Drives Massive MIMO and advanced coding (LDPC, Polar).

6. *(Bonus)* **Network Energy Efficiency** — **100×** of 4G — drives renewable-powered base stations.
7. *(Bonus)* **Area Traffic Capacity** — **10 Mbps/m²** in hot spots — drives ultra-dense networks.

#### Part (b) — Shannon-Limited Peak Rate Calculation

**Given:**
- Bandwidth: $B = 200$ MHz $= 2 \times 10^8$ Hz
- MIMO configuration: $64 \times 64$ → $\min(64, 64) = 64$ spatial streams
- SNR $= 15$ dB

**Step 1 — Convert SNR to linear scale:**

$$
\text{SNR}_{linear} = 10^{15/10} = 31.62
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Shannon spectral efficiency per stream:**

$$
\eta = \log_2(1 + 31.62) = \log_2(32.62) \approx 5.03 \text{ bps/Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by MIMO rank and bandwidth:**

$$
C_{cell} = 64 \times 5.03 \times 2 \times 10^8 = 6.44 \times 10^{10} \text{ bps} \approx 64.4 \text{ Gbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with ITU target:**

$$
C_{computed} = 64.4 \text{ Gbps} \;\gg\; 20 \text{ Gbps (ITU peak)}
$$

> **Engineering implication:** With $200$ MHz bandwidth and $64\times 64$ Massive MIMO, Shannon's theory allows 5G to exceed the IMT-2020 peak by a factor of **$\sim 3.2\times$**. The *real* challenge in 5G is not peak rate but **maintaining high rates at the cell edge** under realistic mmWave propagation (path loss, blocking, atmospheric absorption), and **simultaneously serving URLLC and mMTC** in the same cell. This motivates **network slicing**, **MEC**, and **beamforming** as the practical differentiators.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing 4G and 5G peak rates.** 4G LTE-A is **1 Gbps**, not 20 Gbps. Writing 5G as a 10× improvement of 4G instead of **20×** costs a mark.
> 2. **Mixing up URLLC and mMTC.** URLLC = low latency + high reliability (vehicles, surgery). mMTC = many devices + low power (sensors, meters). Examiners will give **0** if you swap the use cases.
> 3. **Forgetting the "1 ms" over-the-air latency** is *one-way* (not round-trip). Latency budget must include TX + propagation + processing + queueing.
> 4. **Not converting dB to linear** in Shannon problems. Writing $\log_2(1 + 15) = 4.0$ instead of $\log_2(1 + 31.62) = 5.03$ costs full marks.
> 5. **Writing "5G = faster 4G".** This is a *common conceptual error* that the KTU board penalizes heavily. Always emphasize the **3 service classes + network slicing + flexible numerology** as the real differentiators.
> 6. **Confusing IMT-2020 (ITU) with 3GPP NR.** IMT-2020 is the *vision* by ITU-R; 5G NR is the *standard* by 3GPP that satisfies IMT-2020 requirements.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU 2024 revision bullet list for "Basics of 5G: 5G Potential and Applications":**

- **Definition:** 5G = IMT-2020, defined by **ITU-R**, standardized as **5G NR** by **3GPP** (Release 15+).
- **Three Service Classes:** **eMBB** (high data rate), **URLLC** (1 ms latency + $5$ nines), **mMTC** ($10^6$ devices/km²).
- **Eight KPIs (memorize the numbers):** 20 Gbps peak, 100 Mbps user-rate, 1 ms URLLC latency, $10^6$/km² density, 500 km/h mobility, 3× spectrum efficiency, 10 Mbps/m² area capacity, 100× energy efficiency.
- **Evolution Highlights:** 1G (FDMA, 2.4 kbps) → 2G (TDMA, 64 kbps) → 3G (CDMA, 2 Mbps) → 4G (OFDMA, 1 Gbps) → 5G (flexible numerology, 20 Gbps).
- **Key 5G Enablers:** Massive MIMO, mmWave ($24$–$52$ GHz), flexible numerology ($\Delta f = 15$–$240$ kHz), network slicing, MEC, SDN/NFV, beamforming.
- **Shannon Capacity Formula:** $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ — convert dB to linear before plugging in.
- **OFDM Symbol Duration:** $T_{sym} = 1/\Delta f$. Higher $\Delta f$ → shorter symbol → lower latency (URLLC).
- **Mini-slot feature** in 5G NR allows URLLC pre-emption of eMBB traffic → achieves 1 ms.
- **Network Slicing** = multiple virtual networks over a shared physical infrastructure.
- **5G NR is not backward compatible** with LTE; runs in standalone (SA) or non-standalone (NSA) mode.
- **Practical misconception to avoid:** 5G is *not* "faster 4G" — it is a *service-class unified network* with a *programmable air interface*.
- **Application verticals to mention in any 14-mark answer:** Autonomous driving (V2X), smart manufacturing (Industry 4.0), remote healthcare, smart cities, immersive AR/VR, precision agriculture, drone swarm control.
- **KTU 2024 buzzwords for high marks:** *flexible numerology, IMT-2020, SBA, network slicing, eMBB/URLLC/mMTC, beamforming, mmWave, MEC*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Usage Scenarios: Enhanced Mobile Broadband (eMBB), Ultra Reliable Low Latency Communications (URLLC), Massive Machine Type Communications (MMTC), D2D Communications, V2X Communications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT741 - ADVANCED MOBILE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Usage Scenarios: Enhanced Mobile Broadband (eMBB), Ultra Reliable Low Latency Communications (URLLC), Massive Machine Type Communications (MMTC), D2D Communications, V2X Communications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of 5G / Beyond-5G Usage Scenarios

According to the **ITU-R IMT-2020 Vision** and the **3GPP Release 15/16/17** specifications adopted by KTU's 2024 syllabus for Advanced Mobile Communication, the modern mobile ecosystem is officially classified into **five primary usage scenarios** that define the operational envelope of 5G and beyond networks. These are not merely marketing categories — they are **rigorously defined technical profiles** with quantified Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for data rate, latency, reliability, and connection density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEECT741 – Module 2):**
> The five canonical usage scenarios of 5G/IMT-2020 are: (1) **eMBB** – Enhanced Mobile Broadband, (2) **URLLC** – Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communications, (3) **mMTC** – Massive Machine-Type Communications, (4) **D2D** – Device-to-Device Communications, and (5) **V2X** – Vehicle-to-Everything Communications. Each scenario targets a fundamentally different operational regime of the radio access network (RAN).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **highway system**:
- **eMBB** is like building a **multi-lane superhighway** for cars that need to travel at very high speeds (high data rate) — think 4K Netflix in a moving train.
- **URLLC** is like an **air-traffic-control radar system** — even a 1-second delay or a missed signal could cause a crash. The communication must be **instant and flawless**.
- **mMTC** is like connecting **millions of tiny sensors** in a smart city (streetlights, parking meters, soil sensors) — each one sends only a tiny ping, but there are **millions of them** simultaneously.
- **D2D** is like two people **talking directly** across a table, without the waiter (base station) relaying every word.
- **V2X** is like giving every car a **constant radio conversation** with nearby cars, traffic lights, and pedestrians — to prevent accidents before they happen.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Each scenario is a *different engineering trade-off*. A network cannot simultaneously optimize for all five; hence, **Network Slicing** in 5G Core (5GC) allocates dedicated logical resources per scenario on a shared physical infrastructure.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Defined by ITU-R

| Scenario | Peak Data Rate | User-Plane Latency | Reliability | Connection Density |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **eMBB** | **20 Gbps DL / 10 Gbps UL** | **4 ms (one-way)** | 99.999% | 10⁶ devices/km² |
| **URLLC** | ~50 Mbps | **1 ms (one-way)** | **99.999% (5-nines)** | 10⁵ devices/km² |
| **mMTC** | 1–10 Mbps | ~10 s (tolerable) | 99.9% | **10⁶ devices/km²** |
| **D2D** | Up to 1 Gbps | 1–10 ms | 99.99% | Variable |
| **V2X** | 1 Gbps | **3–10 ms (for safety)** | **99.999%** | High mobility density |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bolded values** are the *defining* KPIs for that scenario. For KTU exams, you **must** remember the one-way latency target of **1 ms for URLLC** and the connection density of **10⁶ devices/km² for mMTC** — these are the most-frequently asked numerical figures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 eMBB – Enhanced Mobile Broadband

**Operational Philosophy:** eMBB is the **evolutionary continuation** of 4G LTE broadband. It targets the human-centric use cases of mobile internet: video streaming, cloud gaming, VR/AR, and fixed wireless access.

**Core Technical Drivers:**
- **Wide bandwidth allocation:** Use of mmWave bands (24–52 GHz), sub-6 GHz mid-bands (3.5 GHz), and low-bands (<1 GHz) for coverage.
- **Massive MIMO:** 64 to 256 antenna elements at the gNB (next-generation Node B).
- **Higher-order modulation:** Up to **256-QAM** in the downlink.
- **Carrier Aggregation:** Up to **16 component carriers** in 5G NR (vs. 5 in LTE).

**Why and How Each Element Matters:**
- *Why mmWave?* Because spectrum below 6 GHz is congested. mmWave offers **contiguous bandwidths of 800 MHz to 2 GHz** per carrier.
- *How does Massive MIMO help?* Beamforming gain scales with $\log_2(N_t)$ in capacity, where $N_t$ is the number of antenna elements.

**The Shannon Capacity Bound** governs eMBB throughput:

$$C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bits/s/Hz]}$$

Where $C$ is the spectral efficiency in bps/Hz, $B$ is bandwidth (Hz), and $S/N$ is the signal-to-noise ratio.

### 2.2 URLLC – Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communications

**Operational Philosophy:** URLLC targets **mission-critical** applications where a packet failure or delay can have catastrophic consequences — industrial automation, remote surgery, smart-grid protection, autonomous vehicle control.

**Core Technical Drivers:**
- **Mini-slot scheduling:** 5G NR supports mini-slots as short as **2 OFDM symbols** (vs. 14 symbols in a full slot). At 30 kHz SCS, this is **~71.4 µs** per mini-slot.
- **Packet duplication:** Transmitting the same packet over two independent paths (e.g., 4G + 5G, or two frequency bands).
- **Grant-free access:** Removing the scheduling request → grant handshake (which takes 4+ round trips in LTE).
- **Diverse redundancy:** Multi-connectivity and repetition coding.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reliability definition (3GPP TR 38.913):** A message of size $L$ bytes is transmitted within a latency budget of $T$ ms with success probability of $(1 - 10^{-5})$, i.e., **99.999% reliability**.

### 2.3 mMTC – Massive Machine-Type Communications

**Operational Philosophy:** mMTC powers the **Internet of Things (IoT)** at scale. It targets low-cost, energy-efficient devices that transmit small amounts of data infrequently — smart meters, agricultural sensors, asset trackers, environmental monitors.

**Core Technical Drivers:**
- **Narrowband IoT (NB-IoT):** 180 kHz channel bandwidth, supporting **52 dB maximum coupling loss** (MCL).
- **LTE-M (Cat-M1):** 1.4 MHz bandwidth, supports voice and mobility.
- **Grant-free random access:** ALOHA-like access for sporadic traffic.
- **Extended Discontinuous Reception (eDRX):** Sleep cycles up to **~3 hours** for battery life of **10+ years** on a single coin cell.

### 2.4 D2D – Device-to-Device Communications

**Operational Philosophy:** D2D enables **direct physical-layer communication** between two user equipments (UEs) **without routing data through the gNB/eNB**, while still being controlled by the network. It is foundational for **proximity-based services (ProSe)** in 3GPP.

**Operational Modes:**
1. **In-band D2D:** Uses licensed cellular spectrum (3.5 GHz or 28 GHz). The gNB allocates radio resources.
2. **Out-band D2D:** Uses unlicensed spectrum (e.g., Wi-Fi 2.4 GHz or Bluetooth) — typically with a secondary radio interface.
3. **Relay-assisted D2D:** D2D link uses a relay UE to extend coverage.

**Technical Challenges:**
- **Interference management** between D2D and cellular links.
- **Peer discovery** (the two UEs must find each other).
- **Session continuity** when devices move.

**The D2D Link Budget** follows the same path-loss equation as cellular:

$$P_{RX} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - PL(d)$$

Where $PL(d)$ is the path loss at distance $d$, typically modeled as $PL(d) = 36.7 \log_{10}(d) + 22.7 + 26 \log_{10}(f_c)$ in urban micro-cells (3GPP TR 36.843).

### 2.5 V2X – Vehicle-to-Everything Communications

**Operational Philosophy:** V2X enables **low-latency, high-reliability** communication between vehicles and their environment. It is a specialization of URLLC for the automotive vertical.

**V2X Sub-categories:**
| Sub-category | Full Form | Communication Link |
|---|---|---|
| **V2V** | Vehicle-to-Vehicle | Car ↔ Car |
| **V2I** | Vehicle-to-Infrastructure | Car ↔ Roadside Unit (RSU) |
| **V2P** | Vehicle-to-Pedestrian | Car ↔ Phone/pedestrian device |
| **V2N** | Vehicle-to-Network | Car ↔ Cloud |
| **V2G** | Vehicle-to-Grid | EV ↔ Power grid |

**Two Implementation Paths:**
1. **C-V2X (Cellular V2X)** defined by 3GPP Release 14+: Uses LTE/5G sidelink (PC5 interface) at 5.9 GHz.
2. **DSRC (Dedicated Short-Range Communications):** IEEE 802.11p-based, primarily in the US.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** V2X is officially a **3GPP-defined sidelink technology**, where the radio interface is called the **PC5 interface** (proximity-communication services), and the data is transmitted over the **Sidelink** rather than the traditional Uu (gNB-UE) interface.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | Boundary / Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shannon Capacity | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bps | $S/N \ge 0$; AWGN channel |
| Spectral Efficiency (Max) | $\eta = \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bps/Hz | Shannon limit |
| Path Loss (Urban Micro) | $PL_{UMi}(d) = 36.7 \log_{10}(d) + 22.7 + 26 \log_{10}(f_c) + X_\sigma$ | dB | $d$ in meters, $f_c$ in GHz |
| Free-Space Path Loss | $FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 20 \log_{10}(4\pi/c)$ | dB | $d$ in m, $f$ in Hz |
| Mini-slot Duration | $T_{ms} = N_{sym} \cdot T_{sym}$ | seconds | $N_{sym} \in \{2,4,7\}$ for mini-slots |
| OFDM Symbol Duration | $T_{sym} = \frac{1}{\Delta f} \cdot \frac{1}{N_{FFT}}$ × cyclic prefix factor | seconds | $\Delta f$ = subcarrier spacing |
| SNR from SINR | $S/N = E_b R_b / (N_0 B)$ | dimensionless | $E_b$ = energy/bit |
| Maximum Coupling Loss (mMTC) | $MCL_{dB} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - S_{RX,min}$ | dB | mMTC: 164 dB (NB-IoT) |
| Latency (one-way) | $L = T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{proc} + T_{queue}$ | ms | URLLC: $L \le 1$ ms |
| D2D Distance Threshold | $d_{D2D} \le R_{cov,gNB}$ | meters | Within gNB coverage |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Do not confuse **eMBB** spectral efficiency (target ~30 bps/Hz peak) with **URLLC** (target ~0.1–3 bps/Hz) — URLLC trades throughput for reliability and latency. This is a classic KTU trick question.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **eMBB** is what your 5G phone uses for streaming and video calls.
- **URLLC** is being piloted in **remote robotic surgery** (e.g., 5G-connected da Vinci surgical systems).
- **mMTC** underpins **smart agriculture** with millions of soil-moisture sensors.
- **D2D** powers **public safety networks** (e.g., police/firefighter push-to-talk when cellular is congested).
- **V2X** is the foundation of **cooperative autonomous driving**, where cars share trajectory data at 10 Hz to avoid collisions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mini-Slot Duration for URLLC

**Problem Setup:** Compute the duration of a 2-symbol mini-slot in 5G NR with subcarrier spacing (SCS) $\Delta f = 30$ kHz. Also compute the percentage time saved compared to a full 14-symbol slot.

**Step 1 — Recall the OFDM symbol duration formula.**

$$T_{sym} = \frac{1}{\Delta f} \cdot \frac{1}{N_{useful}}$$

The 5G NR numerology defines a useful symbol duration of $N_{useful} = 2048$ samples per OFDM symbol, with an additional cyclic prefix (CP). For normal CP at 30 kHz SCS, each symbol has a total duration of approximately $T_{sym} \approx 35.7 \, \mu s$ (computed below).

**Step 2 — Compute the base symbol period from SCS.**

$$T_{base} = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{30 \times 10^3} = 33.33 \, \mu s$$

This is the *useful* symbol duration (without CP). The 30 kHz SCS corresponds to a 33.33 µs useful period.

**Step 3 — Add cyclic prefix.**

For 30 kHz SCS, the normal CP length is $\approx 2.34 \, \mu s$ per symbol. Hence total symbol duration:

$$T_{sym} = T_{useful} + T_{CP} = 33.33 + 2.34 = 35.67 \, \mu s$$

**Step 4 — Compute 2-symbol mini-slot duration.**

$$T_{mini\text{-}slot} = 2 \cdot T_{sym} = 2 \times 35.67 = 71.34 \, \mu s$$

**Step 5 — Compute full-slot duration for comparison.**

$$T_{slot} = 14 \cdot T_{sym} = 14 \times 35.67 = 499.38 \, \mu s \approx 0.5 \, ms$$

**Step 6 — Percentage time saved.**

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{T_{slot} - T_{mini\text{-}slot}}{T_{slot}} \times 100\% = \frac{499.38 - 71.34}{499.38} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Savings} = \frac{428.04}{499.38} \times 100\% \approx 85.71\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This ~85.7% reduction in air-interface latency is *the* enabler of URLLC. A 14-symbol slot takes 0.5 ms, but a 2-symbol mini-slot completes in 71.34 µs — leaving headroom in the 1 ms latency budget for processing, queuing, and retransmission.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Shannon Capacity for eMBB Peak Throughput

**Problem Setup:** 5G NR allocates a carrier bandwidth $B = 100$ MHz at 3.5 GHz with a measured SNR of 20 dB. Compute the peak spectral efficiency and total throughput.

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear scale.**

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{SNR_{dB}/10} = 10^{20/10} = 10^{2} = 100$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's formula for spectral efficiency.**

$$\eta = \log_2(1 + S/N) = \log_2(1 + 100) = \log_2(101)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm.**

Using $\log_2(101) = \frac{\ln(101)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{4.615}{0.6931} \approx 6.658$ bps/Hz.

**Step 4 — Compute total throughput across 100 MHz.**

$$C = B \cdot \eta = 100 \times 10^{6} \times 6.658 = 6.658 \times 10^{8} \text{ bps} = 665.8 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with 5G target.**

Theoretical Shannon bound ≈ **665.8 Mbps** in 100 MHz. Practical 5G NR (using 256-QAM) reaches ~93% of Shannon, giving ≈ **619 Mbps** in this scenario. With $4 \times 4$ MIMO, this scales to **~2.48 Gbps**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: NB-IoT Maximum Coupling Loss (mMTC Link Budget)

**Problem Setup:** A NB-IoT sensor transmits with $P_{TX} = 23$ dBm. The base-station antenna gain is $G_{RX} = 12$ dBi. The sensor antenna gain is $G_{TX} = 0$ dBi. The minimum detectable receive power is $S_{min} = -141$ dBm (for 180 kHz NB-IoT channel at the lowest MCS). Compute the maximum allowable path loss.

**Step 1 — Recall the link budget equation.**

$$MCL_{dB} = P_{TX,dBm} + G_{TX,dBi} + G_{RX,dBi} - S_{RX,min,dBm}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$MCL_{dB} = 23 + 0 + 12 - (-141) = 23 + 12 + 141$$

**Step 3 — Sum the terms.**

$$MCL_{dB} = 176 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Connection:** 3GPP TR 45.820 specifies **164 dB MCL** for NB-IoT in deep indoor scenarios. Our calculation of 176 dB shows a **12 dB margin** — this is the *headroom* that allows NB-IoT sensors to operate from basements, manholes, and concrete-walled industrial environments.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: D2D Link Range

**Problem Setup:** Two UEs in D2D mode operate at 2.6 GHz with transmit power $P_{TX} = 20$ dBm, antenna gains of 0 dBi, receiver sensitivity $S_{min} = -90$ dBm. Compute the maximum D2D range assuming free-space path loss.

**Step 1 — Apply free-space path-loss formula.**

$$FSPL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.45$$

where $d$ is in km and $f$ is in MHz.

**Step 2 — Convert the operating frequency.**

$$f = 2.6 \text{ GHz} = 2600 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 3 — Compute maximum allowable path loss from link budget.**

$$PL_{max} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - S_{min} = 20 + 0 + 0 - (-90) = 110 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for distance $d$.**

$$110 = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(2600) + 32.45$$

**Step 5 — Compute the frequency term.**

$$20 \log_{10}(2600) = 20 \times 3.415 = 68.30 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and solve.**

$$110 = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 68.30 + 32.45$$

$$20 \log_{10}(d) = 110 - 68.30 - 32.45 = 9.25$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $d$ in km.**

$$\log_{10}(d) = \frac{9.25}{20} = 0.4625$$

$$d = 10^{0.4625} \approx 2.90 \text{ km}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** Under free-space conditions, a 20 dBm D2D link at 2.6 GHz can reach **~2.9 km**. In urban NLOS (non-line-of-sight) environments using the UMi model, this drops to **~500 m or less** — but that's still sufficient for ProSe and public-safety D2D use cases.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Scenario Comparison

| Parameter | eMBB | URLLC | mMTC | D2D | V2X |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary KPI** | Peak Data Rate | Latency & Reliability | Connection Density | Proximity Range | Mobility & Latency |
| **Target User** | Human (smartphone) | Machine (industrial) | Sensor (IoT) | UE-UE | Vehicle |
| **Latency Budget** | 4 ms | **1 ms** | 10 s | 1–10 ms | **3–10 ms** |
| **Reliability** | 99.999% | **99.999% (5-nines)** | 99.9% | 99.99% | **99.999%** |
| **Data Rate** | **20 Gbps DL** | 50 Mbps | 1–10 Mbps | Up to 1 Gbps | 1 Gbps |
| **Mobility** | Up to 500 km/h | Up to 500 km/h | Stationary | Walking speed | **Up to 250 km/h** |
| **Spectrum** | mmWave + Sub-6 | Sub-6 + mmWave | Sub-1 GHz | Sub-6 GHz | 5.9 GHz (C-V2X) |
| **Frame Structure** | Full slot (14 sym) | **Mini-slot (2 sym)** | eDRX cycles | Sidelink (PC5) | Sidelink (PC5) |
| **Air Interface** | Uu (gNB ↔ UE) | Uu (gNB ↔ UE) | Uu (gNB ↔ UE) | **PC5 (UE ↔ UE)** | **PC5 (UE ↔ UE)** |
| **Coding** | LDPC | **Polar codes** (control) | Repetition | LDPC | LDPC + Repetition |
| **Modulation** | 256-QAM | QPSK / 16-QAM | QPSK / BPSK | 64-QAM | 64-QAM |
| **MIMO Order** | 64–256 | 2×2 | 1×1 / 2×1 | 2×2 | 2×2 / 4×4 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The PC5 interface is the **sidelink** used by D2D and V2X. The Uu interface is the **traditional cellular** link (gNB ↔ UE). Confusing these two interfaces is the most common mistake students make in V2X questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between eMBB, URLLC, and mMTC usage scenarios of 5G in terms of their primary Key Performance Indicators (KPIs).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Scenario | Primary KPI | Numerical Target |
|---|---|---|
| **eMBB** | Peak Data Rate | 20 Gbps DL / 10 Gbps UL |
| **URLLC** | Latency & Reliability | 1 ms one-way, 99.999% reliability |
| **mMTC** | Connection Density | 10⁶ devices/km² |

eMBB focuses on **throughput** for human-centric broadband (video, VR). URLLC focuses on **low latency and high reliability** for mission-critical applications (industrial automation, remote surgery). mMTC focuses on **massive device connectivity** for IoT (smart meters, sensors). They are **orthogonal** KPIs that cannot be simultaneously maximized in a single radio frame — hence **network slicing** is used to allocate separate resources per scenario.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for each correct KPI pairing (eMBB–throughput, URLLC–latency, mMTC–density). Award 1 mark for the explanation of orthogonality.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]`
**Explain the role of the PC5 interface in 5G V2X and D2D communications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **PC5 interface** is the 3GPP-defined **sidelink** radio interface used for **direct device-to-device communication**, bypassing the gNB data path. In **V2X**, the PC5 interface enables vehicles to exchange **CAM (Cooperative Awareness Messages)** and **DENM (Decentralized Environmental Notification Messages)** directly with neighboring vehicles (V2V) or with road-side units (V2I) over the 5.9 GHz band, achieving latencies of **3–10 ms** essential for collision avoidance. In **D2D** communications, PC5 supports **ProSe (Proximity Services)** for public safety, content sharing, and relaying.

Key features:
- Operates at **5.9 GHz** (ITS band) for V2X, or licensed cellular bands for D2D.
- Supports **out-of-coverage** operation — vehicles can communicate even without cellular signal.
- Uses **sidelink resource allocation modes** (Mode 1: scheduled by gNB; Mode 2: autonomous UE-scheduling).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark for PC5 = sidelink definition. 1 mark for V2X application. 1 mark for D2D/ProSe application.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**(a)** Discuss in detail the **Enhanced Mobile Broadband (eMBB)** usage scenario of 5G. Enumerate the technical enablers (massive MIMO, mmWave, carrier aggregation) and compute the **Shannon spectral efficiency** for a 100 MHz carrier with **SNR = 15 dB**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **mMTC** usage scenario. Derive the **Maximum Coupling Loss (MCL)** for an NB-IoT link with the following parameters: $P_{TX} = 23$ dBm, $G_{TX} = 0$ dBi, $G_{RX} = 12$ dBi, $S_{RX,min} = -137$ dBm. Comment on how this MCL supports deep-indoor deployments. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — eMBB Deep Dive (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** eMBB is the **broadband evolution** of 4G LTE, targeting **high data rate**, **high capacity**, and **wide-area coverage** for human-centric use cases such as UHD video streaming, cloud gaming, AR/VR, and fixed wireless access. The 5G target is **20 Gbps peak DL** and **10 Gbps peak UL**.

**Technical Enablers (3 marks):**
1. **mmWave spectrum (24–52 GHz):** Provides 800 MHz – 2 GHz contiguous bandwidth per carrier, enabling multi-Gbps throughput.
2. **Massive MIMO (64–256 elements):** Beamforming gain scales as $10 \log_{10}(N_t)$ dB; 64-element array gives ~18 dB gain. Enables **spatial multiplexing** of multiple users on the same time-frequency resource.
3. **Carrier Aggregation (up to 16 CCs in 5G NR):** Combines fragmented spectrum across FDD and TDD bands.
4. **256-QAM modulation:** 8 bits/symbol, ~33% higher throughput over 64-QAM.
5. **Flexible Numerology (15/30/60/120 kHz SCS):** Allows optimization for different bands and mobility profiles.

**Shannon Spectral Efficiency Calculation (3 marks):**

Step 1: Convert SNR to linear scale.
$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{15/10} = 10^{1.5} = 31.623$$

Step 2: Apply Shannon's formula.
$$\eta = \log_2(1 + S/N) = \log_2(1 + 31.623) = \log_2(32.623)$$

Step 3: Evaluate.
$$\eta = \frac{\ln(32.623)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{3.485}{0.6931} \approx 5.027 \text{ bps/Hz}$$

Step 4: Total capacity for $B = 100$ MHz.
$$C = B \cdot \eta = 100 \times 10^{6} \times 5.027 = 502.7 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Conclusion:** Shannon gives the upper bound. With practical coding/modulation (e.g., LDPC + 256-QAM at 75% efficiency), the achievable throughput is ~377 Mbps. With $4 \times 4$ MIMO, this scales to ~1.5 Gbps.

---

**(b) — mMTC and MCL Derivation (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** mMTC is the 5G usage scenario targeting **massive-scale IoT connectivity**, with up to **10⁶ devices per km²**, each transmitting small, infrequent data packets. Battery life target is **10+ years** on a single cell.

**Key Technologies (2 marks):**
- **NB-IoT (Narrowband IoT):** 180 kHz channel, deep coverage, low cost.
- **LTE-M (Cat-M1):** 1.4 MHz channel, supports mobility and voice.
- **Grant-free access:** ALOHA-style random access without scheduling handshake.
- **eDRX (Extended Discontinuous Reception):** Sleep cycles up to 2.91 hours to save battery.
- **Power Saving Mode (PSM):** Device is reachable only at preset intervals.

**MCL Derivation (4 marks):**

Step 1: Write the MCL equation.
$$MCL_{dB} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - S_{RX,min}$$

Step 2: Substitute the given values.
$$MCL_{dB} = 23 + 0 + 12 - (-137)$$

Step 3: Compute the sum.
$$MCL_{dB} = 23 + 0 + 12 + 137 = 172 \text{ dB}$$

**Comment on Deep-Indoor Deployment (extra 0 marks, but recommended):** A 172 dB MCL means the device can tolerate a total path loss of 172 dB between the sensor's antenna and the gNB's antenna connector. This is sufficient for **basement meters**, **concrete-walled industrial IoT**, and **underground parking sensors**. 3GPP TR 45.820 specifies **164 dB** as the baseline requirement; our 172 dB calculation shows an **8 dB implementation margin**, which translates to ~2× coverage distance in free space (since path loss scales as $20 \log_{10}(d)$).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the **URLLC** usage scenario in detail. Compute the **mini-slot duration** for a 2-symbol mini-slot at 60 kHz SCS, and show how it enables sub-1 ms one-way latency. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **V2X** communication modes. A V2X transmitter operates at **5.9 GHz** with $P_{TX} = 23$ dBm, $G_{TX} = 6$ dBi, $G_{RX} = 8$ dBi, and receiver sensitivity $S_{min} = -95$ dBm. Compute the **maximum free-space range**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — URLLC Deep Dive (7 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** URLLC targets **ultra-reliable** and **low-latency** communication with a one-way latency of **1 ms** and reliability of **99.999% (5-nines)** for 32-byte messages. It enables **mission-critical IoT** (industrial control, smart grid, remote surgery, V2X safety messages).

**Enabling Techniques (2 marks):**
1. **Mini-slot scheduling (2/4/7 OFDM symbols):** Reduces air-interface latency.
2. **Grant-free uplink access:** Eliminates the 4-step scheduling handshake.
3. **Packet duplication across two gNBs:** Spatial redundancy.
4. **Pre-emptive scheduling:** Pre-reserved radio resources.
5. **Faster HARQ:** Reduced retransmission round-trip time.
6. **Polar codes for control channels:** Better BLER performance at low SNR.

**Mini-slot Duration Calculation (4 marks):**

Step 1: Base useful symbol duration for 60 kHz SCS.
$$T_{base} = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{60 \times 10^3} = 16.67 \, \mu s$$

Step 2: Add cyclic prefix (for 60 kHz, normal CP ≈ 1.17 µs).
$$T_{sym} = 16.67 + 1.17 = 17.84 \, \mu s$$

Step 3: Compute 2-symbol mini-slot.
$$T_{mini\text{-}slot} = 2 \times 17.84 = 35.68 \, \mu s$$

Step 4: Compare with full 14-symbol slot.
$$T_{slot} = 14 \times 17.84 = 249.76 \, \mu s \approx 0.25 \, ms$$

Step 5: Comment on latency budget.
The mini-slot occupies only ~14.3% of a full slot's duration. Even with **3 mini-slots in a 1 ms subframe** (since a 1 ms subframe at 60 kHz SCS contains 4 slots × 14 sym each = 56 sym, divided by 2 sym/mini-slot = 28 mini-slots), the air-interface contribution is ~107 µs, leaving **~900 µs** of latency budget for processing, propagation, and HARQ ACK. This easily fits the **1 ms URLLC budget**.

---

**(b) — V2X Diagram and Range Calculation (7 marks):**

**V2X Diagram (3 marks):** A neatly labeled diagram showing:
- **V2V:** Car ↔ Car via PC5 sidelink.
- **V2I:** Car ↔ RSU via PC5 sidelink.
- **V2P:** Car ↔ Pedestrian smartphone via PC5 sidelink.
- **V2N:** Car ↔ Cloud via Uu (gNB) interface.
- **V2G:** EV ↔ Smart grid via Uu interface.

**Maximum Range Calculation (4 marks):**

Step 1: Compute maximum allowable path loss.
$$PL_{max} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - S_{min} = 23 + 6 + 8 - (-95) = 132 \text{ dB}$$

Step 2: Apply free-space path-loss formula.
$$PL_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.45$$

where $d$ is in km and $f$ is in MHz.

Step 3: Compute the frequency term.
$$20 \log_{10}(5900) = 20 \times 3.771 = 75.42 \text{ dB}$$

Step 4: Substitute and solve for $d$.
$$132 = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 75.42 + 32.45$$
$$20 \log_{10}(d) = 132 - 75.42 - 32.45 = 24.13$$
$$\log_{10}(d) = 1.2065$$
$$d = 10^{1.2065} \approx 16.08 \text{ km}$$

**Conclusion:** Under ideal free-space conditions, a V2X link at 5.9 GHz with the given parameters can reach **~16 km**. In **realistic urban NLOS** (using 3GPP UMi LOS/NLOS models), the typical V2X communication range is **300 m to 1 km**, which is sufficient for **cooperative collision avoidance** at urban driving speeds.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse FSPL constants.** The formula $20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.45$ requires $d$ in **km** and $f$ in **MHz**. A common error is using $d$ in meters, which gives an answer that is **30 dB too low** (because $\log_{10}(1000) = 3$).
> 2. **Mini-slot ≠ short slot.** A "slot" in 5G NR is always 14 symbols; a "mini-slot" can be 2, 4, or 7 symbols. Do not interchange.
> 3. **PC5 vs. Uu confusion.** V2X uses **PC5 (sidelink)** for V2V/V2I/V2P and **Uu (cellular)** for V2N/V2G. Do not claim V2V uses the gNB data path.
> 4. **Shannon SNR units.** SNR must be in **linear scale** before applying $\log_2(1 + S/N)$. Forgetting to convert from dB gives a wrong answer.
> 5. **MCL negative sign trap.** $S_{RX,min}$ is always **negative** (e.g., -141 dBm). Subtracting a negative number *adds* to the budget. Students often write $- 141 = -141$ and lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Points (Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam):**

- **eMBB:** Peak rate **20 Gbps DL / 10 Gbps UL**; latency **4 ms**; mmWave + Massive MIMO + 256-QAM.
- **URLLC:** Latency **1 ms (one-way)**; reliability **99.999% (5-nines)**; mini-slot = **2/4/7 OFDM symbols**; uses **Polar codes** for control.
- **mMTC:** Connection density **10⁶ devices/km²**; battery life **10 years**; **NB-IoT (180 kHz)** and **LTE-M (1.4 MHz)**; MCL target **164 dB**.
- **D2D:** **PC5 interface** (sidelink) for direct UE ↔ UE; in-band (licensed) and out-band (unlicensed Wi-Fi/Bluetooth) modes; **ProSe** is the 3GPP service.
- **V2X:** **PC5 sidelink** at **5.9 GHz**; sub-modes **V2V, V2I, V2P, V2N, V2G**; CAM and DENM message types; latency **3–10 ms**.
- **Shannon Limit:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — must use **linear SNR**, not dB.
- **Mini-slot savings:** ~85.7% air-interface latency reduction at 30 kHz SCS.
- **Network Slicing:** 5G core allocates **dedicated logical slices** per scenario on shared physical infrastructure.
- **Free-space path loss formula:** $PL = 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 32.45$ dB.
- **3GPP Release timeline:** eMBB in **Rel-15**; URLLC in **Rel-15**; mMTC (NB-IoT/LTE-M) in **Rel-13/14**; C-V2X in **Rel-14**; 5G NR V2X (5G-V2X) in **Rel-16**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** When asked to "compare" two scenarios, always use a **4-column table** (Parameter, Scenario A, Scenario B, Common/Notes) — this is the format KTU examiners look for and earns full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spectrum for 5G, Spectrum Access/Sharing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT741 - ADVANCED MOBILE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Spectrum for 5G, Spectrum Access/Sharing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Spectrum" in 5G?

### Formal KTU Definition
In wireless communications, **radio spectrum** refers to the range of electromagnetic frequencies (typically from **3 kHz to 300 GHz**) allocated for over-the-air communication. For 5G, the spectrum is divided into two primary Frequency Ranges as defined by **3GPP Release 15/16**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3GPP 5G NR (New Radio) Frequency Ranges**
> - **FR1 (Sub-6 GHz):** **410 MHz – 7125 MHz** — also called "Sub-6"
> - **FR2 (mmWave):** **24250 MHz – 52600 MHz** — also called "mmWave" or "Above-24 GHz"

### Conceptual Analogy: The Highway Analogy
Imagine the radio spectrum as a **multi-lane highway system**:
- **Low-band spectrum (<1 GHz)** = Rural highways — few lanes, but each lane is very long, carries signals far, and penetrates buildings easily. Used for wide-area coverage.
- **Mid-band spectrum (1–6 GHz)** = Urban expressways — balanced lanes with good speed and reasonable reach. The "sweet spot" for 5G.
- **High-band spectrum (mmWave, 24–40+ GHz)** = Race tracks — extremely wide lanes carrying massive data, but signals are short-range and blocked by walls or even rain.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Higher frequency → more bandwidth available → faster data rates, BUT shorter range and poorer propagation. This fundamental trade-off shapes all 5G spectrum decisions.

---

## 1.2 Spectrum Access Models — The Three Pillars

Spectrum can be accessed in fundamentally different ways:

| Access Type | Owner | Who Can Use | Example |
|-------------|-------|-------------|---------|
| **Licensed (Exclusive)** | Regulator auctions to single operator | Only the licensee | Verizon C-band (3.7 GHz), Jio 26 GHz |
| **Unlicensed (License-exempt)** | Public/Shared | Anyone following rules | 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz Wi-Fi |
| **Shared (Dynamic/Licensed Shared)** | Incumbent + Multiple users via database | Coordinated secondary access | CBRS (3.5 GHz USA), LSA (2.3 GHz Europe) |

### The Spectrum Sharing Philosophy
> [!NOTE]
> **Spectrum Scarcity Paradox:** Spectrum is *not* physically scarce — we only use about **5–10%** of licensed bands efficiently at any moment. Sharing unlocks dormant capacity.

Think of it like **co-working spaces** vs. **private offices**: instead of giving one company a building 24/7 (licensed), you let multiple companies book desks by the hour (shared) — total productivity rises.

---

## 1.3 Why Spectrum Matters for 5G

The three 5G use cases (defined by **ITU-R IMT-2020**) each demand different spectrum characteristics:

1. **eMBB (enhanced Mobile BroadBand)** — needs **wide bandwidth** → favors high-band (mmWave)
2. **URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication)** — needs **predictable, interference-controlled** bands → favors licensed mid-band
3. **mMTC (massive Machine-Type Communication)** — needs **deep coverage, low power** → favors low-band (sub-1 GHz, e.g., 700 MHz)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** No single spectrum band can satisfy all three 5G pillars simultaneously. This is why 5G networks are **heterogeneous** — they use multiple frequency bands simultaneously (carrier aggregation + multi-connectivity).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier 5G Spectrum Architecture

5G deployment is structured into a **three-tier spectrum model**:

### Tier 1: Low-Band Spectrum (< 1 GHz)
- **Range:** 600 MHz, 700 MHz, 800 MHz, 900 MHz
- **Bandwidth:** Typically 5–20 MHz per channel
- **Use Case:** Wide-area coverage, indoor penetration, IoT (NB-IoT, LTE-M evolution)
- **Pros:** Long range (cell radius 5–15 km), excellent building penetration
- **Cons:** Limited bandwidth → peak speeds ~100 Mbps

### Tier 2: Mid-Band Spectrum (1–6 GHz) — The 5G Sweet Spot
- **Sub-categories:**
  - **C-band (3.4–4.2 GHz):** The premier 5G band globally
  - **2.5 GHz (Band 41/n41):** Used by T-Mobile USA, also in India
  - **3.5 GHz (Band n78):** Workhorse for global 5G
- **Bandwidth:** Up to 100 MHz per channel (with carrier aggregation → 200–400 MHz)
- **Use Case:** Urban capacity, eMBB primary band, network densification
- **Pros:** Good balance of coverage and capacity; realistic cell radius 1–5 km

### Tier 3: High-Band / mmWave (24–52.6 GHz)
- **Bands:** n257 (26 GHz), n258 (26 GHz), n260 (37 GHz), n261 (28 GHz)
- **Bandwidth:** 400 MHz – 800 MHz per channel (up to 2.4 GHz aggregated)
- **Use Case:** Hotspots, fixed wireless, stadiums, factories
- **Pros:** Massive bandwidth → multi-Gbps speeds
- **Cons:** Cell radius 100–300 m; blocked by foliage, glass, human body

---

## 2.2 5G NR Frequency Band Numbering (3GPP TS 38.101)

3GPP defines **"n" bands** for 5G NR. Critical bands for KTU:

| NR Band | Frequency Range | Duplex Mode | Common Use |
|---------|-----------------|-------------|------------|
| **n5** | 824–894 MHz | FDD | Low-band coverage |
| **n7** | 2500–2690 MHz | FDD | Mid-band capacity |
| **n28** | 703–803 MHz | FDD | Rural/suburban |
| **n78** | 3300–3800 MHz | TDD | **Global 5G mid-band** |
| **n257** | 26500–29500 MHz | TDD | mmWave 28 GHz |
| **n258** | 24250–27500 MHz | TDD | mmWave 26 GHz |
| **n261** | 27500–28350 MHz | TDD | US 28 GHz |
| **n259** | 39500–43500 MHz | TDD | Future 5G |

> [!NOTE]
> **FDD (Frequency Division Duplex):** Separate frequencies for uplink and downlink.
> **TDD (Time Division Duplex):** Same frequency, time-shared between UL/DL.

---

## 2.3 Spectrum Sharing Mechanisms — Detailed Taxonomy

### 2.3.1 Licensed Shared Access (LSA)
- **Origin:** Developed by **ETSI (European Telecommunications Standards Institute)** and **CEPT** around 2014–2015.
- **Mechanism:** Incumbent (e.g., government, satellite) shares spectrum with licensed secondary users via an **LSA Repository** + **LSA Controller** architecture.
- **Pilot band:** 2.3–2.4 GHz in Europe (was used by wireless cameras, defense, etc.)
- **Benefit:** Guarantees QoS for both primary and secondary users.

### 2.3.2 CBRS (Citizens Broadband Radio Service) — US Model
- **Band:** **3550–3700 MHz** (150 MHz total, called "the 3.5 GHz band")
- **Three-Tier Architecture:**
  - **Tier 1 — Incumbent Access:** Navy radar, fixed satellite → protected highest
  - **Tier 2 — Priority Access License (PAL):** Auctioned 10-MHz channels per census tract
  - **Tier 3 — General Authorized Access (GAA):** Unlicensed, opportunistic
- **Coordination:** Via a **Spectrum Access System (SAS)** — a cloud-based dynamic database.

### 2.3.3 Dynamic Spectrum Sharing (DSS)
- **Mechanism:** Allows **LTE and 5G NR to share the same spectrum dynamically**, frame-by-frame.
- **Benefit:** Operators can migrate from LTE to 5G without refarming spectrum.
- **Used by:** Verizon, AT&T, Vodafone in mid-band frequencies.

### 2.3.4 TV White Spaces (TVWS)
- **Concept:** Use idle UHF/VHF bands (470–790 MHz) between TV broadcasts.
- **Database:** TVWS database (e.g., Google, Microsoft Spectrum Database) tells devices which channels are free in their location.
- **Use:** Rural broadband, IoT, agriculture.

### 2.3.5 Authorized/Licensed Shared Access in 6 GHz (Wi-Fi 6E)
- **Band:** **5.925–7.125 GHz** (1200 MHz)
- **Modes:** LPI (Low Power Indoor), VLP (Very Low Power), Standard Power (AFC-controlled)
- **Purpose:** Give Wi-Fi massive bandwidth while protecting incumbents (microwave links, satellite).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Equations

| Concept | Formula / Relation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Maximum data rate (Shannon)** | $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | $C$ = capacity (bps), $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), SNR = linear ratio | Predicts throughput from bandwidth |
| **Free-space path loss (Friis)** | $\text{FSPL} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ (dB) | $d$ = km, $f$ = MHz | Higher $f$ → higher loss |
| **Phased array beamforming gain** | $G = 10\log_{10}(N) + 10\log_{10}(\eta)$ | $N$ = antenna elements, $\eta$ = efficiency | mmWave beamforming compensates loss |
| **Maximum cell radius (link budget)** | $R_{max} = 10^{\frac{P_{tx} + G_{tx} + G_{rx} - PL_{max} - \text{margins}}{10n}}$ | $n$ = path loss exponent | Determines coverage |
| **Spectrum utilization** | $\eta_{spectrum} = \frac{\text{Used bandwidth-time}}{\text{Total licensed capacity}} \cdot 100\%$ | Percentage | Often only **5–10%** in licensed bands |
| **Carrier aggregation bandwidth** | $B_{CA} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} B_i$ | Sum of component carriers | Up to 16 carriers in 5G |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do not confuse **band number** (e.g., n78) with **band frequency**. n78 spans **3300–3800 MHz**, but the *band number* is just an identifier.

---

## 2.5 Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Network Planning** | Decide which band to deploy in which region (urban vs rural) |
| **Regulatory Policy** | National spectrum auctions, pricing, re-farming of 2G/3G bands |
| **Chipset Design** | Phones must support FR1 + selected FR2 bands (modem RF front-end complexity) |
| **Economics** | 5G C-band auctions raised **$81 billion** in the US alone (2021) |
| **AI/ML** | Real-time SAS decisions, predictive spectrum allocation |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Higher Frequency Means Higher Path Loss

This is **the most important derivation for KTU exams** on spectrum. It directly justifies why mmWave cells are tiny.

### Starting Point: Friis Transmission Equation

For two isotropic antennas separated by distance $d$ meters, transmitting at frequency $f$ Hz:

$$
\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

Where:
- $P_r$ = received power (W)
- $P_t$ = transmitted power (W)
- $G_t, G_r$ = antenna gains (linear)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (m), related to frequency by $\lambda = c / f$
- $c$ = **speed of light = $3 \times 10^8$ m/s**

### Step-by-Step Expansion

**Step 1:** Substitute $\lambda = c/f$ into the equation:

$$
\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \left(\frac{c / f}{4\pi d}\right)^2
$$

**Step 2:** Expand the square:

$$
\frac{P_r}{P_t} = G_t G_r \cdot \frac{c^2}{f^2 \cdot (4\pi d)^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Take $10\log_{10}$ of both sides to convert to decibels (dB):

$$
10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_r}{P_t}\right) = 10\log_{10}(G_t G_r) + 20\log_{10}(c) - 20\log_{10}(f) - 20\log_{10}(4\pi d)
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize $-10\log_{10}(P_r/P_t) = \text{Path Loss (dB)}$. Rearranging for path loss **PL**:

$$
\text{PL (dB)} = -10\log_{10}(G_t G_r) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}(4\pi d) - 20\log_{10}(c)
$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the constants. With $f$ in **Hz** and $d$ in **meters**:

$$
\text{PL (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}(d) - 147.55 \quad \text{(for isotropic antennas)}
$$

**Step 6:** Convert to convenient engineering units ($f$ in **MHz**, $d$ in **km**). Using $1 \text{ MHz} = 10^6$ Hz and $1 \text{ km} = 10^3$ m, we add $20\log_{10}(10^6) = 120$ dB and $20\log_{10}(10^3) = 60$ dB to the right side, then rebalance:

$$
\boxed{\text{PL (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 32.44}
$$

### Step 7: Numerical Comparison — KTU-Style Calculation

**Given:** Compare free-space path loss at:
- $f_1 = 3.5 \text{ GHz} = 3500 \text{ MHz}$, $d = 1 \text{ km}$
- $f_2 = 28 \text{ GHz} = 28000 \text{ MHz}$, $d = 1 \text{ km}$

**For 3.5 GHz:**

$$
\text{PL}_1 = 20\log_{10}(3500) + 20\log_{10}(1) + 32.44
$$

Computing each term:
- $20\log_{10}(3500) = 20 \cdot 3.5441 = 70.88 \text{ dB}$
- $20\log_{10}(1) = 0 \text{ dB}$
- Constant = $32.44 \text{ dB}$

$$
\text{PL}_1 = 70.88 + 0 + 32.44 = 103.32 \text{ dB}
$$

**For 28 GHz:**

$$
\text{PL}_2 = 20\log_{10}(28000) + 20\log_{10}(1) + 32.44
$$

Computing each term:
- $20\log_{10}(28000) = 20 \cdot 4.4472 = 88.94 \text{ dB}$
- $20\log_{10}(1) = 0 \text{ dB}$
- Constant = $32.44 \text{ dB}$

$$
\text{PL}_2 = 88.94 + 0 + 32.44 = 121.38 \text{ dB}
$$

**Difference:**

$$
\Delta\text{PL} = \text{PL}_2 - \text{PL}_1 = 121.38 - 103.32 = 18.06 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Moving from **3.5 GHz** to **28 GHz** at the same distance adds **~18 dB of extra path loss**. To compensate, the 28 GHz system needs either **~63× more power** or **~63× more antenna gain** (since $10\log_{10}(63) \approx 18$ dB). This is exactly why **massive MIMO + beamforming** is mandatory at mmWave.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Band Selection for 5G Use Cases

| Use Case | Required Bandwidth | Latency Tolerance | Best Spectrum Tier | Reasoning |
|----------|--------------------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------|
| **Fixed Wireless Access (FWA)** | High (>100 MHz) | Loose | mmWave (n257/n258) | Small cell, stationary user, line-of-sight |
| **Urban eMBB** | Medium–High | Loose | C-band (n78) | Balance of capacity + coverage |
| **Rural eMBB** | Low | Loose | Low-band (n5, n28) | Long range, building penetration |
| **Autonomous Vehicles (URLLC)** | Medium | Very strict (<1 ms) | Licensed mid-band | Predictable interference |
| **Smart Meters (mMTC)** | Very low | Very loose | Low-band (<1 GHz) | Deep indoor, low power |
| **Factory Automation (URLLC)** | Medium | Very strict | Licensed mid-band + private 5G | Reliability + control |
| **Stadium / Concert** | Very high | Loose | mmWave + small cells | Massive capacity in small area |
| **Satellite-5G Integration** | Medium | Medium | L-band / S-band | Coverage in dead zones |

---

## 3.3 Quick Derivation: Bandwidth Requirement for a 5G eMBB Peak Rate

**Given target:** Peak downlink rate **C = 1 Gbps** with spectral efficiency **SE = 30 bps/Hz** (achievable with Massive MIMO in mid-band).

$$
B_{required} = \frac{C}{SE} = \frac{10^9 \text{ bps}}{30 \text{ bps/Hz}} \approx 33.3 \text{ MHz}
$$

To push to **10 Gbps** (5G target at mmWave with SE = 50 bps/Hz):

$$
B_{required} = \frac{10^{10}}{50} = 200 \text{ MHz}
$$

To reach **20 Gbps** (theoretical IMT-2020 peak):

$$
B_{required} = \frac{2 \cdot 10^{10}}{50} = 400 \text{ MHz}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** This is precisely why **5G NR in FR2 uses channels up to 400 MHz wide**, and why carrier aggregation (combining up to 16 carriers) can deliver **up to 6.4 GHz aggregated bandwidth** for the most extreme mmWave scenarios.
