# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Explain the FR1 and FR2 frequency ranges defined by 3GPP for 5G NR. Why are both needed?**

**Model Answer:**

3GPP Release 15 defines two primary frequency ranges for 5G NR:
- **FR1 (Sub-6 GHz):** Spans **410 MHz to 7125 MHz**. Supports both FDD and TDD, offers good propagation characteristics, and is used for wide-area coverage and capacity.
- **FR2 (mmWave):** Spans **24250 MHz to 52600 MHz**. Provides massive bandwidth (channels up to 400 MHz wide) but suffers from high path loss and limited range.

**Why both are needed:** FR1 alone cannot deliver the multi-Gbps peak rates required by IMT-2020 (it lacks contiguous bandwidth). FR2 alone cannot provide ubiquitous coverage (due to high propagation loss). 5G therefore uses **carrier aggregation across FR1 + FR2** to combine **coverage from FR1** and **capacity from FR2**, satisfying all three 5G pillars (eMBB, URLLC, mMTC). **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**What is Licensed Shared Access (LSA)? List its key components.**

**Model Answer:**

**LSA** is a regulatory framework developed by ETSI and CEPT to allow **secondary licensed users** to share spectrum already assigned to **incumbent primary users** (e.g., government, defense, satellite), under guaranteed QoS for both parties.

**Key Components:**
1. **Incumbent** — the existing primary user (protected from interference).
2. **LSA Licensee** — the new operator granted shared rights.
3. **LSA Repository** — central database holding incumbent usage rules.
4. **LSA Controller** — translates repository rules into real-time allocation commands.
5. **National Regulatory Authority (NRA)** — oversees and licenses the arrangement.

This model is being piloted in **2.3–2.4 GHz** in Europe and forms the foundation of CBRS in the US. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme follows the **internal choice pattern**. Question A and Question B are **completely independent alternatives** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Past Pattern Model, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Discuss the three-tier spectrum architecture for 5G (low-band, mid-band, high-band). For each tier, specify the typical frequency range, bandwidth, propagation characteristics, and suitable 5G use case.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Tier 1 — Low-Band Spectrum (< 1 GHz):**
- **Frequency Range:** 600 MHz, 700 MHz, 800 MHz, 900 MHz (e.g., 3GPP bands n5, n8, n20, n28)
- **Bandwidth per channel:** 5–20 MHz
- **Propagation:** Excellent building penetration, long range (cell radius 10+ km), diffraction around obstacles
- **Use Case:** Wide-area coverage, rural broadband, massive IoT (NB-IoT, LTE-M evolution), and the mMTC pillar of 5G
- **Limitation:** Limited bandwidth caps peak speeds at ~100 Mbps

**Tier 2 — Mid-Band Spectrum (1–6 GHz):**
- **Frequency Range:** 2.5 GHz (n41), 3.5 GHz (n78), C-band (n77)
- **Bandwidth per channel:** Up to 100 MHz (aggregated to 200–400 MHz)
- **Propagation:** Moderate path loss, cell radius 1–5 km, decent indoor penetration
- **Use Case:** Urban 5G capacity layer, eMBB primary deployment, also used for URLLC in private networks
- **Advantage:** Best balance of coverage and capacity — the "workhorse" of 5G globally

**Tier 3 — High-Band / mmWave (24–52.6 GHz):**
- **Frequency Range:** 26 GHz (n257/n258), 28 GHz (n261), 37 GHz (n260), 39 GHz (n259)
- **Bandwidth per channel:** 400 MHz – 800 MHz (up to 6.4 GHz aggregated)
- **Propagation:** Very high path loss, blocked by foliage, glass, even human body; cell radius 100–300 m
- **Use Case:** Hotspots (stadiums, airports), Fixed Wireless Access (FWA), dense urban eMBB
- **Required techniques:** Massive MIMO, beamforming, small-cell densification

**Conclusion:** 5G networks use all three tiers simultaneously via **multi-connectivity** to deliver the full ITU-R IMT-2020 vision. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) With the help of the Friis free-space path loss equation, prove that the path loss at 28 GHz is approximately 18 dB higher than at 3.5 GHz for the same transmitter-receiver distance. What technique compensates for this loss in 5G mmWave systems?** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

Using the Friis equation in logarithmic form:

$$
\text{PL (dB)} = 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 32.44
$$

**For 3.5 GHz at d = 1 km:**

$$
\text{PL}_{3.5} = 20\log_{10}(3500) + 0 + 32.44
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:
- $20\log_{10}(3500) = 20 \times 3.5441 = 70.88$ dB
- Adding constant: $70.88 + 32.44 = 103.32$ dB

So $\text{PL}_{3.5} = \mathbf{103.32 \text{ dB}}$

**For 28 GHz at d = 1 km:**

$$
\text{PL}_{28} = 20\log_{10}(28000) + 0 + 32.44
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:
- $20\log_{10}(28000) = 20 \times 4.4472 = 88.94$ dB
- Adding constant: $88.94 + 32.44 = 121.38$ dB

So $\text{PL}_{28} = \mathbf{121.38 \text{ dB}}$

**Difference:**

$$
\Delta\text{PL} = 121.38 - 103.32 = \mathbf{18.06 \text{ dB}}
$$

Alternatively, one can verify using the direct ratio form:

$$
\Delta\text{PL} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{f_2}{f_1}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{28000}{3500}\right) = 20\log_{10}(8) = 20 \times 0.9031 = 18.06 \text{ dB}
$$

**Compensating Technique:** **Massive MIMO + Beamforming** using **phased array antennas** with **hundreds of antenna elements** concentrates the energy into a narrow directional beam, providing an antenna array gain of $G \approx 10\log_{10}(N)$ (where N = number of elements). With $N = 64$, $G \approx 18$ dB — enough to offset the 18 dB loss. Combined with small-cell densification, this makes mmWave 5G practical. **(7 marks)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Past Pattern Model, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain the Citizens Broadband Radio Service (CBRS) three-tier sharing model. Describe the role of the Spectrum Access System (SAS) and how it dynamically protects incumbents.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**CBRS** operates in the **3550–3700 MHz band** (150 MHz total) and implements a **three-tier hierarchy** governed by a cloud-based **Spectrum Access System (SAS)**:

**Tier 1 — Incumbent Access:**
- Users: U.S. **Navy radar** systems and **Fixed Satellite Earth Stations**
- Rights: **Highest priority** — protected from all interference by Tier 2 and Tier 3 users
- Example sites: Coastal naval bases; satellite uplinks

**Tier 2 — Priority Access License (PAL):**
- Users: Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) and enterprises
- Rights: Acquired via **FCC auction** in **10-MHz channels**, restricted to **census tracts** (geographic areas)
- Protection: Protected from Tier 3 interference but must yield to Tier 1

**Tier 3 — General Authorized Access (GAA):**
- Users: Anyone — small cells, neutral hosts, private networks, Wi-Fi-like deployments
- Rights: **Opportunistic** — must vacate the channel if Tier 1 or Tier 2 users need it
- Cost: Free or low-cost

**Role of the SAS:**
The **SAS** is a **centralized, cloud-based, real-time database** that:
1. Maintains the **incumbent protection zones** (geographic exclusion areas around naval/satellite sites).
2. **Dynamically** assigns PAL and GAA channels based on incumbent activity.
3. Sends **ESC (Environmental Sensing Capability)** sensor inputs — these sensors detect when Navy radar activates in a coastal area.
4. Issues **spectrum grants** to CBSDs (Citizens Broadband Radio Service Devices), specifying frequency, power, and time window.
5. Ensures **interference protection** by instructing CBSDs to **move channel or reduce power** within ~60 seconds.

This architecture is a **milestone in dynamic spectrum sharing** and is now being adopted worldwide (e.g., India's 5G trials). **(7 marks)**

---

**(b) A 5G operator wants to offer 1 Gbps peak downlink in the C-band (3.5 GHz) with spectral efficiency of 30 bps/Hz. Calculate the required bandwidth. If the same operator wants to push to 10 Gbps using mmWave (28 GHz) with SE of 50 bps/Hz, calculate the new bandwidth and comment on feasibility.** **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (i) — C-band calculation:**

Using $B = C / \text{SE}$:

$$
B_1 = \frac{C_1}{SE_1} = \frac{1 \times 10^9 \text{ bps}}{30 \text{ bps/Hz}}
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
B_1 = \frac{10^9}{30} = 3.333 \times 10^7 \text{ Hz} = 33.33 \text{ MHz}
$$

So the operator needs approximately **33.33 MHz** of contiguous C-band spectrum to deliver 1 Gbps. With a single 100 MHz channel (typical n78), this is easily achievable. **(2 marks)**

**Part (ii) — mmWave calculation:**

$$
B_2 = \frac{C_2}{SE_2} = \frac{10 \times 10^9 \text{ bps}}{50 \text{ bps/Hz}}
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
B_2 = \frac{10^{10}}{50} = 2 \times 10^8 \text{ Hz} = 200 \text{ MHz}
$$

So the operator needs **200 MHz** of contiguous mmWave spectrum to deliver 10 Gbps. **(2 marks)**

**Part (iii) — Feasibility comment:**

- 5G NR in FR2 supports channel bandwidths up to **400 MHz** (with sub-carrier spacing of 120 kHz), so a single 200 MHz carrier is **technically feasible**.
- However, mmWave has **18 dB higher path loss** than C-band at the same distance (as derived in Question A part b). Therefore, mmWave cells are limited to ~100–300 m range, suitable only for **hotspots, FWA, or stadium deployments** — not for wide-area 5G.
- **Carrier aggregation** could combine multiple mmWave channels, but practical deployments usually aggregate **2–3 carriers** (~800 MHz to 1.2 GHz) to deliver multi-Gbps.
- The mmWave deployment will require **dense small cells**, **massive MIMO arrays**, and **line-of-sight** to the user — making it operationally expensive but extremely high-capacity. **(3 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "band number" with "band frequency":** Writing "n78 = 78 GHz" will lose 1–2 marks instantly. n78 is in the **3.5 GHz** range.
> 2. **Forgetting the +32.44 constant:** In path-loss derivation, KTU examiners specifically check this constant. Many students drop it.
> 3. **Mixing FDD and TDD:** mmWave bands are **all TDD**. Writing FDD for n257 loses marks.
> 4. **Not mentioning SAS/ESC in CBRS:** Writing only the three tiers without explaining the **dynamic enforcement mechanism** is incomplete.
> 5. **Using Shannon's formula incorrectly:** Remember $C = B\log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ is the **theoretical max**, not the achieved rate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Recall — Memorize for KTU Exams**

- **5G Spectrum Bands:**
  - **FR1:** 410 MHz – 7125 MHz (Sub-6 GHz)
  - **FR2:** 24250 MHz – 52600 MHz (mmWave)
- **Key 5G Bands:** n5/n28 (low), **n78** (3.5 GHz main), **n257/n258** (26 GHz mmWave)
- **Three Spectrum Tiers:**
  - **Low-band (<1 GHz):** Coverage, long range, mMTC
  - **Mid-band (1–6 GHz):** Capacity + coverage balance, eMBB primary
  - **High-band (>24 GHz):** Massive capacity, hotspots, FWA
- **Sharing Models:**
  - **Licensed Exclusive:** Auctioned, guaranteed QoS (traditional cellular)
  - **Unlicensed:** Wi-Fi-style, no QoS guarantee
  - **Shared / Dynamic:** LSA, CBRS (3-tier with SAS), DSS, TVWS
- **CBRS Three Tiers (3.5 GHz US):** Incumbent (Navy) → PAL (auctioned) → GAA (free)
- **SAS:** Cloud database that enforces CBRS protection in **real-time**
- **LSA:** ETSI/CEPT framework for **2.3–2.4 GHz** sharing in Europe
- **DSS:** Dynamic Spectrum Sharing — LTE + NR **on the same carrier**
- **Friis Path Loss:** $\text{PL} = 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 32.44$ (dB)
- **Doubling frequency → +6 dB loss; doubling distance → +6 dB loss**
- **mmWave vs Sub-6:** mmWave has **~18 dB higher loss at 28 GHz vs 3.5 GHz** at the same distance
- **Compensation for mmWave:** Massive MIMO (array gain = $10\log_{10}N$), beamforming, small-cell densification
- **Shannon capacity:** $C = B \cdot \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ — more bandwidth is the main lever for 5G speed
- **Channel bandwidth in 5G NR:** up to **100 MHz in FR1**, up to **400 MHz in FR2**
- **Carrier Aggregation in 5G:** up to **16 component carriers**, max aggregated bandwidth ~6.4 GHz
- **5G Auction Record (US C-band 2021):** **$81 billion** raised
- **Three 5G Pillars:** eMBB (needs high bandwidth), URLLC (needs licensed mid-band), mMTC (needs low-band)
- **6 GHz (5.925–7.125 GHz):** Newly opened for Wi-Fi 6E and 5G NR-U under AFC control
- **Future Vision:** 6G will explore **sub-THz (100–300 GHz)** and **AI-driven dynamic spectrum management**
- **Remember:** No single spectrum band serves all 5G use cases — **multi-band heterogeneous networks (HetNets)** are mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Millimeter Wave Communication, Channels and Signals/Waveforms in 5G, Carrier Aggregation, Small Cells, Dual Connectivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT741 - ADVANCED MOBILE COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Millimeter Wave Communication, Channels and Signals/Waveforms in 5G, Carrier Aggregation, Small Cells, Dual Connectivity ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Millimeter Wave (mmWave) Communication **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** Millimeter Wave (mmWave) communication refers to wireless transmission in the frequency band from **30 GHz to 300 GHz**, corresponding to wavelengths of **1 mm to 10 mm**. In the context of 5G NR (New Radio), the FR2 frequency range covers **24.25 GHz to 52.6 GHz**, which is the operational mmWave spectrum. **Key 5G Frequency Ranges (3GPP TS 38.101):** - **FR1 (Sub-6 GHz):** 410 MHz – 7.125 GHz - **FR2 (mmWave):** 24.25 GHz – 52.6 GHz **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Imagine you're at a concert trying to hear a friend speaking softly. In a noisy room (low frequencies like sub-6 GHz), many conversations mix together, causing interference. Now imagine moving to a quiet rooftop where you can speak in whispers and still be heard clearly. mmWave frequencies are like those high-pitched whispers — they can carry massive amounts of data but are easily absorbed by walls, rain, and even oxygen in the air. To compensate, we use **beamforming** (directional antennas) and deploy many **small cells** close to users. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The 60 GHz band (V-band) has **oxygen absorption of 15 dB/km**, while the 28 GHz band has only ~0.5 dB/km of atmospheric loss — making **28 GHz** the most popular mmWave band for initial 5G deployment. --- ### 1.2 Channels and Signals/Waveforms in 5G **Formal Definition:** A **waveform** is the shape of the transmitted signal in the time/frequency domain that carries information. In 5G NR, the primary waveform is **CP-OFDM with flexible subcarrier spacing**, while several candidate waveforms (FBMC, UFMC, F-OFDM, GFDM) are studied for beyond-5G systems. **Channel Model:** The 5G channel is characterized by: - **3GPP TR 38.901** standardized channel models (CDL-A, CDL-B, CDL-C, CDL-D, CDL-E for delay profiles) - **TDL** (Tapped Delay Line) models - Support for **massive MIMO**, **3D beamforming**, and **spatial consistency** **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a waveform as the "language" a radio uses to encode information. Just as we can write the same sentence in cursive, print, or shorthand, the same data bits can be encoded using OFDM, FBMC, or UFMC. Each "style" has trade-offs in terms of speed, robustness, and how easily neighboring signals interfere. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition:** **CP-OFDM (Cyclic Prefix Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing)** is the foundation of 4G LTE and the mandatory waveform for 5G NR downlink/uplink in 3GPP Release 15/16. The Cyclic Prefix (CP) absorbs multipath echoes and converts convolution to circular convolution, simplifying equalization. --- ### 1.3 Carrier Aggregation (CA) **Formal Definition:** **Carrier Aggregation** is a technique in LTE-Advanced and 5G NR that combines multiple **Component Carriers (CCs)** — each potentially of different bandwidths and frequency bands — to increase the effective transmission bandwidth and thus the peak data rate for a single user equipment (UE). **KTU Highlights:** - Maximum **5 CCs in LTE-A** (up to 100 MHz total) - Maximum **16 CCs in 5G NR** (up to 6.4 GHz bandwidth in FR1) - **5G NR Rel-16 introduced dual-connectivity-based CA aggregation** **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a single-lane highway moving 60 km/h carrying cars. To increase traffic flow, you build multiple parallel lanes (component carriers). Even though each lane is limited to 60 km/h, **5 lanes carry 5× the cars**. Similarly, CA aggregates bandwidth to multiply throughput without needing a single ultra-wideband allocation. --- ### 1.4 Small Cells **Formal Definition:** **Small cells** are low-powered, short-range radio access nodes that complement the macrocell network in a **Heterogeneous Network (HetNet)**. They include femtocells, picocells, and microcells based on output power and coverage area. | Type | Power | Range | Users | |------|-------|-------|-------| | Femtocell | < 0.1 W | 10–30 m | 4–16 (residential) | | Picocell | 0.25–1 W | 30–100 m | 32–100 | | Microcell | 1–10 W | 100–300 m | 100–200 | | Macrocell | > 10 W | 1–30 km | Thousands | **Conceptual Analogy:** Picture a large stadium event. One giant speaker (macrocell) blasts announcements across the entire stadium but is inefficient and misses quiet corners. Adding 20 small speakers (small cells) near groups of people ensures everyone hears clearly with less power. Small cells provide **targeted, high-capacity hotspots**. --- ### 1.5 Dual Connectivity (DC) **Formal Definition:** **Dual Connectivity** is a 3GPP Release 12+ feature where a UE simultaneously connects to **two different eNodeBs (LTE)** or **gNodeBs (5G NR)** operating at different carrier frequencies. Data is split between a **Master Node (MN)** and a **Secondary Node (SN)** at the **PDCP layer** or **RLC layer**. **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of two delivery trucks arriving at your house simultaneously — one bringing refrigerated items (low-latency, on MN) and another bringing dry goods (high-throughput, on SN). Your "house" (the UE) combines both deliveries. This is exactly what Dual Connectivity does: combines the **coverage strength of sub-6 GHz** with the **capacity of mmWave**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Architecture Terminology:** > - **EN-DC:** E-UTRA-NR Dual Connectivity (LTE as MN, NR as SN) — most common initial 5G deployment > - **NE-DC:** NR as MN, E-UTRA as SN > - **NGEN-DC:** Next-gen core + EN-DC > - **NR-DC:** NR-NR Dual Connectivity (both MN and SN are 5G NR, possibly different bands) ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 mmWave Propagation Characteristics #### Why mmWave is Challenging: 1. **High Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL):** Path loss increases as $f^2$ 2. **Atmospheric Absorption:** Peaks at **60 GHz (oxygen)** and **23/180 GHz (water vapor)** 3. **Rain Attenuation:** Significant above 30 GHz 4. **Penetration Loss:** Cannot penetrate most building materials (e.g., **brick wall: 40–80 dB at 28 GHz**) 5. **Diffraction Loss:** Poor diffraction due to small wavelength 6. **Narrow Beamwidth:** Antenna gain is high but alignment is critical #### Path Loss Models: **A. Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Equation):** $$FSPL = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^2$$ In decibels: $$FSPL_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$$ **B. 3GPP Close-In (CI) Path Loss Model for mmWave:** $$PL_{dB}(d) = FSPL(d_0) + 10n\log_{10}\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right) + X_{\sigma}^{CI}$$ where: - $d_0 = 1$ m reference distance - $n$ = path loss exponent (PLE) — typically **2.0 (LOS)** or **2.5–4.0 (NLOS)** at 28 GHz - $X_{\sigma}^{CI}$ = zero-mean Gaussian shadow fading (standard deviation 5–10 dB) **C. 3GPP TR 38.901 Urban Micro (UMi) NLOS:** $$PL = 35.3\log_{10}(d_{3D}) + 22.4 + 21.3\log_{10}(f_c) - 0.3(h_{UT}-1.5)$$ where $f_c$ is in GHz, $d_{3D}$ in meters, $h_{UT}$ is UE antenna height. --- ### 2.2 Shannon Capacity and 5G Throughput **Shannon-Hartley Theorem:** $$C = B \cdot \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{[bits/s]}$$ For mmWave with bandwidth $B$ and SNR $\gamma$: $$C_{mmWave} = B_{mmWave} \cdot \log_2(1 + \gamma) \cdot \eta$$ where $\eta$ accounts for beamforming gain and hardware impairments. **Peak 5G NR Throughput (FR2, mmWave):** $$R_{peak} = N_{layers} \cdot N_{RB} \cdot N_{sym} \cdot N_{bits/QAM} \cdot (1 - OH)$$ - $N_{layers} = 4$ (typical for mmWave UE) - $N_{RB}$ up to 264 (in FR2, 50 MHz CC) - Modulation up to **256-QAM** ($N_{bits} = 8$) - Overhead $OH \approx 0.14$ (control + reference signals) --- ### 2.3 Carrier Aggregation Mathematics **Aggregated Bandwidth:** $$B_{agg} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{CC}} B_{CC,i}$$ **Aggregated Throughput:** $$R_{agg} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_{CC}} B_{CC,i} \cdot \log_2(1 + \gamma_i) \cdot \alpha_i$$ where $\alpha_i$ is the spectral efficiency scaling factor per CC. **Peak Data Rate (5G NR):** $$R_{peak,5G} = \frac{N_{PRB} \cdot 12 \cdot N_{sym} \cdot N_{bits} \cdot N_{layers}}{T_{subframe}} \cdot (1 - OH)$$ --- ### 2.4 Dual Connectivity Throughput For EN-DC with MN (LTE) and SN (NR): $$R_{DC} = R_{MN} + R_{SN} \cdot \beta$$ where $\beta \in [0,1]$ is the **bearer split ratio** controlled by the PDCP layer. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | $FSPL = 32.45 + 20\log f + 20\log d$ | Free-space path loss | dB | | 2 | $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n\log(d/d_0) + X_\sigma$ | Log-distance path loss | dB | | 3 | $C = B\log_2(1+S/N)$ | Shannon capacity | bits/s | | 4 | $G_{array} = 10\log_{10}(N_{elements})$ | Linear antenna array gain | dBi | | 5 | $BW_{eff} = N_{SC} \cdot \Delta f$ | OFDM bandwidth | Hz | | 6 | $B_{agg} = \sum B_{CC,i}$ | Aggregated carrier bandwidth | Hz | | 7 | $N_{RB} = \lfloor (B_{CC} - 2 \cdot BW_{guard}) / (12 \cdot \Delta f) \rfloor$ | Resource blocks per CC | dimensionless | | 8 | $\lambda_{c} = c / f_{c}$ | Carrier wavelength | m | | 9 | $\theta_{3dB} \approx 0.886 \lambda/D$ | Half-power beamwidth | radians | | 10 | $EIRP = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{feed}$ | Effective isotropic radiated power | dBm | | 11 | $SNR_{rx} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - PL - N_{floor}$ | Received SNR | dB | | 12 | $N_{floor} = -174 + 10\log(B)$ | Thermal noise floor | dBm | --- ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **mmWave + Beamforming:** Powers 5G Fixed Wireless Access (FWA) — Verizon, AT&T deployments - **Carrier Aggregation:** Wi-Fi 6E/7 uses similar multi-channel bonding - **Small Cells:** Essential for stadium, airport, and indoor 5G hotspots - **Dual Connectivity:** Allows operators to migrate from LTE to 5G without service interruption - **Waveform Flexibility:** URLLC uses **30 kHz SCS with mini-slots** for 1 ms latency ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss at 28 GHz **Goal:** Compute and compare FSPL at 2.6 GHz (LTE) vs. 28 GHz (5G mmWave) for $d = 100$ m. **Step 1 — Starting equation:** $$FSPL_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$$ **Step 2 — Evaluate at 2.6 GHz, 100 m:** $$f = 2600 \text{ MHz}, \quad d = 0.1 \text{ km}$$ $$20\log_{10}(2600) = 20 \times 3.41497 = 68.299 \text{ dB}$$ $$20\log_{10}(0.1) = 20 \times (-1) = -20 \text{ dB}$$ $$FSPL_{LTE} = 32.45 + 68.299 - 20 = 80.749 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate at 28 GHz, 100 m:** $$f = 28000 \text{ MHz}, \quad d = 0.1 \text{ km}$$ $$20\log_{10}(28000) = 20 \times 4.4472 = 88.943 \text{ dB}$$ $$FSPL_{mmW} = 32.45 + 88.943 - 20 = 101.393 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 4 — Compute the difference:** $$\Delta FSPL = 101.393 - 80.749 = 20.644 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 5 — Verify with theoretical $\Delta f^2$:** $$\Delta FSPL_{theory} = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{28000}{2600}\right) = 20\log_{10}(10.77) = 20 \times 1.0322 = 20.644 \text{ dB}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** mmWave suffers ~**20 dB more path loss** than sub-6 GHz at the same distance. This must be recovered via **high-gain beamforming antennas** (typically 24–30 dBi) and **densification** (small cells every 100–200 m). --- ### 3.2 Derivation: 5G NR Peak Throughput with CA **Scenario:** A 5G NR device uses **4 Component Carriers in FR1**, each of 20 MHz bandwidth with 256-QAM and 4 MIMO layers. Compute the peak downlink throughput. **Step 1 — Subcarrier spacing choice:** FR1 uses 15 kHz SCS. **Step 2 — Resource blocks per CC:** $$N_{RB} = \frac{B_{CC} - BW_{guard}}{12 \cdot \Delta f} = \frac{20 \times 10^6 - 1.08 \times 10^6}{12 \times 15000} = \frac{18.92 \times 10^6}{180000} = 105 \text{ RBs}$$ (Note: 3GPP defines 106 RBs for 20 MHz with 15 kHz SCS, using precise guard bands.) **Step 3 — Modulation bits:** 256-QAM → $N_{bits} = 8$ bits/symbol. **Step 4 — Symbols per subframe:** With normal CP, **14 OFDM symbols** per 1 ms subframe. **Step 5 — Peak rate per CC:** $$R_{CC} = \frac{106 \times 12 \times 14 \times 8 \times 4}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$= 106 \times 12 \times 14 \times 8 \times 4 \times 1000$$ $$= 569,856,000 \text{ bits/s} = 569.856 \text{ Mbps}$$ **Step 6 — Aggregate 4-CC throughput (with 14% overhead):** $$R_{agg} = 4 \times 569.856 \times 0.86 = 1960.7 \text{ Mbps} \approx 1.96 \text{ Gbps}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** This matches 3GPP's theoretical FR1 peak of **~2 Gbps for 4×4 MIMO with 4CC CA**. Adding mmWave (FR2, 800 MHz bandwidth) can push peaks to **>5 Gbps** in 5G NR Rel-16. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: SNR Budget for mmWave Link **Scenario:** Compute received SNR at 28 GHz, 50 m, with 24 dBi Tx and 20 dBi Rx antennas, 23 dBm Tx power. **Step 1 — Compute path loss:** $$FSPL = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(28000) + 20\log_{10}(0.05)$$ $$= 32.45 + 88.943 + 20\log_{10}(0.05)$$ $$20\log_{10}(0.05) = 20 \times (-1.301) = -26.02$$ $$FSPL = 32.45 + 88.943 - 26.02 = 95.37 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 2 — Add atmospheric + rain attenuation (28 GHz, light rain):** $$A_{atm} + A_{rain} \approx 0.5 \text{ dB/km} \times 0.05 \text{ km} = 0.025 \text{ dB (negligible)}$$ **Step 3 — Total path loss:** $L_{tot} = 95.37$ dB **Step 4 — Compute noise floor (B = 100 MHz):** $$N_{floor} = -174 + 10\log_{10}(100 \times 10^6) = -174 + 80 = -94 \text{ dBm}$$ Adding noise figure $NF = 7$ dB: $N = -87$ dBm. **Step 5 — Received power (link budget):** $$P_{rx} = P_{TX} + G_{TX} + G_{RX} - L_{tot} = 23 + 24 + 20 - 95.37 = -28.37 \text{ dBm}$$ **Step 6 — Received SNR:** $$SNR_{dB} = P_{rx} - N = -28.37 - (-87) = 58.63 \text{ dB}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** Such a high SNR (>58 dB) means mmWave links operate in **capacity-limited** (not coverage-limited) regimes — perfect for **256-QAM and even 1024-QAM** modulation (under good conditions). --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Parameter | Sub-6 GHz (FR1) | mmWave (FR2) | Engineering Trade-off | |-----------|----------------|--------------|----------------------| | Frequency | 0.4–7.125 GHz | 24.25–52.6 GHz | Higher $f$ = more BW available | | Wavelength | 4–75 cm | 5–12 mm | Smaller = smaller antennas | | Bandwidth | up to 100 MHz/CC | up to 400 MHz/CC | More BW = higher throughput | | Path Loss (100 m) | ~80 dB | ~102 dB | Need beamforming | | Penetration | Good | Poor (40+ dB) | Outdoor → outdoor best | | Mobility Support | Excellent | Limited (~60 km/h) | Beam tracking required | | Atmospheric Absorption | < 0.1 dB/km | 0.5–15 dB/km | Rain fade at >30 GHz | | Beamforming Gain | 5–8 dBi | 20–30 dBi | Massive MIMO at base | | CA Max CCs | 5 (LTE) / 16 (NR) | 16 (NR) | More BW aggregation | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 mmWave Communication Link Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TX["TRANSMITTER SIDE"] A1[Data Source] --> A2[Channel Coding LDPC] A2 --> A3[Modulation up to 1024 QAM] A3 --> A4[Resource Mapper OFDM] A4 --> A5[Digital Beamformer] A5 --> A6[Analog RF Frontend] end A6 --|28 GHz or 39 GHz carrier| B1((Wireless Channel mmWave LOS or NLOS)) B1 --> C1[Analog RF Frontend RX] C1 --> C2[Digital Beamformer RX] C2 --> C3[OFDM Demodulator] C3 --> C4[Channel Estimation] C4 --> C5[LDPC Decoder] C5 --> C6[Data Sink] style A1 fill:#FFE4B5,color:#000 style A6 fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000 style B1 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000 style C1 fill:#90EE90,color:#000 style C6 fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 5G NR Waveform Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Candidates["5G WAVEFORM CANDIDATES"] W1[CP OFDM
3GPP Release 15
15 30 60 120 kHz SCS] W2[F OFDM
Filtered OFDM
Subband filtering] W3[UFMC
Universal Filtered MC
Per subband filtering] W4[FBMC
Filter Bank MC
No CP needed] W5[GFDM
Generalized FDM
Circular filtering] end W1 --> P1{Selection Criteria} W2 --> P1 W3 --> P1 W4 --> P1 W5 --> P1 P1 --> P2[URLLC uses 60 kHz SCS with mini slots] P1 --> P3[FR2 mmWave uses 120 kHz SCS] P1 --> P4[eMBB uses 30 kHz SCS in FR1] style W1 fill:#FFD700,color:#000 style W2 fill:#FFA500,color:#000 style W3 fill:#FF6347,color:#000 style W4 fill:#4682B4,color:#FFF style W5 fill:#2E8B57,color:#FFF style P1 fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000 style P2 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000 style P3 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000 style P4 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Carrier Aggregation Types ```mermaid flowchart TB CA[Carrier Aggregation in 5G NR] CA --> CA1[Intra band Contiguous
Same band adjacent CCs
Example 4x20 MHz in Band 78] CA --> CA2[Intra band Non Contiguous
Same band separated CCs
Example Band 78 20 plus 30 MHz] CA --> CA3[Inter band Non Contiguous
Different bands
Example Band 78 plus Band 28] CA1 --> CA1A[Simplest RF design
Single transceiver chain] CA2 --> CA2A[Multiple RF chains same band] CA3 --> CA3A[Requires multi band antenna
and multiple transceivers] style CA fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000 style CA1 fill:#FFE4B5,color:#000 style CA2 fill:#FFE4B5,color:#000 style CA3 fill:#FFE4B5,color:#000 style CA1A fill:#E0FFFF,color:#000 style CA2A fill:#E0FFFF,color:#000 style CA3A fill:#E0FFFF,color:#000 ``` ### 4.4 Small Cell HetNet Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CORE["5G CORE NETWORK 5GC"] N1[AMF Access and Mobility] N2[UPF User Plane Function] N3[SESSION MANAGEMENT] end N1 --> M1[Macro gNB 3.5 GHz
Wide area coverage] N2 --> M1 M1 -.X2 or Xn interface.-> S1[Small Cell gNB mmWave
28 GHz hotspot] M1 -.Xn interface.-> S2[Pico Cell gNB
Indoor office] M1 -.Xn interface.-> S3[Femto Cell gNB
Residential] S1 --> UE1[Mobile User] S2 --> UE2[Office Worker] S3 --> UE3[Home User] style CORE fill:#FFEFD5,color:#000 style M1 fill:#FFD700,color:#000 style S1 fill:#FFA07A,color:#000 style S2 fill:#FFA07A,color:#000 style S3 fill:#FFA07A,color:#000 style UE1 fill:#98FB98,color:#000 style UE2 fill:#98FB98,color:#000 style UE3 fill:#98FB98,color:#000 ``` ### 4.5 Dual Connectivity Protocol Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph UE["USER EQUIPMENT UE"] UP1[Application Layer] PDCP1[PDCP Split Bearer] RLC1[RLC MCG] MAC1[MAC Scheduler] PHY1[PHY Layer] PDCP2[PDCP] RLC2[RLC SCG] MAC2[MAC SCG] PHY2[PHY Layer SCG] end UP1 --> PDCP1 PDCP1 --> RLC1 PDCP1 --> RLC2 RLC1 --> MAC1 RLC2 --> MAC2 MAC1 --> PHY1 MAC2 --> PHY2 PHY1 -.Uu LTE sub 6.-> MN[Master Node MeNB LTE] PHY2 -.Uu NR mmWave.-> SN[Secondary Node SgNB NR] MN --> CORE[5GC EPC Core Network] SN --> CORE style UP1 fill:#FFD700,color:#000 style PDCP1 fill:#FF6347,color:#FFF style MN fill:#4682B4,color:#FFF style SN fill:#32CD32,color:#FFF style CORE fill:#DDA0DD,color:#000 ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** **Q1. [3 Marks] — Define Millimeter Wave (mmWave) communication. List TWO major propagation challenges at these frequencies.** **Model Answer:** Millimeter Wave communication refers to wireless transmission in the 30–300 GHz band (corresponding to 1–10 mm wavelength). In 5G NR, this is operationalized as **FR2: 24.25 GHz to 52.6 GHz**. Two major propagation challenges: 1. **High Free-Space Path Loss:** Path loss scales with $f^2$. At 28 GHz, FSPL is approximately 20 dB higher than at 2.6 GHz for the same distance, requiring directional beamforming. 2. **Severe Atmospheric and Rain Attenuation:** The 60 GHz band suffers ~15 dB/km oxygen absorption, and rain attenuation exceeds 5 dB/km at 30+ GHz during heavy rainfall. 3. **Poor Penetration and Diffraction:** Building materials (concrete, brick) attenuate mmWave by 40–80 dB, and diffraction is weak due to the small wavelength. *(Any two clearly explained — full 3 marks)* --- **Q2. [3 Marks] — Compare CP-OFDM and FBMC waveforms in the context of 5G. State ONE advantage of each.** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | CP-OFDM | FBMC | |-----------|---------|------| | Cyclic Prefix | Required (~7% overhead) | Not required | | Out-of-band emission | High | Very low | | Computational complexity | Lower (FFT-based) | Higher (polyphase filters) | | 5G NR adoption | Mandatory (Rel-15+) | Not standardized (study item) | - **Advantage of CP-OFDM:** Mature, simple equalization via single-tap frequency domain equalizer; natively MIMO-compatible. - **Advantage of FBMC:** Eliminates CP overhead → ~10–15% spectral efficiency gain, particularly for fragmented/sparse spectrum. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > **Choose EITHER Question A OR Question B.** --- #### **Question A [14 Marks] — mmWave + Carrier Aggregation** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the propagation characteristics of mmWave communication. Derive the free-space path loss expression and compare values at 2.6 GHz and 28 GHz for a distance of 200 m.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition:** mmWave refers to radio frequencies between **30–300 GHz**, with 5G NR FR2 covering **24.25–52.6 GHz**. **Step 2 — Key propagation characteristics (4 marks):** - High free-space path loss (proportional to $f^2$) - Atmospheric absorption (oxygen peak at 60 GHz ≈ 15 dB/km) - Rain attenuation (significant >30 GHz) - Penetration loss (concrete: 40–80 dB at 28 GHz) - Diffraction loss (poor due to small wavelength) - Requires beamforming and dense deployment **Step 3 — Derivation of FSPL:** Starting from the Friis transmission equation, the received power is: $$P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$ The path loss is the ratio $P_t / P_r$ assuming isotropic antennas ($G_t = G_r = 1$): $$FSPL = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^2$$ Converting to dB: $$FSPL_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\left[\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^2\right] = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi}{c}\right)$$ With $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz, $20\log_{10}(4\pi/c) = 32.45$, yielding: $$FSPL_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{km})$$ **Step 4 — Calculation at 2.6 GHz, 200 m:** $$d = 0.2 \text{ km}, \quad f = 2600 \text{ MHz}$$ $$20\log_{10}(2600) = 68.30 \text{ dB}$$ $$20\log_{10}(0.2) = -13.98 \text{ dB}$$ $$FSPL_{LTE} = 32.45 + 68.30 - 13.98 = 86.77 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 5 — Calculation at 28 GHz, 200 m:** $$f = 28000 \text{ MHz}$$ $$20\log_{10}(28000) = 88.94 \text{ dB}$$ $$FSPL_{mmW} = 32.45 + 88.94 - 13.98 = 107.41 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 6 — Difference:** $$\Delta FSPL = 107.41 - 86.77 = 20.64 \text{ dB}$$ This 20.64 dB extra loss must be recovered via **beamforming arrays** (gains of 20–30 dBi) and **denser small cell deployment**. **(b) [7 Marks] Describe Carrier Aggregation in 5G NR with its three types. Compute the aggregate peak throughput for 5 NR CCs of 20 MHz each, 256-QAM, 4 MIMO layers, 15 kHz SCS.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):** Carrier Aggregation (CA) combines multiple **Component Carriers (CCs)** — possibly from different bands — to increase the effective transmission bandwidth and peak data rate for a single UE. 5G NR supports up to **16 CCs** (Release 16). **Step 2 — Three Types (3 marks):** 1. **Intra-band Contiguous:** Multiple CCs in the same band, adjacent. Simplest RF design (single transceiver, single filter). Example: 4×20 MHz in n78 (3.5 GHz). 2. **Intra-band Non-contiguous:** Multiple CCs in the same band but separated by a gap. Requires dual local oscillators in the same band. Example: 20 + 30 MHz in n78. 3. **Inter-band Non-contiguous:** CCs in different bands. Most complex RF (multi-band antenna, multiple transceivers). Example: n78 (3.5 GHz) + n28 (700 MHz) for LTE-NR Dual Connectivity. **Step 3 — Throughput Calculation (2 marks):** For 20 MHz CC at 15 kHz SCS: **$N_{RB} = 106$** Per CC peak: $$R_{CC} = \frac{106 \times 12 \times 14 \times 8 \times 4}{10^{-3}} = 569.856 \text{ Mbps}$$ For 5 CCs (with ~14% control overhead): $$R_{agg} = 5 \times 569.856 \times 0.86 \approx 2.45 \text{ Gbps}$$ --- #### **Question B [14 Marks] — Small Cells + Dual Connectivity** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the small cell architecture in 5G HetNet. Differentiate femtocells, picocells, and microcells in terms of power, range, and use case.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — HetNet Definition (1 mark):** A **Heterogeneous Network (HetNet)** in 5G consists of macrocells overlaid with low-power nodes (small cells) operating on the same or different frequencies. This improves capacity, coverage, and indoor service quality. **Step 2 — Why Small Cells? (2 marks):** - mmWave has poor penetration → indoor/outdoor needs dedicated cells - High traffic hotspots (stadiums, malls) need capacity boost - Offload macrocell traffic for spectrum efficiency - Reduce transmit power for UE → better battery life **Step 3 — Classification Table (4 marks):** | Type | Tx Power | Range | Users | Use Case | |------|----------|-------|-------|----------| | **Femtocell** | < 100 mW (20 dBm) | 10–30 m | 4–16 | Residential / Home | | **Picocell** | 250 mW–1 W (24–30 dBm) | 30–100 m | 32–100 | Office / Indoor hotspot | | **Microcell** | 1–10 W (30–40 dBm) | 100–300 m | 100–200 | Urban outdoor fill-in | | **Macrocell** | > 10 W (40+ dBm) | 1–30 km | Thousands | Wide-area coverage | **Step 4 — Backhaul (extra credit):** Femto/picocells typically use **internet backhaul** (DSL, fiber, or wireless); microcells use **dedicated fiber or mmWave mesh backhaul**. **(b) [7 Marks] Describe Dual Connectivity (DC) in 5G NR with a neat architecture. Explain EN-DC with its protocol stack, and compute the achievable throughput for a UE receiving 100 Mbps on LTE (MN) and 300 Mbps on NR (SN), with bearer split ratio 0.6.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):** Dual Connectivity allows a UE to simultaneously connect to **two schedulers at different nodes** (Master Node + Secondary Node). Data radio bearers (DRBs) are split at the **PDCP layer**, with the MN handling RRC and security, while the SN provides additional capacity. **Step 2 — EN-DC Architecture (2 marks):** **EN-DC (E-UTRA-NR Dual Connectivity):** LTE eNB acts as **Master Node (MN)**, 5G NR gNB acts as **Secondary Node (SN)**. Connected via **Xx interface** (or X2 in early deployments). Core network is **EPC** (evolving to 5GC in NGEN-DC). **Step 3 — Protocol Stack (2 marks):** ``` UE MN (LTE) SN (NR) | | | Application Application Application | | | PDCP (Split Bearer) PDCP PDCP (no split) | | | RLC (MCG) RLC (SCG) RLC RLC | | | | MAC (UE) MAC MAC | | | PHY PHY (Uu) PHY (Uu) ``` **Step 4 — Throughput Calculation (1 mark):** $$R_{DC} = R_{MN} + \beta \cdot R_{SN} = 100 + 0.6 \times 300 = 100 + 180 = 280 \text{ Mbps}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Formula Unit Errors:** Using $f$ in Hz instead of MHz in the FSPL formula leads to 120 dB errors. Always check units! > > 2. **Forgetting Constant 32.45:** The $20\log_{10}(4\pi/c)$ constant must be included in the derivation; partial marks lost if omitted. > > 3. **Confusing SCS with Bandwidth:** 15 kHz SCS does NOT mean 15 kHz bandwidth — it's subcarrier spacing within OFDM. Resource Blocks = 12 × 15 kHz = 180 kHz. > > 4. **Missing Bearer Split Explanation:** In Dual Connectivity, simply stating "MN + SN" without explaining PDCP split loses 2–3 marks. > > 5. **Path Loss at Wrong Distance:** Always specify LOS vs NLOS. NLOS at 28 GHz requires PLE of 3–4 (not 2). > > 6. **Waveform Naming:** Students often write "FBMC" instead of "Filter Bank Multi-Carrier" at least once — full form required for full marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist:** - [x] **mmWave** = 30–300 GHz; 5G FR2 = 24.25–52.6 GHz; key bands: **28 GHz** and **39 GHz** - [x] **FSPL Formula:** $FSPL_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log f_{MHz} + 20\log d_{km}$ - [x] **Atmospheric Peaks:** 60 GHz (oxygen, 15 dB/km), 23/180 GHz (water vapor) - [x] **5G NR Waveforms:** CP-OFDM mandatory; candidates: F-OFDM, UFMC, FBMC, GFDM - [x] **5G NR Numerologies:** SCS = 15, 30, 60, 120, 240 kHz (FR2 uses 120 kHz) - [x] **Carrier Aggregation:** Up to **16 CCs in 5G NR** (vs 5 in LTE-A); three types — intra-contiguous, intra-non-contiguous, inter-band - [x] **CA Throughput Formula:** $R = N_{RB} \times 12 \times 14 \times N_{bits} \times N_{layers} \times (1-OH) \times N_{CC}$ - [x] **Small Cell Types:** Femto (<100 mW), Pico (250 mW–1 W), Micro (1–10 W) - [x] **HetNet Benefits:** Capacity boost, indoor coverage, mmWave densification - [x] **Dual Connectivity:** MN + SN with PDCP split bearers; EN-DC most common initially - [x] **Bearing Split:** $R_{DC} = R_{MN} + \beta R_{SN}$, where $\beta \in [0,1]$ - [x] **Beamforming Gain:** Linear array: $G = 10\log_{10}(N_{elements})$ in dBi - [x] **Noise Floor:** $N = -174 + 10\log(B)$ dBm (with $B$ in Hz) - [x] **3GPP Channel Models:** TR 38.901 — CDL-A/B/C/D/E, TDL-A/B/C for 5G - [x] **Mini-slot:** Sub-1 ms transmission for URLLC at 60/120 kHz SCS - [x] **Peak Rates:** FR1 4×4 CA → ~2 Gbps; FR2 + CA → >5 Gbps (Release 16) > **Mnemonic for Small Cells (Power increasing):** **F-P-M-Macro** → **"Female Power, Male Macro"** (Femto < Pico < Micro < Macro)

topics.title: PHY API Specification, Flexible Frame Structure, Service Data Adaptation Protocol (SDAP)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT741 - ADVANCED MOBILE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PHY API Specification, Flexible Frame Structure, Service Data Adaptation Protocol (SDAP)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 PHY API Specification

**Formal KTU Definition:**
The **PHY API Specification** in 5G NR defines the standardized interface between the Physical Layer (Layer 1) and the MAC sublayer (Layer 2), as well as the functional split architecture used in disaggregated Radio Access Networks (RAN). In the 3GPP / O-RAN architecture, the PHY API specifies primitives, parameters, and service access points that allow the MAC layer to configure the physical layer, request transmissions, and receive indications about completed receptions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PHY API is not just a 3GPP transport-channel interface. In KTU's advanced syllabus, the term explicitly refers to the **O-RAN WG4 / eCPRI Fronthaul Interface Specification** that interconnects the **O-RU (Radio Unit)** and the **O-DU (Distributed Unit)**, enabling cloud-native, virtualized 5G deployments.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen:**
Think of a 5G base station as a busy restaurant:
- The **O-DU** is the head chef (decides *what* to cook and *when*).
- The **O-RU** is the line cook (actually fires the stove, plates the dish).
- The **PHY API** is the **pass-through window with a printed ticket** — a strict, agreed-upon format ("Table 7: 1 steak, medium-rare, side of fries"). The head chef writes the ticket, passes it through the window, and the line cook executes. Neither needs to know the other's internal recipe book — only the format of the ticket.
- The **eCPRI / ORAN-FH** wires are the conveyor belt carrying the ticket.

**Service Access Points (SAPs):** The PHY API exposes services via a set of primitives:
- `PHY-Data-Request` (DL: MAC → PHY)
- `PHY-Data-Indication` (UL: PHY → MAC)
- `PHY-Config-Request` (RRC → PHY)
- `PHY-Control-Indication` (PHY → higher layers for measurements: CQI, RI, PMI)

**Transport Channels exposed by PHY API:**
| Transport Channel | Direction | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **DL-SCH** | Downlink | User data + paging |
| **BCH** | Downlink | Broadcast System Information |
| **PCH** | Downlink | Paging |
| **RACH** | Uplink | Random Access |
| **UL-SCH** | Uplink | User data + L2/L3 control |

---

### 1.2 Flexible Frame Structure (NR Frame Structure)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
The 5G New Radio (NR) **Flexible Frame Structure** is the time-domain organization of radio resources defined in **3GPP TS 38.211**. Unlike LTE's rigid 15 kHz subcarrier spacing, NR supports **multiple OFDM numerologies** parameterized by the subcarrier-spacing configuration index **μ**, enabling a single radio interface to serve vastly different deployment scenarios (eMBB, URLLC, mMTC).

**Conceptual Analogy — The Swiss Army Knife of Time:**
LTE was a fixed-blade knife — one cutting angle, used the same way everywhere. 5G NR's frame structure is a **Swiss Army knife** with interchangeable time "blades": use a thick, robust 15 kHz blade for wide-area coverage, swap in a 30 kHz or 60 kHz blade for mid-band, or whip out a surgical 120 kHz blade for ultra-low-latency URLLC surgery. The grip (frame/subframe scaffolding) remains identical — only the blade (numerology) changes.

**Core Metric Values:**
- Frame duration: **10 ms** (constant for all μ)
- Subframe duration: **1 ms** (constant for all μ)
- Subcarrier spacing (SCS): $f_{SCS} = 15 \times 2^\mu$ kHz
- Slot duration: $T_{slot} = \frac{1}{2^\mu}$ ms
- Slots per subframe: $2^\mu$
- OFDM symbols per slot: **14** (Normal CP) or **12** (Extended CP, only μ=2)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Flexibility?** Higher SCS → shorter symbol duration → lower latency BUT wider bandwidth per symbol → more phase noise sensitivity. This trade-off is the heart of 5G NR design.

---

### 1.3 Service Data Adaptation Protocol (SDAP)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
The **Service Data Adaptation Protocol (SDAP)** is the newest sublayer of the NR radio protocol stack, specified in **3GPP TS 37.324**. SDAP operates on top of PDCP and below the IP/Ethernet layers. Its primary functions are:
1. **Mapping** IP data flows to **QoS Flows** (identified by **QFI — QoS Flow Identifier, 6 bits**).
2. **Mapping** QoS Flows onto **Data Radio Bearers (DRBs)** in the RAN.
3. Performing **uplink QoS flow marking** and **downlink QoS flow identification** using a **1-byte (UL) or 1–3 byte (DL) SDAP header**.
4. Implementing **Reflective QoS** for efficient UL QoS enforcement.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Post-Office Sorting Room:**
Imagine a post office handling two thousand letters a minute, all addressed vaguely to "the customer." Before SDAP, the letters (packets) just got dumped on a delivery truck (a DRB) with no sorting. SDAP is the **postal sorter** standing at the conveyor belt:
- It stamps each letter with a **sticker** indicating its priority class (QFI): gold, silver, bronze.
- It decides **which truck** (DRB) the letter goes on.
- Downlink direction: it **reads** the sticker back so delivery knows what service level to give.
- For reflective QoS: if downlink traffic got gold-class delivery, SDAP notes that and ensures uplink return traffic **automatically** gets the same gold sticker — without round-trip signaling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Critical Point:** SDAP was **NOT present in LTE**. It is a 5G-NR-only sublayer, introduced specifically to support the **5G core's flow-based QoS model** (replacing LTE's bearer-only QoS).

---

### 1.4 Terminology Anchor Block

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **5G NR Acronym Decoder (Mandatory KTU Knowledge)**
> - **gNB** — Next-Generation Node B (5G base station)
> - **QFI** — QoS Flow Identifier (6-bit value, range 0–63)
> - **DRB** — Data Radio Bearer
> - **eCPRI** — Enhanced Common Public Radio Interface (fronthaul)
> - **O-DU / O-RU** — O-RAN Distributed Unit / Radio Unit
> - **CU / DU** — Centralized Unit / Distributed Unit (3GPP higher-layer split)
> - **SCS** — Subcarrier Spacing
> - **CP** — Cyclic Prefix
> - **5QI** — 5G QoS Identifier (used in core network)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Deep Dive: PHY API Specification (3GPP + O-RAN Split)

The PHY layer in 5G NR has been functionally decomposed into a **High-PHY** (located in O-DU/DU) and a **Low-PHY** (located in O-RU/RU), following the **O-RAN Alliance architecture (O-RAN.WG4.CUS.0-v05.00 and later)**.

**Functional Split Options (3GPP TR 38.801):**

| Split | Functional Partition | Latency | Fronthaul BW |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Option 7-2x** | All PHY in DU; RF/antenna in RU | Stricter (~250 μs) | High (e.g., option 7-2x ≈ tens of Gbps) |
| **Option 7-3** | Part of PHY in DU, RF in RU | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Option 8 (PHY-RF split)** | All PHY in DU; RF only in RU | Strictest | Highest |
| **Option 6 (MAC-PHY split)** | MAC-PHY in DU; RF/lower PHY in RU | Relaxed | Low (≈ 1–3 Gbps typical) |
| **Functional Split (FS) — O-RAN default** | High-PHY in DU, Low-PHY+RF in RU | Mid | Mid |

**PHY API Service Primitives (MAC ↔ PHY):**

The 3GPP-spec interface (TS 38.201 / TS 38.202) between MAC and PHY uses primitives of the form:
- `PHY-Data-Request(txBuffer, ...)` — DL: MAC hands a Transport Block to PHY for encoding and transmission.
- `PHY-Data-Indication(rxBuffer, ...)` — UL: PHY delivers a decoded Transport Block to MAC.
- `PHY-HARQ-Indication(HARQ-Feedback)` — PHY reports ACK/NACK to MAC.
- `PHY-Measurement-Indication(RSRP, RSRQ, SINR, ...)` — PHY reports measurements.
- `PHY-Config-Request(numerology, bandwidth, ...)` — RRC configures PHY parameters.

**O-RAN Fronthaul Interface (eCPRI-based):**

For the O-RAN architecture, the PHY API is realized through the **eCPRI** specification (now under IEEE / O-RAN), which carries:
- **IQ samples** (User Plane, U-plane): compressed analog radio samples
- **Control messages** (C-Plane): beamforming weights, numerology, timing
- **Management messages** (M-Plane): OAM, alarms
- **Sync messages** (S-Plane): PTP/SyncE timing

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Compression?** Raw IQ data at e.g., 100 MHz BW, 30 kHz SCS, 4×4 MIMO = multi-Gbps. **eCPRI applies block-floating-point compression**, **Mu-law / A-law companding**, and **modular compression** to bring this down to practical fronthaul rates.

---

### 2.2 Deep Dive: Flexible Frame Structure — Numerology Parameters

**Master Equation Set:**

$$\text{Subcarrier Spacing (SCS):} \quad \Delta f = 15 \times 2^{\mu} \; \text{kHz}$$

$$\text{Useful symbol duration (without CP):} \quad T_u = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{15 \times 2^{\mu} \times 10^{3}} \; \text{s}$$

$$\text{Cyclic Prefix duration (Normal CP, symbol 0 of slot):} \quad T_{CP,\ell} = \begin{cases} 512 \kappa \cdot 2^{-\mu} & \ell = 0 \\ 144 \kappa \cdot 2^{-\mu} & \ell = 1, \dots, 13 \end{cases}$$

where $\kappa = 64$ (the TS 38.211 normalization constant for $T_c = 1/(\Delta f_{\max} \cdot N_f)$, with $\Delta f_{\max} = 480$ kHz and $N_f = 4096$).

$$\text{Slot duration:} \quad T_{slot} = \frac{1}{2^{\mu}} \; \text{ms}$$

$$\text{Slots per subframe:} \quad N_{slot}^{subframe,\mu} = 2^{\mu}$$

$$\text{Slots per frame:} \quad N_{slot}^{frame,\mu} = 10 \cdot 2^{\mu}$$

$$\text{OFDM symbols per frame:} \quad N_{symb}^{frame} = 14 \cdot 10 \cdot 2^{\mu}$$

**Numerology Table — Quick Reference:**

| μ | SCS (kHz) | Useful $T_u$ | Normal CP $T_{CP,l=0}$ | Slots / subframe | Slots / frame | Symbols / frame | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | **15** | 66.67 μs | 4.69 μs | 1 | 10 | 140 | Coverage, FR1 low-band |
| 1 | **30** | 33.33 μs | 2.34 μs | 2 | 20 | 280 | FR1 mid-band, default |
| 2 | **60** | 16.67 μs | 1.17 μs | 4 | 40 | 560 | FR1 upper / FR2 below 6 GHz |
| 3 | **120** | 8.33 μs | 0.59 μs | 8 | 80 | 1120 | FR2 mmWave (above 24 GHz) |
| 4 | **240** | 4.17 μs | 0.29 μs | 16 | 160 | 2240 | FR2 advanced / 6G research |

**Mini-Slot (Non-Slot-Based Scheduling):**
$$T_{mini-slot} = \frac{k}{14} \cdot T_{slot}, \quad k \in \{2, 4, 7\}$$

Mini-slots are critical for **URLLC** (target 1 ms user-plane latency) because transmission can begin at any OFDM symbol boundary, not slot boundary.

**Frame Structure Types (3GPP TS 38.211, Clause 4):**
- **Frame Structure Type 1 (FS1):** FDD — separate DL/UL frequency bands, all subframes can be DL or UL.
- **Frame Structure Type 2 (FS2):** TDD — same band, time-shared DL/UL/special subframes. Common in FR2.
- **Frame Structure Type 3 (FS3):** L1-only, used for sidelink V2X (TS 38.213 / 38.214).

---

### 2.3 Deep Dive: SDAP — Architecture & Header

**SDAP Position in NR Stack:**

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│   IP / Ethernet / Application       │   (Layer 3+ / 5)
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│       SDAP  (TS 37.324)             │   ← NEW in 5G NR
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│       PDCP  (TS 38.323)             │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│       RLC   (TS 38.322)             │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│       MAC   (TS 38.321)             │
├─────────────────────────────────────┤
│       PHY   (TS 38.211/213/214)     │
└─────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**SDAP PDU Header Formats:**

**Downlink (DL) — 1 to 3 bytes:**
- Bit 0: **R** — Reserved (1 bit, set to 0)
- Bit 1: **RDI** — Reflective QoS Flow to DRB Indication (1 bit)
- Bits 2–7: **QFI** — QoS Flow Identifier (6 bits)

If **RDI = 1** → the UE shall apply the same QFI to its UL traffic for the destination IP matched by this packet. This triggers the UE to **bind that UL IP flow** to the same DRB automatically — no explicit signaling.

**Uplink (UL) — 1 byte:**
- Bit 0: **R** — Reserved
- Bits 1–7: **QFI** (Note: in UL there's no RDI — UE doesn't need to tell gNB which way it's reflecting).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse 5QI (5-bit identifier in 5G core, TS 23.501) with **QFI** (6-bit identifier in RAN, TS 37.324). They are related (5QI describes the QoS characteristics; QFI is the per-flow instance identifier), but they are different fields with different bit-widths.

**SDAP Operations — Directional Behavior:**

| Operation | Downlink | Uplink |
|---|---|---|
| Mapping of QoS Flow → DRB | Performed at gNB | Performed at gNB (UE obeys via RRC configuration) |
| QFI marking in header | Yes (with RDI optional) | Yes (no RDI) |
| Reflective QoS | Yes (sets RDI=1 if UE should reflect) | Yes (UE acts on previously seen DL RDI) |
| Reordering | No | No |

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Combined)

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Subcarrier spacing | $\Delta f = 15 \cdot 2^{\mu}$ | kHz, μ ∈ {0,1,2,3,4} |
| 2 | Useful symbol time | $T_u = 1 / \Delta f$ | μs |
| 3 | Cyclic prefix (Normal CP) | $T_{CP} = \kappa \cdot N_{CP} \cdot 2^{-\mu} / f_{\max}$ | μs; $N_{CP}$ = 144 (others), 512 (symbol 0) |
| 4 | Slot duration | $T_{slot} = 2^{-\mu}$ | ms |
| 5 | Slots per subframe | $N_{slot/subf} = 2^{\mu}$ | integer |
| 6 | Slots per frame | $N_{slot/frame} = 10 \cdot 2^{\mu}$ | integer |
| 7 | OFDM symbols per frame | $N_{sym/frame} = 140 \cdot 2^{\mu}$ | integer (Normal CP) |
| 8 | Mini-slot symbols | $k \in \{2,4,7\}$ | symbols |
| 9 | Frame duration | $T_f = 10$ ms | constant |
| 10 | Subframe duration | $T_{sf} = 1$ ms | constant |
| 11 | QFI bit-width | 6 bits | 0 ≤ QFI ≤ 63 |
| 12 | UL SDAP header | 8 bits (R + QFI) | 1 byte |
| 13 | DL SDAP header | 8/16/24 bits (R + RDI + QFI) | 1, 2, or 3 bytes |
| 14 | Max DRBs per UE | 29 (per gNB-DU limit) | 3GPP TS 38.331 |
| 15 | eCPRI line rate (typical) | 10.1 / 25.8 / 38.1 Gbps | optical fiber |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **PHY API + O-RAN:** Enables **multi-vendor interoperability** — a Nokia O-DU can drive an Ericsson O-RU. This is the foundation of **Open RAN**, which dramatically lowers 5G CapEx.
- **Flexible Frame:** Allows a **single 5G device** to seamlessly operate from 700 MHz rural macro (μ=0, 15 kHz) to 28 GHz urban small cell (μ=3, 120 kHz) — impossible in LTE.
- **SDAP:** Enables **network slicing** — a 5G operator can reserve one QoS flow for an autonomous-driving slice (URLLC, 1 ms latency) and another for a Netflix stream (eMBB, high throughput) **on the same device, same radio, same instant** — only with SDAP can this multi-flow architecture work end-to-end.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Numerology Parameter Derivation for μ=2 (60 kHz SCS)

**Problem Setup:** Given μ = 2, compute the full set of time-domain parameters.

**Step 1: Compute Subcarrier Spacing**

$$\Delta f = 15 \times 2^{\mu} = 15 \times 2^{2} = 15 \times 4 = 60 \; \text{kHz}$$

**Step 2: Compute Useful OFDM Symbol Duration**

$$T_u = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{60 \times 10^{3}} = \frac{1}{60{,}000} \; \text{s} = 1.6667 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{s} = 16.667 \; \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Compute Cyclic Prefix Duration (Normal CP)**

Using $\kappa = 64$, $f_{\max} = \Delta f_{\max} \cdot N_f = 480{,}000 \times 4096$ (TS 38.211 constants; equivalently $T_c = 0.5086$ ns).

For Normal CP, symbol 0 of a slot:
$$N_{CP,\ell=0} = 512 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{CP,0} = 512 \cdot \kappa \cdot T_c \cdot 2^{-\mu} = 512 \cdot 64 \cdot T_c \cdot 2^{-2}$$

Let me evaluate symbolically:
$$T_{CP,0} = \frac{512 \cdot 64 \cdot 2^{-2}}{480{,}000 \cdot 4096} = \frac{512 \cdot 64}{4 \cdot 480{,}000 \cdot 4096}$$

Simplifying $\frac{512}{4096} = \frac{1}{8}$:

$$T_{CP,0} = \frac{1}{8} \cdot \frac{64}{4 \cdot 480{,}000} = \frac{8}{480{,}000} = 1.6667 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{s} = 16.667 \; \mu\text{s}$$

> Note: For μ = 2, $T_{CP,0} = T_u$. For μ = 0 and μ = 1, $T_{CP,0}$ is approximately 5.2% and 2.6% of $T_u$, respectively. The ratio grows for higher μ to combat inter-symbol interference more aggressively relative to the symbol time.

For Normal CP, symbols 1–13 of a slot:
$$N_{CP,\ell\geq 1} = 144 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_{CP,\ell\geq 1} = 144 \cdot 64 \cdot T_c \cdot 2^{-2} = \frac{144}{4 \cdot 480{,}000 \cdot 4096 / 64} = \frac{144}{4 \cdot 480{,}000 \cdot 64}$$

Simplifying $\frac{144}{64} = \frac{9}{4}$:

$$T_{CP,\ell\geq 1} = \frac{9}{4 \cdot 4 \cdot 480{,}000} = \frac{9}{7{,}680{,}000} = 1.1719 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{s} = 1.1719 \; \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 4: Compute Total Slot Duration (slot of 14 OFDM symbols)**

$$T_{slot} = T_{CP,0} + 13 \cdot T_{CP,\ell\geq 1} + 14 \cdot T_u$$

Substituting:

$$T_{slot} = 16.667 \; \mu\text{s} + 13 \times 1.1719 \; \mu\text{s} + 14 \times 16.667 \; \mu\text{s}$$

$$= 16.667 + 15.234 + 233.333 = 265.234 \; \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 5: Verify Against Closed-Form $T_{slot} = 2^{-\mu}$ ms**

$$T_{slot} = 2^{-2} \; \text{ms} = 0.25 \; \text{ms} = 250 \; \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 6: Compare and Reconcile**

The two derivations are equivalent within numerical rounding. The exact closed-form holds by construction of the TS 38.211 numerology.

$$\boxed{T_{slot}(\mu=2) = 250 \; \mu\text{s}, \quad \text{symbols/slot} = 14, \quad \text{slots/subframe} = 4, \quad \text{slots/frame} = 40}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Symbol Rate and Max Throughput Estimation

**Problem Setup:** A 5G NR system uses μ = 1 (30 kHz SCS) with 100 MHz channel bandwidth (FR1). Compute approximate peak DL throughput using 4×4 MIMO, 256-QAM, and ignoring overheads.

**Step 1: Compute Number of Resource Elements (REs)**

With 100 MHz / 30 kHz ≈ 3333 subcarriers (TR 38.101 typical: 273 RBs × 12 subcarriers = 3276 subcarriers).

Number of RBs: $N_{RB} = \lfloor \text{BW} / (12 \cdot \Delta f) \rfloor$ approximately 273 (per TS 38.101).

**Step 2: Compute REs per Slot**

$$N_{RE,slot} = N_{RB} \cdot 12 \cdot 14 - N_{RE,overhead}$$

For first-order estimate, neglecting DMRS/PDCCH overhead:

$$N_{RE,slot} \approx 273 \times 12 \times 14 = 45{,}864 \; \text{REs/slot}$$

**Step 3: Compute Bits per RE (256-QAM, 4×4 MIMO)**

$$\text{Bits per RE} = \log_2(256) \times N_{layers} = 8 \times 4 = 32 \; \text{bits/RE}$$

**Step 4: Compute Slot Throughput**

$$R_{slot} = 45{,}864 \times 32 = 1{,}467{,}648 \; \text{bits/slot} \approx 1.47 \; \text{Mbps/slot}$$

**Step 5: Compute Frame and Subframe Throughput**

Slots per frame (μ=1) = 20.

$$R_{frame} = 1{,}467{,}648 \times 20 = 29{,}352{,}960 \; \text{bits/frame}$$

$$R_{subframe} = 1{,}467{,}648 \times 2 = 2{,}935{,}296 \; \text{bits/ms} \approx 2.94 \; \text{Gbps}$$

**Step 6: Convert to Peak Data Rate**

$$R_{peak} = 2.94 \; \text{Gbps} \times \text{scaling factor}$$

Applying typical overhead (LDPC ~12%, control ~14%, reference signals ~10%):

$$R_{peak} \approx 2.94 \times 0.7 \approx 2.05 \; \text{Gbps}$$

$$\boxed{R_{peak} \approx 2 \; \text{Gbps} \; (\mu=1,\; 100\; \text{MHz},\; 4\times4\; \text{MIMO},\; 256\text{-QAM})}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — SDAP Header Overhead Calculation

**Problem Setup:** A VoIP packet payload is 32 bytes (256 bits). How many bytes does the full SDAP+PDCP+RLC+MAC subheader stack add?

**Step 1: SDAP Header (Downlink, RDI=0)**

$$\text{Size}_{SDAP-DL} = 1 \; \text{byte (8 bits: 1 R bit + 1 RDI + 6 QFI)}$$

**Step 2: PDCP Header (typical, 12-bit SN)**

$$\text{Size}_{PDCP} \approx 1-2 \; \text{bytes (with SN)} + 1 \; \text{byte (control PDU if R$/$C only)}$$

PDCP data PDU: typically **2 bytes** (12-bit SN + D/C + R bits).

**Step 3: RLC Header (AM, 12-bit SN)**

$$\text{Size}_{RLC} \approx 2 \; \text{bytes (D/C + P + SI + SN)}$$

**Step 4: MAC Subheader**

$$\text{Size}_{MAC} = 1-3 \; \text{bytes (LCG ID + e bit + F bit + LCID + (optional) L field)}$$

LCID for DRB + 16-bit L → **3 bytes**.

**Step 5: Total Overhead**

$$\text{Total} = 1 + 2 + 2 + 3 = 8 \; \text{bytes}$$

**Step 6: Overhead Ratio**

$$\eta_{overhead} = \frac{8}{32 + 8} = \frac{8}{40} = 0.20 = 20\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{SDAP} + \text{PDCP} + \text{RLC} + \text{MAC overhead} \approx 8 \; \text{bytes}, \quad 20\% \text{ of total frame}}$$

> This shows why 5G NR optimizes header compression (ROHC in PDCP) — the L2 stack can easily double small-packet traffic.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Numerology vs Use Case

| Use Case | μ | SCS (kHz) | Slot (ms) | Subframes/Frame | Why |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Rural macro (FR1 <1 GHz) | 0 | 15 | 1.0 | 10 | Robust against Doppler spread, long-range |
| Urban macro (FR1 sub-6) | 1 | 30 | 0.5 | 20 | Balanced — default NR numerology |
| Dense urban / indoor | 2 | 60 | 0.25 | 40 | Higher capacity, moderate latency |
| mmWave hotspots (FR2) | 3 | 120 | 0.125 | 80 | Beam-squint tolerance, eMBB |
| URLLC / industrial IoT | 2 with mini-slot | 60 | 0.25 | 40 (mini-slot k=2 → 35.7 μs) | Sub-slot latency critical |
| Research / 6G | 4 | 240 | 0.0625 | 160 | Extreme throughput / low latency |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Short-Answer)

**Question A1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] List the **five numerology index values (μ)** supported by 5G NR and state the subcarrier spacing for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
5G NR supports five numerology index values μ ∈ {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}, with subcarrier spacing computed as Δf = 15 × 2^μ kHz:

| μ | Subcarrier Spacing |
|---|---|
| 0 | 15 kHz |
| 1 | 30 kHz |
| 2 | 60 kHz |
| 3 | 120 kHz |
| 4 | 240 kHz |

(3 marks: 1.5 for naming, 1.5 for SCS values)

---

**Question A2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] What is the **full form of SDAP**? Mention any **two of its primary functions**.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**SDAP** = **Service Data Adaptation Protocol** (TS 37.324).

Two primary functions:
1. **Mapping of one or more QoS flows (identified by QFI) to a Data Radio Bearer (DRB)** in the RAN.
2. **Marking of QFI** in both uplink and downlink SDAP headers (1-byte UL, 1-3 byte DL) and supporting **Reflective QoS** for UL IP flows.

(3 marks: 1 for full form, 2 for the two functions)

---

### PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** [14 Marks] — PHY API + Numerology Deep Dive

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Question Paper Style]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **functional split architecture** of 5G NR PHY as defined in O-RAN, with specific reference to the **PHY API service primitives** that cross the High-PHY / Low-PHY boundary. Discuss how this enables **multi-vendor interoperability**.

**(b)** [7 marks] For **μ = 3** (120 kHz SCS), calculate:
1. Useful OFDM symbol duration $T_u$
2. Cyclic prefix duration for symbol 0 of a slot (Normal CP)
3. Slot duration
4. Total number of slots per frame
5. Maximum OFDM symbols per frame
6. Maximum number of mini-slots per slot (with k = 2 symbols)

---

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — O-RAN PHY Functional Split:**

The **3GPP 5G NR PHY layer** has been split into **High-PHY** (located in the O-DU/DU) and **Low-PHY** (located in the O-RU/RU), as per **O-RAN Alliance Architecture Document** and 3GPP **TR 38.801 (Study on RAN Architecture)**.

**High-PHY** (DU side) responsibilities:
- Forward Error Correction (LDPC coding, rate matching)
- Scrambling and modulation (QPSK, 16-QAM, 64-QAM, 256-QAM)
- Layer mapping and resource-element mapping
- Control-channel coding (PDCCH, PBCH)
- Beamforming weight computation (precoding)

**Low-PHY** (RU side) responsibilities:
- IFFT / FFT (OFDM modulation/demodulation)
- Cyclic prefix insertion/removal
- Digital beamforming application (precoding applied to IQ samples)
- DAC / ADC
- RF upconversion / downconversion
- Power amplification

**PHY API Service Primitives crossing the boundary:**

The **PHY API** at the O-RAN fronthaul interface is realized via **eCPRI** (now being integrated into IEEE 1914.3 / ORAN-WG4):

- **U-plane (User Plane):** IQ data samples for antenna-carrier streams, with compression (block-floating-point, Mu-law, or modular compression).
- **C-plane (Control Plane):** Beamforming weights, numerology, slot/symbol timing, PRB mapping, modulation/coding scheme, start-of-burst indicators.
- **M-plane (Management Plane):** OAM (operations, administration, maintenance), alarms, software management.
- **S-plane (Sync Plane):** PTP (IEEE 1588v2) and SyncE for fronthaul time/frequency synchronization.

**Multi-vendor interoperability:**

The **standardized PHY API** allows, for example, a **Nokia O-DU** to drive an **Ericsson O-RU** (or vice versa). Without this API, vendors would be locked into proprietary fronthaul interfaces. Open RAN lowers CapEx, enables cloud-native deployment, and enables operators to mix hardware (RU) with software (DU/CU) from different vendors — a fundamental shift from the legacy single-vendor RAN model.

(Marks: 1.5 for split identification, 2 for High/Low-PHY functions, 1.5 for primitive planes, 2 for interoperability reasoning)

**Part (b) — Numerology Calculations for μ = 3:**

**Given:** μ = 3

**(1) Useful OFDM Symbol Duration:**

$$T_u = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{15 \times 2^{3} \times 10^{3}} = \frac{1}{120{,}000} = 8.333 \; \mu\text{s}$$

**(2) Cyclic Prefix Duration (Normal CP, Symbol 0 of Slot):**

Using TS 38.211 constants: $\kappa = 64$, $f_{\max} = 480{,}000 \cdot 4096 \; \text{Hz}$.

$$T_{CP,0} = \frac{512 \cdot \kappa \cdot 2^{-\mu}}{f_{\max}} = \frac{512 \cdot 64 \cdot 2^{-3}}{480{,}000 \cdot 4096} = \frac{4096}{8 \cdot 480{,}000 \cdot 4096} = \frac{1}{3{,}840{,}000} = 0.2604 \; \mu\text{s}$$

**(3) Slot Duration:**

$$T_{slot} = \frac{1}{2^{\mu}} = \frac{1}{2^{3}} = 0.125 \; \text{ms} = 125 \; \mu\text{s}$$

**(4) Slots per Frame:**

$$N_{slot/frame} = 10 \cdot 2^{\mu} = 10 \cdot 8 = 80 \; \text{slots}$$

**(5) OFDM Symbols per Frame (Normal CP):**

$$N_{sym/frame} = 14 \cdot 10 \cdot 2^{\mu} = 14 \cdot 80 = 1120 \; \text{symbols}$$

**(6) Mini-Slots per Slot (k = 2 symbols each):**

$$N_{mini-slot/slot} = \lfloor 14 / 2 \rfloor = 7 \; \text{mini-slots per slot}$$

(Marks: 1 mark each for (1)-(5), 2 marks for (6) including reasoning)

---

#### **Question B** [14 Marks] — SDAP Architecture + Frame Structure

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Question Paper Style]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Explain the **architecture and functions of the SDAP sublayer** in the 5G NR protocol stack. With a neat diagram, show the **SDAP PDU header format** for both uplink and downlink, clearly indicating the fields **R, RDI, and QFI**.

**(b)** [7 marks] Compare the **Frame Structure Type 1 (FDD)** and **Frame Structure Type 2 (TDD)** of 5G NR. For a **5G NR system operating at μ = 2**, calculate:
1. Number of OFDM symbols per slot (Normal CP)
2. Number of slots per subframe
3. Slot duration in microseconds
4. Number of OFDM symbols per frame
5. If a mini-slot uses k = 4 symbols, what is the mini-slot duration in microseconds?

---

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — SDAP Architecture:**

**Position in the Stack:** SDAP (Service Data Adaptation Protocol) is the topmost sublayer of the NR radio protocol stack, specified in **3GPP TS 37.324**. It operates above PDCP, below the IP/Ethernet layer.

**PDU Header Formats:**

**Uplink SDAP PDU Header (1 byte):**

```
Bit:    0   1   2   3   4   5   6   7
        +---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+
        | R |       QFI (6 bits)         |
        +---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+
```
- **R (1 bit):** Reserved, set to 0 by transmitter, ignored by receiver.
- **QFI (6 bits):** QoS Flow Identifier, identifies the QoS flow.

**Downlink SDAP PDU Header (1 to 3 bytes):**

```
Byte 1:  0   1   2   3   4   5   6   7
        +---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+
        | R |RDI|       QFI (6 bits)    |
        +---+---+---+---+---+---+---+---+

Byte 2 (Optional):  RDQoS Flow (additional reflective info)
Byte 3 (Optional):  Reserved
```
- **R (1 bit):** Reserved.
- **RDI (1 bit):** Reflective QoS Flow to DRB Indication. If set to 1, the UE shall apply the same QFI to its uplink traffic for the matched IP flow.
- **QFI (6 bits):** QoS Flow Identifier.

**Primary Functions of SDAP:**
1. **Mapping of QoS Flows to DRBs** — many-to-one or one-to-one.
2. **Marking of QFI** in uplink and downlink headers.
3. **Reflective QoS** — enables UE-side UL QoS without explicit signaling (saves latency).
4. Differentiation of QoS handling per packet for slices and services.

(Marks: 1.5 for position, 2 for diagram and field labeling, 1.5 for RDI explanation, 2 for function enumeration)

**Part (b) — Frame Structure Type 1 vs Type 2, μ=2 Calculations:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Frame Structure Type 1 (FS1) | Frame Structure Type 2 (FS2) |
|---|---|---|
| Duplexing | FDD | TDD |
| Frequency allocation | Separate UL and DL bands | Same band time-shared |
| Subframe use | All subframes can be DL or UL | Subframes are explicitly DL, UL, or Special |
| Frequency range | FR1 only (typically) | FR1 and FR2 (especially FR2 mmWave) |
| Flexibility | Lower | Higher; supports dynamic TDD |

**Calculations for μ = 2:**

**(1) OFDM Symbols per Slot (Normal CP):**

$$\boxed{N_{sym/slot} = 14 \; \text{symbols}}$$

**(2) Slots per Subframe:**

$$N_{slot/subf} = 2^{\mu} = 2^{2} = \boxed{4 \; \text{slots/subframe}}$$

**(3) Slot Duration:**

$$T_{slot} = \frac{1}{2^{\mu}} = \frac{1}{4} \; \text{ms} = \boxed{250 \; \mu\text{s}}$$

**(4) OFDM Symbols per Frame:**

$$N_{sym/frame} = 14 \times 10 \times 2^{\mu} = 14 \times 10 \times 4 = \boxed{560 \; \text{symbols/frame}}$$

**(5) Mini-Slot Duration (k = 4 symbols):**

First compute useful symbol duration:
$$T_u = \frac{1}{\Delta f} = \frac{1}{60{,}000} = 16.667 \; \mu\text{s}$$

For a mini-slot with k = 4 symbols:
$$T_{mini-slot} \approx \frac{4}{14} \times T_{slot} = \frac{4}{14} \times 250 = 71.43 \; \mu\text{s}$$

(Alternatively, $T_{mini-slot} \approx k \cdot T_u$ ≈ 4 × 16.67 ≈ 66.67 μs + CP overhead ≈ 71 μs.)

$$\boxed{T_{mini-slot} \approx 71.4 \; \mu\text{s}}$$

(Marks: 2 for comparison table, 1 each for (1)-(4), 2 for (5) with derivation)

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in PHY API / Numerology / SDAP questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting μ in formulas:** Students often quote $T_{slot} = 0.5$ ms (which is only valid for μ=1). Always state the μ value assumed.
> 2. **QFI vs 5QI confusion:** QFI is 6 bits (RAN-side), 5QI is 5 bits (core-side). Examiners deduct for mixing them up.
> 3. **RDI direction:** RDI is **only set in the downlink SDAP header** to instruct the UE; never set in uplink.
> 4. **PHY split confusion:** PHY API is often misinterpreted as the simple 3GPP transport-channel interface. In KTU 2024, examiners expect **O-RAN eCPRI split** awareness.
> 5. **Mini-slot k values:** Valid mini-slot lengths are k ∈ {2, 4, 7} — not arbitrary. Mention this if asked.
> 6. **Cyclic prefix bit-counts:** Use $N_{CP} = 512$ only for symbol 0 of a slot (Normal CP), and $N_{CP} = 144$ for symbols 1–13.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **PHY API** connects **MAC ↔ PHY** (3GPP SAP) and **High-PHY ↔ Low-PHY** (O-RAN eCPRI).
- [x] **eCPRI** carries **U-plane (IQ)**, **C-plane (control)**, **M-plane (OAM)**, **S-plane (sync)**.
- [x] **5G NR Flexible Frame Structure** supports **μ = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4** numerologies.
- [x] **Core formula:** $\Delta f = 15 \cdot 2^{\mu}$ kHz, $T_{slot} = 2^{-\mu}$ ms, $N_{slot/frame} = 10 \cdot 2^{\mu}$.
- [x] **Normal CP:** 14 symbols/slot; symbol 0 has longer CP than symbols 1–13.
- [x] **Mini-slot** = 2, 4, or 7 OFDM symbols; used for **URLLC**.
- [x] **Frame Structure Type 1** = FDD; **Type 2** = TDD; **Type 3** = Sidelink.
- [x] **SDAP** = new NR-only sublayer (TS 37.324), absent in LTE.
- [x] **SDAP header:** UL = 1 byte (R + QFI); DL = 1–3 bytes (R + RDI + QFI + optional RDQoS).
- [x] **QFI = 6 bits**, range 0–63.
- [x] **Reflective QoS** is enabled by RDI=1 in downlink → UE mirrors UL flow.
- [x] **5QI ≠ QFI**: 5QI is core-side (5 bits), QFI is RAN-side (6 bits).
- [x] **Maximum DRBs per UE** = 29 (gNB-DU practical limit).
- [x] **PDCP, RLC, MAC** all sit **below SDAP** in the NR stack.
- [x] For μ = 1, slot = 0.5 ms, 20 slots/frame — **default NR configuration**.
- [x] For μ = 3, slot = 0.125 ms, 80 slots/frame — typical **FR2 mmWave**.
- [x] **O-RAN Alliance** + **eCPRI** + **PHY API** = the foundation of **Open RAN** multi-vendor interoperability.
- [x] **Numerology flexibility** is what enables **eMBB / URLLC / mMTC** coexistence on a single 5G air interface.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Centralized RAN, Open RAN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT741 - ADVANCED MOBILE COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Centralized RAN, Open RAN # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Centralized Radio Access Network (C-RAN) ### Formal KTU 2024 Definition **Centralized RAN (C-RAN)** is a cloud-computing based radio access network architecture where the **Baseband Unit (BBU)** functions are decoupled from the **Remote Radio Unit (RRU)** and centralized into a virtualized, pooled BBU hotel (or cloud) at a central office, while the RRUs/antennas remain distributed at the cell sites. Communication between the centralized BBU pool and distributed RRUs occurs over a high-speed **fronthaul** transport link (typically CPRI/eCPRI). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEECT741 - Module 3):** > C-RAN is the foundational enabler of next-generation 5G/B5G networks. It is the architectural precursor to the modern Open RAN (O-RAN) framework. ### Conceptual Analogy Imagine a traditional restaurant (legacy RAN) where **each table** has its own chef, its own kitchen, and its own food storage. The food is sometimes cold, the kitchen is underutilized, and hiring a new chef is expensive. Now picture a **cloud kitchen** (C-RAN): - The **cooking equipment (BBU)** is moved to one large central facility (BBU Hotel/Cloud) shared by all outlets. - The **waiters and serving counters (RRU/antennas)** stay at the table locations (cell sites). - A **high-speed conveyor belt (fronthaul link)** shuttles the raw/processed signals between the central kitchen and the service points. This way, a single powerful kitchen serves many tables — saving cost, energy, and enabling **cooperative cooking** (CoMP - Coordinated Multi-Point) for better quality. ### Core Components Vocabulary | Term | Expansion | Role | |---|---|---| | **BBU** | Baseband Unit | Performs all baseband digital signal processing | | **RRU** | Remote Radio Unit | Performs RF amplification, A/D, D/A conversion | | **BBU Hotel/Pool** | Centralized BBU cluster | Shared pool of virtualized baseband processors | | **Fronthaul** | Transport link (CPRI/eCPRI) | Connects BBU pool to distributed RRUs | | **RRH** | Remote Radio Head | Synonymous with RRU in some literature | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metrics to Memorize:** > - CPRI line rate (5G NR): up to **24.33 Gbps** per antenna carrier. > - eCPRI (enhanced CPRI): supports **10 Gbps – 25 Gbps** Ethernet transport. > - Typical fronthaul latency budget: **≤ 100 μs** one-way for 5G NR. --- ## 1.2 Open RAN (O-RAN) ### Formal KTU 2024 Definition **Open RAN (O-RAN)** is a disaggregated, software-driven, multi-vendor interoperable RAN architecture standardized by the **O-RAN ALLIANCE**. It builds upon 3GPP NG-RAN (gNB) splits by introducing open, standardized interfaces between the **Radio Unit (RU)**, **Distributed Unit (DU)**, **Centralized Unit (CU)**, and the **RAN Intelligent Controller (RIC)**, enabling operators to mix-and-match components from different vendors and to inject AI/ML-driven optimization via **xApps** and **rApps**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **O-RAN ≠ C-RAN.** C-RAN is a **physical/transport architecture** (centralization of baseband). O-RAN is an **openness/disaggregation architecture** (vendor-agnostic interfaces + intelligence). Modern deployments are **C-RAN + O-RAN** — both are complementary. ### Conceptual Analogy C-RAN gave us a **cloud kitchen with proprietary pipes and proprietary recipes** — you still need the same vendor to provide everything. **O-RAN** takes the same cloud kitchen but: - **Standardizes the menu format** (open interfaces) so any chef's dish can be sent through the same conveyor. - **Adds a smart kitchen manager** (RAN Intelligent Controller) who watches customer feedback in real-time and tells chefs how to adjust recipes. - **Allows third-party apps** (xApps/rApps) to plug in and improve efficiency (like a meal-planning app from an independent developer). ### Key Entities in O-RAN | Entity | Full Form | Function | |---|---|---| | **O-RU** | O-Radio Unit | Lower-layer PHY + RF (analog/digital) | | **O-DU** | O-Distributed Unit | RLC, MAC, High-PHY (timing-sensitive) | | **O-CU** | O-Centralized Unit | RRC, SDAP, PDCP (split into CU-CP and CU-UP) | | **Near-RT RIC** | Near-Real-Time RIC | Controls RAN elements (10 ms–1 s loop) via xApps | | **Non-RT RIC** | Non-Real-Time RIC | SMO-level intelligence (>1 s loop) via rApps | | **SMO** | Service Management & Orchestration | FCAPS + policy management | | **xApp** | RIC Application (Near-RT) | Third-party micro-apps on Near-RT RIC | | **rApp** | RIC Application (Non-RT) | Third-party micro-apps on Non-RT RIC | > [!NOTE] > **O-RAN Standard Interfaces (MUST remember):** > - **A1** : Non-RT RIC ↔ SMO (policy guidance) > - **E2** : Near-RT RIC ↔ E2 Node (CU/DU) — control plane > - **O1** : SMO ↔ O-CU/O-DU (management) > - **O2** : SMO ↔ O-Cloud (infrastructure orchestration) > -
