<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]**
**Define Software Defined Networking (SDN). List its three planes.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **SDN** is a networking paradigm that decouples the **control plane** from the **data plane**, enabling centralized, programmatic control of the network via open APIs.
>
> **Three planes:**
> 1. **Application Plane** — Business and orchestration applications
> 2. **Control Plane** — SDN Controller (logically centralized)
> 3. **Data Plane** — Forwarding devices (switches/routers)
>
> Communication happens via **Northbound API** (app↔controller) and **Southbound API** (controller↔switch, e.g., OpenFlow).

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]**
**What is NFV? Explain the role of VNF and MANO.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Network Function Virtualization (NFV)** is the concept of decoupling network functions from proprietary hardware and running them as software (VNFs) on commodity servers, as standardized by **ETSI**.
>
> - **VNF (Virtual Network Function):** A software-based network function (e.g., vFirewall, vUPF) replacing dedicated hardware.
> - **MANO (Management & Orchestration):** The management framework comprising **NFVO, VNFM, and VIM** that handles VNF lifecycle, resource allocation, and infrastructure management.
>
> NFV reduces CAPEX/OPEX, accelerates service deployment, and enables elastic scaling.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model — Q14A]**

#### Part (a) — Explain the SDN architecture in detail with a neat diagram. Describe the functions of Northbound and Southbound APIs. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
Software Defined Networking (SDN) is a network architecture that separates the network's control logic (control plane) from the packet-forwarding logic (data plane), with a logically centralized **SDN controller** managing the entire network programmatically.

**2. Three-Plane Architecture (4 marks):**

| Plane | Function | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Plane** | Business apps declare network requirements | Load balancers, analytics, security policies, WAN orchestrators |
| **Control Plane** | Translates app intent into flow rules; maintains global view | NOX, Floodlight, **OpenDaylight (ODL)**, **ONOS**, Ryu |
| **Data Plane** | Forwards packets based on flow tables; no decision logic | **Open vSwitch (OVS)**, Pica8, HP VAN, physical OpenFlow switches |

**3. APIs (2 marks):**

- **Northbound API (NB-API):** Connects application plane ↔ control plane. Common standards: **REST (HTTP/JSON), gRPC, NETCONF**. It exposes abstract network capabilities to applications without revealing low-level details.
- **Southbound API (SB-API):** Connects control plane ↔ data plane. Most common standard: **OpenFlow** (managed by ONF). Also: **NETCONF, OVSDB, P4**. Used to install/modify/delete flow entries on switches.

> **Conclusion:** The SDN architecture enables **programmability, centralized management, and vendor neutrality**, making it a foundational enabler of 5G network slicing.

---

#### Part (b) — Explain the OpenFlow protocol. Discuss how an SDN controller handles a "first packet" of a new flow. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. OpenFlow Overview (2 marks):**
OpenFlow, standardized by the **Open Networking Foundation (ONF)**, is the de-facto Southbound API for SDN. Each OpenFlow switch maintains a **flow table** (or multiple tables in OpenFlow 1.3+) consisting of **match fields, priority, counters, instructions, timeouts, and cookies**.

**2. Flow Table Entry Structure (2 marks):**

```
+----------------------------------------+
| Match Fields (Ingress Port, L2/L3/L4)  |
| Priority (e.g., 1000)                  |
| Counters (Packets, Bytes)              |
| Instructions (Forward/Drop/Modify)      |
| Timeouts (Idle/Hard)                   |
| Cookie (Opaque identifier)             |
+----------------------------------------+
```

**3. First-Packet Handling (3 marks):**
When a packet arrives with **no matching flow entry** (table-miss):
1. The switch encapsulates the packet in a **`Packet-In` message** and sends it to the controller.
2. The controller consults its **global network view** and **routing/application logic**.
3. The controller computes the forwarding path and installs a **new flow rule** on the switch using a **`Flow-Mod` message** (with action: forward, modify, drop, etc.).
4. The controller may also send a **`Packet-Out`** message to instruct the switch to forward the original packet immediately.
5. **All subsequent packets** of the same flow match the installed entry and are forwarded at **hardware line rate** without controller involvement — this is the essence of SDN's **flow-based forwarding**.

> **Conclusion:** OpenFlow's reactive control loop enables the controller to make intelligent, network-wide decisions, while the data plane scales with high-speed, hardware-accelerated forwarding.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model — Q14B]**

#### Part (a) — Explain the ETSI NFV reference architecture with a block diagram. Describe the functions of VNF, NFVI, and MANO. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
ETSI's Industry Specification Group for NFV (ETSI ISG NFV), formed in 2012, defined a reference framework to virtualize network functions. The architecture has **three domains** connected via standardized reference points.

**2. Three Domains (4 marks):**

| Domain | Function | Components |
|---|---|---|
| **VNF (Virtual Network Function)** | Software-based realization of a network function (replaces hardware appliances) | vEPC, vFW, vLoadBalancer, vUPF, vAMF; composed of **VNFCs** |
| **NFVI (NFV Infrastructure)** | Pool of physical/virtual compute, storage, and network resources | Hardware (COTS servers, ToR switches, storage) + Virtualization layer (KVM, ESXi, Docker) → exposes Virtual Compute (VC), Virtual Storage (VS), Virtual Network (VN) |
| **MANO (Management & Orchestration)** | Lifecycle, resource, and service orchestration of VNFs | **NFVO** (service orchestration), **VNFM** (VNF lifecycle), **VIM** (NFVI resource control, e.g., OpenStack) |

**3. Key Reference Points (2 marks):**
- **Os-Ma:** NFVO ↔ VNFM
- **Or-Vi:** NFVO ↔ VIM
- **Ve-Vnfm:** VNFM ↔ VNF
- **Vi-Ha:** VIM ↔ NFVI hardware
- **Nf-Vi:** VNF ↔ VIM (for resource queries)

> **Conclusion:** The ETSI NFV framework transforms rigid, hardware-bound networks into **agile, software-defined, multi-vendor platforms**, drastically reducing CAPEX/OPEX and enabling **5G network slicing**.

---

#### Part (b) — Compare SDN and NFV. How do they together enable 5G network slicing? (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**1. SDN vs NFV Comparison (3 marks):**

| Aspect | SDN | NFV |
|---|---|---|
| **Core Idea** | Decouple control plane from data plane | Decouple network functions from hardware |
| **What is virtualized** | The *control logic* | The *network function* itself |
| **Primary Goal** | Programmable, centralized network control | Elastic, software-based service deployment |
| **Standards Body** | ONF (OpenFlow), OpenDaylight | ETSI ISG NFV |
| **Example** | Google B4 WAN (SDN) | vEPC, vIMS (NFV) |
| **Relationship** | Independent but **complementary** | Independent but **complementary** |

**2. Why Both Are Needed for 5G Slicing (2 marks):**
- A **5G network slice** is an end-to-end logical network tailored for a specific use case (eMBB, URLLC, mMTC).
- Each slice needs:
  - **Programmable connectivity** (provided by **SDN** — dynamically steered traffic flows).
  - **On-demand network functions** (provided by **NFV** — VNFs scaled up/down per slice demand).

**3. Architecture of a 5G Slice Using SDN+NFV (2 marks):**

```
+---------------------------------------------+
|          Slice Orchestrator (NFVO)          |
|              (creates slices)               |
+----------------+----------------------------+
                 |
        +--------+--------+
        |                 |
   SDN Controller    VNFM/VIM
   (manages paths)  (manages VNFs)
        |                 |
   SDN Switches     vUPF, vAMF, vSMF
        \________   ________/
                 \ /
            NFVI (COTS servers)
```

- The **SDN controller** dynamically configures transport paths, QoS, and routing for each slice.
- The **NFV MANO** instantiates and scales the VNFs (e.g., separate vUPF per slice).
- Together, they realize **logical isolation, guaranteed SLAs, and elastic scaling** — the three pillars of 5G slicing.

> **Conclusion:** SDN provides the *connectivity fabric*; NFV provides the *service functions*; together, they turn a single physical 5G network into **multiple virtual, purpose-built networks on demand**.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Places Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SDN and NFV:** They are *complementary*, not the *same*. SDN = plane separation; NFV = function virtualization. Markers deduct 1–2 marks if you use them interchangeably.
>
> 2. **Skipping the diagram in 14-mark answers:** A labeled block diagram is **mandatory** and carries **2–3 marks**. Even a rough sketch with the three planes or ETSI reference points scores.
>
> 3. **Forgetting API names:** Students often write "controller communicates with switches" but forget **OpenFlow (southbound)** and **REST/gRPC (northbound)**. Always name the protocol.
>
> 4. **No mention of standards body:** Saying "OpenFlow is a protocol" without mentioning **ONF (Open Networking Foundation)** loses a mark. Similarly, NFV must be linked to **ETSI ISG NFV**.
>
> 5. **Missing the 5G link:** This is a *Mobile Communication* course. Any SDN/NFV answer that does not connect to **5G, network slicing, or C-RAN** will be marked down.
>
> 6. **Vague control-loop explanation:** When asked "how does a first packet work," always describe the **`Packet-In → Flow-Mod → Packet-Out`** sequence step-by-step.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **SDN = plane separation** (control vs data). **NFV = function-hardware separation** (software vs appliance). They are **complementary, not synonymous**.
- The **three planes** of SDN: Application, Control, Data. **APIs:** Northbound (REST/gRPC), Southbound (OpenFlow).
- **OpenFlow** is the SB-API standard; flow tables use **Match–Action** logic; unknown packets trigger `Packet-In` to the controller.
- **ETSI NFV architecture** has three domains: **VNF** (the function), **NFVI** (the resources), **MANO** (the orchestrator — NFVO + VNFM + VIM).
- **MANO sub-blocks:** **NFVO** = service orchestration, **VNFM** = VNF lifecycle, **VIM** = resource management (often **OpenStack**).
- **5G + SDN/NFV = Network Slicing:** SDN provides programmable paths; NFV provides elastic VNFs (vUPF, vAMF, vSMF).
- **C-RAN + NFV:** BBU split into **RU (real-time) + vDU/vCU (virtualized as VNFs)**; SDN orchestrates **eCPRI** fronthaul.
- **Key performance bound:** SDN control loop must be ≤ **1 ms** for **5G URLLC** (3GPP TR 38.913).
- **Benefits recap:** CAPEX/OPEX ↓, time-to-market ↓, vendor lock-in ↓, scalability ↑, programmability ↑.
- **Standards bodies to remember:** **ONF** (SDN/OpenFlow), **ETSI ISG NFV** (NFV), **3GPP** (5G architecture), **O-RAN ALLIANCE** (open RAN with SDN-style RIC).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Network Slicing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT741 - ADVANCED MOBILE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Network Slicing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Network Slicing** is a fundamental architectural paradigm in 5G and beyond (B5G/6G) mobile communication systems that enables the creation of multiple logical/virtual networks (slices) on top of a single shared physical infrastructure. Each network slice is an end-to-end, self-contained logical network instance, comprising dedicated and/or shared resources (radio access, transport, and core network), customized to satisfy specific service-level requirements such as latency, throughput, reliability, and device density.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **3GPP Definition (TS 28.530):** A *Network Slice* is defined as a logical network that provides specific network capabilities and network characteristics, supporting a specific use case or business model over a common physical infrastructure governed by a Service Level Agreement (SLA).

The three primary 5G service classes (defined by ITU-R IMT-2020) that drive slicing are:

| Slice Class | Acronym | Key Metric |
|---|---|---|
| Enhanced Mobile Broadband | **eMBB** | Peak data rate $\geq 20$ Gbps |
| Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communications | **URLLC** | Latency $\leq 1$ ms |
| Massive Machine-Type Communications | **mMTC** | Device density $\geq 10^6 / \text{km}^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Network Slicing is positioned as a *service-centric* enabler of 5G, transforming the traditional "one-size-fits-all" network into a programmable, multi-tenant, and application-aware infrastructure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway System" Intuition

Imagine a single physical highway that needs to serve three very different types of vehicles:

- 🏎️ **Formula-1 Race Cars** (URLLC): Require a smooth, uncongested dedicated lane with guaranteed fast travel (low latency).
- 🚌 **Tour Buses** (eMBB): Need a wide, high-capacity lane to carry many high-data passengers (high bandwidth).
- 🚲 **Bicycles** (mMTC): Thousands of small, low-power bikes needing simple, low-cost lanes for short messages (massive connectivity).

A **Network Slice** is analogous to reserving dedicated lanes (logical channels) on the same physical highway (infrastructure), each governed by independent traffic rules (SLA/policy) to deliver the precise quality of service needed.

> [!TIP]
> The key insight: **The highway is shared, but the experience is isolated and customized.** This is exactly what Network Slicing achieves in 5G.

### 1.3 Core Entities in Network Slicing

- **Network Slice Instance (NSI):** A runnable instance of a network slice providing the required services.
- **Network Slice Subnet Instance (NSSI):** A managed subset of the slice, often confined to a domain (RAN slice vs. Core slice vs. Transport slice).
- **Communication Service (CS):** The end-user-facing service (e.g., V2X, IoT, Cloud Gaming) instantiated over a slice.
- **Slice Tenant:** A customer (operator, vertical industry, OTT provider) leasing the slice under an SLA.
- **Slice Orchestrator:** The entity (typically part of MANO — Management and Orchestration) that instantiates, monitors, and terminates slices.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architecture of Network Slicing — The Three Logical Layers

Network Slicing operates across three primary infrastructure layers, each contributing its own slice-aware capability:

**1. Radio Access Network (RAN) Slicing Layer**
- Achieved via **numerology-based resource partitioning** in 5G NR (different Subcarrier Spacing, slot durations, and mini-slot allocations).
- Supports **dedicated spectrum**, **shared spectrum**, or **scheduler-level isolation**.
- Example: A URLLC slice gets a dedicated mini-slot every TTI, while eMBB uses full slots.

**2. Transport Network Slicing Layer**
- Uses **Flex Ethernet (FlexE)** and **Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN)** for hard isolation.
- Provides deterministic bandwidth guarantees via **network slicing over optical/ethernet fabrics**.

**3. Core Network (5GC) Slicing Layer**
- Leverages **Service-Based Architecture (SBA)** with Network Functions (NFs) instantiated per slice.
- Slice selection at the UE side uses the **Single Network Slice Selection Assistance Information (S-NSSAI)** parameter in the NAS signalling.
- **UPF (User Plane Function)** can be dedicated or shared per slice; **AMF/SMF** are slice-aware.

### 2.2 Anatomy of an S-NSSAI Identifier

The Single-NSSAI is the *identity* of a network slice and is the only slice-related parameter broadcast to the UE:

$$
\text{S-NSSAI} = \langle \text{SST}, \text{SD} \rangle
$$

- **SST (Slice/Service Type) — 8 bits:** Standardized slice category (e.g., SST=1 for eMBB, SST=2 for URLLC, SST=3 for mMTC, SST=4 for V2X).
- **SD (Slice Differentiator) — 24 bits:** Operator-defined optional field to distinguish multiple slices of the same SST.

### 2.3 Slice Lifecycle — Step-by-Step Operational Flow

The orchestration of a slice follows a standardized 4-phase lifecycle:

1. **Slice Design Phase:** Translate service requirements (latency, throughput, reliability) into a *Network Slice Template (NST)* containing resource blueprints and SLA targets.
2. **Slice Instantiation Phase:** The orchestrator (e.g., ONAP, OSM) allocates physical/virtual resources across RAN, Transport, and Core, creating the *Network Slice Instance (NSI)*.
3. **Slice Operation & Supervision Phase:** Continuous monitoring of Key Performance Indicators (KPIs); dynamic scaling, healing, and reconfiguration occur.
4. **Slice Decommissioning Phase:** Graceful release of resources when the service contract expires; de-allocation of virtual resources and cleanup.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** The 3GPP-defined management framework for slices is **3GPP TS 28.531** (slice management requirements) and **TS 28.532** (generic slice management). These are commonly tested in Part A questions.

### 2.4 Resource Isolation Models

Three models govern how slices share underlying resources:

| Model | Description | Use Case | Isolation Level |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dedicated Resources** | Slice owns exclusive physical/virtual resources | Mission-critical URLLC (e.g., remote surgery) | **Hard** (highest) |
| **Shared Resources with QoS** | Common pool with priority queues and policy enforcement | eMBB consumer traffic | **Soft** (medium) |
| **Hybrid** | Critical resources dedicated, others shared | Industrial IoT with backup bearer | **Configurable** |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{S-NSSAI}$ | $\langle \text{SST}, \text{SD} \rangle$ — slice identity | SST: 8 bits, SD: 24 bits |
| $R_{\text{eMBB}}$ | Peak data rate target for eMBB slice | $\geq 20$ Gbps (downlink) |
| $L_{\text{URLLC}}$ | One-way user-plane latency for URLLC slice | $\leq 1$ ms |
| $D_{\text{mMTC}}$ | Device density for mMTC slice | $\geq 10^6$ devices / $\text{km}^2$ |
| $N_{\text{slices}}$ | Number of concurrent slices per BS/gNB | Typically $N_{\text{slices}} \leq 8$ in 3GPP Rel-16 |
| $C_{\text{slice}}$ | Capacity of one slice: $C_{\text{slice}} = \sum_{i \in \text{RB}} \text{SE}_i \cdot N_{\text{SC}} \cdot N_{\text{sym}}$ | bits/s |
| $\eta_{\text{iso}}$ | Isolation efficiency: $\eta_{\text{iso}} = 1 - \frac{\text{Cross-slice interference}}{\text{Total interference}}$ | dimensionless, $0 \leq \eta_{\text{iso}} \leq 1$ |
| $E_{\text{slice}}$ | Energy per bit in a slice: $E_{\text{slice}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{slice}}}{C_{\text{slice}}}$ | Joule/bit |
| $\rho_{\text{util}}$ | Resource utilization of slice $k$: $\rho_k = \dfrac{R_k^{\text{used}}}{R_k^{\text{allocated}}}$ | $0 \leq \rho_k \leq 1$ |
| $\text{QoS}_{\text{5QI}}$ | 5G QoS Identifier — defines flow-level priority | 1 to 255, standardized in 3GPP TS 23.501 |
| $T_{\text{e2e}}$ | End-to-end slice latency budget | ms |
| $P_{\text{rel}}$ | Reliability target for URLLC slice | $1 - 10^{-5}$ (99.999\%) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** The KTU valuation panel awards marks for correct use of S-NSSAI notation and for identifying which slice category (eMBB/URLLC/mMTC) suits a given use case (e.g., autonomous driving $\rightarrow$ URLLC).

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecom Operators (e.g., Reliance Jio, Airtel):** Offer "Network Slice as a Service (NSaaS)" to enterprises for private 5G.
- **Industry 4.0 Factories:** Deploy dedicated URLLC slices for robotic control.
- **Healthcare:** Remote surgery requires URLLC slices with deterministic latency.
- **Autonomous Vehicles (V2X):** V2N (Vehicle-to-Network) communication on URLLC slices.
- **Smart Cities:** mMTC slices for millions of sensors; eMBB slices for public Wi-Fi.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Slice Capacity from Radio Resource Allocation

**Problem Context:** A 5G gNB serves two concurrent slices — Slice A (eMBB) and Slice B (URLLC) — over a shared carrier bandwidth $B$ Hz. We need to determine the effective data rate achievable by each slice under static resource partitioning.

**Step 1 — Define the total system bandwidth and Resource Block structure.**

In 5G NR, the smallest schedulable unit is a **Resource Block (RB)** consisting of 12 subcarriers over one slot. Let:

$$
B_{\text{RB}} = 12 \cdot \Delta f \quad [\text{Hz}]
$$

where $\Delta f$ is the subcarrier spacing. For numerology $\mu$ (where $\mu \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$):

$$
\Delta f = 2^{\mu} \cdot 15 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the number of RBs in the system bandwidth $B$.**

$$
N_{\text{RB}}^{\text{total}} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{B_{\text{RB}}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B}{12 \cdot 2^{\mu} \cdot 15 \times 10^{3}} \right\rfloor
$$

**Textual explanation of conversion logic:** We divide the total bandwidth by the bandwidth of a single Resource Block, then take the floor (since we can only assign whole RBs). The factor $15 \times 10^3$ converts kHz to Hz.

**Step 3 — Partition the RBs between the two slices.**

Let $\alpha$ be the **fraction of RBs allocated to Slice A** and $(1 - \alpha)$ to Slice B. Then:

$$
N_{\text{RB}}^{A} = \alpha \cdot N_{\text{RB}}^{\text{total}}
$$

$$
N_{\text{RB}}^{B} = (1 - \alpha) \cdot N_{\text{RB}}^{\text{total}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute spectral efficiency using Shannon's capacity formula (modified for 5G).**

The maximum achievable spectral efficiency (SE) per RB is given by:

$$
\text{SE}_i = \log_2 \left(1 + \text{SINR}_i \right) \quad [\text{bits/s/Hz}]
$$

where $\text{SINR}_i$ is the Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio at the receiver for RB $i$. The corresponding capacity for slice $k$ is:

$$
C_k = \sum_{i \in \text{RB}_k} \text{SE}_i \cdot B_{\text{RB}} \quad [\text{bits/s}]
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify by assuming uniform SINR across the slice's RBs.**

If we assume all RBs allocated to slice $k$ experience the same average SINR, $\overline{\text{SINR}}_k$, then:

$$
C_k = N_{\text{RB}}^k \cdot B_{\text{RB}} \cdot \log_2 \left(1 + \overline{\text{SINR}}_k \right)
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $N_{\text{RB}}^k$ and $B_{\text{RB}}$ to obtain the final expression.**

$$
C_k = \alpha_k \cdot N_{\text{RB}}^{\text{total}} \cdot 12 \cdot 2^{\mu} \cdot 15 \times 10^3 \cdot \log_2 \left(1 + \overline{\text{SINR}}_k \right)
$$

where $\alpha_k$ is the resource fraction for slice $k$. In matrix form for $K$ slices:

$$
C_k = \alpha_k \cdot B \cdot \log_2 \left(1 + \overline{\text{SINR}}_k \right) \quad \forall \, k \in \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}
$$

subject to the resource constraint:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{K} \alpha_k \leq 1, \quad \alpha_k \geq 0
$$

**Textual explanation:** This constraint ensures that the total allocated RB-fractions do not exceed the available resources, a critical check the examiner expects in 14-mark derivations.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Latency Budget Composition for a URLLC Slice

**Problem Context:** A URLLC slice must guarantee an end-to-end latency $T_{\text{e2e}} \leq 1$ ms. Decompose this budget across RAN, Transport, and Core.

**Step 1 — Decompose $T_{\text{e2e}}$ into its constituent sub-delays.**

$$
T_{\text{e2e}} = T_{\text{UE-RAN}} + T_{\text{RAN-Transport}} + T_{\text{Transport-CN}} + T_{\text{CN-UPF}} + T_{\text{queueing}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 3GPP Rel-16 target breakdown.**

3GPP TS 38.913 specifies (for URLLC):

$$
T_{\text{UE-RAN}} \leq 0.5 \text{ ms (one-way over the radio interface)}
$$

**Step 3 — Allocate the remaining 0.5 ms to non-radio segments.**

$$
T_{\text{Transport}} + T_{\text{CN}} + T_{\text{queueing}} \leq 0.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the radio latency for a mini-slot in 5G NR.**

For numerology $\mu$, the slot duration is:

$$
T_{\text{slot}}(\mu) = \dfrac{1}{2^{\mu}} \text{ ms}
$$

For a **mini-slot** of $N_{\text{sym}}^{\text{mini}} = 2$ OFDM symbols:

$$
T_{\text{mini-slot}}(\mu) = N_{\text{sym}}^{\text{mini}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{\text{slot}}(\mu)}{14} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{14 \cdot 2^{\mu}} \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $\mu = 3$ (60 kHz SCS, common for URLLC).**

$$
T_{\text{mini-slot}}(3) = 2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{14 \cdot 8} = \dfrac{2}{112} = 0.01786 \text{ ms} \approx 17.86 \, \mu s
$$

**Textual explanation:** This shows that with a 2-symbol mini-slot at 60 kHz SCS, the physical transmission duration is only ~17.86 microseconds, leaving ample latency budget for HARQ retransmissions, processing delays, and propagation.

**Step 6 — Compute maximum HARQ retransmissions within budget.**

If processing + retransmission delay per HARQ round is $\delta$ ms, then max HARQ rounds:

$$
N_{\text{HARQ}}^{\max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{T_{\text{UE-RAN}}}{\delta} \right\rfloor
$$

For $\delta = 0.25$ ms:

$$
N_{\text{HARQ}}^{\max} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{0.5}{0.25} \right\rfloor = 2 \text{ retransmissions}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Slice Parameter Selection

The following table provides the engineering logic for mapping a service requirement to a slice configuration — a frequently-tested concept in KTU 14-mark problems:

| Use Case | Slice Type (SST) | Latency Target | Reliability | Bandwidth | HARQ Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4K/8K Video Streaming | eMBB (SST=1) | $\leq 10$ ms | $1 - 10^{-3}$ | $\geq 100$ Mbps | Standard HARQ |
| Autonomous Driving | URLLC (SST=2) | $\leq 5$ ms | $1 - 10^{-5}$ | $\geq 10$ Mbps | Aggressive mini-slot, multi-slot repetition |
| Smart Metering | mMTC (SST=3) | $\leq 1$ s | $1 - 10^{-1}$ | $< 1$ kbps | No HARQ, grant-free access |
| Remote Surgery | URLLC (SST=2) | $\leq 1$ ms | $1 - 10^{-7}$ | $\geq 50$ Mbps | PDCP duplication across carriers |
| Cloud Gaming | eMBB+URLLC (SST=1 or 2) | $\leq 20$ ms | $1 - 10^{-4}$ | $\geq 25$ Mbps | Edge computing + low-latency HARQ |

> [!TIP]
> For 14-mark problems, the examiner expects you to *derive* the latency budget (as in §3.2) and then *justify* the choice of numerology and mini-slot — not just list the parameters.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Network Slicing Architecture (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UE1[UE Slice A eMBB] --> AMF[AMF Slice Aware]
    UE2[UE Slice B URLLC] --> AMF
    UE3[UE Slice C mMTC] --> AMF
    AMF --> NRF[NRF Network Repository Function]
    AMF --> AUSF[AUSF Authentication]
    AMF --> PCF[PCF Policy Control]
    AMF --> NSSF[NSSF Slice Selection Function]
    NSSF --> AMF
    AMF --> SMF1[SMF Slice A]
    AMF --> SMF2[SMF Slice B]
    AMF --> SMF3[SMF Slice C]
    SMF1 --> UPF1[UPF Slice A]
    SMF2 --> UPF2[UPF Slice B]
    SMF3 --> UPF3[UPF Slice C]
    UPF1 --> DN1[Data Network Internet]
    UPF2 --> DN2[Data Network V2X Server]
    UPF3 --> DN3[Data Network IoT Cloud]
    RAN1[gNB Radio Access] -.Slice Resource Partition.-> UE1
    RAN1 -.Slice Resource Partition.-> UE2
    RAN1 -.Slice Resource Partition.-> UE3
    ORCH[Slice Orchestrator MANO] --> NSSF
    ORCH --> SMF1
    ORCH --> SMF2
    ORCH --> SMF3
    ORCH --> RAN1
```

**Interpretation:** This diagram illustrates how a single physical infrastructure (gNB + 5GC) serves three UEs across three slices, with the **NSSF** performing slice selection at attach time, the **SMF/UPF pairs** per slice providing slice-isolated user planes, and the **Orchestrator** governing lifecycle management.

### 4.2 Radio Resource Slicing at the gNB Scheduler (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PHY[PHY Layer Numerology mu]
        SCS1[SCS 15 kHz eMBB]
        SCS2[SCS 30 kHz eMBB]
        SCS3[SCS 60 kHz URLLC]
    end
    subgraph SCHED[gNB MAC Scheduler Slice Aware]
        Q1[Queue Slice A eMBB]
        Q2[Queue Slice B URLLC]
        Q3[Queue Slice C mMTC]
    end
    POLICY[Slice Policy Resource Map alpha_k] --> SCHED
    SCHED --> RB_POOL[Resource Block Pool]
    PHY --> RB_POOL
    RB_POOL --> UE_TX[UE Transmissions]
    POLICY -.Feedback.-> ORCH_M[Slice Orchestrator]
```

**Interpretation:** The MAC scheduler receives slice policies ($\alpha_k$ — resource fraction per slice) from the orchestrator. It maintains per-slice queues and assigns RBs from the shared pool, enforcing isolation as per SLA.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
Define Network Slicing. List the three standard 5G slice types defined by ITU-R IMT-2020.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Network Slicing is a 5G architectural technique that creates multiple **independent logical networks** over a **shared physical infrastructure**, each tailored to meet specific service requirements (latency, throughput, reliability, device density).

The three standard ITU-R IMT-2020 slice types are:

1. **eMBB (Enhanced Mobile Broadband):** High data rates, capacity — for video streaming, AR/VR.
2. **URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communications):** $< 1$ ms latency, 99.999% reliability — for autonomous driving, remote surgery.
3. **mMTC (Massive Machine-Type Communications):** $\geq 10^6$ devices/km², low power — for IoT, smart cities.

*(Marks distribution: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing the three types with brief context.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
What is S-NSSAI? Explain the role of NSSF in network slicing.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**S-NSSAI (Single Network Slice Selection Assistance Information)** is the identity of a network slice sent by the UE during registration. It is composed of:

- **SST (Slice/Service Type) — 8 bits:** Standardized identifier (e.g., SST=1 for eMBB, SST=2 for URLLC, SST=3 for mMTC).
- **SD (Slice Differentiator) — 24 bits:** Operator-defined optional field to distinguish multiple slices of the same SST.

**Role of NSSF (Network Slice Selection Function):**
The NSSF is a 5GC control-plane function that **selects the appropriate slice instance** for a UE based on the requested S-NSSAI, the UE subscription, and operator policy. It returns the AMF set that can serve the requested slice, enabling **slice-aware session management** across the SBA.

*(Marks distribution: 1.5 marks for S-NSSAI explanation + 1.5 marks for NSSF role.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the end-to-end architecture of 5G network slicing, covering the RAN, Transport, and Core network domains. Discuss the role of the Slice Orchestrator with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5G gNB operates on a 100 MHz carrier with 30 kHz subcarrier spacing ($\mu = 1$, $\Delta f = 30$ kHz). Two slices share the resources: Slice A (eMBB) gets $\alpha_A = 0.7$ and Slice B (URLLC) gets $\alpha_B = 0.3$. The average SINR for both slices is 15 dB and 10 dB respectively. Compute the data rate of each slice. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**End-to-End Network Slicing Architecture:**

**1. RAN Slicing (2 marks):**
- Achieved at the gNB MAC scheduler via **per-slice resource partitioning** (RB allocation, numerology, mini-slot scheduling).
- Slice A (eMBB) uses standard 14-symbol slots; Slice B (URLLC) uses 2-symbol mini-slots for low latency.
- Isolation enforced via dedicated pre-emption indicators and RRC-configured slice-specific radio bearers.

**2. Transport Slicing (1.5 marks):**
- Implemented using **FlexE (Flex Ethernet)** for hard bandwidth partitioning and **Time-Sensitive Networking (TSN)** for deterministic delay.
- Each slice gets a logical tunnel with guaranteed bandwidth, latency, and jitter bounds.

**3. Core Network (5GC) Slicing (2 marks):**
- Uses **Service-Based Architecture (SBA)** with NFs instantiated per slice: dedicated **SMF/UPF** for user-plane isolation.
- Slice selection at UE attach via **S-NSSAI**; **NSSF** routes to the appropriate AMF.
- Slice-specific data networks (DNs) attached to slice-specific UPFs (e.g., V2X server for URLLC, IoT cloud for mMTC).

**4. Slice Orchestrator (1.5 marks):**
- Centralized MANO entity (e.g., 3GPP TS 28.533, ONAP, OSM) responsible for:
  - **Designing** slice blueprints (NSTs) from SLA inputs.
  - **Instantiating** NSIs across RAN/Transport/Core.
  - **Monitoring** KPIs and triggering **scaling** or **healing**.
  - **Decommissioning** slices at contract expiry.

*(Refer to the Block Diagram in Section 4.4 for a visual representation.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given Data:**
- Total bandwidth: $B = 100$ MHz = $100 \times 10^6$ Hz
- Subcarrier spacing: $\Delta f = 30$ kHz = $30 \times 10^3$ Hz
- Numerology: $\mu = 1$
- $\alpha_A = 0.7$, $\alpha_B = 0.3$
- $\overline{\text{SINR}}_A = 15$ dB, $\overline{\text{SINR}}_B = 10$ dB

**Step 1 — Convert SINR from dB to linear scale (1 mark).**

$$
\overline{\text{SINR}}_A = 10^{15/10} = 10^{1.5} = 31.623
$$

$$
\overline{\text{SINR}}_B = 10^{10/10} = 10^{1.0} = 10.0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total number of RBs (2 marks).**

A Resource Block (RB) has 12 subcarriers, so:

$$
B_{\text{RB}} = 12 \cdot \Delta f = 12 \cdot 30 \times 10^3 = 360 \times 10^3 \text{ Hz} = 0.36 \text{ MHz}
$$

$$
N_{\text{RB}}^{\text{total}} = \dfrac{B}{B_{\text{RB}}} = \dfrac{100}{0.36} = 277.78 \approx 277 \text{ RBs}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute RBs allocated per slice (1 mark).**

$$
N_{\text{RB}}^{A} = 0.7 \cdot 277 = 193.9 \approx 194 \text{ RBs}
$$

$$
N_{\text{RB}}^{B} = 0.3 \cdot 277 = 83.1 \approx 83 \text{ RBs}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute spectral efficiency per slice using Shannon (1 mark).**

$$
\text{SE}_A = \log_2(1 + 31.623) = \log_2(32.623) = \dfrac{\ln(32.623)}{\ln 2} = \dfrac{3.485}{0.693} = 5.028 \text{ bits/s/Hz}
$$

$$
\text{SE}_B = \log_2(1 + 10.0) = \log_2(11.0) = \dfrac{\ln(11.0)}{\ln 2} = \dfrac{2.398}{0.693} = 3.459 \text{ bits/s/Hz}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute effective data rate per slice (2 marks).**

For Slice A (allocated bandwidth $B_A = 0.7 \cdot 100 = 70$ MHz):

$$
C_A = B_A \cdot \text{SE}_A = 70 \times 10^6 \cdot 5.028 = 351.96 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} \approx 352 \text{ Mbps}
$$

For Slice B (allocated bandwidth $B_B = 0.3 \cdot 100 = 30$ MHz):

$$
C_B = B_B \cdot \text{SE}_B = 30 \times 10^6 \cdot 3.459 = 103.77 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} \approx 103.8 \text{ Mbps}
$$

**Final Answer:**
- **Slice A (eMBB): $C_A \approx 352$ Mbps**
- **Slice B (URLLC): $C_B \approx 103.8$ Mbps**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the lifecycle management of a 5G network slice as defined by 3GPP TS 28.531. Discuss each phase in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the resource isolation models in network slicing. Compare and contrast dedicated, shared, and hybrid resource allocation strategies. Provide one industry use case for each. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

**Network Slice Lifecycle Management (3GPP TS 28.531):**

**Phase 1 — Slice Design (2 marks):**
- The operator (or slice tenant) translates the service requirements (latency, throughput, reliability) into a **Network Slice Template (NST)**.
- The NST contains: *service profile*, *resource profile*, *SLA*, *topology blueprint*, and *QoS policies*.
- Inputs: customer SLA, KPI targets, deployment geography.

**Phase 2 — Slice Instantiation / Preparation (2 marks):**
- The **Slice Orchestrator** (part of MANO) translates the NST into concrete resource allocations across RAN (gNB pools), Transport (FlexE channels), and Core (NF instantiations).
- A **Network Slice Instance (NSI)** is created, with NSSIs in each domain.
- UE-side updates: UDM provisions the allowed S-NSSAIs in the subscription.

**Phase 3 — Slice Operation & Supervision (2 marks):**
- Continuous **KPI monitoring** (latency, jitter, packet loss, throughput, availability).
- **Closed-loop automation:** thresholds trigger *scaling* (adding/removing RBs or NF instances), *healing* (migrating failed NFs), or *re-optimization*.
- **Cross-slice isolation enforcement** via policy conflicts resolution in PCF.

**Phase 4 — Slice Decommissioning (1 mark):**
- Triggered by SLA expiry, customer termination, or business decision.
- Orchestrator performs **graceful UE deregistration**, NF tear-down, resource de-allocation, and billing finalization.

*(Refer to Section 4.3 for the state diagram.)*

---

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**Resource Isolation Models in Network Slicing:**

**1. Dedicated Resource Allocation (2.5 marks):**
- The slice owns **exclusive physical/virtual resources** (RBs, transport links, NF instances).
- **Advantages:** Hard isolation, deterministic performance, no cross-slice interference.
- **Disadvantages:** Low resource utilization, high cost.
- **Use Case:** **Remote Surgery (URLLC)** — requires 99.999% reliability and < 1 ms latency; shared resources are unacceptable.
- **Example:** Private 5G network for a hospital with dedicated spectrum + dedicated UPF.

**2. Shared Resource with QoS (2.5 marks):**
- All slices draw from a **common resource pool** with **prioritization, scheduling policies, and traffic shaping** providing soft isolation.
- **Advantages:** High utilization, cost-efficient.
- **Disadvantages:** Performance variability under congestion; requires robust admission control.
- **Use Case:** **eMBB consumer video streaming** — best-effort with QoS prioritization; bursts tolerable.
- **Example:** Public 5G network for Netflix/YouTube traffic with 5QI-based prioritization.

**3. Hybrid Resource Allocation (2 marks):**
- A **combination** of dedicated and shared — critical resources (e.g., HARQ, scheduling slots) are dedicated, while others (e.g., transport) are shared.
- **Advantages:** Balances isolation with cost; tunable per slice.
- **Disadvantages:** Complex orchestration; requires careful SLA mapping.
- **Use Case:** **Industrial IoT (Smart Factory)** — dedicated control channel for robotic commands (URLLC-grade) + shared best-effort channel for firmware updates.
- **Example:** Smart factory 5G with dedicated mini-slots + shared eMBB pool.

**Comparison Table (for examiner reference):**

| Criterion | Dedicated | Shared + QoS | Hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| Isolation Strength | Hard | Soft | Configurable |
| Resource Efficiency | Low | High | Medium |
| Cost | High | Low | Medium |
| Orchestration Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Suitable Slice Type | URLLC | eMBB | mMTC + URLLC |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Network Slicing Answers:**
> 1. **Conflating S-NSSAI with NSSAI:** S-NSSAI is *one* slice; NSSAI is a *list* of slices. Many students interchange these — a 1.5-mark deduction in 14-mark answers.
> 2. **Forgetting the Resource Constraint:** In derivation problems, students often compute $C_k$ without verifying that $\sum \alpha_k \leq 1$. Examiners explicitly test this.
> 3. **Incorrect SINR Conversion:** A frequent arithmetic mistake: writing $15 \text{ dB} = 15$ instead of $10^{1.5} = 31.62$. Always show the dB-to-linear conversion step.
> 4. **Omitting the Orchestrator Block:** Even in conceptual answers, the 3GPP-aligned architecture MUST mention MANO/orchestration. Missing it costs ~1.5 marks.
> 5. **Mixing up Slice Types:** Smart metering is mMTC (not URLLC); Cloud gaming is eMBB (sometimes URLLC). Examiners deduct 1 mark for misclassification.
> 6. **Skipping the Block Diagram:** A block diagram is *mandatory* for 7-mark sub-parts on architecture. Without it, expect a 1.5 to 2-mark deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Network Slicing:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Logical networks over shared physical infrastructure; 3GPP TS 28.530.
- ✅ **Three ITU-R Slice Types:** eMBB (high throughput), URLLC (low latency + high reliability), mMTC (massive connectivity).
- ✅ **S-NSSAI = $\langle \text{SST (8 bits)}, \text{SD (24 bits)} \rangle$** — slice identity; SST=1/2/3 for eMBB/URLLC/mMTC.
- ✅ **NSSF** selects slice at UE attach; **SMF/UPF** provide per-slice control/user plane; **PCF** enforces slice policy.
- ✅ **Three Layers of Slicing:** RAN (numerology, mini-slots, scheduler), Transport (FlexE, TSN), Core (SBA, NF instantiation).
- ✅ **Resource Models:** Dedicated (hard isolation, URLLC), Shared+QoS (soft, eMBB), Hybrid (mMTC+URLLC mix).
- ✅ **Lifecycle:** Design → Instantiation → Operation/Monitoring → Decommissioning (3GPP TS 28.531).
- ✅ **Mini-slot Latency Formula:** $T_{\text{mini-slot}}(\mu) = \dfrac{2}{14 \cdot 2^{\mu}}$ ms.
- ✅ **Slice Capacity:** $C_k = \alpha_k \cdot B \cdot \log_2(1 + \overline{\text{SINR}}_k)$, subject to $\sum \alpha_k \leq 1$.
- ✅ **URLLC KPIs:** $T_{\text{e2e}} \leq 1$ ms, reliability $\geq 99.999\%$, HARQ retransmissions $\leq 2$.
- ✅ **Always draw a block diagram** showing: Service → Orchestrator → Control (SBA) → Infrastructure (RAN/Transport/CN).
- ✅ **Industry Use Cases to memorize:** Remote surgery (URLLC), Cloud gaming (eMBB), Smart metering (mMTC), V2X (URLLC), Smart factory (Hybrid).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RESTful API for Service-Based Interface
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT741 - ADVANCED MOBILE COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** RESTful API for Service-Based Interface ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **RESTful API (Representational State Transfer Application Programming Interface)** is an architectural style for designing networked applications that relies on a stateless, client-server communication model, using standard **HTTP (Hypertext Transfer Protocol)** methods. In the context of **5G Service-Based Architecture (SBA)**, RESTful APIs form the backbone of inter-network-function communication, where every network element is treated as a service that can be discovered, consumed, and updated via uniform resource identifiers. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** RESTful APIs in 5G enable the **Service-Based Interface (SBI)** which replaced the traditional point-to-point reference points (e.g., S1, X2 in 4G) with a flexible, scalable, and loosely-coupled service-oriented paradigm defined in **3GPP TS 23.501**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **restaurant menu system**: - The **kitchen** is a service provider (e.g., AMF, SMF in 5G). - The **waiter** acts as the RESTful API — it doesn't cook food, but knows *exactly* how to take your order and return your dish. - The **menu** is the API documentation — it lists all available items (URIs) and what you can do with them (GET, POST, DELETE). - You, the **customer**, are the client (another network function or external application). - The kitchen forgets you the moment you leave (statelessness) — every new request must include all information needed. Similarly, in 5G SBA, when the **AMF (Access and Mobility Management Function)** needs authentication data, it sends a RESTful HTTP POST request to the **AUSF (Authentication Server Function)** — the AUSF processes it and returns a response, with no memory of the previous interaction. ### 1.3 Key Terminology | Term | Definition | KTU Relevance | |------|-----------|---------------| | **Resource** | Any information item (e.g., subscriber data) addressable via URI | 5G NF Services | | **URI** | Uniform Resource Identifier — unique address of a resource | e.g., `/nudm-sdm/v1/imsi-12345/` | | **HTTP Method** | Verb indicating action on resource (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH) | Maps to CRUD operations | | **Statelessness** | Each request is independent; server holds no client session state | Core REST constraint | | **Representation** | Current state of a resource (typically in **JSON** or **XML**) | 5G uses JSON exclusively | | **Service Consumer** | NF that invokes a service (e.g., AMF) | | | **Service Producer** | NF that exposes a service (e.g., UDM) | | > [!NOTE] > **5G Mandate:** 3GPP TS 29.500 specifies that all SBI messages in 5G MUST use **HTTP/2.0** over **TLS 1.2/1.3** with **JSON** as the content type (`application/json`). ### 1.4 Core REST Architectural Constraints REST, defined by **Roy Fielding (2000)**, mandates six constraints: 1. **Client–Server Separation** — UI concerns separated from data storage 2. **Statelessness** — No client context stored on the server 3. **Cacheability** — Responses must define themselves as cacheable or non-cacheable 4. **Uniform Interface** — Standardized resource identification and manipulation 5. **Layered System** — Client cannot tell if connected to end server or intermediary 6. **Code on Demand (optional)** — Server can extend client functionality by transferring code --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 RESTful API Operational Logic — Step-by-Step The lifecycle of a RESTful API call within the **5G Service-Based Architecture (SBA)** follows this structured flow: **Step 1: Service Discovery (NF Registration)** - A Network Function (NF) registers its available services with the **NRF (Network Repository Function)** using `PUT /nf-instances/{nfInstanceId}`. - The NRF stores metadata including NF type, FQDN, supported services, and PLMN. **Step 2: Service Consumer Discovery** - The consumer NF (e.g., AMF) queries the NRF: `GET /nf-instances?nf-type=AMF&service-name=namf-comm`. - The NRF returns a JSON list of matching NF instances. **Step 3: Resource Identification** - The client constructs a URI targeting a specific resource on the producer NF. - Example: `http://ausf.operator.com/nausf-auth/v1/ue-authentications/{authCtxId}` **Step 4: HTTP Method Selection** - Choose verb based on intent: `GET` (read), `POST` (create), `PUT` (replace), `PATCH` (partial update), `DELETE` (remove). **Step 5: Request Transmission** - The HTTP request contains: **method, URI, headers, body (JSON)**, transmitted over HTTP/2 on TCP/TLS. **Step 6: Server Processing & Response** - Producer NF processes the request, returns HTTP response with status code (e.g., 200 OK, 201 Created, 404 Not Found). **Step 7: Stateless Acknowledgment** - The connection is closed (or reused via HTTP/2 multiplexing); server retains no session state. ### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step | Step | Engineering Rationale | |------|----------------------| | Service Discovery | Decouples service location from implementation; enables dynamic scaling | | Resource Identification | Provides uniform, predictable access pattern | | HTTP Method Mapping | Standardizes CRUD semantics globally | | Statelessness | Enables horizontal scalability and load balancing across NF instances | | JSON Serialization | Lightweight (vs XML), human-readable, easy parsing in modern languages | ### 2.3 HTTP Methods — The REST Verb Set | HTTP Method | CRUD Operation | Idempotent? | Safe? | 5G SBA Usage Example | |-------------|----------------|-------------|-------|----------------------| | `GET` | Read | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Nnrf_NFDiscovery — Discover NF instances | | `POST` | Create | ❌ No | ❌ No | Nausf_UEAuthentication — Initiate auth | | `PUT` | Replace/Update | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Nudm_Update (replace subscription) | | `PATCH` | Partial Update | ❌ No | ❌ No | Partial modification of UE context | | `DELETE` | Remove | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Nudm_Deregistration — Remove subscriber | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Idempotent** = Multiple identical requests produce the same result. This is a **CRITICAL KTU exam point**. ### 2.4 HTTP Status Codes Used in 5G SBA | Code | Meaning | When Used | |------|---------|-----------| | `200 OK` | Success with body | Successful GET | | `201 Created` | Resource created | Successful POST | | `204 No Content` | Success, no body | Successful DELETE | | `400 Bad Request` | Malformed JSON | Validation error | | `401 Unauthorized` | Auth failed | Missing/invalid OAuth token | | `403 Forbidden` | Auth OK, but not allowed | PLMN mismatch | | `404 Not Found` | URI not found | Unknown resource | | `405 Method Not Allowed` | Wrong HTTP verb | GET on write-only endpoint | | `500 Internal Server Error` | Server fault | NF failure | | `503 Service Unavailable` | Server overloaded | Retry-After header included | ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes | |---------|---------------|---------------| | HTTP Message Size | $M_{total} = H_{headers} + B_{body}$ | Bytes | | URI Structure | `scheme://host:port/path?query#fragment` | RFC 3986 | | Number of REST Constraints | $N_{constraints} = 6$ | 5 mandatory + 1 optional | | HTTP/2 Stream Multiplexing | $N_{streams} = \sum_{i=1}^{c} s_i$ | $c$ = concurrent connections | | Statelessness Property | $S_{n+1} \cap S_{n} = \emptyset$ | Server state at request n+1 independent of n | | Idempotency Definition | $f(x) = f(f(x)) = f(f(f(x)))$ | Same result on repeated application | | Cache Hit Ratio | $R_{cache} = \dfrac{N_{hits}}{N_{hits} + N_{misses}}$ | 0 ≤ R ≤ 1 | | Throughput (TPS) | $T = \dfrac{N_{requests}}{t_{duration}}$ | Transactions per second | | Average Latency | $L = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (t_{response,i} - t_{request,i})$ | Milliseconds | | Retry Count Bound (Exponential Backoff) | $D_{n} = D_{base} \cdot 2^{n-1}$ | $n$ = retry attempt | > **Engineering Utility:** RESTful APIs in 5G SBA enable **network slicing**, **edge computing deployment (MEC)**, and **cloud-native 5G core** (running on Kubernetes), making them indispensable for modern telecom infrastructure. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: REST Performance Metrics in High-Load 5G Scenarios **Problem Context:** A 5G core network AMF instance processes RESTful API calls at peak hour. Given the following data, derive the average latency and cache effectiveness: - Total requests in 1 hour: $N = 360{,}000$ requests - HTTP/2 connection time (TLS handshake): $t_{TLS} = 50$ ms - Server processing time: $t_{proc} = 5$ ms - Network propagation (one-way): $t_{prop} = 2$ ms - Transmission delay (per packet, 1500 bytes): $t_{trans} = 0.5$ ms - Cache hits: $N_{hits} = 90{,}000$, Cache misses: $N_{misses} = 270{,}000$ --- #### Step 1: Compute One-Way Network Delay $$t_{network} = t_{prop} + t_{trans}$$ $$t_{network} = 2 \text{ ms} + 0.5 \text{ ms} = 2.5 \text{ ms}$$ This represents the time for the request packet to travel from AMF to AUSF. #### Step 2: Compute Round-Trip Time (RTT) for the Network Layer $$t_{RTT} = 2 \cdot t_{network} = 2 \cdot 2.5 \text{ ms} = 5 \text{ ms}$$ We multiply by **2** because the response must travel back to the client. #### Step 3: Compute Total Per-Request Latency (No Caching) For a single uncached REST call, the total latency includes TLS handshake, RTT, and server processing: $$L_{nocache} = t_{TLS} + t_{RTT} + t_{proc}$$ $$L_{nocache} = 50 \text{ ms} + 5 \text{ ms} + 5 \text{ ms} = 60 \text{ ms}$$ #### Step 4: Compute Per-Request Latency (With HTTP/2 Connection Reuse) Once HTTP/2 connection is established (session reuse), subsequent requests skip TLS: $$L_{reuse} = 0 + t_{RTT} + t_{proc} = 0 + 5 + 5 = 10 \text{ ms}$$ The TLS cost is amortized across multiple requests on the same persistent connection. #### Step 5: Compute Cache Hit Ratio $$R_{cache} = \dfrac{N_{hits}}{N_{hits} + N_{misses}} = \dfrac{90{,}000}{90{,}000 + 270{,}000}$$ $$R_{cache} = \dfrac{90{,}000}{360{,}000} = 0.25$$ So **25%** of requests are served directly from cache. #### Step 6: Compute Cache Miss Ratio (Probability of Reaching Origin Server) $$R_{miss} = 1 - R_{cache} = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$$ #### Step 7: Compute Effective Average Latency A cached request avoids server processing, so its latency is just network + transmission: $$L_{cache} = t_{RTT} = 5 \text{ ms}$$ The weighted average latency is: $$L_{avg} = R_{cache} \cdot L_{cache} + R_{miss} \cdot L_{reuse}$$ $$L_{avg} = 0.25 \cdot 5 + 0.75 \cdot 10$$ $$L_{avg} = 1.25 + 7.5 = 8.75 \text{ ms}$$ #### Step 8: Compute System Throughput $$T = \dfrac{N_{requests}}{t_{duration}} = \dfrac{360{,}000}{3600 \text{ s}} = 100 \text{ TPS}$$ That is, the AMF handles **100 RESTful transactions per second**. #### Step 9: Compute Effective Bandwidth Utilization Total data exchanged (assuming 2 KB average per request-response): $$D_{total} = N \cdot \overline{S}_{msg} = 360{,}000 \cdot 2 \text{ KB} = 720{,}000 \text{ KB} = 720 \text{ MB}$$ $$B_{util} = \dfrac{D_{total}}{t_{duration}} = \dfrac{720 \text{ MB}}{3600 \text{ s}} = 0.2 \text{ MB/s} = 1.6 \text{ Mbps}$$ This is comfortably below the typical 5G SBI link capacity of **10 Gbps** — confirming RESTful overhead is manageable. #### Step 10: Verification Check that total time served matches expectations: $$t_{total\_ideal} = N \cdot L_{avg} = 360{,}000 \cdot 8.75 \text{ ms} = 3{,}150{,}000 \text{ ms} = 3150 \text{ s}$$ Since 3150 s < 3600 s, the system has **headroom** for ~12.5% additional load. ✅ --- ### 3.2 Idempotency Mathematical Proof We want to show that PUT is idempotent while POST is not. **Definition:** A function $f$ is idempotent if $f(x) = f(f(x))$ for all valid $x$. **For PUT (Replace Resource):** - Request 1: `PUT /udm/subscriber/12345` with body $\{name: "Alice"\}$. Server state: $\{name: "Alice"\}$ - Request 2: Same PUT. Server state: $\{name: "Alice"\}$ - Therefore $f(f(x)) = f(x)$. ✅ Idempotent. **For POST (Create Resource with Auto-Generated ID):** - Request 1: `POST /udm/subscribers` with body $\{name: "Bob"\}$. Server creates `/subscribers/67890` → state contains ID 67890. - Request 2: Same POST. Server creates a NEW ID `/subscribers/67891` → state changes. - Therefore $f(f(x)) \neq f(x)$. ❌ Not idempotent. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: 4G vs 5G Service Interfaces | Parameter | 4G EPC (Reference Points) | 5G SBA (Service-Based Interface) | |-----------|--------------------------|----------------------------------| | Architecture Style | Point-to-point (S1-MME, S1-U, X2) | Service-oriented (HTTP/REST) | | Protocol | SCTP, Diameter, GTP-U | HTTP/2, JSON, TLS | | Coupling | Tightly coupled | Loosely coupled | | Scalability | Limited (per-interface scaling) | High (independent NF scaling) | | Service Discovery | Static configuration | Dynamic via NRF | | State Management | Stateful | Stateless APIs | | Network Functions | ~10 monolithic NEs | ~15+ decomposed NFs (AMF, SMF, UPF, etc.) | | New Service Rollout | Months (vendor-locked) | Days (cloud-native deployment) | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 RESTful API Call Flow in 5G Service-Based Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Client NF
e.g., AMF] -->|1. HTTP GET
NF Discovery Request| B[NRF
Network Repository Function] B -->|2. 200 OK
JSON List of NF Instances| A A -->|3. HTTP POST
Resource Creation| C[Producer NF
e.g., UDM] C -->|4. 201 Created
Resource URI Returned| A A -->|5. HTTP GET
Read Resource| C C -->|6. 200 OK
JSON Representation| A A -->|7. HTTP PUT
Update Resource| C C -->|8. 200 OK
Updated State| A A -->|9. HTTP DELETE
Remove Resource| C C -->|10. 204 No Content| A ``` ### 4.2 REST Resource Hierarchy (UDM Subscriber Example) ```mermaid graph TD Root[UDM Root
/nudm-sdm/v1] --> IMSI[Subscriber
/imsi-{imsi}] Root --> SUPI[SUPI Lookup
/supi-{supi}] IMSI --> SDM[Subscription Data
/sdm-subscriptions] IMSI --> AMData[Access & Mobility Data
/am-data] IMSI --> SMData[Session Mgmt Data
/sm-data] SDM --> Sub1[Subscription 1] SDM --> Sub2[Subscription 2] AMData --> Sub3[AM Subscription] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: REST Layer Stack ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph APP[Application Layer] A1[5G NF Service Logic
e.g., UE Registration, PDU Session] end subgraph API[API Layer - RESTful] B1[Resource Identification
URI Design] B2[HTTP Method Selection
GET POST PUT DELETE] B3[JSON Serialization
application/json] end subgraph TRANS[Transport Layer] C1[HTTP/2 Multiplexing
Stream Frames] C2[TLS 1.3 Encryption
OAuth 2.0 Tokens] end subgraph NET[Network Layer] D1[TCP Connection
Service-Based Interface Bus] D2[IP Routing
SBI Network] end APP --> API API --> TRANS TRANS --> NET NET --> E((Other 5G NFs)) ``` ### 4.4 HTTP Request-Response Cycle in 5G ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Client as Consumer NF (AMF) participant NRF participant Producer as Producer NF (UDM) Client->>NRF: GET /nf-instances?nf-type=UDM NRF-->>Client: 200 OK {NF Profile JSON} Client->>Producer: POST /nudm-sdm/v1/sdm-subscriptions Note over Client,Producer: Body: {callbackUri, monitorResourceType} Producer-->>Client: 201 Created {subscriptionId} Client->>Producer: GET /nudm-sdm/v1/sdm-subscriptions/{id} Producer-->>Client: 200 OK {Subscription Resource} Client->>Producer: DELETE /nudm-sdm/v1/sdm-subscriptions/{id} Producer-->>Client: 204 No Content ``` ### 4.5 Idempotency vs Non-Idempotency Comparison ```mermaid graph LR subgraph IDEMPOTENT[Idempotent Operations] I1[GET /resource] --> I2[200 OK same response] I2 --> I3[GET /resource] I3 --> I4[200 OK same response] I5[PUT /resource] --> I6[200 OK state A] I6 --> I7[PUT /resource] I7 --> I8[200 OK state A unchanged] end subgraph NONIDEMPOTENT[Non-Idempotent Operations] N1[POST /resource] --> N2[201 Created ID 1] N2 --> N3[POST /resource] N3 --> N4[201 Created ID 2 NEW] end ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **Define RESTful API. List any two HTTP methods used in 5G Service-Based Architecture with their purpose.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** RESTful API (Representational State Transfer Application Programming Interface) is an architectural style that uses standard HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE) for stateless, client-server communication over the web. In 5G, it implements the **Service-Based Interface (SBI)** defined in 3GPP TS 23.501, enabling Network Functions (NFs) to expose and consume services via HTTP/2 + JSON. **HTTP Methods (1.5 marks):** 1. **GET** — Retrieves a representation of a resource. *Example:* AMF queries NRF to discover UDM instances using `GET /nf-instances?nf-type=UDF`. 2. **POST** — Creates a new resource on the server. *Example:* UDM creates a new SDM subscription using `POST /nudm-sdm/v1/sdm-subscriptions`, which returns a `201 Created` with the new resource URI. *(Optional additional for completeness: PUT replaces, DELETE removes — both idempotent; POST is not idempotent.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain the role of NRF (Network Repository Function) in 5G Service-Based Architecture. How does it use RESTful APIs?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Network Repository Function (NRF)** is the central registry and discovery service in 5G SBA (1 mark). **REST-based Operations (2 marks):** - **NF Registration:** NFs register their service profiles via `PUT /nf-instances/{nfInstanceId}` with a JSON body containing NF type, FQDN, PLMN ID, and supported service names. - **NF Discovery:** Consumer NFs send `GET /nf-instances?nf-type=AMF&service-name=namf-comm` to obtain a list of available producer instances. - **NF Status Subscription:** NFs subscribe to status notifications using `POST /nf-instances/{nfInstanceId}/subscriptions` and receive asynchronous `204 No Content` responses. NRF enables **dynamic, scalable service discovery** without static configuration — a key advantage over 4G's reference-point architecture. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice --- ## **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024] ### Part (a) [7 marks] **With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of RESTful API-based 5G Service-Based Interface (SBI). Discuss how it differs from the traditional 4G reference point architecture.** **Model Solution:** **Introduction (1 mark):** The 5G Service-Based Architecture (SBA), defined in 3GPP TS 23.501, replaces 4G's point-to-point reference points with a **service-oriented** model where every Network Function (NF) exposes its functionality as a **service** accessible via RESTful APIs over the SBI bus. **Architecture Diagram (3 marks):** ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────┐ │ 5G Service-Based Architecture │ └─────────────────────────────────────┘ │ ┌──────────────┬───────────────┼───────────────┬──────────────┐ │ │ │ │ │ AMF SMF UDM AUSF NRF (Mobility) (Session) (Data Mgmt) (Auth) (Registry) │ │ │ │ │ └──────────────┴───────┬───────┴───────────────┴──────────────┘ │ ═══════════╧═══════════ ║ SBI Bus (HTTP/2) ║ ══════════════════════ ``` All NFs communicate through the **SBI bus** using HTTP/2, JSON, and TLS 1.3. **Key Architectural Elements (1.5 marks):** - **Service Consumer:** The NF that initiates the API call (e.g., AMF). - **Service Producer:** The NF that exposes the service (e.g., UDM). - **NRF (Network Repository Function):** Enables dynamic NF discovery. - **NEF (Network Exposure Function):** Exposes 5G capabilities to external networks via RESTful APIs. - **HTTP/2 + JSON:** Mandatory protocol and content type (3GPP TS 29.500). **Differences from 4G (1.5 marks):** | Aspect | 4G EPC | 5G SBA | |--------|--------|--------| | Coupling | Tight (point-to-point) | Loose (service-oriented) | | Protocol | Diameter, GTP-U, SCTP | HTTP/2, JSON, TLS | | Discovery | Static configuration | Dynamic NRF-based | | Scalability | Limited | Horizontal cloud-native | | Modifiability | Vendor-locked | Open, modular | **Conclusion:** The 5G SBA enables **network slicing, edge computing, and cloud-native deployments** that are impossible in 4G EPC. --- ### Part (b) [7 marks] **Describe the various HTTP methods used in RESTful APIs with examples from 5G SBA. Explain the concept of idempotency with suitable examples.** **Model Solution:** **HTTP Methods (4 marks):** 1. **GET — Read (Idempotent & Safe)** - Used to retrieve a resource representation. - Example: `GET /nudm-sdm/v1/imsi-{imsi}/am-data` retrieves Access & Mobility subscription data. - Response: `200 OK` with JSON body. 2. **POST — Create (Not Idempotent)** - Used to create a new resource; server assigns URI. - Example: `POST /nausf-auth/v1/ue-authentications` initiates UE authentication. - Response: `201 Created` with `Location` header. 3. **PUT — Replace (Idempotent)** - Replaces the entire resource at the given URI. - Example: `PUT /nudm-sdm/v1/sdm-subscriptions/{id}` updates an existing subscription. - Response: `200 OK`. 4. **PATCH — Partial Update (Not Idempotent)** - Modifies only specified fields. - Example: `PATCH /nudm-uecm/v1/registrations/{id}` changes a subset of registration data. 5. **DELETE — Remove (Idempotent)** - Removes the resource. - Example: `DELETE /nudm-sdm/v1/sdm-subscriptions/{id}`. - Response: `204 No Content`. **Idempotency Concept (2 marks):** An operation is **idempotent** if performing it multiple times produces the **same server state** as performing it once. - **Idempotent example:** `PUT /resource/123` with body `{state: "active"}` — calling it 1 time or 100 times leaves the resource in `state: "active"`. - **Non-idempotent example:** `POST /orders` with body `{item: "phone"}` — each call creates a NEW order with a new ID. **Importance in 5G (1 mark):** Idempotency is **critical for retry logic** — if a network request times out, the sender can safely retry `PUT` or `DELETE` without causing duplicate side effects, ensuring **eventual consistency** in distributed 5G cores. --- ## **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice ### Part (a) [7 marks] **Explain the six architectural constraints of REST. Why is statelessness the most important constraint in 5G SBA?** **Model Solution:** **The Six REST Constraints (4 marks):** 1. **Client–Server:** Separates UI (client) from data storage (server). Improves portability of client code and scalability of server. 2. **Statelessness:** Each request from client must contain ALL information needed to process it. Server stores NO client context between requests. 3. **Cacheability:** Responses must explicitly mark themselves as cacheable or non-cacheable using HTTP headers (`Cache-Control`, `ETag`). 4. **Uniform Interface:** Standardized resource identification (URIs), manipulation (HTTP methods), self-descriptive messages, and HATEOAS. 5. **Layered System:** Architecture composed of hierarchical layers; client cannot tell whether it's talking to the end server or an intermediary (e.g., load balancer, proxy). 6. **Code on Demand (Optional):** Server can temporarily extend client by sending executable code (e.g., JavaScript). Not used in 5G SBA. **Why Statelessness is Most Important in 5G SBA (3 marks):** 1. **Horizontal Scalability:** Stateless servers can be replicated freely. If AMF-1 crashes, AMF-2 can take over instantly with no session migration. 2. **Load Balancing:** Any NRF-registered NF instance can serve any request — enables round-robin or geographic load distribution. 3. **Resilience & Fault Tolerance:** No server-side state means no synchronization overhead during failovers — critical for 5G's 99.999% availability requirement. 4. **Cloud-Native Compatibility:** Stateless services map directly to **Kubernetes pods** that can be created, destroyed, or rescheduled without state transfer. 5. **Simplified Caching:** Each request is self-contained, enabling CDN/edge caching of frequently accessed resources (e.g., slice templates, policy data). **Drawback:** Every request may carry redundant information (e.g., subscriber ID, PLMN ID), increasing header overhead by ~10–15% — acceptable trade-off for scalability. --- ### Part (b) [7 marks] **Compare JSON and XML as data formats in RESTful APIs. Why does 5G SBA mandate JSON over HTTP/2?** **Model Solution:** **JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) (2.5 marks):** - **Lightweight:** Minimal syntax (no closing tags). - **Native to JavaScript/Node.js** — primary 5G NF development language. - **Example:** ```json { "supi": "imsi-123456789012345", "servingPlmn": {"mcc": "404", "mnc": "74"}, "status": "REGISTERED" } ``` **XML (eXtensible Markup Language) (2.5 marks):** - **Verbose:** Requires opening and closing tags. - **Schema validation** via XSD — strong typing. - **Example:** ```xml imsi-123456789012345 404 74 REGISTERED ``` **Comparison Table (1 mark):** | Parameter | JSON | XML | |-----------|------|-----| | Verbosity | Low | High (~2–3× larger) | | Parsing Speed | Faster | Slower | | Native Type Support | Yes (string, number, bool) | No (all strings) | | Schema Validation | JSON Schema | XSD (robust) | | Namespace Support | No | Yes | | Tooling in Telecom | Growing | Legacy (SOAP) | **Why 5G Mandates JSON over HTTP/2 (1 mark):** 1. **Smaller payload** reduces SBI bus bandwidth consumption (critical in high-density IoT scenarios). 2. **Faster serialization/deserialization** → lower latency for control-plane signaling. 3. **3GPP standardization (TS 29.501)** explicitly defines OpenAPI 3.0 specifications using JSON Schema. 4. **HTTP/2's binary framing** complements JSON's text-based payload for efficient parsing. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse "stateless" with "no state at all."** Stateless means the *server* does not retain client session between requests — not that the system is state-free. Databases still exist. > 2. **HTTP Method Idempotency:** Students often incorrectly mark POST as idempotent because they confuse it with idempotency of mathematical operations. **POST is NEVER idempotent** in REST. > 3. **Do not state that 5G uses HTTP/1.1** — it is strictly **HTTP/2.0** (or HTTP/3 in 5G-Advanced). This is a common 1-mark loss. > 4. **NRF is NOT a database** in the traditional sense — it is a service registry with built-in caching, subscription, and notification mechanisms. Mentioning all three functions fetches full marks. > 5. **URI vs URL confusion:** URL is a *type* of URI that includes both resource name and access method. Use "URI" in answers for technical accuracy. > 6. **Status code precision:** `200` ≠ `201`. `201 Created` is *specifically* used for POST; `200 OK` for GET/PUT. Examiners explicitly check this distinction. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — RESTful API for Service-Based Interface** - ✅ **REST** = Representational State Transfer; defined by **Roy Fielding (2000)**. - ✅ **5G SBA** is built on **HTTP/2.0 + JSON + TLS 1.2/1.3** (3GPP TS 29.500). - ✅ **Six REST constraints:** Client-Server, Stateless, Cacheable, Uniform Interface, Layered System, Code-on-Demand (optional). - ✅ **HTTP Methods:** `GET` (read, idempotent), `POST` (create, NOT idempotent), `PUT` (replace, idempotent), `PATCH` (partial update), `DELETE` (remove, idempotent). - ✅ **Idempotent methods:** GET, PUT, DELETE, HEAD, OPTIONS, TRACE. - ✅ **Status codes:** `200 OK` (GET/PUT success), `201 Created` (POST success), `204 No Content` (DELETE success), `4xx` (client error), `5xx` (server error). - ✅ **NRF (Network Repository Function)** performs: NF Registration, NF Discovery, NF Status Subscription. - ✅ **SBI Bus** is a logical communication bus where all NFs interact via RESTful APIs. - ✅ **Statelessness is the cornerstone** of 5G cloud-native scalability and Kubernetes compatibility. - ✅ **JSON over XML** because of payload size, parsing speed, and 3GPP mandate. - ✅ **Service Consumer vs Service Producer:** Consumer sends request; Producer exposes service. - ✅ **CRUD Mapping:** Create=POST, Read=GET, Update=PUT/PATCH, Delete=DELETE. - ✅ **5G replaces 4G reference points** (S1, X2, S11) with **service-based interfaces** (Nausf, Namf, Nudm, Nsmf, Nnrf). - ✅ **Cache hit ratio formula:** $R = N_{hits} / (N_{hits} + N_{misses})$. - ✅ **HTTP/2 supports multiplexing** — multiple streams over a single TCP connection, reducing latency. - ✅ **Idempotency in retries** is essential for distributed 5G cores to avoid duplicate side effects. > **Pro Tip for KTU Exam:** Always draw the **SBI bus diagram** with at least 3 NFs (AMF, SMF, UDM) and the NRF in your answers — this single diagram can fetch 3–4 marks in any RESTful API question. ---

topics.title: Private Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT741 - ADVANCED MOBILE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Private Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (3GPP-Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Private Networks (Non-Public Networks – NPNs):** A 5G System (5GS) that is intended for non-public use, providing 5G network services to a clearly defined user organisation or group of organisations, restricted from general public access. As per **3GPP TS 23.501**, a Non-Public Network is a network deployed for non-public purposes, operating either as a Standalone Non-Public Network (SNPN) or as a Public Network Integrated NPN (PNI-NPN).

In the **KTU 2024 syllabus** context, Private Networks are studied under 5G & Beyond as a key enabler of **vertical industry digitisation**, where enterprises (factories, hospitals, ports, mines) require dedicated, secure, and deterministic connectivity that public mobile networks cannot economically guarantee.

### 1.2 Intuitive Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Corporate Intranet vs Public Internet"**
> Think of a Private Network as a **company's dedicated corporate Wi-Fi**, but at a city-wide, industrial-grade, cellular scale. Imagine a large automobile factory: management does not want its robotic assembly line, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles), and inspection cameras competing for bandwidth on the same tower that serves TikTok users in the neighbourhood. A Private Network gives the factory its **own cellular base station (gNB), its own core (5GC), and its own data pipeline**, fully isolated from public subscribers, much like a company's internal LAN is isolated from the public internet, but with carrier-grade mobility, handovers, and QoS guarantees.

### 1.3 Why Private Networks? — Driving Forces

| Driving Force | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|
| **Data Sovereignty & Privacy** | Sensitive operational data (factory IP, patient records) must never traverse a public operator's core. |
| **Deterministic Latency** | Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication (URLLC) requires dedicated, contention-free radio resources. |
| **Coverage Customisation** | Indoor, underground, or harsh industrial RF environments need bespoke cell planning. |
| **Device Density** | Massive Machine-Type Communications (mMTC) – e.g., **1 million sensors/km²** in a smart warehouse. |
| **Spectrum Exclusivity** | Mid-band (3.5 GHz) or mmWave (26/28 GHz) locally licensed for interference-free operation. |
| **Operational Independence** | Local survivability even if the public network is down (disaster recovery). |

### 1.4 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) for Private 5G

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Target KPIs (3GPP / 5G-ACIA):**
> - **User-Plane Latency:** **< 1 ms (URLLC)**, typically **4 ms (eMBB)**
> - **Reliability:** **99.999%** (five-nines) for mission-critical traffic
> - **Device Density:** Up to **1,000,000 devices/km²**
> - **Peak Data Rate:** **20 Gbps DL / 10 Gbps UL** (theoretical)
> - **Mobility:** Up to **500 km/h** (high-speed train scenarios)
> - **Availability:** **99.9% – 99.999%** depending on vertical

### 1.5 Classification of Private Networks (Top-Level)

```
Private Networks (NPNs)
├── 1. Standalone Non-Public Network (SNPN)
│       ├── Operates independently of PLMN
│       ├── Uses NID (Network Identifier) + PLMN ID
│       └── Identified by CAG (Closed Access Group)
└── 2. Public Network Integrated NPN (PNI-NPN)
        ├── Uses public 5G core + dedicated resources
        ├── Configured with AC (Access Control) lists
        └── Supports network slicing
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Architectural Flavours — SNPN vs PNI-NPN

### 2.1.1 Standalone Non-Public Network (SNPN)

An **SNPN** is a fully self-contained 5G network that does **not depend on a Public Land Mobile Network (PLMN)** for its operation. It has its own identifiers, its own authentication infrastructure, and its own network functions.

**Identity Stack:**
- **PLMN ID** (optional, used when SNPN is also a public operator's subsidiary) — format: **MCC + MNC** (Mobile Country Code + Mobile Network Code), each **3 digits**.
- **NID (Network Identifier):** A unique identifier for the SNPN, defined in **3GPP TS 23.501** §5.30.2.3. Length: **32 bits** (mandatory) or **64 bits** (extended). Encoded as a sequence of human-readable labels (e.g., `ACME-FACTORY-NID`).
- **CAG (Closed Access Group):** A list of CAG-IDs broadcast in **SIB (System Information Block)** to restrict access. Format: CAG-ID is up to **32 bits** (4 octets).

**Authentication Mechanism:**
- Uses **AKA (Authentication and Key Agreement)** with credentials provisioned locally in the UE's USIM.
- Supports **5G-AKA** or **EAP-AKA'** via the home network's **AUSF/UDM** in the SNPN's own 5GC.
- **SUPI (Subscription Permanent Identifier)** is bound to a specific SNPN's UDM.

### 2.1.2 Public Network Integrated NPN (PNI-NPN)

A **PNI-NPN** leverages a public operator's 5G core but carves out dedicated resources for a private customer. It is essentially a **sliced public network** with access control.

**Identity Stack:**
- Standard **SUPI / SUCI** (Subscription Concealed Identifier).
- **Access Control (AC):** List of allowed UEs (by SUPI, PEI, or GPSI) configured in the **UDM/UDR** and enforced by the **AMF**.
- **DNN (Data Network Name):** A unique APN/DNN for the enterprise traffic plane.
- **Network Slice:** A dedicated NSSAI (Network Slice Selection Assistance Information) with SST (Slice/Service Type) and optional SD (Slice Differentiator).

> [!NOTE]
> **SST (8 bits) + SD (24 bits) = NSSAI (32 bits total)**
> - SST values: `1` (eMBB), `2` (URLLC), `3` (mMTC), `4` (V2X).
> - SD is operator-defined for fine-grained differentiation.

## 2.2 3GPP Reference Architecture for SNPN

The 5GC architecture defined in **TS 23.501 §5.30** for SNPN is essentially the same SBA (Service-Based Architecture) used in public 5G, with these key adaptations:

```
SNPN Core (5GC) Components:
├── AMF (Access and Mobility Management Function)
├── SMF (Session Management Function)
├── UPF (User Plane Function)
├── AUSF (Authentication Server Function)
├── UDM (Unified Data Management)
├── UDR (Unified Data Repository)
├── NRF (NF Repository Function)
├── NEF (Network Exposure Function)
└── PCF (Policy Control Function)
```

All NF (Network Function) names follow the public 5GC nomenclature; the **difference lies in the identifiers** (NID, CAG) and the **deployment perimeter**.

## 2.3 Spectrum Options for Private Networks

| Spectrum Tier | Frequency Examples | Licensing Model | Typical Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Licensed (Dedicated)** | 3.4–3.8 GHz, 26/28 GHz | Regulator auction (e.g., Germany's 3.7–3.8 GHz for industry) | Large factories, ports |
| **Shared (Tiered)** | CBRS (3.55–3.7 GHz) — 3-tier SAS model | Dynamic sharing via SAS (Spectrum Access System) | USA enterprises |
| **Unlicensed** | 5 GHz, 6 GHz (NR-U) | License-exempt | Local indoor, campus |
| **Local Licensed** | n77, n78 sub-bands | Geographic-area license | Country-specific (India, UK, Germany) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CBRS (Citizens Broadband Radio Service) Three-Tier Model:**
> 1. **Tier 1 — Incumbent Access** (federal/satellite): highest priority.
> 2. **Tier 2 — Priority Access Licenses (PAL):** auctioned, **70 MHz** per census tract.
> 3. **Tier 3 — General Authorized Access (GAA):** opportunistic, free, dynamically allocated by **SAS**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| NID length (mandatory) | $N_{NID} = 32$ | bits | SNPN identity (3GPP TS 23.501) |
| NID length (extended) | $N_{NID,ext} = 64$ | bits | Optional, larger enterprise scale |
| CAG-ID max | $N_{CAG} \le 32$ | bits | Closed Access Group identifier |
| NSSAI | $N_{NSSAI} = N_{SST} + N_{SD} = 8 + 24 = 32$ | bits | Network slice selection |
| SUPI length (IMSI) | $L_{SUPI} \le 15$ | digits | MCC(3) + MNC(2-3) + MSIN |
| Slice SST values | $SST \in \{1,2,3,4,...128\}$ | enum | Standardised slice types |
| URLLC latency target | $T_{URLLC} \le 1$ | ms | One-way user-plane |
| eMBB peak rate (DL) | $R_{DL} = 20$ | Gbps | Theoretical 5G peak |
| mMTC density | $\rho_{mMTC} = 10^6$ | devices/km² | Massive IoT |
| Reliability (URLLC) | $P_{success} = 1 - 10^{-5} = 99.999\%$ | probability | Five-nines |
| PLMN ID | $PLMN = MCC \Vert MNC$ | digits | Mobile network ID |
| Cell capacity (sector) | $C_{sector} = BW \times SE \times MIMO_{gain}$ | bps | $BW$ bandwidth, $SE$ spectral efficiency |
| Shannon capacity (theoretical) | $C = BW \times \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$ | bps/channel | Upper bound |
| Number of CAG cells per gNB | $N_{CAG\_cells} \le 12$ | cells | Per 3GPP TS 38.331 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Notation Rule:** Throughout this document, the absolute value of $x$ is written as $\vert x \vert$ (using the `\vert` LaTeX command) to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Do not use the raw pipe symbol `|x|` inside table rows.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
