> **Industry Verticals Benefiting from Private 5G (per 5G-ACIA):**
> 1. **Smart Manufacturing** — AGVs, robotic arms, predictive maintenance.
> 2. **Healthcare** — Connected ambulances, robotic surgery, MRI image transfer.
> 3. **Logistics & Ports** — Crane automation, container tracking.
> 4. **Energy & Utilities** — Smart grid, mining, oil & gas.
> 5. **Education / Campuses** — Private CBRS networks (e.g., US universities).
> 6. **Defense & Public Safety** — Tactical bubbles, mission-critical push-to-talk (MCPTT).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — SNPN vs PNI-NPN Decision

| Decision Criterion | SNPN (Standalone) | PNI-NPN (Public Integrated) |
|---|---|---|
| **Identifier** | NID + CAG | PLMN ID + AC list + Slice |
| **Dependency on PLMN** | **None** | **Tight** (uses public 5GC) |
| **Authentication Home** | Own AUSF/UDM | Public operator's UDM |
| **Spectrum** | Local licensed (own) | Public operator's licensed band |
| **Coverage Ownership** | Enterprise | MNO (Mobile Network Operator) |
| **Data Sovereignty** | **Full** (on-prem core) | Conditional (depends on slice config) |
| **Mobility to Public 5G** | Not seamless | **Seamless** (same PLMN) |
| **Roaming Support** | Limited (per agreement) | Native (via PLMN) |
| **CAPEX** | **High** (full core stack) | **Lower** (shared core) |
| **OPEX** | Lower long-term | Pay-as-you-go to MNO |
| **Time-to-Market** | **6–12 months** | **2–4 weeks** |
| **Typical Deployer** | Large enterprise, defense, industrial | SME, campus, retail |

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule of Thumb:**
> - If **data never leaves the premises** and **mobility to public is not needed** → choose **SNPN**.
> - If **seamless inter-site mobility** and **lower CAPEX** matter → choose **PNI-NPN**.

## 3.2 Derivation — Maximum Number of Devices per CAG Cell

The 3GPP spec **TS 38.331** defines that a single gNB can broadcast up to **12 CAG cell identities**. This is derived from the CAG-Identity-List information element, which contains up to **12 entries** in a single SIB.

$$
N_{CAG\_per\_gNB} = 12
$$

**Reasoning:** The SIB message size must fit within the **maximum SIB payload of 2976 bits** (for SIB1 in TS 38.331), and each CAG-ID entry is **32 bits** plus overhead, giving approximately:

$$
N_{entries} = \left\lfloor \frac{2976 - \text{overhead}}{32 + \text{overhead}_{per\_entry}} \right\rfloor \approx 12
$$

## 3.3 Derivation — Total Number of Identifiable SNPNs

Given that the **mandatory NID is 32 bits**, the theoretical maximum number of unique SNPN identifiers is:

$$
N_{max\_NID} = 2^{32}
$$

$$
N_{max\_NID} = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \approx 4.29 \times 10^9 \text{ networks}
$$

**Step-by-step expansion:**

$$
2^{10} = 1024 \approx 10^3
$$

$$
2^{32} = 2^{2} \times 2^{30} = 4 \times (2^{10})^3 \approx 4 \times (10^3)^3 = 4 \times 10^9
$$

$$
\therefore \; N_{max\_NID} = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ unique NIDs}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **theoretical** upper bound. The **extended NID (64 bits)** raises this to $2^{64} \approx 1.8 \times 10^{19}$, which is effectively unlimited. Practically, regulators allocate NIDs from a smaller space using **human-readable labels** (e.g., `corp1.factory.example.com`).

## 3.4 Derivation — Shannon Capacity for a Private 5G Cell

For a private gNB with bandwidth $BW$ and average Signal-to-Noise Ratio $SNR$, the theoretical Shannon capacity is:

$$
C = BW \times \log_2(1 + SNR)
$$

**Step 1 — Identify variables:**
- $BW$ = channel bandwidth in Hz.
- $SNR$ = linear (not dB) signal-to-noise ratio.

**Step 2 — Convert SNR from dB to linear:**

$$
SNR_{linear} = 10^{\frac{SNR_{dB}}{10}}
$$

**Step 3 — Plug into Shannon:**

$$
C = BW \times \log_2\!\left(1 + 10^{\frac{SNR_{dB}}{10}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Example calculation:**
Let $BW = 100$ MHz, $SNR_{dB} = 20$ dB:

$$
SNR_{linear} = 10^{20/10} = 10^2 = 100
$$

$$
C = 100 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1 + 100)
$$

$$
C = 10^8 \times \log_2(101)
$$

$$
\log_2(101) = \frac{\ln(101)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{4.615}{0.693} \approx 6.658
$$

$$
\therefore \; C \approx 6.658 \times 10^8 \text{ bps} = 665.8 \text{ Mbps}
$$

## 3.5 Throughput with MIMO Gain

For a private 5G cell with $N_t \times N_r$ MIMO configuration, the effective throughput is:

$$
R_{effective} = C \times N_{streams} \times \eta_{coding}
$$

Where:
- $N_{streams} = \min(N_t, N_r)$ for spatial multiplexing.
- $\eta_{coding}$ = coding efficiency (e.g., 0.9 for LDPC at high SNR).

**Example:** $4 \times 4$ MIMO, $\eta_{coding} = 0.9$:

$$
R_{effective} = 665.8 \text{ Mbps} \times 4 \times 0.9 = 2396.88 \text{ Mbps} \approx 2.4 \text{ Gbps}
$$

## 3.6 Closed Access Group (CAG) Access Control Procedure

The flow when a UE attempts to access a CAG-restricted cell is:

1. UE reads **SIB1** and extracts the **CAG-Identity-List**.
2. UE compares the broadcast CAG-IDs against its **Allowed CAG list** stored in the USIM.
3. If **match found** → UE proceeds with RACH and registration.
4. If **no match** → UE barred from camping; may search for an alternative cell.
5. The **AMF** further validates via **Subscription Access Control** at the **UDM** (SUPI-level check).

> [!NOTE]
> The CAG mechanism was introduced in **3GPP Release 16** (the same release that solidified NPN architecture in TS 23.501).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Private Network Architectural Landscape (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph UE["UE Device"]
        U1["Smartphone / Sensor / AGV"]
    end

    subgraph RAN["Radio Access Network"]
        G1["gNB - Private 5G Base Station"]
        G2["Small Cell - Indoor Picocell"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["5G Core SNPN"]
        A1["AMF - Access Management"]
        A2["SMF - Session Management"]
        A3["UPF - User Plane"]
        A4["AUSF - Authentication"]
        A5["UDM - User Data"]
        A6["NRF - Repository"]
    end

    subgraph EDGE["Enterprise Edge / Data Network"]
        E1["Factory OT Network"]
        E2["Hospital HIS PACS"]
        E3["Edge Cloud MEC"]
    end

    subgraph SPEC["Spectrum Options"]
        S1["Licensed 3.5 GHz"]
        S2["CBRS 3.55 GHz"]
        S3["mmWave 26/28 GHz"]
    end

    U1 -->|Uu Interface| G1
    U1 -->|Uu Interface| G2
    G1 -->|N2 N1 N3| A1
    G1 -->|N2 N1 N3| A1
    A1 -->|N11 N15| A4
    A4 -->|N13| A5
    A1 -->|N7| A2
    A2 -->|N4| A3
    A3 -->|N6 Local Breakout| E1
    A3 -->|N6| E2
    A3 -->|N6| E3
    A1 -.->|N18| A6
    S1 -.-> G1
    S2 -.-> G1
    S3 -.-> G2
```

## 4.2 SNPN Identity Stack (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph IDENT["SNPN Identity Stack"]
        D1["UE Identity: SUPI / SUCI"]
        D2["Network Identity: NID 32 bits"]
        D3["Optional PLMN ID: MCC + MNC"]
        D4["Access Control: CAG Identity 32 bits"]
        D5["Slice: NSSAI SST + SD"]
    end

    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
    D4 --> D5
```

## 4.3 SNPN vs PNI-NPN Comparison Flow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q{"Enterprise Needs Data Sovereignty and Local Survivability"}
    Q -->|Yes| S["Choose SNPN - Standalone NPN"]
    Q -->|No| Q2{"Need Seamless Mobility with Public 5G"}
    Q2 -->|Yes| P["Choose PNI-NPN - Sliced Public Network"]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{"Limited CAPEX Budget and Quick Deployment"}
    Q3 -->|Yes| P
    Q3 -->|No| S

    S --> S1["Own gNB + 5GC On-Premise"]
    S --> S2["CAG based Access Control"]
    S --> S3["Independent Spectrum and Operation"]

    P --> P1["Shared Public gNB + 5GC"]
    P --> P2["Dedicated NSSAI Slice"]
    P --> P3["Subscription Access Control List"]
```

## 4.4 Private 5G Deployment Lifecycle (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1: Spectrum Acquisition"] --> B["Step 2: Core Deployment On-Premise or Cloud"]
    B --> C["Step 3: gNB and Small Cell Installation"]
    C --> D["Step 4: USIM and CAG Configuration"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Integration with OT IT Systems"]
    E --> F["Step 6: SLAs and Monitoring"]
    F --> G["Step 7: Lifecycle Management and Updates"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Non-Public Network (NPN) as per 3GPP TS 23.501. List its two deployment modes.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> A **Non-Public Network (NPN)** is a 5G System (5GS) intended for non-public use, deployed to serve a defined organisation or group of organisations with restricted access. Per 3GPP TS 23.501, the two deployment modes are:
>
> 1. **Standalone Non-Public Network (SNPN)** — operates independently of a PLMN with its own NID and CAG.
> 2. **Public Network Integrated NPN (PNI-NPN)** — uses a public MNO's 5G core with dedicated slices and access control.

### **Q2. What is the role of the Closed Access Group (CAG) in a 5G Private Network?** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **CAG (Closed Access Group)** restricts access to a private 5G cell by broadcasting a list of permitted CAG-IDs in the **SIB1** message. A UE can only camp on a CAG cell if its USIM contains a CAG-ID matching the broadcast list. CAG is the primary access control mechanism for **SNPN** deployments, providing radio-level isolation without requiring a full PLMN subscription.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — SNPN Architecture Deep-Dive** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the architecture of a Standalone Non-Public Network (SNPN) with reference to 3GPP TS 23.501. Discuss the role of NID, CAG, and the local 5G Core functions. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **SNPN architecture** is defined in **3GPP TS 23.501 §5.30**. It reuses the **Service-Based Architecture (SBA)** of the public 5G Core but with these defining attributes:

1. **Identity Elements:**
   - **NID (Network Identifier):** A 32-bit (or optional 64-bit) identifier unique to the SNPN, broadcast alongside the PLMN ID.
   - **CAG (Closed Access Group):** A 32-bit ID list in SIB1 to restrict radio access to authorized UEs.
   - **SUPI:** Standard IMSI-like identifier tied to the SNPN's local UDM.

2. **Core Network Functions (5GC SBA):**
   - **AMF** — handles registration, connection, and mobility.
   - **SMF** — manages PDU sessions and UPF selection.
   - **UPF** — local data plane, often with **local breakout** to enterprise OT networks.
   - **AUSF / UDM** — local authentication and subscription data.
   - **NRF, NEF, PCF** — discovery, exposure, and policy.

3. **Isolation:** Because the SNPN has its own NID, CAG, and optionally a dedicated PLMN ID, it is **completely isolated** from public subscribers and traffic.

4. **Deployment:** All 5GC NFs can run on **on-premise servers, edge cloud, or MEC (Multi-access Edge Computing) platforms** inside the enterprise premises.

**(b) Compare SNPN with PNI-NPN across 10 key parameters. Justify which is preferable for a smart factory that handles classified IP. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | SNPN | PNI-NPN |
|---|---|---|
| Identifier | NID + CAG | PLMN + NSSAI |
| Core | Local 5GC | Public MNO 5GC |
| Spectrum | Own licensed | MNO licensed |
| Authentication | Local AUSF/UDM | MNO UDM |
| Data Sovereignty | **Full** | Partial (slice config) |
| CAPEX | **High** | **Low** |
| OPEX | Low long-term | Pay-as-you-go |
| Mobility to Public | No | **Yes** |
| Time-to-Market | 6–12 months | 2–4 weeks |
| Reliability | Higher (on-prem survivable) | Dependent on MNO |

**Justification for Smart Factory with Classified IP:**

> An SNPN is the **correct choice** because:
> 1. **Data Sovereignty:** All proprietary designs, CAD files, and process data must remain on-premise; the UPF is locally hosted with N6 breakout to the OT network.
> 2. **No MNO Trust Boundary:** The factory does not need to trust a third-party operator with its intellectual property.
> 3. **Deterministic Performance:** Dedicated spectrum and isolated core eliminate contention from public users.
> 4. **Survivability:** The SNPN can continue operating even during public network outages.
> 5. **Compliance:** Easier to meet regulatory frameworks (e.g., ITAR, GDPR) for sensitive industrial data.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Spectrum, Slicing & Use Cases** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a) Describe the three spectrum options available for private 5G networks. Explain the CBRS three-tier sharing model with its relevance to enterprise deployments. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The three spectrum options for private 5G are:

1. **Licensed (Dedicated) Spectrum:** Auctioned by regulators to enterprises (e.g., **Germany's 3.7–3.8 GHz** for industry, **UK's 3.8–4.2 GHz local licenses**, **India's 5G n78** enterprise captive use). Provides **interference-free, exclusive** access with predictable QoS.

2. **Shared Spectrum — CBRS Model (US 3.55 GHz):** Managed by a **SAS (Spectrum Access System)** with three tiers:
   - **Tier 1 — Incumbent Access:** Federal users, satellite earth stations (highest priority, protected from all others).
   - **Tier 2 — Priority Access License (PAL):** Auctioned licenses giving priority over GAA in a census tract; **70 MHz** per PAL.
   - **Tier 3 — General Authorized Access (GAA):** Free, opportunistic access using whatever spectrum is available after higher tiers.
   - **Relevance:** CBRS enables enterprises to deploy private LTE/5G in the US without the cost of a full spectrum auction, accelerating Industry 4.0 adoption.

3. **Unlicensed Spectrum — NR-U (5G New Radio – Unlicensed):** Uses 5 GHz and 6 GHz bands. Requires **Listen-Before-Talk (LBT)** and coexistence mechanisms with Wi-Fi. Suitable for indoor, small-footprint deployments (e.g., retail, warehouses).

**(b) Explain how network slicing enables PNI-NPN. List four key vertical use cases and the slice types (SST) used in each. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Network Slicing in PNI-NPN:**

A **network slice** is a logical, end-to-end network instance with guaranteed resources, isolated from other slices on the same physical infrastructure. In a **PNI-NPN**, the MNO allocates a dedicated NSSAI to the enterprise:

- **NSSAI** = SST (8 bits) + SD (24 bits).
- The **NSSF** in the 5GC maps the requested NSSAI to a slice instance comprising dedicated AMF/SMF/UPF resources.
- The **URSP (UE Route Selection Policy)** in the UE's USIM allows traffic to be steered to the correct slice.

**Four Vertical Use Cases with Slice Types:**

| Vertical | Use Case | SST Value | Slice Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Smart Factory** | AGV control, robotic arms | `2` | URLLC (low latency, high reliability) |
| **Smart Port** | Crane automation, video surveillance | `1` | eMBB (high throughput for HD video) |
| **Smart Metering** | Utility meter reporting | `3` | mMTC (massive device density) |
| **V2X / Logistics** | Autonomous truck platooning | `4` | V2X (low latency, high mobility) |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing SNPN with Private LTE:** SNPN is a 5G concept; do not equate it with older private LTE models (CBRS-LTE, MulteFire). Mentioning 3GPP Release 16 is critical.
> 2. **Mixing CAG and NSSAI:** CAG is for **access control** in SNPN; NSSAI is for **slice selection** in PNI-NPN. Examiners will deduct marks for confusing these.
> 3. **Forgetting On-Premise Local Breakout:** In SNPN, the UPF is typically on-premise with **N6 local breakout** to the enterprise OT network — this is the key data sovereignty enabler.
> 4. **Ignoring SUPI Length:** SUPI is ≤ 15 digits (MCC + MNC + MSIN). Writing 16+ digits is wrong.
> 5. **Not mentioning 3GPP Release number:** SNPN/NPN were solidified in **3GPP Release 16**; missing this is a frequent penalty point.
> 6. **Slice SST values:** Memorize SST 1 (eMBB), 2 (URLLC), 3 (mMTC), 4 (V2X). Wrong SST = direct loss of marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Private Networks:**
>
> - **Definition:** NPN = 5GS for non-public use (3GPP TS 23.501, Release 16).
> - **Two Modes:** SNPN (standalone, NID + CAG) and PNI-NPN (sliced public, NSSAI + AC list).
> - **NID:** 32-bit (mandatory) or 64-bit (extended) unique SNPN identifier.
> - **CAG:** Up to 12 CAG-IDs per gNB in SIB1, each 32 bits.
> - **SUPI:** ≤ 15 digits; SUCI is the encrypted SUPI.
> - **NSSAI:** 32 bits = SST (8) + SD (24). SST ∈ {1:eMBB, 2:URLLC, 3:mMTC, 4:V2X}.
> - **CBRS Tiers:** Incumbent (T1) > PAL (T2, 70 MHz) > GAA (T3).
> - **Spectrum Options:** Licensed, Shared (CBRS), Unlicensed (NR-U).
> - **KPIs:** Latency < 1 ms (URLLC), 1M devices/km² (mMTC), 99.999% reliability.
> - **Shannon Capacity:** $C = BW \times \log_2(1 + SNR)$ — upper bound, not achievable.
> - **5GC Functions in SNPN:** AMF, SMF, UPF, AUSF, UDM, NRF, NEF, PCF (SBA).
> - **N6 Local Breakout:** UPF on-premise to enterprise OT network = data sovereignty.
> - **Vertical Use Cases:** Smart factory (URLLC), ports (eMBB), metering (mMTC), V2X.
> - **MNO vs Enterprise Deployment:** SNPN for high security; PNI-NPN for low CAPEX and mobility.
> - **Standard:** 3GPP TS 23.501 §5.30 governs NPN architecture.
> - **Memorize the acronyms:** NID, CAG, NSSAI, SST, SD, SUPI, SUCI, AUSF, UDM, UPF, SBA, NRF, NEF, PCF, NSSF, URSP, NR-U, CBRS, SAS, PAL, GAA, PLMN, NPN, SNPN, PNI-NPN, OT, IT, MEC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LMLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT741 - ADVANCED MOBILE COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** LMLC # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Land Mobile Large-scale Channel (LMLC)** refers to the statistical and deterministic characterization of the wireless radio propagation channel between a fixed base station (BS) and a mobile station (MS) over **distances on the order of the cell radius** (typically tens to thousands of meters). LMLC describes how the received signal power varies as a function of distance and the large terrain features (buildings, hills, foliage) surrounding the mobile. The LMLC model is fundamentally built upon two major physical phenomena: 1. **Path Loss (PL)** — The deterministic, distance-dependent attenuation of signal power due to wave propagation through free space or obstructed terrain. Mathematically represented as: $$PL(d) = P_t - P_r(d) \quad [\text{dB}]$$ where $P_t$ is the transmitted power and $P_r(d)$ is the received power at distance $d$. 2. **Shadow Fading (Slow Fading, Large-Scale Fading)** — The random attenuation caused by large obstacles (buildings, trees, hills) obstructing the line-of-sight (LOS) path. This is statistically modeled as a **log-normal** random variable. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** LMLC is essential for **cell planning, link budget calculation, frequency reuse design, and coverage prediction** in cellular networks (2G GSM → 5G NR). It does NOT describe fast multipath fading (which is the small-scale / Rayleigh / Ricean component covered in Module 3). --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are standing on top of a hill and shouting towards a friend standing at the bottom of another hill, several kilometers away: - **Path Loss Analogy:** Even if there were no trees, no buildings, and perfectly clear air between you and your friend, your voice would get quieter with distance. This natural decay of energy with distance is **path loss**. It is predictable and deterministic. - **Shadow Fading Analogy:** Now imagine that somewhere along the path, there is a giant concrete wall, a tall building, or a dense forest. Whether the wall is in the way or not changes dramatically whether your friend can hear you clearly. This random blockage effect (which depends on the *random* position of obstacles) is **shadow fading**. - **Small-Scale Fading Analogy (for contrast):** Now imagine that instead of one friend, your voice is hitting many surfaces — the ground, the trees, the walls — and arriving at your friend's ear as a dozen tiny echoes that may add up constructively (loud) or destructively (quiet) within a fraction of a second. This rapid flutter is **small-scale fading**, which is *not* part of LMLC. So, **LMLC = (Deterministic Path Loss) + (Random Log-Normal Shadowing)** — a "macro" view of how signal strength rolls off across a cellular coverage area. > [!NOTE] > **Operating Frequency Range:** In KTU curriculum, LMLC analysis is typically applied for frequencies between **150 MHz** and **6 GHz** (sub-6 GHz bands used in 2G/3G/4G/5G FR1). Standard test frequencies used in empirical models are **900 MHz, 1800 MHz, 2 GHz, and 2.6 GHz**. > > **Cell Radius Range:** Macrocell (1–35 km), Microcell (0.1–1 km), Picocell (4–200 m). --- ## 1.3 Why LMLC Matters in Engineering | Application | LMLC Role | |-------------|-----------| | **Link Budget Design** | Computes the **maximum allowable path loss** for a given $P_t$, antenna gains, and receiver sensitivity | | **Cell Coverage Planning** | Determines the **cell radius** for a given transmitted power | | **Handover Strategy** | Provides average signal levels for triggering handover between cells | | **Frequency Reuse & Interference** | Co-channel interference depends on LMLC path loss exponent | | **Power Control (CDMA/OFDMA)** | Slow power control compensates for **shadow fading** | | **5G NR Beamforming** | Long-term channel statistics drive beam selection | --- ## 1.4 Fundamental Distinction: Large-Scale vs Small-Scale Fading | Parameter | **Large-Scale (LMLC)** | **Small-Scale (Multipath)** | |-----------|------------------------|------------------------------| | Cause | Path loss + Shadowing | Multipath reflection/diffraction | | Distance scale | Tens of meters → kilometers | $\lambda/2$ to tens of $\lambda$ | | Time scale | Seconds → minutes | Milliseconds | | Statistical model | Log-normal | Rayleigh, Ricean, Nakagami | | Frequency dependence | Monotonic with $d$ and $f$ | Frequency-selective / flat | | KTU module coverage | Module 4 (LMLC) | Module 3 | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) — The Baseline The **Friis transmission equation** in free space (no obstructions, no reflections) gives the received power: $$P_r(d) = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$ where: - $P_t$ = transmitted power (W) - $G_t$, $G_r$ = transmitter and receiver antenna gains (linear, dimensionless) - $\lambda$ = carrier wavelength (m) - $d$ = T-R separation distance (m) - $\lambda = c/f$, with **$c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s** The free-space path loss (with isotropic antennas, $G_t = G_r = 1$) is: $$FSPL(d) = \frac{P_t}{P_r(d)} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^2$$ Expressed in dB: $$FSPL(d)[\text{dB}] = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{[\text{km}]}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{[\text{MHz}]})$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the constant **32.44** — it comes from $20\log_{10}(4\pi/c) = 32.44$ when $d$ is in km and $f$ is in MHz. For $d$ in meters: use **$-27.55$** instead. --- ## 2.2 Log-Distance Path Loss Model Empirical measurements show that received power decays **logarithmically** with distance. The general log-distance model is: $$PL(d)[\text{dB}] = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d}{d_0}\right)$$ where: - $d_0$ = reference distance (typically **1 m indoors, 100 m or 1 km outdoors**) - $n$ = **path loss exponent** (PLE) - $PL(d_0)$ = path loss at reference distance (often computed from FSPL at $d_0$) | Environment | **Path Loss Exponent (n)** | |-------------|------------------------------| | Free space | 2.0 | | Urban macrocell (LOS) | 2.0 – 2.5 | | Urban macrocell (NLOS) | 3.0 – 5.0 | | Indoor LOS | 1.6 – 1.8 | | Indoor NLOS | 3.0 – 4.0 | | Obstructed in-building | 4.0 – 6.0 | --- ## 2.3 Two-Ray Ground Reflection Model When a single ground reflection dominates over higher-order multipath (typical in macrocells with elevated BS antenna), the received power at distance $d$ becomes: $$P_r(d) = P_t G_t G_r \frac{h_t^2 h_r^2}{d^4}$$ Thus, the path loss exponent **$n = 4$** for large distances. The "breakpoint" distance beyond which the two-ray model applies: $$d_{bp} = \frac{4 h_t h_r}{\lambda}$$ For $d < d_{bp}$: free-space behavior ($n = 2$). For $d > d_{bp}$: two-ray behavior ($n = 4$). --- ## 2.4 Empirical Path Loss Models (KTU-Favorite) ### (a) Okumura-Hata Model (Urban, Suburban, Open) Applicable for **150 MHz – 1500 MHz**, distances **1–20 km**, BS height 30–200 m. **Urban (path loss in dB):** $$PL_{urban}(d) = 69.55 + 26.16 \log_{10}(f_c) - 13.82 \log_{10}(h_t) - a(h_r) + [44.9 - 6.55 \log_{10}(h_t)]\log_{10}(d)$$ where the **mobile antenna correction factor**: $$a(h_r) = (1.1 \log_{10}(f_c) - 0.7) h_r - (1.56 \log_{10}(f_c) - 0.8) \quad [\text{small/medium city}]$$ $$a(h_r) = 8.29 [\log_{10}(1.54 h_r)]^2 - 1.1 \quad [\text{large city}, f_c \le 300 \text{ MHz}]$$ $$a(h_r) = 3.2 [\log_{10}(11.75 h_r)]^2 - 4.97 \quad [\text{large city}, f_c \ge 300 \text{ MHz}]$$ **Suburban:** $$PL_{suburban} = PL_{urban} - 2[\log_{10}(f_c/28)]^2 - 5.4$$ **Open/Rural:** $$PL_{open} = PL_{urban} - 4.78[\log_{10}(f_c)]^2 + 18.33 \log_{10}(f_c) - 40.94$$ ### (b) COST-231 Hata Model Extended Hata for **1500 MHz – 2000 MHz**, applicable to **4G LTE**: $$PL_{COST231} = 46.3 + 33.9\log_{10}(f_c) - 13.82 \log_{10}(h_t) - a(h_r) + [44.9 - 6.55 \log_{10}(h_t)]\log_{10}(d) + C$$ where $C = 0$ dB for suburban and **$C = 3$ dB** for urban. --- ## 2.5 Shadow Fading — Log-Normal Distribution Shadow fading causes the received power at a fixed $d$ to be **random**. Empirical measurements show the deviation around the mean (in dB) is **Gaussian with zero mean** and standard deviation $\sigma$ (typically **6–10 dB**). The probability density function of the received signal level $P_r$ (in dBm) at distance $d$ is: $$f_{P_r}(p) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(p - \bar{P}_r(d))^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$$ Equivalently, in linear domain, $P_r$ is **lognormal**: $$f_{P_r}(P_r) = \frac{1}{P_r \sigma' \sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(\ln P_r - \mu')^2}{2\sigma'^2}\right]$$ where $\sigma' = \sigma / (10 \log_{10} e) = \sigma / 4.343$ and $\mu' = \ln P_{r,\text{median}}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Median vs Mean:** In log-normal shadowing, the **median** path loss (the value exceeded 50% of locations) is what the deterministic path-loss model predicts. The mean is slightly higher due to the long upper tail of the log-normal distribution. --- ## 2.6 Composite Large-Scale Path Loss Combining log-distance path loss with log-normal shadow fading: $$PL(d)[\text{dB}] = \underbrace{\overline{PL}(d)}_{\text{Deterministic}} + \underbrace{X_\sigma}_{\text{Shadow (Gaussian, dB)}}$$ where $X_\sigma \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. **Probability of outage** (received power below threshold $P_{th}$): $$P_{out} = P[P_r(d) < P_{th}] = \frac{1}{2} \left[1 + \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{P_{th} - \bar{P}_r(d)}{\sigma \sqrt{2}}\right)\right] = Q\!\left(\frac{P_{th} - \bar{P}_r(d)}{\sigma}\right)$$ where $Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$ is the Gaussian Q-function. **Cell radius for $\eta$% coverage probability** is found by inverting $P_{out}(R) = 1 - \eta$. --- ## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Markdown Table) > [!NOTE] > **Strict Table Format Note:** All absolute values and vertical bars are rendered with `\vert` to prevent markdown table corruption. | # | Formula (LaTeX-rendered) | Description | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $PL(d) = \overline{PL}(d) + X_\sigma$ | Composite LMLC path loss | dB | | 2 | $\overline{PL}(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n\log_{10}(d/d_0)$ | Log-distance mean PL | dB | | 3 | $FSPL(d) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | Free-space PL | dB | | 4 | $FSPL(d) = -27.55 + 20\log_{10}(d_m) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | FSPL with $d$ in m | dB | | 5 | $PL_{Okumura-Hata}^{urban} = 69.55 + 26.16\log f_c - 13.82\log h_t - a(h_r) + (44.9 - 6.55\log h_t)\log d$ | Okumura-Hata urban | dB | | 6 | $a(h_r) = (1.1\log f_c - 0.7)h_r - (1.56\log f_c - 0.8)$ | Mobile correction (small city) | dB | | 7 | $d_{bp} = 4h_t h_r / \lambda$ | Two-ray breakpoint distance | m | | 8 | $P_r(d) \propto d^{-n}$ | Received power decay (linear) | W | | 9 | $P_{out} = Q\!\left[(P_{th} - \bar{P}_r(d))/\sigma\right]$ | Outage probability | dimensionless | | 10 | $R_{cell} = d_0 \cdot 10^{[(P_{th} - P_t - G_t - G_r + PL(d_0))/(10n) - \sigma Q^{-1}(1-\eta)] / 10n}$ | Cell radius for $\eta$% coverage | m | --- ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility The LMLC model is the **engineer's first tool** before deploying any base station. It feeds into: - **5G NR gNB placement** — cell radius, antenna downtilt, sectorization. - **IoT LPWAN (LoRa, NB-IoT) range estimation** — link budget directly determines maximum coupling loss. - **Public safety networks (TETRA, P25)** — coverage predictions for emergency responders. - **Satellite-to-mobile (LEO constellations like Starlink)** — log-distance model adapted for slant-range path loss. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Equation) **Step 1: Isotropic Radiated Power Density** Consider an **isotropic** radiator of power $P_t$. By symmetry, the power density at distance $d$ is uniform over a sphere of area $4\pi d^2$: $$\mathcal{S}_{iso}(d) = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad [\text{W/m}^2]$$ **Step 2: Effective Aperture of Receiving Antenna** A receiving antenna with gain $G_r$ has an effective aperture: $$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$ **Step 3: Power Captured by Receiving Antenna** Multiply power density by aperture: $$P_r(d) = \mathcal{S}_{iso}(d) \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$ **Step 4: Include Transmitter Gain $G_t$** If the transmitter also has gain $G_t$, the power density in the main beam direction is increased by $G_t$: $$P_r(d) = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$ This is the famous **Friis transmission equation**. **Step 5: Derive Path Loss** Path loss is the ratio $P_t / P_r$ (with unit gains): $$PL_{FS}(d) = \frac{P_t}{P_r(d)} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^2$$ **Step 6: Convert to dB** Take $10\log_{10}$ of both sides: $$PL_{FS}(d)[\text{dB}] = 10\log_{10}\!\left[\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^2\right] = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)$$ Substituting $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz: $$\frac{4\pi d f}{c} = \frac{4\pi \cdot (d_{km} \cdot 10^3) \cdot (f_{MHz} \cdot 10^6)}{3 \times 10^8} = \frac{4\pi \cdot 10^9}{3 \times 10^8} \cdot d_{km} \cdot f_{MHz}$$ $$\frac{4\pi \cdot 10^9}{3 \times 10^8} = \frac{4\pi}{3} \cdot 10 \approx 41.888$$ Taking $20\log_{10}(41.888) \approx 32.44$: $$\boxed{PL_{FS}(d)[\text{dB}] = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of Two-Ray Model Received Power **Step 1: Geometry Setup** - Transmitter antenna at height $h_t$, receiver at height $h_r$. - Separation distance $d$ (assumed large: $d \gg h_t, h_r$). - LOS path length $\approx d$. - Reflected path length $\approx d + \Delta$, where $\Delta$ is the path difference. **Step 2: Path Difference from Ground Reflection** By geometry of image theory, the reflected ray effectively travels from a mirror image antenna at height $-h_t$. The path difference is: $$\Delta \approx \frac{2 h_t h_r}{d}$$ (derivation via Taylor expansion of $\sqrt{(h_t + h_r)^2 + d^2} - \sqrt{(h_t - h_r)^2 + d^2}$ for $d \gg h_t, h_r$). **Step 3: Phase Difference** $$\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \Delta = \frac{4\pi h_t h_r}{\lambda d}$$ **Step 4: Received Field (LOS + Reflected)** Assuming equal amplitude after reflection (with reflection coefficient $\Gamma \approx -1$ for grazing incidence on ground), the received electric field magnitude is: $$E_r = E_0 \left[\frac{1}{d} + \frac{\Gamma}{d + \Delta} \cos(\Delta\phi)\right] \approx \frac{2 E_0 \Delta}{d^2}$$ (substituting $\Gamma = -1$, $\cos \pi = -1$ for the half-wavelength condition that gives destructive interference). **Step 5: Received Power** Power is proportional to $E_r^2$, so: $$P_r \propto \frac{\Delta^2}{d^4} = \frac{(2 h_t h_r / d)^2}{d^4} = \frac{4 h_t^2 h_r^2}{d^6}$$ The factor $d^6$ in numerator and denominator together give $d^4$: $$\boxed{P_r(d) = P_t G_t G_r \frac{h_t^2 h_r^2}{d^4}}$$ **Step 6: Path Loss Exponent** Since $P_r \propto d^{-4}$, the path loss exponent is **$n = 4$**, which means in dB: $$PL_{2-ray}(d)[\text{dB}] = PL_{2-ray}(d_0) + 40 \log_{10}(d/d_0)$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of Outage Probability **Step 1: Received Power in dB Domain** $$P_r(d)[\text{dBm}] = P_t[\text{dBm}] + G_t + G_r - \overline{PL}(d) - X_\sigma$$ where $X_\sigma \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$. **Step 2: Equivalent Statement** $$P_r(d) \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\bar{P}_r(d), \sigma^2\right) \quad \text{in dBm}$$ **Step 3: Threshold Crossing Probability** $$P_{out} = P\!\left[P_r(d) < P_{th}\right] = P\!\left[\frac{P_r(d) - \bar{P}_r(d)}{\sigma} < \frac{P_{th} - \bar{P}_r(d)}{\sigma}\right]$$ **Step 4: Recognize Standard Normal** Let $Z = (P_r - \bar{P}_r)/\sigma \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$. Then: $$P_{out} = P\!\left[Z < \frac{P_{th} - \bar{P}_r(d)}{\sigma}\right] = \Phi\!\left(\frac{P_{th} - \bar{P}_r(d)}{\sigma}\right)$$ where $\Phi(\cdot)$ is the standard normal CDF. Using the relation $\Phi(x) = 1 - Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}[1 + \text{erf}(x/\sqrt{2})]$: $$\boxed{P_{out} = Q\!\left(\frac{\bar{P}_r(d) - P_{th}}{\sigma}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 - \text{erf}\!\left(\frac{\bar{P}_r(d) - P_{th}}{\sigma \sqrt{2}}\right)\right]}$$ **Step 5: Cell Radius for Target Coverage Probability** For a target coverage probability $1 - P_{out}^{target} = \eta$, set $P_{out} = 1 - \eta$, solve $Q^{-1}(1-\eta)$ for the margin, then invert the log-distance equation. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** A GSM-900 base station transmits at $P_t = 40$ dBm with $G_t = 12$ dBi. The mobile has $G_r = 0$ dBi and a noise floor of $-104$ dBm. The path loss exponent is $n = 3.5$, reference PL at $d_0 = 100$ m is $PL(d_0) = 70$ dB, and shadow std. dev. is $\sigma = 8$ dB. Find: > (a) The mean received power at $d = 2$ km. > (b) The probability that the received power falls below the noise floor (outage). > (c) The maximum cell radius for **90% coverage probability**. ### Part (a) — Mean Received Power Mean PL at 2 km: $$\overline{PL}(2000) = 70 + 10 \times 3.5 \times \log_{10}(2000/100) = 70 + 35 \log_{10}(20)$$ $$\log_{10}(20) = \log_{10}(2) + \log_{10}(10) = 0.301 + 1 = 1.301$$ $$\overline{PL}(2000) = 70 + 35 \times 1.301 = 70 + 45.535 = 115.535 \text{ dB}$$ Mean received power: $$\bar{P}_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \overline{PL} = 40 + 12 + 0 - 115.535 = -63.535 \text{ dBm}$$ ### Part (b) — Outage Probability $$P_{out} = Q\!\left(\frac{\bar{P}_r - P_{th}}{\sigma}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{-63.535 - (-104)}{8}\right) = Q\!\left(\frac{40.465}{8}\right) = Q(5.058)$$ From Q-function table: $Q(5.058) \approx 2.13 \times 10^{-7}$. So **outage probability ≈ $2.13 \times 10^{-7}$** — extremely reliable. ### Part (c) — Cell Radius for 90% Coverage For 90% coverage, $P_{out} = 0.10$, so $Q^{-1}(0.10) \approx -1.2816$. We need: $$\bar{P}_r(R) - P_{th} = \sigma \cdot Q^{-1}(1-\eta) = 8 \times (-1.2816) = -10.253 \text{ dB}$$ So $\bar{P}_r(R) = -104 - 10.253 = -114.253$ dBm. Therefore: $$\overline{PL}(R) = 40 + 12 - (-114.253) = 166.253 \text{ dB}$$ $$166.253 = 70 + 35 \log_{10}(R/100)$$ $$\log_{10}(R/100) = \frac{166.253 - 70}{35} = \frac{96.253}{35} = 2.7501$$ $$R/100 = 10^{2.7501} = 562.3$$ $$\boxed{R \approx 56.23 \text{ km}}$$ This is the theoretical maximum cell radius under the simplified log-distance model — far larger than realistic because shadow std. dev. is small and we ignore interference. --- ## 3.5 Derivations Summary Matrix | # | Concept | Starting Point | Final Result | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Friis Equation | Power density on sphere | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi d)^2$ | | 2 | FSPL (dB) | $PL = (4\pi d/\lambda)^2$ | $32.44 + 20\log d_{km} + 20\log f_{MHz}$ | | 3 | Two-Ray PL | Image theory + reflection | $P_r \propto d^{-4}$ ($n = 4$) | | 4 | Breakpoint Distance | $\Delta\phi = \pi$ condition | $d_{bp} = 4 h_t h_r / \lambda$ | | 5 | Okumura-Hata | Empirical fit (Tokyo measurements) | Urban PL formula | | 6 | Outage Probability | Gaussian shadow tail | $Q[(\bar{P}_r - P_{th})/\sigma]$ | | 7 | Coverage Probability | $1 - P_{out}$ target | Inverted log-distance | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 LMLC Signal Power vs Distance — Conceptual Flow ```mermaid graph TD Tx["Transmitter
P_t (dBm)"] Path1["Free-Space PL
FSPL d^-2"] Path2["Two-Ray PL
d^-4"] Path3["Empirical PL
Okumura-Hata"] Shadow["Shadow Fading
X_sigma ~ N(0,sigma^2)"] Rx["Received Power
P_r(d) dBm"] Tx --> Path1 Path1 -->|Deterministic Component| Shadow Tx --> Path2 Path2 -->|Deterministic Component| Shadow Tx --> Path3 Path3 -->|Deterministic Component| Shadow Shadow --> Rx style Tx fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Path1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Path2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Path3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Shadow fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style Rx fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` **Description:** The transmitter feeds a deterministic path-loss function (chosen among free-space, two-ray, or empirical models), which then has a Gaussian-distributed shadow-fading random variable added (in dB domain) before reaching the receiver. The two parallel rows of blue boxes emphasize that **only one** of the three deterministic models is selected based on environment (open / suburban / urban, frequency range, antenna heights). --- ## 4.2 Log-Normal Shadow Fading — PDF Schematic ```mermaid graph LR AxisX["Received Power Level P_r (dBm)"] Peak["PDF Peak
at P_r = mean_P_r"] TailL["Left Tail
P_r < P_th → Outage"] TailR["Right Tail
P_r > P_th → Coverage"] AxisX --> Peak Peak --> TailL Peak --> TailR style AxisX fill:#F0F0F0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 style Peak fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF style TailL fill:#DC143C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF style TailR fill:#228B22,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` **Description:** The log-normal (Gaussian in dB) PDF of received power is centered at the deterministic mean path-loss prediction. The left tail (below the threshold $P_{th}$) corresponds to **outage events** (dropped calls in cellular context). The right tail corresponds to reliable coverage. The Gaussian Q-function integrates the left tail to give the outage probability. --- ## 4.3 LMLC Block Diagram — Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid graph TD BlockA["Input Parameters
Pt dBm, Gt dBi, Gr dBi
fc MHz, ht hr m
Environment Type"] BlockB["PL Model Selector
FS / Two-Ray / Hata"] BlockC["Path Loss Calculation
PL_bar dB at distance d"] BlockD["Mean Received Power
P_r_mean = Pt + Gt + Gr - PL_bar"] BlockE["Shadow Fading Generator
X_sigma ~ Normal mean 0 std sigma"] BlockF["Instantaneous Received Power
P_r_instant = P_r_mean + X_sigma"] BlockG["Threshold Comparison
P_r_instant vs P_th"] BlockH["Decision Output
COVERAGE or OUTAGE"] BlockA --> BlockB BlockB --> BlockC BlockC --> BlockD BlockD --> BlockF BlockA -.Sigma.-> BlockE BlockE --> BlockF BlockF --> BlockG BlockG --> BlockH style BlockA fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style BlockB fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style BlockC fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style BlockD fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style BlockE fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style BlockF fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style BlockG fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 style BlockH fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` **Description:** End-to-end functional flow of LMLC evaluation. Input parameters are processed by an appropriate path-loss model selector (based on environment). The deterministic mean PL is computed, then a Gaussian random variable representing shadow fading is added. The instantaneous power is compared against a threshold to make the final coverage decision. This is the algorithmic skeleton for any link-budget Monte Carlo simulation used in cellular planning tools (e.g., Atoll, Planet EV). # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Distinguish between large-scale fading and small-scale fading in land mobile channels.** **Model Answer:** | Property | Large-Scale Fading (LMLC) | Small-Scale Fading | |---|---|---| | Cause | Path loss + shadowing | Multipath interference | | Distance scale | 10s of m to km | Order of $\lambda$ | | Time scale | Seconds to minutes | Milliseconds | | Statistical model | Log-normal | Rayleigh / Ricean | | Mitigation | Power control, cell planning | Diversity, equalization, OFDM | (3 marks: 1 for cause, 1 for stat model, 1 for mitigation/example.) --- ### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: What is the breakpoint distance in the two-ray ground reflection model? Derive its expression.** **Model Answer:** The **breakpoint distance** is the T-R separation beyond which the two-ray model (with $n=4$) becomes more accurate than free-space ($n=2$). It arises from the condition that the path difference between the LOS and reflected rays equals $\lambda/2$ (destructive interference starts). $$d_{bp} = \frac{4 h_t h_r}{\lambda}$$ For $d < d_{bp}$: $n = 2$; for $d > d_{bp}$: $n = 4$. (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for expression, 1 for condition.) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has TWO sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Question A OR Question B** choice. --- ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Choice]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Land Mobile Large-Scale Channel (LMLC)** model. Discuss the two main components: **path loss** and **shadow fading**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cellular system operates at $f_c = 900$ MHz with a base station antenna height $h_t = 50$ m and mobile antenna height $h_r = 1.5$ m. The transmit power is $P_t = 33$ dBm, antenna gains are $G_t = 14$ dBi and $G_r = 0$ dBi. Using the **Okumura-Hata urban model**, compute the path loss and received power at a mobile located **5 km** from the base station. Assume a small/medium city. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution #### (a) LMLC Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks) The LMLC model predicts the received signal strength as a function of T-R distance $d$ in cellular land-mobile environments. The block diagram (see Section 4.3) shows the cascade: 1. **Transmitter block**: specifies $P_t$, $G_t$, $f_c$, $h_t$. 2. **Path-loss model selector**: chooses between free-space, two-ray, or empirical (Okumura-Hata) based on environment and frequency. 3. **Path-loss computation block**: computes $\overline{PL}(d)$ in dB. 4. **Shadow-fading generator block**: adds a Gaussian RV $X_\sigma \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma$ typically 6–10 dB. 5. **Threshold comparison block**: produces COVERAGE / OUTAGE decisions. **Component 1: Path Loss** is the deterministic attenuation of signal with distance. It is modeled by the log-distance equation: $$\overline{PL}(d)[\text{dB}] = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}(d/d_0)$$ **Component 2: Shadow Fading** is a random log-normal variation due to obstructions. In dB domain, it is a zero-mean Gaussian with std. dev. $\sigma$. The composite received power is: $$P_r(d)[\text{dBm}] = P_t + G_t + G_r - \overline{PL}(d) - X_\sigma$$ (Marks distribution: 2 for diagram, 3 for path loss, 2 for shadow fading.) --- #### (b) Okumura-Hata Calculation (7 marks) **Given:** $f_c = 900$ MHz, $h_t = 50$ m, $h_r = 1.5$ m, $d = 5$ km. **Step 1: Compute $a(h_r)$ for small/medium city:** $$a(h_r) = (1.1 \log_{10}(900) - 0.7) \cdot 1.5 - (1.56 \log_{10}(900) - 0.8)$$ $$\log_{10}(900) = 2.954$$ $$a(h_r) = (1.1 \times 2.954 - 0.7) \times 1.5 - (1.56 \times 2.954 - 0.8)$$ $$a(h_r) = (3.249 - 0.7) \times 1.5 - (4.609 - 0.8)$$ $$a(h_r) = 2.549 \times 1.5 - 3.809 = 3.824 - 3.809 = 0.015 \text{ dB} \approx 0 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 2: Compute Urban Path Loss:** $$PL_{urban} = 69.55 + 26.16 \log_{10}(900) - 13.82 \log_{10}(50) - 0.015 + [44.9 - 6.55 \log_{10}(50)] \log_{10}(5)$$ Compute each term: - $26.16 \log_{10}(900) = 26.16 \times 2.954 = 77.27$ - $13.82 \log_{10}(50) = 13.82 \times 1.699 = 23.48$ - $44.9 - 6.55 \log_{10}(50) = 44.9 - 6.55 \times 1.699 = 44.9 - 11.13 = 33.77$ - $\log_{10}(5) = 0.699$ $$PL_{urban} = 69.55 + 77.27 - 23.48 - 0.015 + 33.77 \times 0.699$$ $$PL_{urban} = 123.325 + 23.605 = 146.93 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 3: Compute Received Power:** $$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL = 33 + 14 + 0 - 146.93 = -99.93 \text{ dBm}$$ $$\boxed{P_r \approx -99.93 \text{ dBm}}$$ If the receiver sensitivity is $-104$ dBm, the link has a **margin of ≈ 4 dB**, which is acceptable for voice but tight for high-data-rate LTE. (Marks: 2 for $a(h_r)$, 3 for $PL_{urban}$, 2 for $P_r$ and conclusion.) --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4 Choice]` **(a)** Explain the **log-distance path loss model** with the significance of the **path loss exponent $n$**. List typical values of $n$ for various environments. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a wireless link with $P_t = 30$ dBm, $G_t = 10$ dBi, $G_r = 2$ dBi, $PL(d_0) = 40$ dB at $d_0 = 1$ m, $n = 4$, and $\sigma = 8$ dB, determine the **cell radius** such that the **outage probability does not exceed 5%**. Assume the receiver sensitivity is $-95$ dBm. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution #### (a) Log-Distance Path Loss Model (7 marks) The **log-distance path loss model** is an empirical generalization that captures the observed fact that received signal strength decays **logarithmically** with distance (not as $d^2$ or $d^4$ alone): $$\overline{PL}(d)[\text{dB}] = PL(d_0) + 10n \log_{10}(d/d_0)$$ - $d_0$ = close-in reference distance where path loss is known (1 m indoor, 100 m–1 km outdoor). - $n$ = **path loss exponent** — slope of the log-distance curve. **Significance of $n$:** - $n = 2$: Free-space (no obstructions). - $n > 2$: Increasing obstructions and reflections causing faster decay. - $n = 4$: Two-ray ground bounce, severe obstruction. - $n$ quantifies the **propagation harshness** of the environment. **Typical values:** | Environment | $n$ | |---|---| | Free space | 2.0 | | Urban LOS | 2.5–3.0 | | Urban NLOS | 3.0–5.0 | | Indoor LOS | 1.6–1.8 | | Indoor NLOS | 3.0–4.0 | | Obstructed factory | 4.0–6.0 | (Marks: 3 for equation + explanation, 2 for significance, 2 for table.) --- #### (b) Cell Radius Calculation (7 marks) **Given:** $P_t = 30$ dBm, $G_t = 10$ dBi, $G_r = 2$ dBi, $PL(d_0) = 40$ dB, $d_0 = 1$ m, $n = 4$, $\sigma = 8$ dB, $P_{th} = -95$ dBm, $P_{out}^{target} = 0.05$. **Step 1: Compute the shadow margin.** For $P_{out} = 0.05$, we need $Q(\gamma) = 0.05$, where $\gamma = (\bar{P}_r - P_{th})/\sigma$. From standard tables, $Q^{-1}(0.05) = -1.645$, so: $$\gamma = -1.645 \implies \bar{P}_r - P_{th} = -1.645 \times 8 = -13.16 \text{ dB}$$ $$\bar{P}_r = -95 - 13.16 = -108.16 \text{ dBm}$$ **Step 2: Invert the log-distance equation.** Mean received power: $$\bar{P}_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - \overline{PL}(R)$$ $$-108.16 = 30 + 10 + 2 - \overline{PL}(R)$$ $$\overline{PL}(R) = 30 + 10 + 2 + 108.16 = 150.16 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 3: Solve for R.** $$150.16 = 40 + 10 \times 4 \times \log_{10}(R/1)$$ $$110.16 = 40 \log_{10}(R)$$ $$\log_{10}(R) = 110.16 / 40 = 2.754$$ $$R = 10^{2.754} = 567.5 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{R \approx 567.5 \text{ m}}$$ (Marks: 2 for shadow margin, 2 for path loss equation, 2 for solving, 1 for final answer with units.) --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in LMLC Problems:** > > 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing km and m, MHz and GHz. The constant **32.44** requires $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz. Using $d$ in m gives a different constant (−27.55). **−1 to 2 marks.** > > 2. **Linear vs dB Confusion:** Students often substitute $d$ directly into the linear-domain Friis equation and then take log, or vice-versa. **−2 marks.** > > 3. **Wrong $a(h_r)$ formula:** For Okumura-Hata, $a(h_r)$ depends on whether the city is small/medium or large AND on whether $f_c \le 300$ MHz or $\ge 300$ MHz. **−2 marks** if used incorrectly. > > 4. **Forgetting antenna gains:** Link budget equation **must** include $G_t$ and $G_r$. **−1 mark** if omitted. > > 5. **Q-Function vs Erf:** $Q(x) = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$. Do NOT write $Q(x) = \text{erfc}(x)$. **−1 mark.** > > 6. **Confusing $P_{out}$ with $P_{coverage}$:** $P_{out} = Q[(\bar{P}_r - P_{th})/\sigma]$. Some students write the wrong sign in the argument. **−2 marks.** > > 7. **Not stating assumptions:** Always mention environment type (urban/suburban/open), city size, frequency range, antenna height range when applying Okumura-Hata. **−1 mark** if missing. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **LMLC High-Yield Revision Points:** - ☐ **LMLC = Path Loss + Shadow Fading.** No multipath / Doppler. - ☐ **Friis equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda/4\pi d)^2$. Free-space baseline. - ☐ **FSPL (dB):** $32.44 + 20\log d_{km} + 20\log f_{MHz}$. - ☐ **Log-distance model:** $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10n\log(d/d_0)$. - ☐ **Path loss exponent $n$:** $n=2$ (free space), $n=4$ (two-ray), $n=3$–$5$ (urban NLOS). - ☐ **Breakpoint distance:** $d_{bp} = 4 h_t h_r / \lambda$. - ☐ **Okumura-Hata applies:** 150–1500 MHz, $d = 1$–$20$ km, $h_t = 30$–$200$ m. - ☐ **COST-231 Hata** extends to 1500–2000 MHz with $C = 0$ (suburban) or $C = 3$ (urban). - ☐ **Shadow fading is log-normal** (Gaussian in dB). $\sigma = 6$–$10$ dB typical. - ☐ **Composite PL:** $PL(d) = \overline{PL}(d) + X_\sigma$, $X_\sigma \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$. - ☐ **Outage probability:** $P_{out} = Q[(\bar{P}_r - P_{th})/\sigma]$. - ☐ **Coverage probability:** $\eta = 1 - P_{out}$. Use $Q^{-1}(1-\eta)$ for shadow margin. - ☐ **Median path loss** is the deterministic value; **mean is slightly larger** due to log-normal upper tail. - ☐ **Link budget master equation:** $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - PL - $ cable/connector losses. - ☐ **5G/4G cell radius** is determined by LMLC and interference — not by transmit power alone. - ☐ **KTU favourite empirical model:** Okumura-Hata (urban, small/medium city). - ☐ **Don't confuse large-scale with small-scale.** Different causes, different statistics, different remedies. --- **End of KTU Module 4 — LMLC Notes | PEECT741 Advanced Mobile Communication**

topics.title: Possible Solutions for Connectivity in Rural Areas (BharatNet, TVWS, Long-Range WiFi, FSO)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT741 - ADVANCED MOBILE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Possible Solutions for Connectivity in Rural Areas (BharatNet, TVWS, Long-Range WiFi, FSO)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Rural Connectivity Problem

In India, roughly **65% of the population** resides in rural regions, yet these regions house only a tiny fraction of the country's optical fiber backhaul infrastructure. The fundamental challenge is the **"Last-Mile Bottleneck"** — high-capacity fiber core networks terminate at district/block headquarters, but the final leg to villages (1–10 km) is technically and economically infeasible using trenching-based fiber.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Rural connectivity solutions refer to a *heterogeneous mix* of wired, wireless, and optical technologies that bypass the economic burden of fiber trenching in low-ARPU (Average Revenue Per User) areas by leveraging existing infrastructure (towers, TV spectrum, rooftops, optical air paths).

## 1.2 The Four Major Solutions at a Glance

| # | Solution | Transmission Medium | Typical Range | Governing Principle |
|---|----------|---------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **BharatNet** | Optical Fiber Cable (OFC) on electric poles | Inter-village (50–100 km rings) | GPON/EPON fiber-to-the-village |
| 2 | **TV White Space (TVWS)** | UHF/VHF radio (470–790 MHz) | 10–30 km | Cognitive access to guard bands of TV broadcast |
| 3 | **Long-Range WiFi** | 2.4 / 5 GHz ISM band | 1–8 km (with directional antenna) | Modified 802.11 MAC with TDMA + high-gain CPE |
| 4 | **Free Space Optics (FSO)** | Near-IR laser (785–1550 nm) | 0.5–5 km | Line-of-Sight optical transmission |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogies (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **BharatNet — "The National Highway Analogy":**
> Imagine BharatNet as India's digital "NH-44". Just as the highway connects state capitals via massive concrete roads, BharatNet connects Gram Panchayats via **a single dark-fiber highway** laid over the same electric poles that already crisscross rural India (the **OPGW/Aerial OFC** approach). The poles are already there — only the cable needs to be strung.

> [!NOTE]
> **TV White Space — "Empty Seats in a Cinema Hall":**
> In a 500-seat cinema, only 200 seats are sold per show. TVWS is like selling the remaining 300 empty seats to a different audience at a different show, while ensuring no two viewers collide. Similarly, the TV broadcast band uses only ~50% of its allocated spectrum; the rest is "white space" — perfect for low-power rural broadband.

> [!NOTE]
> **Long-Range WiFi — "A Megaphone Instead of a Whisper":**
> Standard WiFi (802.11n) is a whisper meant for a 10-meter room. Long-Range WiFi replaces the whisper with a **directional megaphone (high-gain dish + TDMA MAC)**. The receiver hears the same signal, but the air molecules along the path are now shaken ~30 dB harder. Range extends from 30 m → 8 km.

> [!NOTE]
> **FSO — "Invisible Fiber in the Air":**
> FSO is literally fiber-optic cable without the glass — the laser pulses travel through free air instead of silica. The "fiber" is just the straight line between two rooftop transceivers. Fog and rain are the enemies because they scatter photons the same way a dirty glass fiber would.

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants Used in Rural Link Budgets

- **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
- **Free-space path loss exponent (rural):** $n = 2.0$ to **2.4**
- **Effective Earth radius (k-factor = 4/3):** $R_e = 8493$ **km**
- **TVWS center band:** **470–790 MHz** (varies by region)
- **FSO operating windows:** **785 nm**, **1550 nm** (eye-safe & low-scatter)
- **Standard GPON downstream wavelength:** **1490 nm**
- **Standard GPON upstream wavelength:** **1310 nm**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Solution 1 — BharatNet (India's Rural Backbone)

### 2.1.1 Architecture

BharatNet (formerly NOFN — National Optical Fiber Network) is a **three-tier** architecture that delivers **100 Mbps** minimum to every Gram Panchayat (GP), with the latest Phase-III targeting **1 Gbps**.

**Tier-1 (Backbone):** District → Block (typically **100 Gbps** DWDM rings).
**Tier-2 (Aggregation):** Block → GP using **GPON** (Gigabit Passive Optical Network).
**Tier-3 (Last-Mile):** GP → Households (delivered via WiFi/VSAT/FTTH by local ISPs/CSPs).

### 2.1.2 Key Technologies Used

- **GPON (ITU-T G.984):** Uses **2.488 Gbps** downstream / **1.244 Gbps** upstream, shared among **up to 128 ONTs** per OLT port.
- **Split ratio:** Typically **1:64** in rural BharatNet to amortize feeder-fiber cost.
- **Aerial OFC (OPGW/ADSS):** All-Dielectric Self-Supporting or Optical Ground Wire cables strung on **11 kV / 33 kV** electric poles, eliminating trenching cost.

### 2.1.3 Power Budget Equation

The maximum optical loss between OLT and ONT:

$$P_{budget} = P_{TX} - S_{r} - M_{s} - C_{L} \cdot L - P_{margin}$$

where:
- $P_{TX}$ = Transmitter launch power (typically **+2 dBm** for Class B+ GPON)
- $S_r$ = Receiver sensitivity (**−28 dBm** for GPON at BER $10^{-10}$)
- $M_s$ = System margin (typically **3 dB**)
- $C_L$ = Fiber attenuation (**0.35 dB/km** at 1310 nm)
- $L$ = Fiber length in km
- $P_{margin}$ = Safety margin (typically **2–3 dB**)

---

## 2.2 Solution 2 — TV White Space (TVWS)

### 2.2.1 Spectrum Background

Analog TV switch-off (completed in India by **2017**) freed the UHF band. TVWS devices are **secondary users** that must vacate within a defined time if a primary TV broadcaster activates.

### 2.2.2 The TVWS Link Budget — Friis in Sub-GHz

$$P_{r}(dBm) = P_{t} + G_{t} + G_{r} - 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) - 32.44$$

All terms in dB/dBm, $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why TVWS travels so far:** At $f = 600$ MHz, free-space loss for **10 km** is only about **108 dB**. Compare this to a 2.4 GHz WiFi signal at 10 km which suffers **120 dB** — a **12 dB advantage** simply from the lower frequency (better diffraction around trees and buildings).

### 2.2.3 Path-Loss with Rural Foliage Excess

For vegetated rural areas, ITU-R P.833 recommends an additional **foliage loss**:

$$L_{foliage} = 0.2 \cdot f^{0.3} \cdot d^{0.6} \quad \text{(dB)}$$

with $f$ in MHz, $d$ in meters through the canopy.

### 2.2.4 Cognitive Database / Geo-Location Requirement

Every TVWS device must consult a **geolocation database** before transmitting. The database computes the maximum **EIRP** such that interference to nearby TV receivers stays below the protection threshold (typically **−114 dBm** for DTT reception).

---

## 2.3 Solution 3 — Long-Range WiFi

### 2.3.1 Modifications Over Standard WiFi (802.11)

| Parameter | Standard WiFi | Long-Range WiFi |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| PHY | 802.11n/ac | 802.11n + custom PHY |
| Antenna | Omni (2 dBi) | Dish / Sector (16–24 dBi) |
| MAC | CSMA/CA | TDMA + Polling (no contention) |
| Tx Power | 20 dBm | 30–36 dBm |
| Range | 30–100 m | 1–8 km |
| Throughput | 300 Mbps (short range) | 5–20 Mbps (long range) |

### 2.3.2 Fresnel Zone — The Hidden Constraint

For long-range links, the **first Fresnel zone radius** at midpoint is:

$$r_{1} = 8.656 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{d}{4 \cdot f}}$$

where $d$ is total link length in km and $f$ in GHz. **Result is in meters.** Typically, **60% clearance** of the first Fresnel zone is required.

---

## 2.4 Solution 4 — Free Space Optics (FSO)

### 2.4.1 The Beer–Lambert Atmospheric Attenuation

$$P_{r} = P_{t} \cdot \eta_{t} \cdot \eta_{r} \cdot \left(\frac{d_{R}}{d}\right)^{2} \cdot 10^{-\frac{\sigma \cdot d}{10}}$$

where:
- $\eta_{t}, \eta_{r}$ = Transmitter / receiver optical efficiency
- $d_{R}$ = Receiver aperture diameter
- $\sigma$ = Atmospheric attenuation coefficient (**dB/km**)
- $d$ = Link distance

> [!NOTE]
> Typical clear-weather $\sigma$ ≈ **0.5–2 dB/km**. Heavy fog can push $\sigma$ to **30–100 dB/km**, completely breaking a 1 km link.

### 2.4.2 Visibility-to-Attenuation Model (Kim Model)

$$\sigma = \frac{17}{V} \quad \text{(dB/km)}$$

where $V$ is the visibility in km. For $V = 1$ km, $\sigma = 17$ dB/km; for $V = 10$ km (clear), $\sigma = 1.7$ dB/km.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Use Case | Units |
|---|---------|----------|-------|
| 1 | $L_{fs}(dB) = 32.44 + 20\log f + 20\log d$ | Free-space path loss | f: MHz, d: km |
| 2 | $r_{1} = 8.656 \sqrt{d/4f}$ | First Fresnel radius | d: km, f: GHz, r: m |
| 3 | $L_{foliage} = 0.2 \cdot f^{0.3} \cdot d^{0.6}$ | ITU-R P.833 canopy loss | f: MHz, d: m |
| 4 | $P_{budget} = P_{TX} - S_r - M_s - C_L L$ | GPON optical budget | dBm |
| 5 | $\sigma = 17/V$ | FSO fog attenuation | V: km, σ: dB/km |
| 6 | $SNR = P_r - N_0 - 10\log_{10} B$ | Receiver SNR | dB |
| 7 | $C = B \log_2(1 + SNR)$ | Shannon capacity | bps |
| 8 | $EIRP = P_{TX} + G_{TX} - L_{feed}$ | Regulatory EIRP | dBm |
| 9 | $L_{pl}(dB) = L_{fs} + A_{env}$ | Total path loss rural | dB |
| 10 | $t_{prop} = d / c$ | One-way propagation delay | s |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL:** When using formula #1, ensure $f$ is in **MHz** (not GHz) and $d$ is in **km**. Mixing units gives a 60 dB error — the #1 mistake KTU examiners report.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Friis Free-Space Equation From Maxwell's Equations

Starting from the time-harmonic Poynting vector of a Hertzian dipole:

$$S(\theta) = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}$$

The effective aperture of an isotropic antenna is:

$$A_{e} = \frac{\lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$$

Therefore, power captured by a receive antenna of gain $G_r$ is:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e \cdot G_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$

Converting $\lambda = c/f$ and taking $10\log_{10}$:

$$P_r (dBm) = P_t + G_t + G_r + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{c}{4\pi f d}\right)$$

Plugging $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s and converting $f$ to MHz, $d$ to km, the constant evaluates to **32.44**, yielding:

$$\boxed{\,L_{fs}(dB) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10} f + 20\log_{10} d\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Worked GPON Power-Budget Problem

**Problem:** A BharatNet Phase-III GPON OLT transmits **+5 dBm**, receiver sensitivity is **−27 dBm**, fiber attenuation is **0.4 dB/km** at **1310 nm**, with **1:64** splitter loss **22 dB**, and a margin of **3 dB**. Find maximum reach $L$.

**Step 1 — Write the link equation:**

$$P_{TX} - L_{fiber} - L_{splitter} - M_{margin} \geq S_r$$

$$+5 - (0.4 L) - 22 - 3 \geq -27$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**

$$-20 - 0.4 L \geq -27$$

**Step 3 — Add $0.4L + 27$ to both sides:**

$$7 \geq 0.4 L$$

**Step 4 — Solve for L:**

$$L \leq \frac{7}{0.4} = 17.5 \text{ km}$$

**Step 5 — Apply 2 km engineering safety margin (KTU-style):**

$$L_{actual} = 15.5 \text{ km}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Insight:** The examiner awards **2 marks** for the link-budget equation, **2 marks** for substituting values, **2 marks** for the simplification step, and **1 mark** for the final numerical answer with units. Skipping the engineering margin costs you one full mark.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — TVWS Range Computation vs 2.4 GHz WiFi

**Given:**
- $P_t$ = **30 dBm**
- $G_t$ = $G_r$ = **14 dBi** (typical Yagi)
- $S_r$ = **−95 dBm** for 5 MHz channel at QPSK 1/2 (typical TVWS)
- $d$ = **15 km**

**Step 1 — Compute free-space loss at 600 MHz:**

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(600) + 20\log_{10}(15)$$

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 55.56 + 23.52 = 111.52 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Received power:**

$$P_r = 30 + 14 + 14 - 111.52 = -53.52 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 3 — Compare to 2.4 GHz at same range:**

$$L_{fs}(2.4) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(2400) + 20\log_{10}(15)$$

$$L_{fs}(2.4) = 32.44 + 67.60 + 23.52 = 123.56 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Received power at 2.4 GHz:**

$$P_r(2.4) = 30 + 14 + 14 - 123.56 = -65.56 \text{ dBm}$$

**Step 5 — SNR margin advantage of TVWS:**

$$\Delta = 65.56 - 53.52 = 12.04 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This 12 dB advantage is **why TVWS remains the only sub-GHz technology capable of 15+ km NLOS links** in rural Indian terrain with reasonable EIRP, while 2.4 GHz WiFi requires near-line-of-sight and high towers.

---

## 3.4 Derivation D — FSO Range in Clear vs Foggy Weather

**Given:** $P_t = 10$ mW = **10 dBm**, $G_t = G_r$ = 0 dBi (optical), $d_R = 10$ cm, $d = 2$ km, $S_r = -40$ dBm.

**Step 1 — Compute geometric loss $G_{geo}$:**

$$G_{geo}(dB) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{d_{R}}{d}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.1}{2000}\right) = -86.02 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Apply clear-weather atmospheric loss $V = 10$ km:**

$$\sigma_{clear} = 17/10 = 1.7 \text{ dB/km} \Rightarrow L_{atm} = 1.7 \times 2 = 3.4 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Total loss (clear):**

$$L_{total,clear} = 86.02 + 3.4 = 89.42 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Received power (clear):**

$$P_{r,clear} = 10 - 89.42 = -79.42 \text{ dBm} \;\; < -40 \text{ dBm} \Rightarrow \text{FAIL!}$$

**Step 5 — Try $V = 50$ km (very clear, mountain region):**

$$\sigma = 17/50 = 0.34 \text{ dB/km} \Rightarrow L_{atm} = 0.68 \text{ dB}$$

$$P_r = 10 - 86.02 - 0.68 = -76.70 \text{ dBm} \;\;\text{(still below threshold)}$$

**Step 6 — Increase aperture to 30 cm:**

$$G_{geo} = 20\log_{10}(0.3/2000) = -76.48 \text{ dB}$$

$$P_r = 10 - 76.48 - 0.68 = -67.16 \text{ dBm} \;\;\text{(with 27 dB margin)} \Rightarrow \text{PASS}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Lesson:** A 30 cm receiver dish (vs 10 cm) yields a **20\log(3) ≈ 9.5 dB** gain — enough to convert an unworkable FSO link into a robust one. This is why commercial FSO systems use **30–50 cm Cassegrain telescopes**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What?

| Use Case | Best Fit | Why |
|----------|----------|-----|
| Inter-village backbone (5–50 km) | **BharatNet (OFC)** | Highest capacity, lowest long-term cost |
| Distant hamlet with TV towers (10–30 km) | **TVWS** | NLOS capability, low infrastructure |
| Cluster of villages around a tower (1–8 km) | **Long-Range WiFi** | Reuses existing CPEs |
| Two buildings across a river/highway (0.5–3 km) | **FSO** | No spectrum license, high bandwidth |
| Disaster-zone temporary link | **FSO or Long-Range WiFi** | Rapid deployment |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
