# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the three-tier architecture of BharatNet. List one advantage of using aerial OFC over buried OFC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
BharatNet follows a three-tier architecture:
1. **Tier-1 (Backbone):** State → District over DWDM-based OFC rings carrying 100 Gbps.
2. **Tier-2 (Aggregation):** District → Block → Gram Panchayat using **GPON** (1:64 split, 2.488 Gbps downstream).
3. **Tier-3 (Last-Mile):** Gram Panchayat → households, delivered by local CSPs via WiFi/FTTH/VSAT.

**Advantage of aerial OFC (1 mark):** It is strung on existing **11 kV / 33 kV electric poles**, eliminating trenching cost (saves ~80% civil cost) and reducing deployment time from years to months — a critical factor for rural rollout.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is TV White Space? Why is the UHF band suitable for rural broadband?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**TV White Space (TVWS)** (1 mark): Portions of the TV broadcast spectrum (470–790 MHz in India) that are *allocated but unused* at a given geographic location and time. TVWS devices operate as **secondary users** and must vacate if a primary TV transmitter is detected.

**Why UHF suits rural areas (2 marks):**
1. **Low free-space path loss:** At 600 MHz, the loss for 10 km is only ~108 dB (vs ~120 dB at 2.4 GHz), a **12 dB advantage**.
2. **Excellent NLOS diffraction:** UHF waves bend around trees, huts, and small hills — typical of rural terrain.
3. **Long wavelengths (λ ≈ 0.5 m)** require only modest antenna sizes for high gain, easing CPE design.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 4]`

**(a) Describe the architecture of BharatNet and explain how GPON enables cost-effective rural broadband. Discuss the role of OLT, ONT, and optical splitter. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

BharatNet is structured in **three hierarchical tiers**:

- **Tier 1 — Backbone (State → District → Block):** A high-capacity DWDM-based OFC ring operating at 100 Gbps per wavelength. This ring provides **non-blocking transport** between state capitals, district HQs, and block offices.
- **Tier 2 — Aggregation (Block → Gram Panchayat):** Implemented using **GPON** as per ITU-T G.984. A single OLT port at the Block serves up to **128 ONTs** via a **passive** 1:64 optical splitter. The downstream wavelength **1490 nm** carries 2.488 Gbps; upstream **1310 nm** carries 1.244 Gbps (TDM-based).
- **Tier 3 — Last-Mile (GP → Households):** Delivered by local Cable Service Providers and ISPs using WiFi hotspots, FTTH drops, or VSAT fallback.

**Role of GPON Components:**

| Component | Function | Location |
|-----------|----------|----------|
| **OLT (Optical Line Terminal)** | Central controller, frame scheduler, bandwidth allocator | Block HQ |
| **Splitter (1:64)** | Passive power divider; no power, no maintenance | Street-level pole |
| **ONT (Optical Network Terminal)** | Converts optical to electrical Ethernet | Gram Panchayat / customer premise |

**Why GPON is cost-effective (3 marks):**
1. **Passive splitters** eliminate the need for powered roadside cabinets.
2. **Up to 64 ONTs share one OLT port and one feeder fiber**, reducing fiber-km cost by ~64×.
3. **Long reach (20 km)** enables one OLT to serve many villages, dramatically lowering per-village capex.
4. **Standardized 2.488 Gbps capacity** is over-provisioned for current rural demand (100 Mbps per GP), ensuring future headroom.

---

**(b) A BharatNet GPON link uses a 1:32 splitter (loss 17 dB), +3 dBm OLT launch power, ONT sensitivity −27 dBm, fiber loss 0.35 dB/km, and 3 dB engineering margin. Compute the maximum allowable fiber length. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Write the optical power-budget equation (2 marks):**

$$P_{TX} - L_{fiber} - L_{splitter} - M_{margin} \geq S_r$$

**Step 2 — Substitute given values (2 marks):**

$$+3 - 0.35 L - 17 - 3 \geq -27$$

**Step 3 — Simplify (1 mark):**

$$-17 - 0.35 L \geq -27$$

$$-0.35 L \geq -10$$

$$L \leq \frac{10}{0.35} = 28.57 \text{ km}$$

**Step 4 — Apply engineering safety margin of 2 km (1 mark):**

$$L_{design} = 26.57 \text{ km}$$

**Step 5 — State the final answer with units (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{L_{max} = 26.57 \text{ km} \;\;(\text{approximately } 27 \text{ km})}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often *forget the splitter loss* (treating it as 0 dB) and arrive at $L = 42.8$ km — a wildly unsafe answer. The splitter is the single largest fixed loss in any GPON link. Always draw a **left-to-right link budget diagram** showing OLT → Fiber → Splitter → Fiber → ONT.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4]` *(Internal Choice)*

**(a) Explain the concept of TV White Space. Describe how cognitive radio and geolocation databases enable spectrum sharing. Compute the free-space path loss for a TVWS link at 600 MHz over 20 km. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**TVWS Concept (2 marks):**
TV White Space refers to **geographically and temporally unused portions of the UHF/VHF TV broadcast band (470–790 MHz in India)**. After India's analog-to-digital TV switchover (completed 2017), large swathes of spectrum are unused. TVWS devices opportunistically use this spectrum as **secondary users** without causing harmful interference to incumbent TV receivers.

**Cognitive Radio + Geolocation DB (3 marks):**
1. **Geolocation database:** A centralized server (operated by a regulator-approved entity) maintains real-time records of which TV channels are in use at every latitude/longitude. Before transmitting, the TVWS base station queries the DB with its GPS coordinates.
2. **Maximum EIRP assignment:** The DB returns the *available channels* and the *maximum permissible EIRP* such that aggregate interference at any TV receiver stays below the protection threshold (typically **−114 dBm** for DTT).
3. **Spectrum sensing:** The base station may also perform in-band sensing (energy detection, cyclostationary detection) to verify that no TV signal is present. If a primary user is detected, the device **must vacate the channel within 4 seconds** (FCC/CEPT requirement).

**Path-Loss Computation (2 marks):**

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}(d)$$

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(600) + 20\log_{10}(20)$$

$$L_{fs} = 32.44 + 55.56 + 26.02 = 114.02 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{L_{fs} \approx 114 \text{ dB}}$$

---

**(b) Compare Long-Range WiFi and Free Space Optics for rural backhaul. Which would you deploy for a 4 km river-crossing link that experiences dense winter fog, and why? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Long-Range WiFi (5 GHz) | FSO (1550 nm) |
|-----------|-------------------------|---------------|
| Spectrum license | Not required (ISM) | Not required |
| Bandwidth | 5–20 Mbps typical | 100 Mbps – 1 Gbps |
| Range | 1–8 km | 0.5–3 km (clear), 0.5–1 km (fog) |
| Atmospheric sensitivity | Low (rain fade ~5 dB/km) | **Very high** (fog: 30–100 dB/km) |
| Line of sight | Near-LOS acceptable | Strict LOS + pointing stability |
| Security | Encrypted at MAC layer | Highly secure (narrow beam) |
| Cost per link | $300–800 | $3000–15000 |
| RF interference | Susceptible to WiFi noise | Immune to RF |

**Deployment Decision (3 marks):**

For a **4 km river-crossing link with dense winter fog**, **Long-Range WiFi is the correct choice**. Justification:

1. **FSO is fundamentally defeated by fog.** Using the Kim model, with $V = 0.2$ km in dense fog: $\sigma = 17/0.2 = 85$ dB/km. Over 4 km, atmospheric loss alone is **340 dB** — the link is mathematically impossible regardless of aperture size.
2. **WiFi at 5 GHz suffers only ~0.5 dB/km rain fade**; fog particles are essentially transparent to microwaves (they are tens of microns, while 5 GHz wavelength is ~6 cm).
3. **WiFi is tolerant of slight misalignment** (omnidirectional receiver vs narrow laser beam) — important across a river where tower sway from wind can break the FSO pointing lock.
4. **Lower cost** allows redundant backhaul links (two WiFi radios in 2+0 configuration) for high availability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common 3-mark loss occurs when students give a *generic* "FSO is better" answer without quantifying the fog loss. The examiner explicitly checks for a numerical estimate (Kim model) demonstrating why FSO fails in fog. Always compute $\sigma$ before recommending FSO!

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **BharatNet** = India's 3-tier digital highway: State → District → Block → Gram Panchayat via GPON; uses **aerial OFC on power poles** to avoid trenching.
- ✅ **GPON power-budget formula:** $L_{max} = (P_{TX} - S_r - L_{splitter} - M) / C_L$ — always in dB/dBm.
- ✅ **TVWS** = secondary use of UHF TV band (470–790 MHz); **12 dB advantage** over 2.4 GHz due to lower free-space loss.
- ✅ **Long-Range WiFi** = high-gain directional antenna (16–24 dBi) + TDMA MAC + 30+ dBm Tx power; max practical range ~8 km.
- ✅ **FSO** = laser (1550 nm) over free air; fog is its **Achilles heel** — Kim model: $\sigma = 17/V$ dB/km.
- ✅ **Friis FSPL formula:** $L_{fs} = 32.44 + 20\log f + 20\log d$ — **f in MHz, d in km**.
- ✅ **Fresnel zone** radius: $r_1 = 8.656 \sqrt{d/4f}$ — need **60% clearance** for reliable link.
- ✅ **Cognitive radio + geo-DB** is the regulatory backbone of TVWS; 4-second vacate rule applies.
- ✅ **Hybrid deployments are the norm:** BharatNet (backbone) → GPON (aggregation) → WiFi/TVWS/FSO (last mile) → WiFi/FTTH (access).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "B-T-W-F":** **B**haratNet (backbone), **T**VWS (long NLOS), **W**iFi (mid-range), **F**SO (short high-bandwidth) — remember this order of deployment in any rural-stack answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Terrestrial Fronthaul/Backhaul Solutions: LEOs, HAP/UAV
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT741 - ADVANCED MOBILE COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Non-Terrestrial Fronthaul/Backhaul Solutions: LEOs, HAP/UAV

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Non-Terrestrial Network (NTN)?

According to the **3GPP TR 38.811 / TR 38.821** specifications adopted by KTU 2024 syllabus, a **Non-Terrestrial Network (NTN)** refers to a network where a satellite or unmanned aerial system (UAS) is leveraged to provide fronthaul or backhaul connectivity between the core network and the terrestrial user equipment (UE), or to extend the radio access network (RAN) into remote, unserved, or underserved geographical regions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** NTN refers to any network that uses **spaceborne (satellites)** or **airborne platforms (HAPs/UAVs)** as part of the 3GPP access architecture to deliver fronthaul, backhaul, or direct radio access services.

### 1.2 Fronthaul vs Backhaul – The Two Distinct Traffic Planes

| Plane | Purpose | Typical Bandwidth | Latency Tolerance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fronthaul** | Connects **CU/DU (Baseband Unit)** to **RU (Radio Unit)** using CPRI/eCPRI | **Very High (Gbps)** | **<100 µs** (strict) |
| **Backhaul** | Connects **gNB/cell site** to **Core Network (5GC/EPC)** | Moderate (100s of Mbps) | **<10 ms** (5G) |

> [!NOTE]
> In NTN context, LEO/HAP platforms **cannot reliably carry fronthaul** (especially CPRI) due to high propagation delay and Doppler. They are dominantly used for **backhaul** and **direct access** scenarios.

### 1.3 LEO Satellites – Definition & Intuition

A **Low Earth Orbit (LEO) satellite** is a satellite that orbits the Earth at altitudes between **300 km and 2000 km**, with orbital periods between **~88 minutes and ~127 minutes**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Constant:** LEO altitude ≈ **500–1200 km** (most common operational range), Velocity ≈ **7.5 km/s** (≈ Mach 22), Orbital Period ≈ **90–110 minutes**.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine the Earth as a giant dinner plate and satellites as fireflies hovering at varying heights above it. LEO satellites are the fireflies flying **just inches above the plate** — they move fast, give bright signals, but only stay above any single town for a few minutes before flying past. A constellation of thousands of such fireflies ensures one is always overhead (like Starlink).

### 1.4 HAP and UAV – Definition & Intuition

- **HAP (High Altitude Platform):** An airborne station (balloon, airship, or fixed-wing aircraft) positioned at altitudes of **20 km to 50 km** in the stratosphere, quasi-stationary above a service area.
- **UAV (Unmanned Aerial Vehicle) / Drone:** A low-altitude (typically **<5 km**) aerial platform used for localized coverage, emergency response, or temporary capacity boost.

**Conceptual Analogy:** A HAP is like a **"tower in the sky"** — a cellular base station mounted on a slow-moving blimp parked 25 km above your city, providing blanket 5G coverage to a metropolitan region. A UAV is more like a **"flying hotspot"** — a temporary base station tethered to a drone hovering 100 m above a stadium during a cricket match.

### 1.5 Why NTN for Fronthaul/Backhaul? (The Driving Forces)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight – The Four Use-Cases of NTN Backhaul:**
> 1. **Coverage Extension:** Serving rural, maritime, and mountainous regions where terrestrial fiber is uneconomical.
> 2. **Resilience & Redundancy:** Backup link when terrestrial fiber is cut (e.g., natural disasters).
> 3. **Mobility Support:** Continuous connectivity for high-speed trains, aircraft, and ships (V2X, A2X).
> 4. **Rapid Deployment:** Tactical/emergency networks deployable within hours.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 NTN Architectural Reference Models (3GPP TR 38.811)

The 3GPP defines **two principal reference architectures** for NTN:

**Architecture A — Transparent (Bent-Pipe) Payload:**
The satellite simply acts as a **radio frequency relay**; all gNB functions remain on the ground. Most LEO backhaul systems (e.g., Starlink backhaul to ground stations) follow this model.

**Architecture B — Regenerative (On-Board Processing) Payload:**
The satellite hosts the full or partial gNB stack (gNB-DU). The UE communicates directly with the satellite which performs baseband processing.

### 2.2 Link Budget Fundamentals for LEO/HAP Backhaul

For any NTN link, the received power $P_r$ is governed by the classical Friis transmission equation:

$$P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2 \cdot L_{other}^{-1}$$

Where:
- $P_t$ = Transmit power (W)
- $G_t, G_r$ = Transmit and receive antenna gains (linear, dimensionless)
- $\lambda$ = Carrier wavelength (m)
- $d$ = Slant range between Tx and Rx (m)
- $L_{other}$ = Aggregate of other losses (atmospheric, rain, pointing)

### 2.3 Free Space Path Loss (FSPL) — The Dominant Loss

The single most important quantity in any LEO/HAP link is the **Free Space Path Loss (FSPL)**:

$$FSPL = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4\pi d \cdot f}{c}\right)^2$$

Expressed in decibels:

$$FSPL_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

### 2.4 Atmospheric & Additional Losses

For LEO links operating at Ku/Ka-band, additional impairments include:
- **Atmospheric Gaseous Absorption ($L_{atm}$)**: 0.1–1 dB at Ku; up to 2–4 dB at Ka
- **Rain Attenuation ($L_{rain}$)**: 1–10 dB depending on rain rate (mm/hr) and frequency
- **Scintillation ($L_{scint}$)**: 0.2–0.5 dB (mostly at low elevation angles)
- **Pointing Loss ($L_{point}$)**: 0.5–3 dB (dominant in LEO due to fast motion)

### 2.5 LEO Geometry — Elevation Angle & Slant Range

Given a LEO satellite at altitude $h$ and an Earth station at elevation angle $\theta$, the slant range is:

$$d = \sqrt{R_E^2 \sin^2\theta + 2R_E h + h^2} - R_E \sin\theta$$

Where $R_E$ = Earth's radius = **6371 km**.

### 2.6 Propagation Delay — The Critical NTN Parameter

The one-way propagation delay for a signal traversing distance $d$:

$$\tau_{prop} = \frac{d}{c} \quad \text{seconds}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Numerical Anchor:**
> - **LEO 600 km, elevation 90°** (zenith): $\tau \approx$ **2 ms**
> - **LEO 1200 km, elevation 10°** (low elevation): $\tau \approx$ **13.4 ms**
> - **GEO 35786 km**: $\tau \approx$ **240 ms** (one-way)
> - **HAP at 25 km**: $\tau \approx$ **83 µs** (negligible)

### 2.7 Doppler Shift for LEO — A High-Yield KTU Topic

The maximum Doppler shift experienced at the UE/gateway:

$$f_{doppler} = f_c \cdot \frac{v_{sat}}{c} \cdot \cos\alpha$$

Where $\alpha$ is the angle between the satellite velocity vector and the line-of-sight to the user.

> [!WARNING]
> For LEO at Ku-band (12 GHz), $f_{doppler}$ can reach **±48 kHz** — this is **10,000× greater than for GEO** and breaks standard LTE/NR synchronization procedures. 3GPP Release 17/18 mandates **pre-compensation** at the gNB.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables | Units | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $FSPL_{dB} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | $d$ range, $f$ freq | dB | Backhaul link budget |
| 2 | $\tau_{prop} = d/c$ | $d$ slant range | seconds | Latency budget |
| 3 | $f_d = f_c \cdot v_{sat}/c \cdot \cos\alpha$ | $f_c$, $v$, $\alpha$ | Hz | Doppler comp. |
| 4 | $d = \sqrt{R_E^2 \sin^2\theta + 2R_E h + h^2} - R_E \sin\theta$ | $h$, $\theta$ | km | Slant range calc |
| 5 | $EIRP_{dBW} = P_{t,dBW} + G_{t,dBi}$ | Tx power, gain | dBW | Backhaul EIRP |
| 6 | $\frac{C}{N_0} = EIRP - FSPL + G/T - k$ | link parameters | dB-Hz | Carrier-to-noise |
| 7 | $C/N = \frac{C/N_0}{B}$ | noise density, bandwidth | dB | SNR for modulation |
| 8 | $G_{ant} = 10\log_{10}\left[\eta(\pi D f / c)^2\right]$ | dish diameter $D$, efficiency $\eta$ | dBi | Antenna gain |
| 9 | $h_{min} = R_E\left(\sec\theta_{min} - 1\right)$ | min elevation $\theta_{min}$ | km | Min sat altitude |
| 10 | $T_{orbit} = 2\pi\sqrt{a^3/\mu}$ | semi-major axis $a$ | s | Orbital period |

**Constants used throughout KTU problems:**
- $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- $R_E = 6371$ km
- $k_{Boltzmann} = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $\mu_{Earth} = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s²

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LEO Backhaul** powers Starlink, OneWeb, Kuiper — providing 100+ Mbps backhaul to remote cellular towers.
- **HAP backhaul** is being trialed by Google Loon (defunct), HAPS Mobile (SoftBank), and Airbus Zephyr for 5G coverage.
- **UAV backhaul** is critical in disaster zones where terrestrial fiber is destroyed (e.g., post-earthquake Puerto Rico, 2017).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Slant Range of LEO Satellite at Given Elevation Angle

**Given:** A LEO satellite at altitude $h$ above Earth's surface. An Earth station observes the satellite at elevation angle $\theta$ above the horizon. Find the slant range $d$.

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup:**
Construct a triangle with vertices: (1) Earth's center $O$, (2) Earth station $E$, (3) Satellite $S$. The triangle has sides:
- $\overline{OE} = R_E$ (Earth's radius)
- $\overline{OS} = R_E + h$ (radius to satellite)
- $\overline{ES} = d$ (slant range, what we want)
- The angle at $E$ between the local horizontal and line-of-sight to satellite is $\theta$.

**Step 2 — Interior Angle at Earth Center:**
The local vertical at the Earth station $E$ points radially outward (from $O$ to $E$). The line-of-sight $ES$ makes angle $\theta$ with this local vertical. By the geometry of the triangle, the angle at vertex $E$ inside the triangle is $(90° + \theta)$.

Therefore, by the angle sum property:

$$\angle O + (90° + \theta) + \angle S = 180°$$

Hence the central angle subtended at Earth's center is:

$$\angle O = 90° - \theta$$

**Step 3 — Apply Law of Cosines:**
Apply the law of cosines on side $\overline{OS}$:

$$(R_E + h)^2 = R_E^2 + d^2 - 2 R_E d \cos(90° + \theta)$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the Cosine Term:**
Using the identity $\cos(90° + \theta) = -\sin\theta$:

$$(R_E + h)^2 = R_E^2 + d^2 + 2 R_E d \sin\theta$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into Quadratic in $d$:**

$$d^2 + 2 R_E \sin\theta \cdot d + R_E^2 - (R_E + h)^2 = 0$$

Expand $(R_E + h)^2 = R_E^2 + 2R_E h + h^2$:

$$d^2 + 2 R_E \sin\theta \cdot d - 2 R_E h - h^2 = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve the Quadratic Using the Positive Root:**

$$d = \frac{-2 R_E \sin\theta + \sqrt{4 R_E^2 \sin^2\theta + 4(2R_E h + h^2)}}{2}$$

$$d = -R_E \sin\theta + \sqrt{R_E^2 \sin^2\theta + 2 R_E h + h^2}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{d = \sqrt{R_E^2 \sin^2\theta + 2 R_E h + h^2} - R_E \sin\theta}$$

**Numerical Sanity Check:**
For $h = 600$ km, $\theta = 90°$ (zenith): $d = \sqrt{0 + 2(6371)(600) + 360000} - 0 = \sqrt{8005200} \approx$ **600 km** ✓ (straight up).

For $h = 600$ km, $\theta = 0°$ (horizon): $d = \sqrt{0 + 7645200 + 360000} - 0 \approx$ **2870 km** ✓ (matches 3GPP TR 38.811 Table 5.1-1).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Maximum Doppler Shift for LEO at Zenith Pass

**Given:** LEO satellite at altitude $h$ passing directly overhead (zenith) at its closest approach. Compute the maximum Doppler shift at frequency $f_c$.

**Step 1 — Compute Orbital Velocity:**
Using circular orbit mechanics, equate centripetal force to gravitational force:

$$\frac{v_{sat}^2}{R_E + h} = \frac{\mu}{(R_E + h)^2}$$

Solving:

$$v_{sat} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{R_E + h}}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Velocity Components at Zenith:**
At the moment of closest approach (satellite directly overhead), the satellite velocity vector is **tangent to the line-of-sight** (perpendicular to the radial direction from Earth station to satellite). Hence the angle $\alpha$ between $\vec{v}_{sat}$ and the line-of-sight is **90°**, giving:

$$\cos\alpha = \cos(90°) = 0$$

Wait — at **exact zenith**, the relative radial velocity is zero, so the Doppler shift is momentarily zero. The **maximum Doppler** occurs just before/after zenith. Let's compute it correctly.

**Step 3 — General Doppler Expression:**
For a moving observer at fixed position with satellite at range $r$ and range rate $\dot{r}$:

$$f_d = -f_c \cdot \frac{\dot{r}}{c}$$

**Step 4 — Range Rate in Orbit:**
Differentiating the orbit equation with respect to time gives a range rate that is maximum when the satellite is at the horizon (low elevation). For zenith pass, $\dot{r}$ is zero at the zenith, so $f_d = 0$ at the zenith moment.

**Step 5 — Maximum Range Rate Magnitude (At Low Elevation):**
For a satellite with angular velocity $\omega_{sat}$, the maximum range rate magnitude is approximately:

$$|\dot{r}|_{max} \approx v_{sat} \cdot \sin(90°) = v_{sat}$$

So the **peak Doppler** is:

$$\boxed{f_{d,max} = \frac{f_c \cdot v_{sat}}{c}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Computation:**
For $h = 600$ km: $v_{sat} = \sqrt{3.986 \times 10^{14} / (6371000 + 600000)} = \sqrt{57158} \approx 7.56$ km/s

At $f_c = 12$ GHz (Ku-band):

$$f_{d,max} = \frac{12 \times 10^9 \times 7.56 \times 10^3}{3 \times 10^8} = 302.4 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value (≈ ±300 kHz at Ku, ≈ ±480 kHz at Ka) is **orders of magnitude larger** than the sub-Hz Doppler of GEO satellites and forces significant pre-compensation in 3GPP NTN systems.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Minimum Altitude for Given Minimum Elevation Angle

**Given:** An NTN service requires a minimum elevation angle $\theta_{min}$ (so that buildings/terrain don't block line-of-sight). Derive the minimum altitude at which a satellite must fly.

**Step 1 — Take the Expression for $d$ and Set $\theta = \theta_{min}$:**
This corresponds to the **maximum slant range** for which the satellite is just barely visible above the horizon at $\theta_{min}$.

**Step 2 — Geometric Interpretation:**
At the minimum elevation angle, the line-of-sight is tangent to a cone of half-angle $\theta_{min}$ around the local vertical. The satellite's orbital shell intersects this geometry.

**Step 3 — Derivation Using Spherical Geometry:**
At $\theta = \theta_{min}$, the line-of-sight just grazes the horizon. The geometry yields:

$$h_{min} = R_E \left(\frac{1}{\cos\theta_{min}} - 1\right)$$

**Step 4 — Verification at $\theta_{min} = 0°$:**
$h_{min} = R_E (1/1 - 1) = 0$ ✓ (a satellite at zero altitude is just on the horizon).

**Step 5 — Verification at $\theta_{min} = 90°$:**
$h_{min} = R_E(1/0 - 1) \to \infty$ ✓ (you can never be directly overhead at 90° if you're constrained to the horizon).

**Step 6 — Numerical Example (KTU standard):**
For $\theta_{min} = 20°$: $h_{min} = 6371 \times (1/0.9397 - 1) = 6371 \times 0.0642 \approx$ **409 km**.

So a LEO satellite at 600 km is comfortably above the minimum altitude for 20° elevation.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Backhaul Link Budget (C/N₀ for LEO Backhaul)

**Given:** A LEO backhaul link at 14 GHz, slant range 800 km, gateway EIRP = 60 dBW, satellite G/T = 10 dB/K. Compute the carrier-to-noise spectral density.

**Step 1 — Free Space Path Loss:**

$$FSPL = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(800) + 20\log_{10}(14000)$$

$$FSPL = 32.45 + 58.06 + 82.92 = 173.43 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Compute C/N₀:**

$$\frac{C}{N_0} = EIRP - FSPL + \frac{G}{T} - k_{B}$$

In dB form: $k_B = 10\log_{10}(1.38 \times 10^{-23}) = -228.6$ dB/K/Hz

$$\frac{C}{N_0} = 60 - 173.43 + 10 - (-228.6) = 125.17 \text{ dB-Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to C/N for a 10 MHz channel:**

$$\frac{C}{N} = \frac{C}{N_0} - 10\log_{10}(B) = 125.17 - 70 = 55.17 \text{ dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A C/N of **55 dB** comfortably supports **64-QAM with 3/4 LDPC coding** in a 10 MHz backhaul carrier, delivering **~30 Mbps throughput** per carrier — typical for LEO cellular backhaul.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative NTN Backhaul Technologies

| Parameter | **GEO** | **MEO** | **LEO** | **HAP** | **UAV** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Altitude | 35,786 km | 5,000–12,000 km | 500–1200 km | 20–50 km | 0.1–5 km |
| One-way delay | ~240 ms | ~50–80 ms | ~2–13 ms | ~70–170 µs | ~0.3–17 µs |
| Path loss (dB, 14 GHz) | ~205 | ~190 | ~165–175 | ~133 | ~110–125 |
| Doppler | < 1 Hz | ±10 kHz | ±300 kHz | < 100 Hz | < 50 Hz |
| Coverage/beam | Continental | Regional | Local cluster | City (50 km radius) | Stadium (5 km) |
| Backhaul suitability | Poor (latency) | Limited | **Excellent** | **Very Good** | **Local only** |
| Fronthaul (CPRI) | No | No | Marginal | Yes (with eCPRI) | Yes |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]**
*Define Non-Terrestrial Network (NTN) as per 3GPP. List any two NTN platform types.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Non-Terrestrial Network (NTN)** is a network that uses spaceborne (satellites) or airborne platforms (HAPs, UAVs) as part of the 3GPP access architecture to provide fronthaul, backhaul, or direct radio access to the user equipment.

Two platform types:
1. **Spaceborne platforms** — GEO, MEO, LEO satellites (altitude 300–35,786 km).
2. **Airborne platforms** — HAPs (20–50 km) and UAVs/drones (<5 km).

**(1 mark definition + 1 mark for each platform = 3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model)]**
*Why is GEO satellite unsuitable for 5G fronthaul transmission?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A GEO satellite at 35,786 km altitude introduces a one-way propagation delay of approximately **240 ms**, leading to a round-trip time (RTT) of about **480 ms**. This violates the stringent latency budgets of:
- **5G URLLC** (< 1 ms one-way)
- **5G eMBB** (< 4 ms one-way for some applications)
- **Fronthaul (CPRI/eCPRI)** (< 100 µs)

Additionally, GEO satellites cannot host **on-board baseband processing** for regenerative architectures due to payload limitations and the need for Doppler compensation (which is negligible for GEO anyway, but the latency alone disqualifies it).

**(3 marks — emphasize the 240 ms number, as KTU examiners award specific credit for the figure.)**

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 (Model)]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **two NTN reference architectures** defined in 3GPP TR 38.811 (Transparent vs Regenerative payload). Compare their merits for LEO-based 5G backhaul. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A LEO satellite orbits at **800 km** altitude. An Earth station observes it at an elevation angle of **30°**. Compute:
  (i) The slant range between the Earth station and the satellite.
  (ii) The one-way propagation delay.
  (iii) The maximum Doppler shift at carrier frequency **12 GHz**.
  (Take $R_E = 6371$ km, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $\mu = 3.986 \times 10^{14}$ m³/s²). **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-A (a):**

| Feature | **Transparent (Bent-Pipe)** | **Regenerative (On-Board Processing)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Block Diagram** | UE ↔ Satellite (RF relay) ↔ Gateway ↔ Core | UE ↔ Satellite (gNB-DU) ↔ Gateway ↔ Core |
| **Baseband Processing** | On ground only | On board satellite (partial or full gNB) |
| **Latency** | Higher (bent-pipe adds no extra delay but all processing is ground-side) | Lower for inter-satellite routing |
| **Complexity in Space** | Low | High (radiation-hardened gNB) |
| **Spectrum Reuse** | Single-hop, no inter-satellite link (ISL) | Enables ISLs (laser/microwave) |
| **Use Case** | Starlink v1, OneWeb — backhaul to ground gateway | Starlink v2, Amazon Kuiper — direct-to-cell |

**Comparison for 5G Backhaul:**
- **Transparent** is sufficient when the ground gateway has a fiber connection to the core (typical backhaul scenario). Latency = 1 × slant range / c.
- **Regenerative** is preferred when multiple satellites must be daisy-chained via **Inter-Satellite Links (ISLs)** to route traffic across the constellation without bouncing off the ground.

**(7 marks: 2 for diagram + 3 for architectural detail + 2 for comparison)**

---

**Solution to Q-A (b):**

**(i) Slant Range at $\theta = 30°$, $h = 800$ km:**

$$d = \sqrt{R_E^2 \sin^2\theta + 2R_E h + h^2} - R_E \sin\theta$$

Substitute $R_E = 6371$ km, $h = 800$ km, $\sin 30° = 0.5$:

$$d = \sqrt{(6371)^2 (0.5)^2 + 2(6371)(800) + (800)^2} - (6371)(0.5)$$

Compute each term inside the square root:
- $(6371)^2 \times 0.25 = 40,589,641 \times 0.25 = 10,147,410.25$
- $2 \times 6371 \times 800 = 10,193,600$
- $(800)^2 = 640,000$
- Sum = $20,981,010.25$
- $\sqrt{20,981,010.25} \approx 4580.5$ km
- $R_E \sin\theta = 6371 \times 0.5 = 3185.5$ km

$$\boxed{d \approx 4580.5 - 3185.5 = 1395 \text{ km}}$$

**(ii) One-Way Propagation Delay:**

$$\tau = \frac{d}{c} = \frac{1,395,000}{3 \times 10^8} \approx 4.65 \text{ ms}$$

**(iii) Maximum Doppler Shift at 12 GHz:**

Orbital velocity:

$$v_{sat} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu}{R_E + h}} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{6,371,000 + 800,000}}$$

$$v_{sat} = \sqrt{\frac{3.986 \times 10^{14}}{7.171 \times 10^6}} = \sqrt{5.559 \times 10^7} \approx 7456 \text{ m/s}$$

Maximum Doppler:

$$f_{d,max} = \frac{f_c \cdot v_{sat}}{c} = \frac{12 \times 10^9 \times 7456}{3 \times 10^8}$$

$$\boxed{f_{d,max} \approx 298.2 \text{ kHz}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1:** Students often confuse the angle in the Doppler formula — the angle is between the **satellite velocity vector** and the **line-of-sight to the user**, not the elevation angle. Maximum Doppler occurs near the horizon (≈ zenith for the satellite vector being tangential). Using $\theta = 30°$ here would be wrong.

**(7 marks: 3 for slant range, 2 for delay, 2 for Doppler)**

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model)]`

**(a)** Compare **LEO satellites, HAPs, and UAVs** as NTN backhaul solutions under the headings: altitude, propagation delay, coverage area, and backhaul suitability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 5G base station at a remote village requires backhaul connectivity. Design a **wireless backhaul link** using a HAP positioned at 20 km altitude with minimum elevation angle of 10°. Calculate:
  (i) The minimum slant range between the HAP and the base station.
  (ii) The corresponding one-way propagation delay.
  (iii) Comment on whether this delay is acceptable for 5G eMBB backhaul. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q-B (a):**

| Parameter | **LEO Satellite** | **HAP** | **UAV** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Altitude** | 500–1200 km | 20–50 km | 0.1–5 km |
| **Propagation Delay (one-way)** | 2–13 ms | 70–170 µs | 0.3–17 µs |
| **Coverage Area** | Regional cluster (per sat) | City-scale (~50 km radius) | Hotspot (~5 km radius) |
| **Backhaul Suitability** | Excellent for remote/rural; latency acceptable for eMBB; Doppler is challenging | Very good for metro/maritime backhaul; quasi-stationary (low Doppler) | Excellent for **temporary** or **emergency** backhaul; cannot provide always-on service due to flight time limits |
| **Mobility** | Very fast (7.5 km/s) — handover heavy | Slow drift (~10s of km/h) | Hover or slow patrol |
| **Cost** | $500M+ constellation | $10–50M per platform | $10K–$1M per drone |
| **Best Use Case** | Connecting **isolated BTS sites** | **City-wide 5G coverage extension** | **Disaster recovery, event-driven** |

**(7 marks: 1.5 per row × 4 rows + 1 for synthesis)**

---

**Solution to Q-B (b):**

**(i) Slant Range at $h = 20$ km, $\theta_{min} = 10°$:**

The minimum slant range corresponds to the **minimum** elevation angle at the edge of the HAP coverage footprint.

$$d = \sqrt{R_E^2 \sin^2\theta + 2R_E h + h^2} - R_E \sin\theta$$

Since $h \ll R_E$, we use the simplified approximation valid for low altitudes:

$$d \approx \frac{h}{\sin\theta} = \frac{20}{\sin 10°} = \frac{20}{0.1736} \approx 115.2 \text{ km}$$

For exact calculation:

$$d = \sqrt{(6371)^2 (0.1736)^2 + 2(6371)(20) + 400} - (6371)(0.1736)$$

$$= \sqrt{1,222,887 + 254,840 + 400} - 1105.9$$

$$= \sqrt{1,478,127} - 1105.9 = 1215.7 - 1105.9 \approx 109.8 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{d \approx 110 \text{ km}}$$

**(ii) One-Way Propagation Delay:**

$$\tau = \frac{110,000}{3 \times 10^8} \approx 367 \text{ \mu s}$$

**(iii) Comment on Acceptability:**

5G eMBB backhaul has a typical one-way latency budget of **4–10 ms**. A HAP-induced delay of **367 µs** is **< 10% of the budget** and is **highly acceptable** for eMBB services. Additionally, for **URLLC** (1 ms budget), 367 µs still leaves 633 µs margin for processing and forwarding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** HAP backhaul at 20 km altitude with 10° minimum elevation is **fully compliant** with 5G eMBB and **marginally acceptable** for URLLC depending on additional processing delays.

**(7 marks: 3 for slant range, 2 for delay, 2 for the qualitative comment)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing slant range vs ground distance:** Slant range is the line-of-sight distance through 3D space, NOT the great-circle ground distance. (Lose 2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting the angle sum (90° + θ) in geometry:** The interior angle of the triangle at the Earth station is 90° + θ, NOT θ. (Lose 2 marks)
> 3. **Using dB vs linear units inconsistently:** Mixing dBW and W in the same equation. Always convert all to dB OR all to linear. (Lose 1–2 marks)
> 4. **Mis-stating Doppler formula:** Some textbooks write $f_d = (v/c) f_c \cos\alpha$ but neglect to mention this is the radial component. (Lose 1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer:** KTU deducts 0.5 mark for missing km / ms / kHz units.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Quick-Fire Revision (Memorize before exam):**

- **NTN Definition:** 3GPP networks using spaceborne/airborne platforms for access/backhaul.
- **LEO altitude range:** **500–1200 km**; MEO: 5,000–12,000 km; GEO: **35,786 km**.
- **HAP altitude:** **20–50 km**; UAV altitude: **<5 km**.
- **LEO one-way delay:** **2–13 ms** (vs GEO's 240 ms).
- **LEO max Doppler (Ku 12 GHz):** **~300 kHz** (vs GEO's <1 Hz).
- **FSPL formula (dB):** $32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$.
- **Propagation delay formula:** $\tau = d/c$.
- **Orbital velocity formula:** $v_{sat} = \sqrt{\mu/(R_E + h)}$.
- **Slant range formula:** $d = \sqrt{R_E^2 \sin^2\theta + 2R_E h + h^2} - R_E \sin\theta$.
- **Min altitude for given elevation:** $h_{min} = R_E(\sec\theta_{min} - 1)$.
- **Transparent vs Regenerative payload:** Transparent = RF relay only; Regenerative = on-board gNB-DU processing.
- **Fronthaul is impractical over LEO** (latency too high for CPRI/eCPRI timing); **Backhaul is the primary use case**.
- **3GPP Releases:** Rel-17 (2022) introduced 5G NR-NTN; Rel-18 (2024) expanded to direct-to-device.
- **Real-world operators:** Starlink (LEO), OneWeb (LEO), HAPS Mobile (HAP), Google Loon (HAP — discontinued 2021).
- **Carrier frequencies:** LEO backhaul typically operates in **Ka-band (17–30 GHz)** or **Ku-band (12–18 GHz)**.
- **C/N₀ formula:** $EIRP - FSPL + G/T - k_B$ (in dB).
- **Atmospheric loss to remember:** ~1 dB at Ku zenith, 3–5 dB at Ka zenith, up to 10 dB during heavy rain.
- **Orbital period (Kepler's 3rd):** $T = 2\pi\sqrt{a^3/\mu}$ — for LEO ~90 min; GEO = 24 hours.
